![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-034.jpg?height=104&width=387&top_left_y=1683&top_left_x=815)

Cubic polynomial has either all roots are rational or one is rational and two are irrational. Irrational roots always occur in conjugate pairs.

## RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN ZERO(ES) AND COEFFICIENT OF A POLYNOMIAL

(i) Zero of a linear polynomial $a x+b$, is $x=-\frac{b}{a}$ or $x=-\frac{\text { constant term }}{\operatorname{coefficient} \text { of } x}$
(ii) If quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c=k(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)$, where $k$ is any real constant; then $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zeroes of quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c$, where $a, b, c \in R$ and $a \neq 0$.
$\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}$, i.e., sum of zeroes $=-\frac{\text { coefficient of } x}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$ and $\alpha . \beta=-\frac{c}{a}$ i.e., product of zeroes $=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$
We can obtain the quadratic polynomial $p(x)$ as $p(x)=k\left[x^{2}-\right.$ (sum of zeroes) $x+$ (product of zeroes)]
(iii) If cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d=k(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)(x-\gamma)$, where $k$ is any real constant; then $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ are zeroes of cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ where $a, b, c, d \in \mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{a} \neq 0$, then
$\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{b}{a}, \quad \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a} \quad$ and $\alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{d}{a}$

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 8

If zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$ are $\alpha$ and $\frac{2}{\alpha}$, then find the value of $\alpha$.

## SOLUTION :

Given, $\alpha$ and - are the zeroes of $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$.
Now, product of zeroes $=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \times \frac{2}{\alpha}=2 a \Rightarrow 2=2 \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=1$

## Some useful formulas :

(i) $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta$
(ii) $\alpha^{2}-\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)(\alpha-\beta)=\alpha+\beta \sqrt{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta}$
(iii) $(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta$
(iv) $\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=(\alpha+\beta)^{3}-3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta)$
(v) $\alpha^{3}-\beta^{3}=(\alpha-\beta)^{3}+3 \alpha \beta(\alpha-\beta)$

## DIVISION OF POLYNOMIALS

On dividing a polynomial $p(x)$ by a polynomial $d(x)$, let the quotient be $q(x)$ and the remainder be $r(x)$, then $p(x)=d(x) . q(x)+r(x)$, where either $r(x)=0$ or deg. $r(x)<\operatorname{deg} . d(x)$
Here, Dividend $=p(x)$, Divisor $=d(x)$, Quotient $=q(x)$ and Remainder $=r(x)$.
Division algorithm of a polynomial by a polynomial :
Step 1: Arrange the terms of the dividend and the divisor in descending order of their degrees.
Step 2 : Divide the first term of the dividend by the first term of the divisor to obtain the first term of the quotient.
Step 3 : Multiply all the terms of the divisor by the first term of the quotient and subtract the result from the dividend.
Step 4 : Consider the remainder as new dividend and proceed as before.
Step 5 : Repeat this process till we obtain a remainder which is either 0 or a polynomial of degree less than that of the divisor.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-035.jpg?height=61&width=118&top_left_y=1468&top_left_x=1018)

## Learn More

- Factor Theorem
(i) If ( $x-r$ ) is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, then $r$ is a root of polynomial equation $f(x)=0$.
(ii) Conversely, if the polynomial equation $f(x)=0$ has a root $r$, then $(x-r)$ is factor of polynomial $f(x)$.
- Remainder Theorem:

Let $p(x)$ be any polynomial of degree greater than or equal to one and ' $a$ ' be any real number. If $p(x)$ is divided by linear polynomial ( $x-a$ ), then remainder is $p(a)$.

## ILLUSTRATION : 9

Divide $x^{3}+x^{2}-2 x-30$ by $x-3$

## SOLUTION :

$$
\begin{gathered}
x - 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - 2 x - 3 0 } ( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x + 1 0 \\
-\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}}{4 x^{2}-2 x-30} \\
-\frac{4 x^{2}-12 x}{+} \\
-\frac{10 x-30}{+}
\end{gathered}
$$

$\therefore\left(x^{3}+x^{2}-2 x-30\right) \div(x-3)=x^{2}+4 x+10$

## ILLUSTRATION : 10

Find the remainder obtained on dividing $x^{3}+3$ by $x+1$.

## SOLUTION :

$$
\begin{gathered}
x + 1 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } + 3 } ( x ^ { 2 } - x + 1 \\
x^{3}+x^{2} \\
\frac{--}{-x^{2}+3} \\
\frac{-x^{2}-x}{+\quad+} \\
\frac{x+3}{x+1} \\
\frac{--}{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, remainder $=2$
Alternate Method : To find remainder we can use remainder theorem.
Let $f(x)=x^{3}+3$
Put Divisor $=0$
$\Rightarrow x+1=0 \quad \Rightarrow x=-1$
Then remainder $=f(-1)=(-1)^{3}+3=-1+3=2$
So, 2 is the remainder.

## ILLUSTRATION : 11

Find all the zero(es) of $\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{4}}-\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}-\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\mathbf{6} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{2}$, if you know that two of its zero(es) are $\sqrt{\mathbf{2}}$ and $-\sqrt{\mathbf{2}}$.

## SOLUTION :

Two zeroes are $\sqrt{2}$ and $-\sqrt{2}$. Hence, $(x-\sqrt{2})$ and $(x+\sqrt{2})$ are the factors of given polynomial.
$\Rightarrow(x-\sqrt{2})(x+\sqrt{2})=x^{2}-2$, is a factor of the given polynomial.
Now, we divide the given polynomial by $x^{2}-2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \begin{aligned}
x^{2}-2 \sqrt{\frac{2 x^{2}-3 x+1}{2 x^{4}-3 x^{3}-3 x^{2}+6 x-2}-4 x^{2}} & \\
\frac{-3 x^{3}+x^{2}+6 x-2}{-3 x^{3}} & \quad \text { (First term of quotient is } \frac{2 x^{4}}{x^{2}}=2 x^{2} \text { ) }
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{ll}
x^{2} & -2 \\
- & +
\end{array} \text { (Third term of quotient is } \frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}}=1 \text { ) }
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $2 x^{4}-3 x^{3}-3 x^{2}+6 x-2=\left(x^{2}-2\right)\left(2 x^{2}-3 x+1\right)$.
Now, by splitting $-3 x$, we factorise $2 x^{2}-3 x+1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 x^{2}-3 x+1 & =2 x^{2}-2 x-x+1 \\
& =2 x(x-1)-1(x-1) \\
& =(x-1)(2 x-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the remaining two zero(es) are given by $x=\frac{1}{2}$ and $x=1$. Therefore, the zero(es) of the given polynomial are $\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{2}, \frac{1}{2}$ and 1 .

# CASE STUDY : Division Algorithm, Factor Theorem, Remainder Theorem and zero(es) of the Polynomial 

- Division Algorithm :
$($ Dividend $)=($ Quotient $) \times($ Divisor $)+($ Remainder $)$
- Factor Theorem :
$(x-a)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f(a)=0$
$(x+a)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f(-a)=0$
$(a x-b)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)=0$
- Remainder Theorem :
$(a x+b)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)=0$
If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(x-a)$, then remainder $=f(a)$
If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(x+a)$, then remainder $=f(-a)$
If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(a x-b)$, then remainder $=f\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$
- Zeroes of a Polynomial

If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(a x+b)$, then remainder $=f\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)$
Zeroes of a Polynomial

A number $\alpha$ is a zero of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f(\alpha)=0$.
Number of zero(s) of a polynomial $=$ Degree of the polynomial.
Hence number zero(s) of alinear polynomial, quadratic polynomial, cubic polynomial and biquadratic polynomial are $1,2,3$ and 4 respectively.

CASE-II : To find the quotient and remainder using division algorithm
Illustration: Apply the division algorithm to find the quotient and remainder on dividing $f(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6$, by $g(x)=x+2$.

Sol. We have,
$f(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6$ and $g(x)=x+2$
We find that degree $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=3$ and degree $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=1$. Therefore, quotient $\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x})$ is of degree $3-1=2$ and the remainder $\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x})$ is of degree zero.

Let $\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{ax}^{2}+\mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{c}$ and $\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{k}$.
By using division algorithm, we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) \times \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x}) \\
\Rightarrow \quad & x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6=\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)(x+2)+k \\
\Rightarrow \quad & x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6=a x^{3}+(2 a+b) x^{2}+(2 b+c) x+(2 c+k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating the coefficients of like powers of $x$ and can start term on both sides, we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
1=a & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{3}\right] } \\
-6=2 a+b & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{2}\right] } \\
11=2 b+c & {[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x] } \\
\text { and },-6=2 c+k & {[\text { On equating the coefficients terms }] }
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving these equations, we get $a=1, b=-8, c=27$ and $k=-60$
$\therefore \quad$ Quotient $q(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c=x^{2}-8 x+27$ and, Remainder $r(x)=k=-60$.

CASE-I : To find the remaining zeroes of a polynomial when some of its zeroes are given

Illustration: Obtain all the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=3 x^{4}+6 x^{3}-2 x^{2} -10 x-5$, if two of its zeroes are $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$.

Sol. It is given that $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ are two zeroes of $f(x)$. Therefore, $\left(x-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)$ is a factor of $f(x)$. But, $\left(x-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right) =\left(x^{2}-\frac{5}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{3}\left(3 x^{2}-5\right)$. Therefore, $\left(3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-5\right)$ is a factor of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$.

Let us now divide $f(x)$ by $3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-5$. Using long division method, we obtain

$$
\begin{gathered}
3 x ^ { 2 } - 5 \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 4 } + 6 x ^ { 3 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 0 x - 5 } x ^ { 2 } + 2 x + 1 \\
\frac{3 x^{4}-5 x^{2}}{-\quad+} \\
\begin{array}{c}
6 x^{3}+3 x^{2}-10 x-5 \\
\frac{6 x^{3}-10 x}{+} \\
\frac{3 x^{2}-5}{3 x^{2}-5} \\
\frac{-+}{0}
\end{array}
\end{gathered}
$$

Clearly, Quotient $=x^{2}+2 x+1$ and Remainder $=0$.

By division algorithm, we obtain
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left(3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-5\right)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{x}+1\right)+0$
$\Rightarrow f(x)=(\sqrt{3} x+\sqrt{5})(\sqrt{3} x-\sqrt{5})(x+1)^{2}$
Thus, the factors of $f(x)$ are $\sqrt{3} x+\sqrt{5}, \sqrt{3} x-\sqrt{5}, x+1$ and $x+1$.
Equating each factor to zero, we obtain $x=-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}, \sqrt{\frac{5}{3}},-1,-1$. Hence,
the zeroes of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ are $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}, \sqrt{\frac{5}{3}},-1$ and -1 .

## CASE-III : To check whether a given polynomial in a factor of the other polynomial by applying the Division Algorithm

Illustration: By applying division algorithm prove that the polynomial $g(x)=x^{2}+3 x+1$ is a factor of the polynomial $f(x)=3 x^{4}+5 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+2 x+2$
Sol. We find that degree of $f(x)=4$ and degree $g(x)=2$. Therefore, quotient $q(x)$ is of degree $2(=4-2)$ and the remainder $r(x)$ is of degree 1 or less. Let $q(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ and $r(x)=p x+q$.
Using division algorithm, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=g(x) \times q(x)+r(x) \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^{4}+5 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+2 x+2=\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)\left(x^{2}+3 x+1\right)+(p x+q) \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^{4}+5 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+2 x+2=a x^{4}+(3 a+b) x^{3}+(a+3 b+c) x^{2}+(b+3 c+p) x+(c+q)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating coefficients of various powers of $x$ and constant term from both sides, we get

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
a=3 & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{4}\right]} \\
3 a+b=5 & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{3}\right]} \\
a+c+3 b=-7 & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{2}\right]} \\
b+3 c+p=2 & {[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x]} \\
c+q=2 & {[\text { On equating the constant terms }]}
\end{array}
$$

Solving these equations, we get

$$
a=3, b=-4, c=2, p=0 \text { and } q=0
$$

∴ Quotient $q(x)=3 x^{2}-4 x+2$ and, Remainder $r(x)=0 x+0=0$
Since, remainder $r(x)=0$. Hence, $g(x)$ is a factor of $f(x)$.

## Think Out of the Box

Q. 1. If $x=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}$ is a root of the polynomial $f(x)=2 k x^{2}-7 x+k$, then find other root of the polynomial $f(x)$.
[Hint: $f\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right)=0 \Rightarrow k=\sqrt{3} . \quad$ Ans. $x=\sqrt{3}$ ]
Q. 2. If the quotient obtained on dividing the polynomial $f(x)=3 x^{3}+11 x^{2}+34 x+106$ by $g(x)=x-3$ using division algorithm is $3 x^{2}+p x+q$, then find $p$, $q$ and also the reminder.
[Hint: $f(x)=g(x)\left(3 x^{2}+20 x+94\right)+388$.
Ans. $p=20, q=94$, remainder $=388$ ]

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. $f(x)=x^{2}-2 x-3$. Find the roots of $f(x)$, and sketch the graph of $y=f(x)$.

Sol. $f(x)=x^{2}-2 x-3=(x+1)(x-3)$. Therefore, the roots are -1 and 3 .
The graph intersects the X -axis at $x=-1$ and 3 .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=551&width=609&top_left_y=724&top_left_x=758)
2. How many real roots, i.e., roots that are real numbers, has the parabola of each graph ?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=337&width=339&top_left_y=1356&top_left_x=324)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=337&width=335&top_left_y=1356&top_left_x=687)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=357&width=332&top_left_y=1336&top_left_x=1037)

Sol. Graph (a) has two real roots. It has two x -intercepts (i.e., the graph intersects the X -axis at two different points)
Graph (b) has no real roots. It has no x-intercepts. Both roots are complex.
Graph (c) has two real roots which are equal.
3. Inspect the following graph to solve this equation : $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{5}=\mathbf{0}$

Sol. The quadratic polynomial will have the value 0 at $x=-1$ and 5 .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=613&width=684&top_left_y=1941&top_left_x=719)
4. Find the zero(es) of the polynomial $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{- 3}$ and verify the relationship between the zero(es) and the coefficients.

Sol. Recall the identity $a^{2}-b^{2}=(a-b)(a+b)$. Using it, we can write:
$x^{2}-3=(x-\sqrt{3})(x+\sqrt{3})$
So, the value of $x^{2}-3$ is zero when $x=\sqrt{3}$ or $x=-\sqrt{3}$
Therefore, the zeroes of $x^{2}-3$ are $\sqrt{3}$ and $-\sqrt{3}$

Now, Sum of zeroes $=\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3}=0=\frac{-(\text { Coefficient of } x)}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$

$$
\text { Product of zeroes }=(\sqrt{3})(-\sqrt{3})=-3=\frac{-3}{1}=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}
$$

5. Show that $\boldsymbol{x}=\mathbf{2}$ is a root of $\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}+\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{7} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{6}$

Sol. Let $p(x)=2 x^{3}+x^{2}-7 x-6$
$p(2)=2(2)^{3}+(2)^{2}-7(2)-6=16+4-14-6=0$
Hence $x=2$ is a root of $p(x)$.
6. If $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{\mathbf{4}}{\mathbf{3}}$ is a root of the polynomial $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})=\mathbf{6} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}-\mathbf{1 1} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\boldsymbol{k} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{2 0}$, then find the value of $\boldsymbol{k}$.

Sol. Let $f(x)=6 x^{3}-11 x^{2}+k x-20$ and if $x=\frac{4}{3}$ is root of polynomial, then $f\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)=0$
$\therefore f\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)=6\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{3}-11\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2}+k\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)-20=0$
$\Rightarrow 6\left(\frac{64}{27}\right)-11\left(\frac{16}{9}\right)+\frac{4 k}{3}-20=0 \Rightarrow 128-176+12 k-180=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12 k+128-356=0 \Rightarrow 12 k=228 \Rightarrow k=19$
7. Find the remainder when $f(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}+2 x-4$ is divided by $g(x)=1-2 x$.

Sol. Put $1-2 x=0 \Rightarrow 2 x=1 \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}$
Remainder $=f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3}-6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)-4=\frac{1}{8}-\frac{3}{2}+1-4=\frac{1-12+8-32}{8}=-\frac{35}{8}$
8. The polynomials $a x^{3}+3 x^{2}-13$ and $2 x^{3}-5 x+a$ are divided by $x+2$ if the remainder in each case is the same, find the value of $\boldsymbol{a}$.
Sol. Let $p(x)=a x^{3}+3 x^{2}-13$ and $q(x)=2 x^{3}-5 x+a$
When $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ are divided by $x+2=0 \Rightarrow x=-2$
$\therefore p(-2)=q(-2) \Rightarrow a(-2)^{3}+3(-2)^{2}-13=2(-2)^{3}-5(-2)+a \Rightarrow-8 a+12-13=-16+10+a$
$\Rightarrow-9 a=-5 \Rightarrow a=\frac{5}{9}$
9. Find $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ if $\boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{1}$ and $\boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{2}$ are factors of $\boldsymbol{p}(\boldsymbol{x})=\boldsymbol{x}^{3}+3 \boldsymbol{x}^{2}-2 \alpha \boldsymbol{x}+\beta$

Sol. Put $x+1=0$ or $x=-1$ and $x+2=0$ or $x=-2$ in $p(x)$
Then, $p(-1)=0$ and $p(-2)=0$

$$
\Rightarrow p(-1)=(-1)^{3}+3(-1)^{2}-2 \alpha(-1)+\beta=0 \Rightarrow-1+3+2 \alpha+\beta=0 \Rightarrow \beta=-2 \alpha-2
$$

$$
p(-2)=(-2)^{3}+3(-2)^{2}-2 \alpha(-2)+\beta=0 \quad \Rightarrow-8+12+4 \alpha+\beta=0 \Rightarrow \beta=-4 \alpha-4
$$

By equalising equations (i) and (ii), we get
$-2 \alpha-2=-4 \alpha-4 \Rightarrow 2 \alpha=-2 \Rightarrow \alpha=-1$
put $\alpha=-1$ in eq. (i) $\Rightarrow \beta=-2(-1)-2=2-2=0$ Hence, $\alpha=-1, \beta=0$
10. What must be added to $\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}+\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{2} \mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{9}$, so that the result is exactly divisible by $\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\mathbf{7} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{6}$.

Sol. Let $p(x)=3 x^{3}+x^{2}-22 x+9$ and $r(x)=3 x^{2}+7 x-6$
We know that if $p(x)$ is not exactly divisible by $q(x)$, then the remainder be $r(x)$ and degree of $r(x)$ is less than $q(x)$ or divisor.

Let, we add $a x+b$ (linear polynomial) in $p(x)$, so that $p(x)+a x+b$ is exactly divisible by $3 x^{2}+7 x-6$
Hence, $\quad p(x)+a x+b=s(x)=3 x^{3}+x^{2}-22 x+9+a x+b$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=3 x^{3}+x^{2}-x(22-a)+(9+b) \\
3 x ^ { 2 } + 7 x - 6 \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - x ( 2 2 - a ) + 9 + b ( x - 2 } \\
\begin{array}{l}
3 x^{3}+7 x^{2}-6 x \\
-\quad+\quad+ \\
-6 x^{2}+6 x-(22-a) x+9+b
\end{array} \\
\text { or } \\
\begin{array}{l}
-6 x^{2}+x(-16+a)+9+b \\
-6 x^{2}-14 x+12 \\
+\quad- \\
x(-2+a)+(b-3)=0
\end{array}
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, $\quad x(a-2)+b-3=0 . x+0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a-2=0$ and $b-3=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=2$ or $b=3$
Hence, if in $p(x)$ we add $a x+b$ or $2 x+3$ then it is exactly divisible by $3 x^{2}+7 x-6$.

## Alternate Method :

Let $p(x)=3 x^{3}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-22 x+9$
and $\mathrm{r}(x)=3 x^{2}+7 x-6$
Let $q$ be added in polynomial $p(x)$, so that it will be exactly divisible by $r(x)$
Clearly the degree of $q$ will be less than $r(x)$.
So, $p(x)+q=3 x^{3+} x^{2}-22 x+9+q$

$$
\begin{gathered}
3 x ^ { 2 } + 7 x - 6 \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - 2 2 x + 9 + q } ( x - 2 \\
\frac{3 x^{3}+7 x^{2}-6 x}{-\quad+} \\
\frac{-6 x^{2}-16 x+9}{-6 x^{2}-14 x+12} \\
\frac{+\quad+\quad}{-2 x-3+q}
\end{gathered}
$$

Since, $p(x)+q$ is exactly divisible by $r(x)$, then remainder must be zero.
$\therefore-2 x-3+q=0 \Rightarrow q=2 x+3$
Hence, $2 x+3$ must be added to make polynomial exactly divisible by $3 x^{2}+7 x-6$.
11. What must be subtracted from $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}-\mathbf{6} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{1 5} \boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{8 0}$, so that the result is exactly divisible by $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{1 2}$.

Sol. In above problem, we see that if in $p(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-15 x+80$ we subtracted $a x+b$ so that is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+x-12$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q(x) & =x^{3}-6 x^{2}-15 x+80-(a x+b) \\
& =x^{3}-6 x^{2}-(15+a) x+(80-b)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividend $=$ Divisor × quotient + remainder
But remainder will be zero.
∴ Dividend $=$ Divisor × quotient
$q(x)=\left(x^{2}+x-12\right) \times$ quotient
$q(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-(15+a) x+(80-b)=x\left(x^{2}+x-12\right)-7\left(x^{2}+x-12\right)=x^{3}+x^{2}-7 x^{2}-12 x-7 x+84=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-19 x+84$
Hence, $x^{3}-6 x^{2}-19 x+84=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-(15+a) x+(80-b)$
$-15-a=-19 \Rightarrow a=+4$ and $80-b=84 \Rightarrow b=-4$
Hence, if in $p(x)$ we subtracted $4 x-4=(a x+b)$, then it is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+x-12$
Alternate Method :
Factors of $x^{2}+x-12=(x+4)(x-3)$ Put $x^{2}+x-12=0 \Rightarrow(x+4)(x-3)=0$
then either $x=-4$ or $3 q(-4)=(-4)^{3}-6(-4)^{2}-(15+a)(-4)+(80-b) \Rightarrow 4 a-b=20$
and $q(3)=(3)^{3}-6(3)^{2}-(15+a)(3)+80-b \Rightarrow 3 a+b=8$

Solving equations (i) \& (ii), we get

$$
a=4 \& \mathrm{~b}=-4
$$

$\therefore \quad a x+b=4 x-4$, must be subtracted

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which only one is correct.

1. If the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=k^{2} x^{2}-17 x+k+2$, $(k>0)$ are reciprocal of each other than value of $k$ is
(a) 2
(b) -1
(c) -2
(d) 1
2. If one zero of the quadratic polynomial $2 x^{2}-8 x-m$ is $\frac{5}{2}$, then the other zero is
(a) $\frac{2}{3}$
(b) $-\frac{2}{3}$
(c) $\frac{3}{2}$
(d) $\frac{-15}{2}$
3. Let $p(y)=y^{4}-3 y^{2}+2 y+5$, then the remainder when $p(y)$ is divided by $(y-1)$.
(a) 2
(b) 3
(c) -5
(d) 5
4. If the polynomials $a x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x-4$ and $x^{3}-4 x+a$ leave same remainder when divided by ( $x-3$ ), find the value of $a$.
(a) -1
(b) 1
(c) $\frac{1}{2}$
(d) $-\frac{1}{2}$
5. Let $f(x)=x^{2}-27 x+196$. If $f(a)=a$, then what is the value of $a$.
(a) 7
(b) 14
(c) 21
(d) 6
6. If $f(x)=2 x^{3}-6 x+4 x-5$ and $g(x)=3 x^{2}-9$, then the value of $f(1)+g(-2)$ is
(a) -3
(b) -2
(c) 3
(d) 2
7. Factor of the polynomial $x^{3}-3 x^{2}-10 x+24$ are:
(a) $(x-2)(x+3)(x-4)$
(b) $(x+2)(x+3)(x+4)$
(c) $(x+2)(x-3)(x-4)$
(d) $(x-2)(x-3)(x-4)$
8. The zeroes of the polynomial are
$p(x)=x^{2}-10 x-75$
(a) $5,-15$
(b) 5,15
(c) $15,-5$
(d) $-5,-15$
9. If $a$ and $b$ are zeroes of the polynomial
$2 t^{2}-4 t+3$, then the value of $a^{2} b+a b^{2}$ is :
(a) $\frac{3}{4}$
(b) 2
(c) 3
(d) 4
10. The zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}-3 x-m(m+3)$ are
(a) $m, m+3$
(b) $-m, m+3$
(c) $m,-(m+3)$
(d) $-m,-(m+3)$

## Fill in the Blanks

DIRECTIONS : Complete the following statements with an appropriate word / term to be filled in the blank space(s).

1. Polynomials of degrees 1,2 and 3 are called $\_\_\_\_$
$\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ polynomials respectively.
2. The zeroes of a polynomial $p(x)$ are precisely the $x$-coordinates of the points, where the graph of $y=p(x)$ intersects the $\_\_\_\_$ -axis.

## Column-I (Zeroes)

(A) 3 and -5
(B) $5+\sqrt{2}$ and $5-\sqrt{2}$
(C) -9 and $1 / 9$
(D) 5 and - 5

## Column-II (Quadratic polynomial)

(p) $x^{2}-25$
(q) $x^{2}+2 x-15$
(r) $x^{2}+(80 / 9) x-1$
(s) $x^{2}-10 x+21$
3. A quadratic polynomial can have at most 2 zeroes and a cubic polynomial can have at most $\_\_\_\_$ zeroes.
4. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c$, then $\alpha+\beta=\frac{-b}{\ldots \ldots} \& \alpha \beta=\frac{c}{\ldots . .}$
5. If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of the cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d=0$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\frac{-b}{\ldots . .}$
6. Zero of a polynomial is always $\_\_\_\_$ . $\_\_\_\_$ zeroes.
7. A polynomial of degree $n$ has at the most $\_\_\_\_$ zeroes.

## True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. For polynomials $p(x)$ and any non-zero polynomial $g(x)$, there are polynomials $q(x)$ and $r(x)$ such that $p(x)=g(x) q(x)+r(x)$, where $r(x)=0$ or degree $r(x)<$ degree $g(x)$.
2. Sum of zeroes of quadratic polynomial $=-\frac{(\text { coefficient of } x)}{\left(\text { coefficient of } x^{2}\right)}$
3. $\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} x^{\frac{1}{2}}+1$ is a polynomial
4. $\frac{6 \sqrt{x}+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{x}}$ is a polynomial, $x \neq 0$
5. Product of zeroes of quadratic polynomial
$=-\frac{\text { constant term }}{\left(\text { coefficient of } x^{2}\right)}$
6. A polynomial cannot have more than one zero
7. The degree of the sum of two polynomials each of degree 5 is always 5 .
8. $3,-1,1 / 3$ are the zeroes of the cubic polynomial
$p(x)=3 x^{3}-5 x^{2}-11 x-3$.
9. Zeroes of quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+7 x+10$ are 2 and -5
10. Sum of zeroes of $2 x^{2}-8 x+6$ is -4

## Match the Following

DIRECTIONS : Each question contains statements given in two columns which have to be matched. Statements ( $A, B, C, D$ ) in column-I have to be matched with statements ( $p, q, r, s$ ) in column-II.
1.

Column-I (Polynomial)

Column-II (Remainder)
(A) $\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2}{x^{2}-x+1}$ (p) 8
(B) $\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}+5 x-3}{x+2}$ (q) $\quad x-5$
(C) $\frac{x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-25 x+10}{x^{2}-2 x+5}$ (r) -33
(D) $\frac{x^{4}-3 x^{2}+4 x+5}{x^{2}-x+1}$
(s) $-2 x+4$

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ then evaluate $\frac{1}{\alpha^{3}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{3}}$.
2. Find a quadratic polynomial, the sum and product of whose zeroes are -3 and 2 , respectively.
3. Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be the roots of $2 x^{3}-3 x^{2}+6 x+1$. Find the value of $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha$.
4. For some polynomials $p(x)$ the graph of $y=p(x)$ are given below. Find the number of zeroes of $p(x)$ in each case.

(i)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-043.jpg?height=286&width=291&top_left_y=1375&top_left_x=370)

(ii)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-043.jpg?height=286&width=291&top_left_y=1375&top_left_x=758)

5. The graph of $y=p(x)$ are given in fig. below for some polynomials $p(x)$. Find the number of zeroes of $p(x)$ in each case.

(i)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-043.jpg?height=286&width=287&top_left_y=1777&top_left_x=374)

(ii)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-043.jpg?height=282&width=282&top_left_y=1777&top_left_x=767)

6. Find out the degrees of following polynomials.
(i) $p(x)=7 x+5 x^{2}-\sqrt{3}$
(ii) $q(x)=5 x^{4}-32 x^{2}+5 x-8$
(iii) $r(x)=x^{3}-x^{6}-5 \sqrt{2}$
(iv) $h(x)=\frac{1}{2}-3 x$
7. Find the zeroes of the polynomial
$p(x)=x^{2}-10 x-75$
8. Give example of polynomials $p(x), g(x), q(x), r(x)$ which satisfy the division algorithm and degree $q(x)=\operatorname{deg} r(x)$
9. If one root of quadratic equation $(k+1) x^{2}-5 x+2 k$ is reciprocal of other, then find $k$.
10. If two zeroes of the polynomial $p(x)=x^{3}-4 x^{2}-3 x+12$ are $\sqrt{3}$ and $-\sqrt{3}$, then find the third zero.

## Short Answer Questions

1. Find the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ and verify the relationship between the zeroes and the coefficients.
2. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ then evaluate $\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}+\frac{\beta^{2}}{\alpha^{2}}$
3. Find the polynomial having $5 \pm \sqrt{3}$ as its zeroes.
4. Find the condition which must be satisfied by the coefficients of the polynomial $f(x)=x^{3}-\ell x^{2}+m x-n$. (Given that the sum of the two zeroes is zero).
5. Given that the sum of the zeroes of the polynomial $(a+1) x^{2}+(2 a+3) x+(3 a+4)$ is -1 . Find the product of its zeroes.
6. If $a x^{3}+b x+c$ has a factor of the form $x^{2}+p x+1$, show that $a^{2}-c^{2}=a b$.
7. If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$, then find the value of $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}+\frac{1}{\gamma}$.
8. What must be added to the polynomial
$f(x)=x^{4}+2 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+x-1$ so that the resulting polynomial is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$ ?
9. If the zeroes of the polynomial $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+1$ are $a-b$, $a$ and $a+b$, find $a$ and $b$.
10. If zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$ are $\alpha$ and $\frac{2}{\alpha}$, then find the value of $a$.
11. On dividing $x^{3}-5 x^{2}+6 x+4$ by a polynomial $g(x)$, the quotient and the remainder were $x-3$ and 4 respectively. Find $g(x)$.
12. Find all zeroes of the polynomial $\left(2 x^{4}-9 x^{3}+5 x^{2}+\right. 3 x-1)$ if two of its zeroes are $(2+\sqrt{3})$ and $(2-\sqrt{3})$
13. Find the value of $k$ such that the polynomial $x^{2}-(k+6) x+2(2 k-1)$ has sum of its zeroes equal to half of their product.
14. A teacher asked 10 of his students to write a polynomial in one variable on a paper and then to handover the paper. The following were the answers given by the students :
$2 x+3,3 x^{2}+7 x+2,4 x^{3}+3 x^{2}+2$, $x^{3}+\sqrt{3 x}+7,7 x+\sqrt{7}, 5 x^{3}-7 x+2$,
$2 x^{2}+3-\frac{5}{x}, 5 x-\frac{1}{2}, a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d, x+\frac{1}{x}$
Answer the following questions:
(i) How many of the above ten, are not polynomials?
(ii) How many of the above ten, are quadratic polynomials?

## Long Answer Questions

1. Verify that $2,1,-1$ are the zeroes of $x^{3}-2 x^{2}-x+2$. Also verify the relationship between the zeroes and the coefficients.
2. Find the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial
$f(x)=a b x^{2}+\left(b^{2}-a c\right) x-b c$
Also,verify the relationship between the zeroes and its coefficients.
3. On dividing $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2$ by a polynomial $h(x)$, the quotient and the remainder are ( $x-2$ ) and ( $-2 x+4$ ) respectively. Determine $h(x)$.
4. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}+4 x+3$, find the polynomial whose zeroes are $1+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}$ and $1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$.
5. Polynomial $x^{4}+7 x^{3}+7 x^{2}+p x+q$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+7 x+12$, then find the value of $p$ and $q$.
6. If two zeroes of a polynomial $x^{3}+5 x^{2}+7 x+3$ are -1 and -3 , then find the third zero.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. The HCF of two polynomials $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ using long division method was found in two steps to be $3 x-2$ and the first two quotients obtained are $x+2$ and $2 x+1$. Find $p(x)$ and $q(x)$, where the degree of $p(x)>$ the degree of $q(x)$.
2. If $f(x)=x^{2}+6 x+a, g(x)=x^{2}+4 x+b, h(x)=x^{2}+14 x+c$ and the LCM of $f(x), g(x)$ and $h(x)$ is $(x+8)(x-2)(x+6)$, then find $a+b+c$. ( $a, b$ and $c$ are constants).
3. If $x^{2}+x-1$ is a factor of $x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1$, then find the values of $p$ and $q$.
4. If $f(x)=(x+2)^{7}(x+4)^{p}, g(x)=(x+2)^{q}(x+4)^{9}$ and the LCM of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ is $(x+2)^{q}(x+4)^{9}$, then find the maximum value of $p-q$.
5. Find the zeroes of the polynomial $p(x)=x^{3}-5 x^{2}-2 x+24$, if it is given that the product of two of its zeroes is 12 .

## HOTS Questions

1. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=2 x^{2}-5 x+7$, find a polynomial whose zeroes are $2 \alpha+3 \beta$ and $3 \alpha+2 \beta$.
2. Form a cubic polynomial having sum, sum of the product of its zeroes taken two at a time, and the product of its zeroes are $2,-7$ and -14 respectively.
3. Draw the graphs of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=3-2 x-x^{2}$.
4. For which values of $a$ and $b$, are the zeroes of $q(x)=x^{3}+2 x^{2}+a$ also the zeroes of the polynomial $p(x)=x^{5}-x^{4}-4 x^{3}+3 x+b$ ? which zeroes of $p(x)$ are not the zeroes of $q(x)$ ?

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. Due to heavy storm an electric wire got bent as shown in the figure. It followed a mathematical shape. Answer the following questions below.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-044.jpg?height=369&width=451&top_left_y=552&top_left_x=1192)
[CBSE Sample Paper]
(i) Name the shape in which the wire is bent
(a) spiral
(b) ellipse
(c) linear
(d) parabola
(ii) How many zeroes are there for the polynomial (shape of the wire)
(a) 2
(b) 3
(c) 1
(d) 0
(iii) The zeroes of the polynomial are
(a) $-1,5$
(b) $-1,3$
(c) 3,5
(d) $-4,2$
(iv) What will be the expression of the polynomial?
(a) $x^{2}+2 x-3$
(b) $x^{2}-2 x+3$
(c) $x^{2}-2 x-3$
(d) $x^{2}+2 x+3$
(v) What is the value of the polynomial if $\mathrm{x}=-1$ ?
(a) 6
(b) -18
(c) 18
(d) 0
2. Soniya and Anuj are students of class $X$ and they given a polynomial such that "If one zero of the polynomial $3 x^{2}-8 x+2 k+5$ is four times the other $4 x^{2}-12 x+3 k+8$. Then, answer the following questions.
(i) Find the sum of zeroes.
(a) 3
(b) 4
(c) $\frac{12}{3}$
(d) $\frac{12}{5}$
(ii) For quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c, a \neq 0$, write the formula to find product of zeroes.
(a) $\frac{b}{a}$
(b) $-\frac{b}{a}$
(c) $-\frac{c}{a}$
(d) $\frac{c}{a}$
(iii) If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes of given polynomial. Then, what is the relation between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ ?
(a) $\alpha+\beta=4$
(b) $\alpha \beta=4$
(c) $\beta=4 \alpha$
(d) $\alpha^{2}=16 \beta$
(iv) If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes of the given polynomial, then find value of $\alpha$.
(a) $\frac{1}{5}$
(b) $\frac{7}{4}$
(c) $\frac{2}{5}$
(d) $\frac{3}{5}$
(v) Find the value of $k$. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes of given polynomials.
(a) $\frac{56}{75}$
(b) $-\frac{56}{75}$
(c) $\frac{75}{56}$
(d) $\frac{65}{75}$

## Text-book Questions

1. The graphs of $y=p(x)$ are given in fig. below, for some polynomials $p(x)$. Find the number of zeroes of $p(x)$, in each case.
(i)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-045.jpg?height=276&width=319&top_left_y=762&top_left_x=374)
(ii)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-045.jpg?height=268&width=319&top_left_y=1032&top_left_x=374)
2. Find the zeroes of the following quadratic polynomials and verify the relationship between the zeroes and the coefficients.
(i) $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$
(ii) $4 u^{2}+8 u$
3. Find a quadratic polynomial each with the given numbers as the sum and product of its zeroes respectively.
(i) $\sqrt{2}, \frac{1}{3}$
(ii) $-\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}$
4. Divide the polynomial $p(x)$ by the polynomial $g(x)$ and find the quotient and remainder in each of the following:
(i) Divide $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+5 x-3$ by $x^{2}-2$ and verify the division algorithm.
(ii) $p(x)=x^{4}-5 x+6, g(x)=2-x^{2}$
5. Check whether the first polynomial is factor of the second polynomial by dividing the second polynomial by the first polynomial :
$x^{3}-3 x+1, x^{5}-4 x^{3}+x^{2}+3 x+1$
6. Obtain all other zeroes of
$3 x^{4}+6 x^{3}-2 x^{2}-10 x-5$, if two of its zeroes are $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$.
7. Give example of polynomials $p(x), g(x), q(x), r(x)$ which satisfy the division algorithm and
(i) degree $p(x)=$ degree $q(x)$
(ii) degree $q(x)=\operatorname{deg} r(x)$
(iii) degree $r(x)=0$

## Exemplar Questions

1. Find the zeroes of the polynomial by factorization and verify the relations between the zeroes and the coefficients of the polynomials:
(a) $4 x^{2}+5 \sqrt{2} x-3$
(b) $y^{2}+\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{5} x-5$
2. If on division of a polynomial $p(x)$ by a polynomial $g(x)$, the quotient is zero, what is the relation between the degrees of $p(x)$ and $g(x)$ ?
3. Can the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+k x+k$ have equal zeroes for some odd integer $k>1$ ?
4. Given the $\sqrt{2}$ is a zero of the cubic polynomial $6 x^{3}+\sqrt{2} x^{2}-10 x-4 \sqrt{2}$, find its other two zeroes.
5. If one zero of the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+3 x+k$ is 2 , then the value of $k$ is
(a) 10
(b) -10
(c) 5
(d) -5
6. Given that two of the zeroes of the cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ are 0 , the third zero is
(a) $\frac{-b}{a}$
(b) $\frac{b}{a}$
(c) $\frac{c}{a}$
(d) $-\frac{d}{a}$
7. If one of the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $(k-1) x^{2}+k x+1$ is -3 , then the value of $k$ is
(a) $\frac{4}{3}$
(b) $\frac{-4}{3}$
(c) $\frac{2}{3}$
(d) $\frac{-2}{3}$
8. The zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+99 x+127$ are
(a) both positive
(b) both negative
(c) one positive and one negative
(d) both equal

## Single Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. The value of $x$, for which the polynomials $x^{2}-1$ and $x^{2}-2 x+1$ vanish simultaneously, is
(a) 2
(b) -2
(c) -1
(d) 1
2. The value of the polynomial $x^{8}-x^{5}+x^{2}-x+1$ is
(a) positive for all the real numbers
(b) negative for all the real numbers
(c) 0
(d) depends on value of $x$
3. If $x=0 . \overline{7}$, then $2 x$ is
(a) $1 . \overline{4}$
(b) $1 . \overline{5}$
(c) $1 . \overline{54}$
(d) $1 . \overline{45}$
4. Lowest value of $x^{2}+4 x+2$ is
(a) 0
(b) $\quad-2$
(c) 2
(d) 4
5. If $a^{3}-3 a^{2} b+3 a b^{2}-b^{3}$ is divided by $(a-b)$, then the remainder is
(a) $a^{2}-a b+b^{2}$
(b) $a^{2}+a b+b^{2}$
(c) 1
(d) 0
6. A quadratic polynomial when divided by $x+2$ leaves a remainder of 1 and when divided by $x-1$, leaves a remainder of 4 . What will be the remainder if it is divided by $(x+2) (x-1)$ ?
(a) 1
(b) 4
(c) $x+3$
(d) $x-3$
7. If the sum of the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=2 x^{3}-3 k x^{2}+4 x-5$ is 6 , then the value of $k$ is
(a) 2
(b) -2
(c) 4
(d) -4
8. If the polynomial $x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-25 x+10$ is divided by another polynomial $x^{2}-2 x+k$, the remainder comes out to be $x+a$, find $k$ and $a$.
[NTSE]
(a) $3,-2$
(b) $5,-5$
(c) $-3,-5$
(d) $-5,1$
9. If the polynomials $a x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x-4$ and $x^{3}-4 x+$ a leave the same remainder when divided by $x-3$, then the value of $a$ is
[JSTSE]
(a) 1
(b) -1
(c) $19 / 14$
(d) $-5 / 14$
10. If the value of a quadratic polynomial $\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x})$ is 0 only at $x=-1$ and $p(-2)=2$, then the value of $p(2)$ is
[NTSE]
(a) 18
(b) 9
(c) 6
(d) 3
11. Suppose the quadratic polynomial $p(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ has positive coefficients $a, b, c$ in arithmetic progression in that order. If $p(x)=0$ has integer roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Then, $\alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta$ is equal to
[KVPY]
(a) 3
(b) 5
(c) 7
(d) 14
12. If $x^{2}-4$ is the factor of $2 x^{3}+k_{1} x^{2}+k_{2} x+12$, where $k_{1}$, $k_{2}$ are constant, then the value of $k_{1}+k_{2}$ is
[NTSE]
(a) 11
(b) 5
(c) -11
(d) -5
13. If the polynomial $x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16^{2}-25 x+10$ is divided by another polynomial $x^{2}-2 x+k$, the remainder comes out to be $x+a$, then the value of $a$ is
[NTSE]
(a) -1
(b) -5
(c) 1
(d) 5
14. The number of real roots of the polynomial equation $x^{4}-x^{2}+2 x-1=0$ is
[KVPY]
(a) 0
(b) 2
(c) 3
(d) 4
15. If $x=3+3^{2 / 3}+3^{1 / 3}$, then the value of $x^{3}-9 x^{2}+18 x-12$ is
[JSTSE]
(a) 1
(b) 0
(c) -1
(d) 2
16. Let $P(x)$ be a polynomial of degree 3 and $P(n)=\frac{1}{2}$ for $n=1,2,3,4$. Then the value of $P(5)$ is
[NTSE]
(a) 0
(b) $\frac{1}{5}$
(c) $-\frac{2}{5}$
(d) $\frac{3}{5}$
17. Let $p(x)=x^{2}+a x+b$ have two distinct real roots, where $a, b$ are real numbers. Define $g(x)=p\left(x^{3}\right)$ for all real numbers $x$. Then which of the following statements are true?
[KVPY]
I. $g$ has exactly two distinct real roots
II. $g$ can have more than two distinct real roots
III. There exists a real number $\alpha$ such that $g(x) \geq \alpha$ for all real $x$
(a) Only I
(b) Only I and III
(c) Only II
(d) Only II and III
18. The polynomial, $f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+(x-2)^{2}+(x-3)^{2}+(x -4)^{2}$ has minimum value, when $x=$
[NTSE]
(a) 40
(b) 20
(c) 10
(d) 2.5

## More Than One Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.

1. Which of the following given options is/are not correct?
(a) $\frac{2}{x}+3$ is a polynomial
(b) $\sqrt{x}+5$ is a polynomial
(c) $\frac{2}{3 x-4}$ is a polynomial
(d) $\sqrt{5} x^{2}+\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{3}{7}$ is a polynomial
2. Which of the following given options is/are not correct?
(a) Degree of a zero polynomial is ' 0 '
(b) Degree of a zero polynomial is not defined
(c) Degree of a constant polynomial is not defined
(d) A polynomial of degree $n$ must have $n$ zeroes
3. Which of the following given options is/are incorrect? If $p(x)=q(x) g(x)+r(x)$ (By Division Algorithm) where $p(x), g(x)$ are any two polynomials with $g(x) \neq 0$, then
(a) $r(x)=0$ always
(b) degree of $r(x)<$ degree of $g(x)$ always
(c) either $r(x)=0$ or degree of $r(x)<$ degree of $g(x)$
(d) $r(x)=g(x)$
4. Which of the following is/are not a Polynomial?
(a) $x^{2}+\frac{1}{x}$
(b) $2 x^{2}-3 \sqrt{x}+1$
(c) $x^{3}-3 x+1$
(d) $2 x^{\frac{3}{2}}-5 x$
5. Which of the following is/are not graph of a quadratic polynomial?

(a)

(b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=223&width=259&top_left_y=1178&top_left_x=749)

(c)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=222&width=255&top_left_y=1430&top_left_x=383)

(d)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=227&width=259&top_left_y=1425&top_left_x=744)

## Passage Based Questions

## DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph (s) and answer the

## following questions.

## Passage - I

If $\alpha, \beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial
$f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$, then $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}, \alpha \beta=\frac{c}{a}$

1. If $\alpha, \beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial
$f(x)=x^{2}-p x+q$, then $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}=$
(a) $p-q$
(b) $p+q$
(c) $\frac{p}{q}$
(d) $\frac{q}{p}$

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of $x^{3}-2 x^{2}+q x-r$ and $\alpha+\beta=0$, then $2 q=r$.
Reason : If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{b}{a}, \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a}, \alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{d}{a}$.
2. Assertion : If one zero of polynomial $p(x)=\left(k^{2}+4\right) x^{2}+ 13 x+4 k$ is reciprocal of other, then $k=2$.
Reason : If $(x-\alpha)$ is a factor of $p(x)$, then $p(\alpha)=0$ i.e. $\alpha$ is a zero of $p(x)$
3. Assertion : $x^{3}+x$ has only one real zero.

Reason : A polynomial of $n$th degree must have $n$ real zeroes.
4. Assertion : Degree of a zero polynomial is not defined.

Reason : Degree of a non-zero constant polynomial is '0'
5. Assertion : Zeroes of $f(x)=x^{2}-4 x-5$ are $5,-1$.

Reason : The polynomial whose zeroes are $2+\sqrt{3}, 2-\sqrt{3}$ is $x^{2}-4 x+7$.
6. Assertion : $x^{2}+4 x-5$ has two zeroes.

Reason : A quadratic polynomial can have at the most two zeroes.

## True / False

1. True

2. True
3. False, because the exponent of the variable is not a whole number.
$\begin{aligned} & \text { 4. True, because } \\ & \text { 5. }\end{aligned} \frac{6 \sqrt{x}+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{x}}=6+x$, which is a polynomial.
4. False

6. False, $x^{5}+1$ and $-x^{5}+2 x+3$ are two polynomials of degree 5 but the degree of the sum of the two polynomials is 1 .

1. $\frac{1}{\alpha^{3}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{3}}=\frac{\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}}{(\alpha \beta)^{3}}=\frac{\frac{3 a b c-b^{3}}{a^{3}}}{\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{3}}$
$\left[\because \alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=(\alpha+\beta)^{3}-3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\alpha^{3}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{3}}=\frac{3 a b c-b^{3}}{c^{3}}$
2. Let the quadratic polynomial be $a x^{2}+b x+c$, and its zeroes be $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

If $a=1$, then $b=3$ and $c=2$.
So, one quadratic polynomial which fits the given conditions is $x^{2}+3 x+2$.

(ii) 4 zeroes [curve cuts the $x$-axis at 4 points]
6. (i) $p(x)$ is of degree 2 as highest power term is $5 x^{2}$.i.e., $p(x)$ is quadratic.
(ii) $q(x)$ is of degree 4 .
(iii) $r(x)$ is of degree 6 .
(iv) $h(x)$ is of degree 1. i.e. $h(x)$ is linear.
7. We have, $p(x)=x^{2}-10 x-75=x^{2}-15 x+5 x-75$

$$
=x(x-15)+5(x-15)=(x-15)(x+5)
$$

$\therefore \quad p(x)=(x-15)(x+5)$
So, $p(x)=0$ when $x=15$ or $x=-5$. Therefore, required zeroes are 15 and -5 .

r(x)=x+3 & {[\text { degree } r(x)=1]}

$$

so product of root $=\frac{\text { Constant team }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}} \frac{1}{\alpha}=\frac{2 k}{k+1}$

## Short Answer Questions

1. We have, $6 x^{2}-7 x-3=6 x^{2}-9 x+2 x-3$
$=3 x(2 x-3)+1(2 x-3)=(2 x-3)(3 x+1)$
Value of $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ is zero
when $2 x-3=0 \quad$ or $3 x+1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{3}{2} \quad$ or $\quad x=-\frac{1}{3} \quad \therefore$ Zeroes are $\frac{3}{2}$ and $-\frac{1}{3}$
Now sum of zeroes
$=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{9-2}{6}=\frac{7}{6}=-\frac{\text { coefficient of } x}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$
Product of zeroes
$=\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{-1}{3}=\frac{-1}{2}=\frac{-3}{6}=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$
2. $\because \alpha+\beta=\frac{-b}{a}$ and $\alpha \beta=\frac{c}{a}$

Also, $\alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}=\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}\right)^{2}-2 \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}$
$=\left[(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta\right]^{2}-2 \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}$
$\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}+\frac{\beta^{2}}{\alpha^{2}}=\frac{\alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}}{\alpha^{2} \beta^{2}}=\frac{\left(b^{2}-2 a c\right)^{2}-2 a^{2} c^{2}}{a^{4}\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{2}}$
$=\frac{b^{4}+4 a^{2} c^{2}-4 a b^{2} c-2 a^{2} c^{2}}{a^{4}\left(\frac{c^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)}$
$=\frac{b^{4}+2 a^{2} c^{2}-4 a b^{2} c}{a^{2} c^{2}}=2-\frac{4 b^{2}}{a c}+\frac{b^{4}}{a^{2} c^{2}}$
3. Let $\alpha=5+\sqrt{3}, \beta=5-\sqrt{3}$

We know, $p(x)$ having zeroes as $\alpha, \beta$ is given by
$p(x)=(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore & p(x)=[x-(5+\sqrt{3})][x-(5-\sqrt{3})] \\
& =x^{2}-(5+\sqrt{3}) x-(5-\sqrt{3}) x+(5+\sqrt{3})(5-\sqrt{3}) \\
& =x^{2}-(5+\sqrt{3}+5-\sqrt{3}) x+\left(5^{2}-3\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore \quad p(x)=x^{2}-10 x+22$
4. Let $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ be the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)$ such that $\alpha+\beta=0$.
Now, $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$
Since, $\gamma$ is a zero of the polynomial $f(x)$. Therefore, $f(\gamma)=0$
$\Rightarrow \gamma^{3}-\ell \gamma^{2}+m \gamma-n=0 \quad[\because \gamma=\ell]$
$\Rightarrow \ell^{3}-\ell^{3}+m \ell-n=0 \Rightarrow m \ell=n$
This is the required condition.
5. Since, the sum of the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial
$(a+1) x^{2}+(2 a+3) x+(3 a+4)$ is -1 .
$\therefore \frac{-(2 a+3)}{a+1}=-1$
$\left[\because\right.$ Sum of zeroes $\left.=\frac{-(\text { Coefficient of } x)}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow a+1=2 a+3 \Rightarrow a=-2$
Now, product of zeroes $\quad \frac{3 \quad 4}{-2+1}=\frac{3(-2)+4}{-2}$

$$
\left[\because \text { Product of zeroes }=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}\right]
$$

6. By actual division, we get,
$\frac{a x^{3}+b x+c}{x^{2}+p x+1}=a x-a p+\frac{R(x)}{x^{2}+p x+1}$
Remainder polynomial, $R(x)=\left(b-a+a p^{2}\right) x+c+a p$ If $a x^{3}+b x+c$ has a factor of the form $x^{2}+p x+1$, then $R(x)$ must be identically zero, if $b-a+a p^{2}=0$ and $c+a p=0$ Eliminating $p$ from these equations, we get
$b-a+a\left(-\frac{c}{a}\right)^{2}=0 \quad$ or $\quad a^{2}-c^{2}=a b$
7. Polynomial is $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ and zeroes are $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$.

Let $p=\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}+\frac{1}{\gamma} \Rightarrow p=\frac{\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha+\alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta \gamma} ; \alpha \beta \gamma=-d / a$
and $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=c / a \Rightarrow p=\frac{c / a}{-d / a}=-\frac{c}{d}$
8. The given polynomial is $f(x)=x^{4}+2 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+x-1$. To make this polynomial exactly divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$, let us add $p$ then $x^{4}+2 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+x-1+p$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$.

$$
\begin{gathered}
x ^ { 2 } + 2 x - 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 4 } + 2 x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + x - 1 + p ( x ^ { 2 } } \\
\frac{x^{4}+2 x^{3}-3 x^{2}}{x^{2}+x-1+p}
\end{gathered}
$$

For remainder to be zero, $x^{2}+2 x-3=x^{2}+x-1+p$
So, $p=x-2$,
So, $x-2$ to be added to make the polynomial divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$.
9. Zeroes of polynomial $p(x)=x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+1$ are $a-b, a$ and $a+b$
∵ sum of zeroes $=\frac{- \text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$
So, $a-b+a+a+b=-\frac{(-3)}{1} \Rightarrow 3 a=3 \Rightarrow a=1$.
$\alpha \beta \gamma=\frac{d}{a} \Rightarrow(a-b) a(a+b)=\frac{-1}{1}=-1$
$\Rightarrow(1-b) \times 1 \times(1+b)=-1 \quad[\because$ Put $a=1]$
$1-b^{2}=-1 \Rightarrow b^{2}=2 \Rightarrow b= \pm \sqrt{2} \quad$ So, $a=1, b= \pm \sqrt{2}$
10. Given, $\alpha$ and $\frac{2}{\alpha}$ are the zeroes of $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$.

Now
Product of the zeroes $=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha \times \frac{2}{\alpha}=\frac{2 a}{1} \\
& 2=2 a \\
\therefore \quad & a=1
\end{aligned}
$$

11. According to question,

$$
\begin{gathered}
x^{3}-5 x^{2}+6 x+4=g(x)(x-3)+4 \\
\Rightarrow g(x)=\frac{x^{3}-5 x^{2}+6 x}{x-3} \\
x - 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } - 5 x ^ { 2 } + 6 x } ( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \\
\frac{+x^{3}-3 x^{2}}{-2 x^{2}+6 x} \\
\frac{\frac{-2 x^{2}+6 x}{x}}{\frac{+}{x}}
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, $g(x)=x^{2}-2 x$.
12. Let $f(x)=2 x^{4}-9 x^{3}+5 x^{2}+3 x-1$

It is given that $(2+\sqrt{3})$ and $(2-\sqrt{3})$
are zeroes of $f(x)$
∴ By factor theorem,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(x-(2+\sqrt{3}))(x-(2-\sqrt{3})) \\
& =(x-2-\sqrt{3})(x-2+\sqrt{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(x-2)^{2}-\sqrt{3}^{2} \\
& =x^{2}+4-4 x-3 \\
& =x^{2}-4 x+1 \text { is factor of } f(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, we divide $f(x)$ by $x^{2}-4 x+1$ to get then 2 fuctors.

$$
\left.\begin{array}{l}
x ^ { 2 } - 4 x + 1 \longdiv { 2 x ^ { 4 } - 9 x ^ { 3 } + 5 x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 1 } ( 2 x ^ { 2 } - x - 1 \\
\frac{2 x^{4}-8 x^{3}+2 x^{2}}{+\quad-} \\
\begin{array}{c}
-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+3 x-1 \\
\frac{-x^{3}+4 x^{2}-x}{+\quad+}
\end{array} \\
\frac{-x^{2}+4 x-1}{+x^{2}+4 x-1}+\frac{0}{+}
\end{array}\right] \begin{aligned}
& \therefore f(x)=\left(x^{2}-4 x+1\right)\left(2 x^{2}-x-1\right) \\
& {[x-(2+\sqrt{3})][x-(2-\sqrt{3})]\left(2 x^{2}+x-2 x-1\right)} \\
& =[x-(2+\sqrt{3})][x-(2-\sqrt{3})][x(2 x+1)-1(2 x+1)] \\
& =(x-(2+\sqrt{3}))(x-(2-\sqrt{3}))(x-1)(2 x+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, zeroes of $f(x)$ are $2+\sqrt{3}, 2-\sqrt{3}, 1, \frac{-1}{2}$
13. Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the polynomial
$x^{2}-(k+6) x+2(2 k-1)$.
given, $\alpha+\beta=\frac{\alpha \beta}{2}$
$\mathrm{k}+6=\frac{2(2 \mathrm{k}-1)}{2}[\because \alpha+\beta=k+6$ and $\alpha \beta=2(2 k-1]$
$\Rightarrow k+6=2 k-1 \Rightarrow 6+1=2 k-k \Rightarrow k=7$.
Therefore, the value of $k$ is 7 .

## Long Answer Questions

1. Let $p(x)=x^{3}-2 x^{2}-x+2$

Comparing it with $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$,
We get, $\quad a=1, b=-2, c=-1, d=2$
Now, $p(2)=2^{3}-2(2)^{2}-2+2=8-8-2+2=0$
$p(1)=1^{3}-2(1)^{2}-1+2=1-2-1+2=0$

$$
p(-1)=(-1)^{3}-2(-1)^{2}+1+2=-1-2+1+2=0
$$

$\Rightarrow 2,1,-1$ are the zeroes of the polynomial $p(n)$
Let $\alpha=2, \beta=1, \gamma=-1$
$\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2+1-1=2=\frac{(-2)}{1}=-\frac{b}{a}$
$\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=2 \times 1+1(-1)+(-1) 2$
$=2-1-2=-1=-\frac{1}{1}=\frac{c}{a}$
$\alpha \beta \gamma=2(1)(-1)=-2=-\frac{2}{1}=-\frac{d}{a}$
2. $f(x)=a b x^{2}+\left(b^{2}-a c\right) x-b c=a b x^{2}+b^{2} x-a c x-b c$
$=b x(a x+b)-c(a x+b)=(a x+b)(b x-c)$
Thus, the zeroes of $f(x)$ are $\alpha=-\frac{b}{a}$ and $\beta=\frac{c}{b}$.
Now, $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}+\frac{c}{b}=\frac{a c-b^{2}}{a b}$ and $\alpha \beta=-\frac{b}{a} \times \frac{c}{b}=-\frac{c}{a}$
From the given polynomial $f(x)$,
sum of zeroes :
$\alpha+\beta=-\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}=-\left(\frac{b^{2}-a c}{a b}\right)=\frac{a c-b^{2}}{a b}$
And product of zeroes :
$\alpha \beta=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}=-\frac{b c}{a b}=-\frac{c}{a}$
Hence, the sum and product of the zeroes are same in both cases. Therefore the relationship between the zeroes and its coefficients is verified.
3. By division theorem,

Dividend $=$ Divisor × Quotient + Remainder
$\therefore x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2=h(x) \times(x-2)+(-2 x+4)$
$\Rightarrow h(x) \times(x-2)=x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2-(-2 x+4)$
$\Rightarrow h(x)=\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}+3 x-2}{x-2}$
To find $h(x)$, divide $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+3 x-2$ by $x-2$ as follows

$$
\begin{gathered}
x - 2 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 2 } x ^ { 2 } - x + 1 \\
\frac{x^{3}-2 x^{2}}{-+} \\
\frac{\begin{array}{r}
-x^{2}+3 x \\
-x^{2}+2 x \\
+- \\
x-2 \\
-+ \\
0
\end{array}}{\frac{1}{-2}}
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, $h(x)=x^{2}-x+1$.
4. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the cubic polynomial
$x^{2}+4 x+3$
then, $\alpha+\beta=-4$ and $\alpha \beta=3$

Sum of zeroes $=1+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}+1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$
$=\frac{\alpha \beta+\beta^{2}+\alpha \beta+\alpha^{2}}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+2 \alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}$
$=\frac{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{(-4)^{2}}{3}=\frac{16}{3}$
Product of zeroes $=\left(1+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)\left(1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)$
$=1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}+\frac{\alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}$
$\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+2 \alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{(-4)^{2}}{3}=\frac{16}{3}$
So, required polynomial
$=x^{2}-$ (Sum of the zeroes) $x+$ Product of the zeroes
$=x^{2}-\left(\frac{16}{3}\right) x+\frac{16}{3}=\left(3 x^{2}-16 x+16\right) \frac{1}{3}$
5. Factors of $x^{2}+7 x+12$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^{2}+7 x+12=0 \\
\Rightarrow & x^{2}+4 x+3 x+12=0 \\
\Rightarrow & (x+4)(x+3)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & x=-4,-3
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $p(x)=x^{4}+7 x^{3}+7 x^{2}+p x+q$
If $p(x)$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+7 x+12$, then $x=-4$ and $x=-3$ are zeroes of $p(x)$ [from eq (i)]
$p(x)=x^{4}+7 x^{3}+7 x^{2}+p x+q$
$p(-4)=(-4)^{4}+7(-4)^{3}+7(-4)^{2}+p(-4)+q$
but $p(-4)=0$
$\therefore 0=256-448+112-4 p+q$
$\Rightarrow \quad 0=-4 p+q-80$
$\Rightarrow 4 p-q=-80$
and $p(-3)=(-3)^{4}+7(-3)^{3}+7(-3)^{2}+p(-3)+q$
but $p(-3)=0$
$\therefore 0=81-189+63-3 p+q$
$\Rightarrow 0=-3 p+q-45$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 p-q=-45$

By elimination method
$4 p-q=-80$
$3 p-q=-45$
$-\quad+\quad+$
$P=-35$
Put the value of $p$ in eq. (i),

$$
\begin{array}{ll} 
& 4(-35)-q=-80 \\
\Rightarrow & -140-q=-80 \\
& -q=140-80 \\
\therefore & -q=60 \\
\text { Hence, } & q=-60 \\
\text { H } & p=-35, q=-60
\end{array}
$$

6. $\quad x=-1$ and $x=-3$ are zeroes.
$\therefore(x+1)(x+3)=x^{2}+4 x+3$

$$
\begin{gathered}
x ^ { 2 } + 4 x + 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } + 5 x ^ { 2 } + 7 x + 3 ( x + 1 } \\
\frac{x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x}{-} \\
\frac{x^{2}+4 x+3}{x^{2}+4 x+3}-- \\
0
\end{gathered}
$$

$$
x+1=0
$$

Remainder is 0

$$
\therefore \quad(n+1) \text { is a factor }
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad x=-1$
$\therefore \quad$ The third zero is -1 .

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Since, HCF of $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ is $3 x-2$ and it was found in two steps by long division method.
Then, according to the question
$p(x)=q(x)(x+2)+(3 x-2)[\because$ degree $p(x)>$ degree $q(x)]$
$q(x)=(3 x-2)(2 x+1)=6 x^{2}-x-2$
Now, $p(x)=q(x)(x+2)+(3 x-2)$
$=\left(6 x^{2}-x-2\right)(x+2)+(3 x-2)$
$=6 x^{3}+12 x^{2}-x^{2}-2 x-2 x-4+3 x-2$
$=6 x^{3}+11 x^{2}-x-6$
2. $f(x)=x^{2}+6 x+a, g(x)=x^{2}+4 x+b$,
$h(x)=x^{2}+14+c$
$\mathrm{LCM}=(x+8)(x-2)(x+6)$
Since, three given polynomials are quadratic and their LCM is cubic, then each polynomial is the product of two factors of LCM. By inspection method,
$f(x)=(x+8)(x-2) \Rightarrow x^{2}+6 x+a$
$=(x+8)(x-2) \Rightarrow a=-16$
$g(x)=(x-2)(x+6) \Rightarrow x^{2}+4 x+b$
$=(x-2)(x+6) \Rightarrow b=-12$
$h(x)=(x+8)(x+6) \Rightarrow x^{2}+14 x+c$
$=(x+8)(x+6) \Rightarrow c=48$
Hence, $a+b+c=-16-12+48=20$.
3. Divide the polynomial $x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1$ by $x^{2}+x-1$ using long division method,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^{2}+x-1 \sqrt{x^{2}+(p-1) x+(q+2-p)} \\
& \frac{x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1}{(p-1) x^{3}+(q+1) x^{2}-1} \\
& \frac{-(p-1) x^{3}+(p-1) x^{2}-(p-1) x}{-\quad+} \\
& \frac{-(q+2-p) x^{2}+(p-1) x-1}{(q+2-p) x^{2}+(q+2-p) x-(q+2-p)}+ \\
&(2 p-q-3) x+(q-p+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since, $x^{2}+x-1$ is a factor of the polynomial $x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1$, then remainder must be zero.
Then,

$$
(2 p-q-3) x+(q-p+1)=0
$$

As the remainder is zero, then coefficient of $x$ and constant term must be zero.
So, $2 p-q-3=0$ and $q-p+1=0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 p-q=3 \\
& p-q=1
\end{aligned}
$$

From (i) - (ii);

$$
p=2
$$

Now, substitute the value of $p$ in eqn. (ii), then

$$
2-q=1 \Rightarrow q=2-1=1
$$

Hence, required value of $p$ is 2 and $q$ is 1 .
4. It is given that
$f(x)=(x+2)^{7}(x+4)^{\mathrm{p}}$
$g(x)=(x+2)^{4}(x+4)^{9}$
LCM of $f(x)$ and $g(x)=(x+2)^{q}(x+4)^{9}$
$\therefore q \geq 7$ and $p \leq 9$
For maximum value of $p-q, p$ is maximum and $q$ is minimum.
Hence, maximum value of $p-q$ is $9-7=2$.
5. Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be the zeroes of the given polynomial
$x^{3}-5 x^{2}-2 x+24$ such that
$\alpha \beta=12$
∵ Sum of the zeroes $=-\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{(-5)}{1}=5$
$\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{-2}{1}=-2$
and product of the zeroes $=-\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient } x^{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{24}{1}=-24 \quad(\because \alpha \beta=12)$
$\Rightarrow(12) \gamma=-24$
$\Rightarrow \quad \gamma=-\frac{24}{12}=-2$
Substituting this value of $\gamma$ in (ii), we get
$\alpha+\beta-2=5 \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=7$

Also, $(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta=7^{2}-4 \times 12=49-48$
$\Rightarrow(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=1 \Rightarrow \alpha-\beta=1$ or -1 .
Case I. Taking $\alpha-\beta=1$

Add (iv) and (v), to get $2 \alpha=8 \Rightarrow \alpha=4$
and then from (iv), $\beta=7-\alpha=7-4=3$.
Hence, we get $\quad \alpha=4, \beta=3, \gamma=-2$.
Case II. Taking $\alpha-\beta=-1$

Add (iv) and (vi) to get $2 \alpha=6 \Rightarrow \alpha=3$ and then from (iv),

$$
\beta=7-\alpha=7-3=4
$$

Hence, $\alpha=3, \beta=4, \gamma=-2$.

## HOTS Questions

1. Since, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zeroes of polynomial
$f(x)=2 x^{2}-5 x+7$.
So, $\alpha+\beta=-\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right)=\frac{5}{2}$ and $\alpha \beta=\frac{7}{2}$
Let $S$ and $P$ denote respectively the sum and product of zeroes of the required polynomial. Then, polynomial is $p(x)=k\left(x^{2}-S x+P\right)$
$\mathrm{S}=(2 \alpha+3 \beta)+(3 \alpha+2 \beta)=5(\alpha+\beta)=5 \times \frac{5}{2}=\frac{25}{2}$ and,
$P=(2 \alpha+3 \beta)(3 \alpha+2 \beta)$
$\Rightarrow P=6\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}\right)+13 \alpha \beta=6 \alpha^{2}+6 \beta^{2}+12 \alpha \beta+\alpha \beta$
$=6(\alpha+\beta)^{2}+\alpha \beta$
$\Rightarrow P=6 \times\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{7}{2}=\frac{75}{2}+\frac{7}{2}=41$
Hence, the required polynomial is given by
$p(x)=k\left(x^{2}-S x+P\right)$
or, $p(x)=k\left(x^{2}-\frac{25}{2} x+41\right)$, where $k$ is any non-zero real number.
2. Let the cubic polynomial be $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$

Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be the zeroes of the required cubic polynomial, then
$\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\frac{-b}{a} ; \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a} ;$ and $\alpha \beta \gamma=\frac{-d}{a}$.
Now, according to the question, $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-b}{a}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=-2 \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=-7 \Rightarrow \frac{c}{a}=-7$
And $\quad \alpha \beta \gamma=-14 \Rightarrow \frac{-d}{a}=-14 ; \frac{d}{a}=14$
If $a=1$, then $b=-2, c=-7, d=14$
Substituting the values of $a, b, c$ and $d$ in (i), we get the required cubic polynomial $x^{3}-2 x^{2}-7 x+14$
This polynomial satisfies all the given conditions.
3. Let $y=f(x)$ or , $y=3-2 x-x^{2}$

Let us list a few values of $y=3-2 x-x^{2}$ corresponding to a few values of $x$ as follows:

| $\mathbf{x}$ | -5 | -4 | -2 | -1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{3}-\mathbf{2} \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{2}}$ | -12 | -5 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 0 | -5 | -12 | -21 |

Thus, the following points lie on the graph of polynomial $y=3-2 x-x^{2}$ :
$(-5,-12),(-4,-5),(-3,0),(-2,3),(-1,4),(0,3),(1,0)$, $(2,-5),(3,-12)$ and $(4,-21)$
Let us plot these points on a graph paper. The curve thus obtained represents a parabola, as shown in figure.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-054.jpg?height=538&width=782&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1023)
4. $\quad a=-1, b=-2$

1 and 2 are the zeroes of $q(x)$ which are not the zeroes of $p(x)$.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. (i) (d) parabola
(ii) (a) 2
(iii) (b) $-1,3$
(iv) (c) $\quad \mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-3$
(v) (d) 0
2. (i) (a) Sum of zeroes $=-\frac{\operatorname{coefficient} \text { of } x}{\operatorname{coefficient} \text { of } x^{2}}$

$$
=-\frac{(-12)}{4}=3
$$

(ii) (d) Product of zeroes $=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$

$$
=\frac{c}{a}
$$

(iii) (c) ∵ One zero of the polynomial is four times the other

$$
\therefore \beta=4 \alpha
$$

(iv) (d) $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}=3$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \alpha+\beta=3 \Rightarrow \alpha+4 \alpha=3 \quad[\because \beta=4 \alpha] \\
\Rightarrow & \alpha=\frac{3}{5}
\end{array}
$$

(v) (b) $\because \alpha \beta=\frac{3 k+8}{4}$

$$
\begin{array}{ll} 
& \alpha \cdot(4 \alpha)=\frac{3 k+8}{4} \quad[\because \beta=4 \alpha] \\
& 4 \alpha^{2}=\frac{3 k+8}{4} \\
& 4\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}=\frac{36}{25}=\frac{3 k+8}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & 144=75 k+200 \\
\Rightarrow \quad & k=\frac{144-200}{75}=-\frac{56}{75} \\
\therefore \quad & k=-\frac{56}{75}
\end{array}
$$

## Text-book Questions

1. (i) Graph of $y=p(x)$ does not intersect the $x$-axis.

Hence, polynomial $p(x)$ has no zero.
(ii) Polynomial $p(x)$ has four real zeroes.
[ ∵ it cut $x$-axis at four points]
2. (i) We have, $6 x^{2}-3-7 x=6 x^{2}-7 x-3$
$=6 x^{2}-9 x+2 x-3=3 x(2 x-3)+1(2 x-3)$
$=(3 x+1)(2 x-3)$
∴ The zeroes of $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ are $-\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{3}{2}$.
Therefore, sum of the zeroes
$=-\frac{1}{3}+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{7}{6}=\frac{- \text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$
and, product of zeroes $=\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)$

$$
=\frac{-1}{2}=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}
$$

(ii) We have, $p(u)=4 u^{2}+8 u=0$.
$\Rightarrow p(u)=4 u(u+2)$
So, the value of $p(u)=4 u^{2}+8 u$ is zero,
when $u=0$ or $u=-2$
The zeroes of $4 u^{2}+8 \mathrm{u}$ are $\mathrm{u}=0$ or $u=-2$
Sum of its zeroes $=0+(-2)=-2$
$=\frac{-8}{4}=\frac{-(\text { Coefficient of } u)}{\text { Coefficient of } u^{2}}$
Product of its zeroes $=0(-2)=0$
$=\frac{0}{4}=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } u^{2}}$
3. (i) $-\frac{b}{a}=\sqrt{2}, \frac{c}{a}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{1}, \frac{c}{a}=\frac{1}{3}$
Select $a=$ L.C.M. $(1,3)=3$.
Then $\frac{b}{3}=-\sqrt{2}$ and $\frac{c}{3}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\Rightarrow b=-3 \sqrt{2}$ and $c=1$.
Substituting $a=3, b=-3 \sqrt{2}$ and $c=1$
in $a x^{2}+b x+c$,
we get the required polynomial $3 x^{2}-3 \sqrt{2} x+1$.
(ii) Let the polynomial be $a x^{2}+b x+c$ and its zero be $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Then,
$\alpha+\beta=-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{-b}{a}$ and, $\alpha \beta=\frac{1}{4}=\frac{c}{a}$
If $a=4$, then $b=-1$ and $c=1$.
∴ One quadratic polynomial which fits the given conditions is $4 x^{2}+x+1$.
4. (i) Let $p(x)=x^{3}-3 x^{2}+5 x-3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& g(x)=x^{2}-2 \\
& x ^ { 2 } - 2 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + 5 x - 3 } ( x - 3 \\
& \frac{x^{3}+2 x}{{ }^{-3 x^{2}+7 x-3}} \\
& \frac{\begin{array}{l}
-3 x^{2}+6 \\
+\quad- \\
7 x-9
\end{array}}{}
\end{aligned}
$$

We stop here, since degree of $7 x-9$
$=1<2=$ degree of $x^{2}-2$.
So, we can write. $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+5 x-3$
$=\left(x^{2}-2\right)(x-3)+(7 x-9)$
Hence, $p(x)=g(x) \cdot q(x)+r(x)$
where, $q(x)=x-3$ and $r(x)=7 x-9$.
(ii) To carry out the division, we first write divisor in the standard form.
So, divisor $=-x^{2}+2$
We have,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& - x ^ { 2 } + 2 \longdiv { x ^ { 4 } - 5 x + 6 } \\
& \frac{x^{4}-2 x^{2}}{2 x^{2}-5 x+6} \\
& \frac{-\quad \begin{array}{r}
2 x^{2}+4 \\
-\quad 5 x+10
\end{array}}{}
\end{aligned}
$$

∴ The quotient is $-x^{2}-2$
and the remainder is $-5 x+10$.
5. Let us divide $x^{5}-4 x^{3}+x^{2}+3 x+1$
by $x^{3}-3 x+1$, we get

$$
\begin{gathered}
x^{2}-1 \\
x^{3}-3 x+1 \begin{array}{rc}
x^{5}-4 x^{3}+x^{2}+3 x+1 \\
x^{5}-3 x^{3}+x^{2} \\
-\quad+\quad-
\end{array} \\
\hline \begin{array}{cc}
-x^{3} & +3 x+1 \\
-x^{3} & +3 x-1 \\
+\quad & + \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{gathered}
$$

Here, remainder is $2(\neq 0)$.
Therefore, $x^{3}-3 x+1$ is not a factor of $x^{5}-4 x^{3}+x^{2}+3 x+1$
6. Since, two zeroes are $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$, $\therefore\left(x-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)=\left(x^{2}-\frac{5}{3}\right)$ is a factor of the given polynomial. Now, dividing the given polynomial by $x^{2}-\frac{5}{3}$, we have,

$$
\begin{gathered}
x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 5 } { 3 } \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 4 } + 6 x ^ { 3 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 0 x - 5 } ( 3 x ^ { 2 } + 6 x + 3 \\
\frac{-3 x^{4}+{ }^{-5 x^{2}}}{6 x^{3}+3 x^{2}-10 x-5} \\
\frac{-6 x^{3}+-10 x}{-\begin{array}{ll}
3 x^{2}-5 \\
3 x+-5 \\
-
\end{array}} \\
\frac{-\begin{array}{r}
- \\
+
\end{array}}{}
\end{gathered}
$$

So, $3 x^{4}+6 x^{3}-2 x^{2}-10 x-5$
$=\left(3 x^{2}+6 x+3\right)\left(x^{2}-\frac{5}{3}\right)$
$=3\left(x^{2}+2 x+1\right)\left(3 x^{2}-5\right) / 3$
$=(x+1)^{2}\left(3 x^{2}-5\right)$
So, remaining zeroes are $-1,-1$
7. (i) We can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 x^{2}-12 x+10=2\left(x^{2}-6 x+5\right)+0 \\
& \text { Here, } p(x)=2 x^{2}-12 x+10[\text { degree } \\
& q(x)=x^{2}-6 x+5[\text { degree } q(x)=2] \\
& r(x)=0, g(x)=2
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) $2 x^{3}-4 x^{2}+5=\left(2 x^{2}-1\right)(x-2)+(x+3)$

Here, $p(x)=2 x^{3}-4 x^{2}+5$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
g(x)=2 x^{2}-1 & \\
q(x)=x-2 & {[\text { degree } q(x)=1]} \\
r(x)=x+3 & {[\text { degree } r(x)=1]}
\end{array}
$$

(iii) $x^{3}-11=(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)+(-10)$

Here, $p(x)=x^{3}-11, q(x)=x^{2}+x+1$

$$
g(x)=x-1, r(x)=-10
$$

so, degree $r(x)=0$

## Exemplar Questions

1. (a) $4 x^{2}+5 \sqrt{2} x-3=4 x^{2}+6 \sqrt{2} x-\sqrt{2} x-3$
$=2 \sqrt{2} \cdot x(\sqrt{2} \cdot x+3)-1(\sqrt{2} x+3)=(2 \sqrt{2} x-1)(\sqrt{2} x+3)$
The zeroes of the given polynomial are $x=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$ and
$-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$. Sum of the roots $=-\frac{b}{a}=\frac{-5 \sqrt{2}}{4}$
Sum $=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}-6 \sqrt{2}}{2 \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}} \quad(\because a=4, b=5 \sqrt{2})$
$=\frac{-5 \sqrt{2}}{4}=\frac{-b}{a}$. Product of the roots $=\frac{c}{a}=\frac{-3}{4}$
Product $=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \times-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}(\because a=4, c=-3)$
$=\frac{-3}{4}=\frac{c}{a}$
Hence verified the relation between the zeroes and the coefficients of the polynomial.
(b) $y^{2}+\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{5} y-5=2 y^{2}+3 \sqrt{5} y-10$
$=2 y^{2}+4 \sqrt{5} y-\sqrt{5} y-10=2 y(y+2 \sqrt{5})-\sqrt{5}(y+2 \sqrt{5})$
$=(2 y-\sqrt{5})(y+2 \sqrt{5})$

The zeroes of the given polynomial are $y=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$ and $-2 \sqrt{5}$.
Sum of the roots $=-\frac{b}{a}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{5}}{2}$
Sum $=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}-2 \sqrt{5}=\frac{-3 \sqrt{5}}{2}=-\frac{b}{a}$
Product of the roots $=\frac{c}{a}=-5$
Product $=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} \times-2 \sqrt{5}=-\sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{5}=-5$
2. $\operatorname{deg} p(x)<\operatorname{deg} g(x)$
3. No
4. Let $p(x)=6 x^{3}+\sqrt{2} x^{2}-10 x-4 \sqrt{2}$
$g(x)=x-\sqrt{2}$ is a factor of $p(x)$

$$
\begin{gathered}
x - \sqrt { 2 } \longdiv { 6 x ^ { 3 } + \sqrt { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - 1 0 x - 4 \sqrt { 2 } } ( 6 x ^ { 2 } + 7 \sqrt { 2 } x + 4 \\
\frac{-6 x^{3}+6 \sqrt{2} x^{2}}{7 \sqrt{2} x^{2}-10 x} \\
\frac{7 \sqrt{2} x^{2}-14 x}{+} \\
\frac{-4 x-4 \sqrt{2}}{x}
\end{gathered}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& p(x)=g(x) \cdot 6 x^{2}+7 \sqrt{2} x+4 \\
& \quad 6 x^{2}+7 \sqrt{2} x+4=6 x^{2}+3 \sqrt{2} x+4 \sqrt{2} x+4 \\
& =3 \sqrt{2} x(\sqrt{2} x+1)+4(\sqrt{2} x+1)=(3 \sqrt{2} x+4)(\sqrt{2} x+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Other two roots of the given polynomial are $\frac{-4}{3 \sqrt{2}}$ and $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ i.e., $-\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}$ and $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$
5. (b) Since, 2 is the zero of $x^{2}+3 x+k$,
$\therefore(2)^{2}+3(2)+k=0 \Rightarrow k+10=0 \Rightarrow k=-10$
6. (a) [Because if third zero is $\alpha$, sum of the zeroes

$$
\left.=\alpha+0+0=\frac{-b}{a}\right]
$$

7. (a) Since -3 is the zero of $(k-1) x^{2}+k x+1$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad(k-1)(-3)^{2}+k(-3)+1=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 9 k-9-3 k+1=0 \Rightarrow 6 k-8=0 \Rightarrow k=\frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

8. (b) Coefficient of all the terms are positive. So, both roots will be negative.

## Exercise 3 Foundation Builder

## Single Option Correct

1. (d) The expressions $(x-1)(x+1)$ and $(x-1)(x-1)$ which vanish if $x=1$.
2. (a) Let $f(x)=x^{8}-x^{5}+x^{2}-x+1$. For $x=1$ or 0 $f(x)=1>0$. For $x<0$, each term of $f(x)$ is + ve and so first $f(x)>0$.
Hence $f(x)$ is + ve for all real $x$.
3. (b) $10 x=7 . \overline{7}$ or $x=0 . \overline{7}$

Subtracting, $9 x=7 \quad \therefore \quad x=\frac{7}{9}$

$$
2 x=\frac{14}{9}=1.555 \ldots \ldots \ldots=1 . \overline{5}
$$

4. (b) $x^{2}+4 x+2=\left(x^{2}+4 x+2\right)-2=(x+2)^{2}-2$

Lowest value $=-2$ when $x+2=0$
5. (d) Put $a=b$ in given polynomial. Remainder comes to be 0 .
6. (c)
7. (c) Sum of the zeroes $=\frac{3 k}{2} \Rightarrow 6=\frac{3 k}{2} \Rightarrow k=\frac{12}{3}=4$
8. (b) By division algorithm, we have

Dividend $=$ Divisor × Quotient + Remainder
⇒ Dividend - Remainder $=$ Divisor × Quotient
⇒ Dividend - Remainder is always divisible by the divisor.
Hence, $f(x)-(x+a)=x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-26 x+10-a$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}-2 x+k$.
Let us now divide $x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-26 x+10-a$ by $x^{2}-2 x+k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^{2}-2 x+k & \overline{x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-26 x+10-a}\left(x^{2}-4 x+(8-k)\right. \\
& \begin{array}{l}
x^{4}-2 x^{3}+k x^{2} \\
-+-
\end{array} \\
& -4 x^{3}+(16-k) x^{2}-26 x+10-a \\
& +4 x^{3}+8 x^{2}-4 k x \\
+\quad+ & \begin{array}{c}
(8-k) x^{2}-(26-4 k) x+10-a \\
(8-k) x^{2}-(16-2 k) x+\left(8 k^{2}-k\right) \\
+ \\
-
\end{array} \\
& (-10+2 k) x+\left(10-a-8 k+k^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $f(x)-(x+a)=x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-26 x+10-a$ to be exactly divisible by $x^{2}-2 x+k$, we must have $(-10+2 k) x+\left(10-a-8 k+k^{2}\right)=0$ for all $x$. Equating co-efficients of $x$ and constant terms
$-10+2 k=0$ and $10-a-8 k+k^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow k=5$ and $10-a-40+25=0 \Rightarrow k=5$ and $a=-5$.
9. (b) $P(x)=a x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x-4$
$P(3)=27 a+36+9-4=27 a+41$
$P(x)=x^{3}-4 x+a ; P(3)=27-12+a=15+a$
$\therefore 27 a+41=15+a \Rightarrow a=-1$
10. (a) $x=-1$ is the root of the quadratic polynomial $p(x)$

So, quadratic polynomial $p(x)=k(x+1)^{2}$
$p(-2)=k(-2+1)^{2}=2 \Rightarrow k=2 \therefore p(x)=2(x+1)^{2}$
Also, $p(2)=2(2+1)^{2}=2 \times 3 \times 3=18$
11. (c) We have, $p(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$, where $a, b, c$ are in AP and $a, b, c$, are positive real numbers.
Since, $\alpha, \beta$ are roots of $p(x)=0$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are integers. Then, $p(x)=a x^{2}+b x+\mathrm{c}=0$
Sum of roots of the equation $=-\frac{b}{a} \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=\frac{-b}{a}$,
Product of roots $=\frac{c}{a} \Rightarrow \alpha \beta=\frac{c}{a}$
$\because \alpha, \beta$ are integers. $\quad \therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=\frac{-b}{a}=-\lambda, \lambda \in I$
$\Rightarrow b=a \lambda$
$\because \quad a, b, c$ are in AP. $\quad \therefore \quad b=\frac{a+c}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{a+c}{2}=a \lambda$
$\Rightarrow c=a(2 \lambda-1) \quad \therefore a x^{2}+a \lambda x+a(2 \lambda-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+\lambda x+(2 \lambda-1)=0 \quad[\because \alpha \neq 0]$
$D=\lambda^{2}-4(2 \lambda-1)$ is a perfect square for integeral roots.
$\because \lambda^{2}-8 \lambda+4=k^{2}$
$\Rightarrow(\lambda-4)^{2}-12=k^{2} \Rightarrow(\lambda-4)^{2}-\mathrm{k}^{2}=12$
$\Rightarrow(\lambda-4-k)(\lambda-4+k)=2 \times 6$
$\Rightarrow \lambda-4-k=2$ and $\lambda-4+k=6$
$\because \lambda=8$ and $k=2$
$\therefore \alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta=\frac{-b}{a}+\frac{c}{a}=\frac{-a \lambda+a(2 \lambda-1)}{a}=\frac{a(\lambda-1)}{a}$
$=\lambda-1=8-1=7$
12. (c) $x^{2}-4=(x-2)(x+2)$ are the factors
$\therefore x=2,-2$ are roots of polynomial
∴ at $x=2 ; P(2)=2(2)^{3}+k_{1}(2)^{2}+k_{2}(2)+12=0$
$\Rightarrow 16+4 k_{1}+2 k_{2}+12=0 \Rightarrow 2 k_{1}+k^{2}=-14$
at $x=2 ; P(-2)=2(-2)^{3}+k_{1}(-2)^{2}+k_{2}(-2)+12=0$
$\Rightarrow-16+4 k_{1}-2 k_{2}+12=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 k_{1}-k_{2}=2$

From (i) \& (ii), $k_{1}=-3 \therefore k_{1}+k_{2}=-11$
13. (b) The remainder when the polynomial
$x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-25 x+10$ is divided by $x^{2}-2 x+k$ is
$(2 k-9) x+\left(k^{2}-8 k+10\right)$
But, the remainder is $x+a$ Then,
$(2 k-9) x+\left(k^{2}-8 k+10\right)=x+a$
Compare the coefficients of $x$ and constant term on both the sides.
Then, $2 k-9=1$ and $k^{2}-8 k+10=a$
$2 k=10 \Rightarrow k=5$
Now $a=k^{2}-8 k+10=25-40+10=35-40=-5$
Hence, the required value of a is -5 .
14. (b) Given, $x^{4}-x^{2}+2 x-1=0$
$\Rightarrow x^{4}-(x-1)^{2}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad\left(x^{2}-x+1\right)\left(x^{2}+x-1\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-x+1=0$ or $x^{2}+x-1=0$
Here, $x^{2}-x+1=0$ has no real roots
and $x^{2}+x-1=0$ has two real roots.
15. (b) $x=3+3^{2 / 3}+3^{1 / 3}$
$(x-3)^{3}=\left(3^{\frac{2}{3}}+3^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^{3}$
$x^{3}-27-9 x^{2}+27 x=3^{2}+3+3 \times 3^{2 / 3} \times 3^{1 / 3}\left(3^{2 / 3}+3^{1 / 3}\right)$
$x^{3}-27-9 x^{2}+27 x-9-3=9(x-3)$
$x^{3}-39-9 x^{2}+27 x-9 x+27=0$
$x^{3}-9 x^{2}+18 x-12=0$
16. (a) $P(x)$ is a polynomial of degree 3 .
and $P(n)=\frac{1}{n} \Rightarrow n P(n)-1=0$
$n(P(n))$ is a polynomial of degree 4
$\therefore n P(n)-1=k(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)(n-4)$
For $n=0 ;-1=24 k \Rightarrow k=\frac{-1}{24}$
For $n=5 ; 5 \times P(5)-1=\frac{-1}{24}(4)(3)(2)(1)$
$\Rightarrow 5 \cdot P(5)-1=-1 \Rightarrow P(5)=0$
17. (b) Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of $p(x)=0$, then
$p(x)=(x-\alpha)(x-\beta) \Rightarrow g(x)=\left(x^{3}-\alpha\right)\left(x^{3}-\beta\right)$
Let $\alpha=a_{1}^{3}$ and $\beta=b_{1}^{3} \Rightarrow g(x)=\left(x^{3}-a_{1}^{3}\right)\left(x^{3}-b_{1}^{3}\right)$
$g(x)=\left(x-a_{1}\right)\left(x-b_{1}\right) \times \frac{\left(x^{2}+a_{1} x+a_{1}^{2}\right)}{D<0} \frac{\left(x^{2}+b_{1} x+b_{1}^{2}\right)}{D<0}$
For exactly two values of $x, g(x)=0$
$\because g(x)=x^{6}+a x^{3}+b$ and it is even degree polynomial.
So, $g(x) \geq \alpha, \forall x \in \mathrm{R}$
Hence, I and III are correct.
18. (d) $f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+(x-2)^{2}+(x-3)^{2}+(x-4)^{2}$
$=4\left(x-\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}+5$
$f(x)$ is minimum at $x=\frac{5}{2}=2.5$

## More Than One Option Correct

1. (a, b, c)

In (a) power of $x$ is -1 i.e. negative ∴ (a) is not true.
In (b) power of $x=\frac{1}{2}$, not an integer. ∴ (b) is not true
In(c) Here also power of $x$ is not an integer ∴ (c) is not true
(d) holds [ ∵ all the powers of $x$ are non-negative integers.]
2. (a, c, d)
(a) is not true [By def.]
(b) holds
[ ∵ degree of a zero polynomial is not defined]
(c) is not true
[ ∵ degree of a constant polynomial is ' 0 ']
(d) is not true
[ ∵ a polynomial of degree $n$ has at most $n$ zeroes].
3. (a, b, d)
(a) If $p(x)$ is not divisible by $g(x)$, then $r(x) \neq 0 \therefore$ (a) is not true.
(b) If $p(x)$ is divisible by $g(x)$, then
$r(x)=0$ for all $x$ i.e., $r(x)$ is a zero polynomial whose degree is not defined.
∴ (b) is not true.
(c) is clearly true [ ∵ division algorithm rule]
(d) Since degree of $r(x)<$ degree of $g(x)$ or $r(x)=0$, but $g(x) \neq 0$.
$\therefore r(x)=g(x)$ is not true.
4. (a, b, d)
(a) $x^{2}+\frac{1}{x}=x^{2}+x^{-1}$ is not a polynomial since the exponent of variable in 2nd term is negative
(b) $2 x^{2}-3 \sqrt{x}+1=2 x^{2}-3 x^{\frac{1}{2}}+1$ is not a polynomial, since the exponent of variable in 2nd term is a rational number.
(c) $x^{3}-3 x+1$ is a polynomial.
(d) $2 x^{\frac{3}{2}}-5 x$ is also not a polynomial, since the exponents of variable in 1st term is a rational number
Hence, (a), (b) and (d) is not a polynomial.
5. (a, b, c)
(a) It is quadratic polynomial [ ∵ the graph meets the $x$-axis in two points]
(b) It is not a quadratic polynomial [ ∵ the graph meets the $x$-axis in two points]
(c) It is a quadratic polynomial [ ∵ the graph meets the $x$-axis in two points]
(d) It is a quadratic polynomial [ ∵ the graph meets the $x$-axis in two points]

Passage Based Questions

1. (c) $\alpha+\beta=p, \alpha \beta=q \therefore \frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{p}{q}$
2. (a) $\alpha+\beta=-1, \alpha \beta=-2$
$\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\beta}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\beta-\alpha}{\alpha \beta}\right)^{2}=\frac{(\beta+\alpha)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta}{(\alpha \beta)^{2}}$
$=\frac{(1)^{2}-4(-2)^{2}}{4}=\frac{9}{4}$
3. (b) $\alpha+\beta=5, \alpha \beta=4$
$\therefore \frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}-2 \alpha \beta=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}-2 \alpha \beta=\frac{5}{4}-8=-\frac{27}{4}$
4. (b) $\alpha \beta \gamma=-24$

⇒ (12) $\gamma=-24 \Rightarrow \gamma=-2$
5. (a) $a-b+a+a+b=3 \Rightarrow a=1$
$(a-b) a(a+b)=\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right) a=-1$
$\Rightarrow 1-b^{2}=-1 \Rightarrow b^{2}=2 \therefore a+b^{2}=1+2=3$
6. (a) Let $\alpha,-\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes

Then, $\alpha-\alpha+\beta=5 \Rightarrow \beta=5$
and $-\alpha^{2} \beta=-80 \Rightarrow \alpha^{2}=\frac{80}{5}=16 \Rightarrow \alpha= \pm 4$
Required zeroes are $4,-4,5$

## Assertion \& Reason

1. (a) Clearly, reason is true. [Standard Result] $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-(-2)=2 \Rightarrow 0+\gamma=2 \therefore \gamma=2$.
$\alpha \beta \gamma=-(-r)=r \quad \therefore \alpha \beta(2)=r \quad \Rightarrow \alpha \beta=\frac{r}{2}$
$\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=q \Rightarrow \frac{r}{2}+r(\alpha+\beta)=q \Rightarrow \frac{g}{2}+\gamma(0)=q$
$\Rightarrow \gamma=2 q \therefore$ Assertion is true
Since, reason gives assertion.
2. (b) Reason is true.

Let $\alpha, \frac{1}{\alpha}$ be the zeroes of $p(x)$, then
$\alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha}=\frac{4 k}{k^{2}+4} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{4 k}{k^{2}+4}$
$\therefore k^{2}-4 k+4=0 \Rightarrow(k-2)^{2}=0 \therefore k=2$
∴ Assertion is true.
Since, reason is not correct explanation for assertion.
3. (c) Reason is false $[\therefore$ a polynomial of $n$th degree has at most $x$ zeroes.]
Again, $x^{3}+x=x\left(x^{2}+1\right)$
which has only one real zero ( $x=0$ )
$\left[\because x^{2}+1 \neq 0\right.$ for all $\left.x \in R\right]$
∴ Assertion is true.
4. (b) 5. (c) 6. (d)
