## Very Short Answer Questions

1. $P Q \| R S$ (Given)

So, $\angle P=\angle S$ (Alternate angles)
and $\angle Q=\angle R$
Also, $\angle P O Q=\angle S O R$ (Vertically opposite angles)
Therefore, $\triangle P O Q \sim \triangle S O R$ (AAA similarity criterion)
2. Proof : ∵ $\triangle A B C$ is an isosceles right angled triangle.
$\therefore A C=B C$

Using Pythagoras' theorem, we have
$A B^{2}=A C^{2}+B C^{2}$
$A B^{2}=A C^{2}+A C^{2} \quad[\because A C=B C$ (Given)
$A B^{2}=2 A C^{2}$
3. In $\triangle A B C$, we have, $A B=A C$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-178.jpg?height=330&width=435&top_left_y=1027&top_left_x=1201)
$\therefore \quad \angle B=\angle C$ (angles opposite to equal sides)
In $\triangle A B D$ and $\triangle E C F, \angle B=\angle C$
$\angle A D B=\angle E F C$ (each $90^{\circ}$ as $A D \perp B C, E F \perp A C$ )
$\therefore \quad \triangle A B D \sim \triangle E C F($ By $A A)$
4. Let $A B C$ and $D E F$ be two triangles such that
$\triangle A B C \sim \triangle D E F$.
Let $A L, D M$ be their medians, respectively
$\therefore \frac{\operatorname{area}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{A L^{2}}{D M^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{121}{64}=\frac{(12.1)^{2}}{D M^{2}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-178.jpg?height=282&width=320&top_left_y=1786&top_left_x=1101)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-178.jpg?height=232&width=252&top_left_y=1818&top_left_x=1480)

$$
D M^{2}=\frac{64 \times(12.1)^{2}}{121}
$$

$\Rightarrow D M=\frac{8 \times 12.1}{11}=8 \times 1.1=8.8 \mathrm{~cm}$
Hence, required corresponding median $=8.8 \mathrm{~cm}$.
5. Let $A B C$ and $D E F$ be two given triangles such that $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle D E F$. Draw $A L \perp B C, D M \perp E F, A L=4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ we know that, $\frac{\operatorname{area}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{A L^{2}}{D M^{2}}$

## Mathematics

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-179.jpg?height=95&width=108&top_left_y=2544&top_left_x=141)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-179.jpg?height=330&width=324&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=328)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-179.jpg?height=264&width=245&top_left_y=447&top_left_x=731)
$\Rightarrow \frac{81}{49}=\frac{(4.5)^{2}}{D M^{2}} \Rightarrow D M^{2}=\frac{(4.5)^{2} \times 49}{81}$
$\Rightarrow D M=\frac{(4.5) \times(7)}{9}=\frac{31.5}{9}=3.5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
6. Given, $A B=2 D E$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \sim \triangle \mathrm{DEF}$

Hence, $\frac{\operatorname{area}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{A B^{2}}{D E^{2}}$
or $\frac{56}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{4 D E^{2}}{D E^{2}}=4[\because A B=2 D E]$
$\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)=\frac{56}{4}=14 \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm}$.
7. $A B^{2}=A D^{2}+B D^{2}$ and $A C^{2}=A D^{2}+D C^{2}$
$\therefore A B^{2}+A C^{2}=2 A D^{2}+B D^{2}+D C^{2}$
$=(B D+D C)^{2}=B C^{2}$
$\left[\because A D^{2}=B D . D C\right]$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-179.jpg?height=213&width=382&top_left_y=1425&top_left_x=461)
$\therefore A B C$ is right triangle $\Rightarrow \angle B A C=90^{\circ}$
(By converse of Pythagoras theorem)
8. Since $\triangle A C B \sim \triangle A P Q$
$\therefore \quad \frac{A C}{A P}=\frac{C B}{P Q} \Rightarrow \frac{A C}{2.8}=\frac{8}{4}=2$
$\Rightarrow A C=5.6 \mathrm{~cm}$
9. $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle P Q R$
(Given)
$\frac{A B}{P Q}=\frac{B C}{Q R}=\frac{A C}{P R}$ (By similarity of triangles)
$\Rightarrow \frac{z}{3}=\frac{8}{6}=\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{y}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{z}{3}=\frac{8}{6}$ and $\frac{8}{6}=\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{y}$
$\Rightarrow z=\frac{8 \times 3}{6}$ and $y=\frac{4 \sqrt{3} \times 6}{8}$
$\therefore \quad z=4$ and $y=3 \sqrt{3}$
$\therefore \quad y+z=4+3 \sqrt{3}$
∴ The length of the altitude is $12 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
10.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-179.jpg?height=337&width=332&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1151)

By Pythagoras theorem:

$$
(3 \sqrt{3})^{2}=h^{2}+\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad 27=h^{2}+\frac{27}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}=27-\frac{27}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \quad h^{2}=\frac{81}{4}$
$\therefore \quad h=\frac{9}{2}=4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
11. As, $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \sim \triangle \mathrm{PQR}$
[Given]
$\Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{PQR})}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}^{2}}{\mathrm{PQ}^{2}}[\because$ Ratio of areas of two similar triangles is equal to the ratio of the square of their corresponding sides]

$$
\therefore \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{PQR})}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{9}
$$

12. If two triangles are similar, then the ratio of the area of both triangles is proportional to the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides.
$\because \frac{\mathrm{AD}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{1}{3} \therefore \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ADE})}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{AD}}{\mathrm{AB}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{9}$.
13. Construction: Draw $\mathrm{AM} \perp \mathrm{BC}$ and $\mathrm{DN} \perp \mathrm{BC}$.

Proof: In $\triangle \mathrm{AMO}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{DNO}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-179.jpg?height=211&width=327&top_left_y=2332&top_left_x=1311)
$\angle \mathrm{AMO}=\angle \mathrm{DNO}=90^{\circ}$
(By construction)
$\angle \mathrm{AOM}=\angle \mathrm{DON}$
∴ By AA-similarity
$\triangle \mathrm{AMO} \sim \triangle \mathrm{DNO}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{OA}}{\mathrm{OD}}=\frac{\mathrm{AM}}{\mathrm{DN}}$
$\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{DBC})}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{BC} \times \mathrm{AM}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{BC} \times \mathrm{DN}}=\frac{\mathrm{AM}}{\mathrm{DN}}$
$\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{DBC})}=\frac{\mathrm{OA}}{\mathrm{OD}}$
[From (i)]
Hence, proved.

## OR

Given: In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{AD} \perp \mathrm{BC}$
To prove: $\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{CD}^{2}=\mathrm{BD}^{2}+\mathrm{AC}^{2}$
Proof: In right $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{AB}^{2}=\mathrm{AD}^{2}+\mathrm{BD}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AD}^{2}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}-\mathrm{BD}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

In right $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$

$$
\mathrm{AC}^{2}=\mathrm{AD}^{2}+\mathrm{CD}^{2}
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AD}^{2}=\mathrm{AC}^{2}-\mathrm{CD}^{2}$

From (i) and (ii)
$\mathrm{AB}^{2}-\mathrm{BD}^{2}=\mathrm{AC}^{2}-\mathrm{CD}^{2}$
$\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{CD}^{2}=\mathrm{AC}^{2}+\mathrm{BD}^{2}$
Hence, proved.

## Short Answer Questions

1. In $\triangle O A B, D E \| A B$
$\Rightarrow \frac{O D}{A D}=\frac{O E}{E B}$ [Basic proportionality theorem]

Again in $\triangle O B C, E F \| B C$
$\Rightarrow \frac{O E}{E B}=\frac{O F}{F C}$ [Basic proportionality theorem]

From (i) and (ii), we get, $\frac{O D}{A D}=\frac{O F}{F C}$
As in $\triangle O A C, \frac{O D}{A D}=\frac{O F}{F C} \Rightarrow D F \| A C$
[ ∵ In a triangle if a line divides the two sides in the same proportion then it is parallel to the third side.]
2. Let $A B C$ be an isosceles triangle, where base $A B=a$ and equal sides $A C=B C=b$. Let $C D$ be the perpendicular on $A B$.
So, $A D=D B=\frac{1}{2} A B=\frac{a}{2}$
Altitude, $C D=$ height of the $\triangle A B C$ and is given by
$h=\sqrt{A C^{2}-A D^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4 b^{2}-a^{2}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-180.jpg?height=328&width=261&top_left_y=2220&top_left_x=731)

Area of the $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2} \times$ base × altitude
$=\frac{1}{2} \times a \times \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4 b^{2}-a^{2}}=\frac{a}{4} \sqrt{4 b^{2}-a^{2}}$.
3. Given, $A B=A D$ and $A E, A F$ are angle bisectors of $\angle B A C$ and $\angle D A C$ respectively.
Now in $\triangle A B C, A E$ is angle bisector of $\angle B A C$
$\therefore \quad \frac{A B}{A C}=\frac{B E}{E C}$

Similarly, $\frac{A D}{A C}=\frac{D F}{F C} \Rightarrow \frac{A B}{A C}=\frac{D F}{F C}$
( $\because A B=A D)$
Comparing eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\frac{B E}{E C}=\frac{D F}{F C}$
$\therefore E F \| B D$ in $\triangle B C D$.
4. In figure, $A B$ represents the stick and $B C$ is its shadow.

Therefore $A B=12 \mathrm{~m}$ and $B C=8 \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-180.jpg?height=304&width=270&top_left_y=1133&top_left_x=1142)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-180.jpg?height=378&width=241&top_left_y=1073&top_left_x=1452)

Again $P Q$ is tower and $Q R$ is its shadow. Therefore $Q R=40 \mathrm{~m}$
Now, $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle P Q R$
$\therefore \quad \frac{P Q}{Q R}=\frac{A B}{B C} \Rightarrow \frac{P Q}{40}=\frac{12}{8} \Rightarrow P Q=60 \mathrm{~m}$
5. In $\triangle A C B$
$A B^{2}=2 A C^{2}$ and $A C=B C$
Now in $\triangle A B C$,
$A B^{2}=2 A C^{2}=A C^{2}+A C^{2}$
$A B^{2}=A C^{2}+B C^{2}(\because B C=A C)$
$\angle A C B=90^{\circ}$ and
$\triangle A B C$ is a right triangle
(Converse of Pythagoras theorem)
6. Ar. $(\triangle A B C)=25 \times 4=100$ sq. cm.
7. In $\triangle A B C$ and $\triangle A D C$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \angle B A C=\angle A D C \\
& \angle A C B=\angle A C D \\
\therefore \quad & \triangle B A C \sim \triangle A D C \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \frac{C A}{C D}=\frac{C B}{C A}
\end{aligned}
$$

[Given]
[Common]
[AA similarity]
8. In $\triangle A B C, D E \| A B$
$\Rightarrow \frac{C D}{A C}=\frac{C E}{B C}$
[Corollary of basic proportionality theorem]

Again in $\triangle C D B, E F \| B D$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{C F}{C D}=\frac{C E}{C B}
$$

[Corollary of Basic proportionality theorem]
From (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{C D}{A C}=\frac{C F}{C D} \Rightarrow C D^{2}=C F \times A C$
9. $\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{E F^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{8}{11}=\frac{B C}{E F}$
$\Rightarrow B C=\frac{(15.4)(8)}{11}=1.4 \times 8=11.2 \mathrm{~cm}$
10. Draw $A M \perp B C, D N \perp B C$

In $\triangle A M O$ and $\triangle D N O$,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-181.jpg?height=387&width=390&top_left_y=1046&top_left_x=456)
$\angle 1=\angle 4$
......(each $90^{\circ}$ )
$\angle 2=\angle 3$
......(vertically opposite angles)
$\triangle A M O \sim \triangle D N O \quad \ldots .$. (By $A A$ rule of similarity)
$\frac{A O}{D O}=\frac{A M}{D N}$

Now, $\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D B C)}=\frac{(1 / 2)(B C)(A M)}{(1 / 2)(B C)(D N)}=\frac{A M}{D N}=\frac{A O}{D O}$
Hence, $\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D B C)}=\frac{A O}{D O}$
11.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-181.jpg?height=369&width=373&top_left_y=1914&top_left_x=324)

In $\triangle A B C$, given that $F, E$ and $D$ are the mid-points of $A B$, $A C$ and $B C$ respectively.
Hence, $F E\|B C, D E\| A B$ and $D F \| A C$
By mid-point theorem.
If $\quad D E \| B A$
then $\quad D E \| B F$
and if $\quad F E \| B C$
then $\quad F E \| B D$
$\therefore \quad F E D B$ is a parallelogram in which $D F$ diagonal of parallelogram divides it into two equal areas.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { So, } \\
& \text { Similarly }(\triangle B D F)=\operatorname{ar}(\triangle C D E)=\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F) \\
& \\
& \\
& \\
& \\
& \\
& \\
& \\
& \operatorname{ar}(\triangle A F E) \\
& =\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)
\end{aligned}=\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F) 3
$$

On adding equations (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) $\operatorname{ar}(\triangle B D F)+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle C D E)+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A F E)+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F) =4 \operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)$
$\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)=4 \operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)$

$$
\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}=\frac{1}{4}
$$

12. 

Given : $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \sim \triangle \mathrm{DEF}$ and $\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)=\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)$
To prove : $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \cong \triangle \mathrm{DEF}$
Proof: As $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle D E F$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{A B^{2}}{D E^{2}}=\frac{B C^{2}}{E F^{2}}=\frac{A C^{2}}{D F^{2}}$
[ ∵ Ratio of area of two similar triangles is equal to ratio of squares of their corresponding sides.]
But ar $(\triangle A B C)=$ ar $(\triangle D E F)$
$\Rightarrow 1=\frac{A B^{2}}{D E^{2}}=\frac{B C^{2}}{E F^{2}}=\frac{A C^{2}}{D F^{2}}$
or $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{DE}, \mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{EF}, \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{DF}$
∴ By SSS congruence rule, $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \cong \triangle \mathrm{DEF}$
13.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-181.jpg?height=262&width=437&top_left_y=1708&top_left_x=1151)

Since, a perpendicular is drawn from the vertex of the right angle of a triangle to the hypotenuse, then triangles on both sides of the perpendicular are similar to the whole triangle and to each other.
Hence, $\triangle \mathrm{ACD} \sim \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. So, $\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{AD}}{\mathrm{AC}}$.

$$
\text { or, } \mathrm{AC}^{2}=\mathrm{AB} \cdot \mathrm{AD}
$$

Using Pythagoras theorem in $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$,
$(\mathrm{AC})^{2}=(\mathrm{CD})^{2}+(\mathrm{AD})^{2} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{AC})^{2}-(\mathrm{AD})^{2}=(\mathrm{CD})^{2}$
Using eqn. (i),
$\mathrm{AB} . \mathrm{AD}-(\mathrm{AD})^{2}=(\mathrm{CD})^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AD}(\mathrm{AB}-\mathrm{AD})=(\mathrm{CD})^{2}$
$\Rightarrow A D \times D B=(C D)^{2} \Rightarrow(C D)^{2}=B D \times A D$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-182.jpg?height=332&width=465&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=360)

Using Pythagoras theorem in $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{PCQ}$,
$(\mathrm{AB})^{2}=(\mathrm{AC})^{2}+(\mathrm{CB})^{2}$
and $(\mathrm{PQ})^{2}=(\mathrm{PC})^{2}+(\mathrm{QC})^{2}$

Adding equations (i) and (ii),
$(\mathrm{AB})^{2}+(\mathrm{PQ})^{2}=(\mathrm{AC})^{2}+(\mathrm{CB})^{2}+(\mathrm{PC})^{2}+(\mathrm{QC})^{2}$

Since, $(\mathrm{AQ})^{2}=(\mathrm{CQ})^{2}+(\mathrm{AC})^{2}$ and $(\mathrm{BP})^{2}=(\mathrm{CP})^{2}+(\mathrm{BC})^{2}$.
Hence, from equation (iii),
$(\mathrm{AB})^{2}+(\mathrm{PQ})^{2}=(\mathrm{AQ})^{2}+(\mathrm{BP})^{2}$.
or, $\left(\mathrm{AQ}^{2}+\mathrm{BP}^{2}\right)=\left(\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{PQ}^{2}\right)$.
14. $\because \triangle \mathrm{ABC} \sim \triangle \mathrm{DEF}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{DE}}=\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{EF}}=\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{DF}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 x-1}{18}=\frac{2 x+2}{3 x+9}=\frac{3 x}{6 x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 x-1}{18}=\frac{3 x}{6 x}$
$\frac{2 x+2}{3 x+9}=\frac{3 x}{6 x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 x-1}{18}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\frac{2 x+2}{3 x+9}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x-1=\frac{1}{2} \times 18$
$4 x+4=3 x+9$
$\Rightarrow 2 x=9+1$
$4 x-3 x=9-4$
$\Rightarrow 2 x=10$
$x=5$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=5$
In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{AB}=2 x-1=10-1=9 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& \mathrm{BC}=2 x+2=10+2=12 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& \mathrm{AC}=3 x=15 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

In $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{DE}=18 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{EF}=3 x+9=15+9=24 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& \mathrm{DF}=6 x=30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Note

When two triangle are similar then their corres-ponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional.

## Long Answer Questions

1. Given :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-182.jpg?height=298&width=392&top_left_y=2256&top_left_x=397)

Construction : Draw $O F \| D C$
Proof: In $\triangle A D C, F O \| D C$,
$\therefore \quad \frac{A F}{F D}=\frac{A O}{O C}$
...(i) [B.P. Theorem]

But $\frac{A O}{O C}=\frac{B O}{O D}$
[Given]
Comparing eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$
\frac{A F}{F D}=\frac{B O}{O D}
$$

$\therefore F O \| A B$ [Converse of B.P. theorem]
Also, $F O \| D C$
[By construction]
$\therefore A B \| D C$
$\therefore A B C D$ is a trapezium.
2. We have, $\frac{Q T}{P R}=\frac{Q R}{Q S}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{Q T}{Q R}=\frac{P R}{Q S}$

Now $\angle 1=\angle 2$ (Given)
$\therefore P Q=P R$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-182.jpg?height=284&width=305&top_left_y=991&top_left_x=1507)
(Sides opposite to equal angles are equal)
$\therefore \quad \frac{Q T}{Q R}=\frac{P Q}{Q S}$ [From (i) and (ii)]

Now in $\triangle P Q S$ and $\triangle T Q R$,
$\frac{P Q}{Q S}=\frac{Q T}{Q R} \quad$ (from (iii))
and $\angle Q=\angle Q(\angle 1$ each $)$
$\therefore \quad \triangle P Q S \sim \triangle T Q R$ (By SAS similarity)
3. We are given that $\triangle A B E \cong \triangle A C D$

Therefore, $A B=A C$ (By CPCT)
and $A E=A D$ (By CPCT)
$\therefore \quad \frac{A B}{A D}=\frac{A C}{A E} \quad[$ From (i) and (ii) $]$
i.e., $\frac{A B}{A C}=\frac{A D}{A E}$
and $\angle D A E=\angle B A C$ (Common angle)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-182.jpg?height=268&width=211&top_left_y=1736&top_left_x=1603)

Further, $\frac{A B}{A C}=\frac{A D}{A E}$
[From (iii)]
$\therefore \triangle A D E \sim \triangle A B C$
(SAS similarity)
4. Given : $A D \perp B C$

$$
2 D B=3 C D
$$

To prove : $5 A B^{2}=5 A C^{2}+B C^{2}$
Proof: $2 D B=3 C D$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{D B}{C D}=\frac{3}{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-182.jpg?height=332&width=344&top_left_y=2161&top_left_x=1468)
$\Rightarrow \quad D B=3 x, C D=2 x$ and $B C=5 x$

In $\triangle A D B, \angle B D A=90^{\circ} A B^{2}=A D^{2}+D B^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A B^{2}=A D^{2}+(3 x)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A B^{2}=A D^{2}+9 x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 5 A B^{2}=5 A D^{2}+45 x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 5 A D^{2}=5 A B^{2}-45 x^{2}$
and In $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}, A C^{2}=A D^{2}+C D^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A C^{2}=A D^{2}+(2 x)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A C^{2}=A D^{2}+4 x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 5 A C^{2}=5 A D^{2}+20 x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 5 A D^{2}=5 A C^{2}-20 x^{2}$

On comparing equations (i) and (ii),

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5 A B^{2}-45 x^{2}=5 A C^{2}-20 x^{2} \\
& 5 A B^{2}=5 A C^{2}-20 x^{2}+45 x^{2} \\
& 5 A B^{2}=5 A C^{2}+25 x^{2} \\
& 5 A B^{2}=5 A C^{2}+(5 x)^{2} \\
& 5 A B^{2}=5 A C^{2}+B C^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$[\because B C=5 x]$
5.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-183.jpg?height=309&width=488&top_left_y=1037&top_left_x=383)

Let us take a right triangle ABC , right angled at B . Let BD be the perpendicular to the hypotenuse AC .
In $\triangle \mathrm{ADB}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,
$\angle \mathrm{A}=\angle \mathrm{A}$
(Common)
and $\angle \mathrm{ADB}=\angle \mathrm{ABC} \quad\left(\because \angle \mathrm{B}=90^{\circ}\right)$
So, $\triangle \mathrm{ADB} \sim \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
then, $\frac{\mathrm{AD}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{AC}}$ (sides are proportional)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AD} \cdot \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}$

Similarly, $\triangle \mathrm{BDC} \sim \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
then, $\frac{\mathrm{CD}}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{AC}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{CD} \cdot \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}^{2}$

Adding equations (i) and (ii),
$\mathrm{AD} \cdot \mathrm{AC}+\mathrm{CD} \cdot \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{BC}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AC}(\mathrm{AD}+\mathrm{CD})=\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{BC}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AC} \cdot \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{BC}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AC}^{2}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{BC}^{2}$.
Therefore, in a right angle triangle, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of squares of the other two sides.
6. Draw $\mathrm{AD} \perp \mathrm{BC}$

In right $\triangle \mathrm{ADB}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$, we have $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AC}$,
$\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{AC}$ and
$\angle \mathrm{ADB}=\angle \mathrm{ADC}$
(each $90^{\circ}$ )
$\therefore \triangle A D B \cong \triangle A D C$
(By RHS)
$\therefore B D=D C=\frac{1}{2} B C$

Now, $B T=\frac{1}{3} B C$,
$T C=\frac{2}{3} B C$ and $D C=\frac{1}{2} B C$
In $\triangle \mathrm{ATC}, \angle \mathrm{C}=60^{\circ}$, which is acute angle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-183.jpg?height=350&width=380&top_left_y=621&top_left_x=1286)
$\therefore \mathrm{AT}^{2}=\mathrm{AC}^{2}+\mathrm{TC}^{2}-2 \mathrm{TC} \times \mathrm{DC}$
$=A C^{2}+\left(\frac{2}{3} B C\right)^{2}-2 \times \frac{2}{3} B C \times \frac{1}{2} B C$
$=A C^{2}+\left(\frac{4}{9}-\frac{2}{3}\right) B C^{2}$
$=A C^{2}-\frac{2}{9} B C^{2}=A B^{2}-\frac{2}{9} A B^{2}(\because \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{AB})$
$=\frac{7}{9} A B^{2}$
$\therefore \quad 9 \mathrm{AT}^{2}=7 \mathrm{AB}^{2}$, which is the required result.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-183.jpg?height=310&width=369&top_left_y=1548&top_left_x=1151)

Given, $A B=8 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $B C=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore A C=\sqrt{8^{2}+6^{2}}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
Also, given $A C: C E=2: 1$
Now, produce $B C$ to meet $D E$ at the point $P$
As $C P$ is parallel to $A D$,
$\triangle E C P \sim \triangle E A D$
$\therefore \frac{C P}{A D}=\frac{C E}{A E}=\frac{C P}{6}=\frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow C P=2 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\triangle C P D$ is a right triangle.
$\therefore D P=\sqrt{C D^{2}+C P^{2}}=\sqrt{68}=2 \sqrt{17} \mathrm{~cm}$
But $D P: P E=2: 1$ (from eqn. (i))
$P E=\sqrt{17} \mathrm{~cm} \quad \therefore D E=D P+P E$

$$
=2 \sqrt{17}+\sqrt{17}=3 \sqrt{17} \mathrm{~cm} .
$$

2. In $\triangle P O Q$ and $\triangle R O S$.
$\angle P O Q=\angle R O S$ (vertically opposite angles)
$\angle P Q O=\angle R S O$ (alternate angles)
$\angle Q P O=\angle S R O$ (alternate angles)
Then, by $A A A$ similarity criterion
$\triangle P O Q \sim \triangle R O S$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle P O Q)}{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle R O S)}=\left(\frac{P Q}{S R}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{225}{900}=\left(\frac{P Q}{S R}\right)^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-184.jpg?height=204&width=330&top_left_y=605&top_left_x=664)
$\Rightarrow \frac{P Q}{S R}=\frac{15}{30} \Rightarrow \frac{P Q}{S R}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow S R=2 P Q$
3. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-184.jpg?height=323&width=314&top_left_y=927&top_left_x=269)

Let $A B=B C=A C=3 x$
$\therefore B P=B Q=P Q=2 x$
( $\because A P: B P=C Q: B Q=1: 2$ )
As $\triangle B P Q$ and $\triangle B A C$ are equilateral triangle, the centroid of $\triangle B P Q$ lies on $B R$
(where $B R$ is median drawn on to $A C$ )
We know that centroid divides the median in the ratio 2:1.
$\therefore B G=\frac{2}{3} \frac{[\sqrt{3}(2 x)]}{2}=\frac{2 \sqrt{3} x}{3}$
But, $B R=\frac{\sqrt{3}(3 x)}{2}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3} x}{2}$
Now, $G R=B R-B G$
$=\frac{3 \sqrt{3} x}{2}-\frac{2 \sqrt{3} x}{3}=\frac{5 \sqrt{3} x}{6}$
Now, $B G: G R=\frac{2 \sqrt{3} x}{3}: \frac{5 \sqrt{3} x}{6}=4: 5$.
4. Let $A B C$ be a right triangle with $\angle A B C=90^{\circ}, A B=16 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $B C=8 \mathrm{~cm}$. Let $B R P Q$ be the largest square that can be inscribed in this triangle.
Let $B Q=x \mathrm{~cm}$ so that $A Q=(16-x) \mathrm{cm}$.
In $\triangle A Q P$ and $\triangle A B C$,
$\angle Q A P=\angle B A C$,
$\angle A Q P=\angle A B C$ (each $90^{\circ}$ )
Then, by AA similarity criterion
$\triangle A Q P \sim \triangle A B C$
$\Rightarrow \frac{A Q}{A B}=\frac{Q P}{B C} \Rightarrow \frac{(16-x)}{16}=\frac{x}{8}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-184.jpg?height=400&width=216&top_left_y=2145&top_left_x=776)
$\Rightarrow(16-x) \times 8=16 x \Rightarrow 128-8 x=16 x$
$\Rightarrow 128=16 x+8 x \Rightarrow 128=24 x$
$\Rightarrow x=\frac{128}{24}=\frac{16}{3}$

Hence, the side of the largest square that can be inscribed in the given triangle is $\frac{16}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.

HOTS Questions
1.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-184.jpg?height=193&width=300&top_left_y=888&top_left_x=1055)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-184.jpg?height=241&width=399&top_left_y=840&top_left_x=1379)
$\triangle A B C \sim \triangle P Q R$ (given) $\therefore \frac{A B}{P Q}=\frac{B C}{Q R}=\frac{C A}{R P}$

Let $A D$ and $P S$ be angle bisectors in $\triangle A B C$ and $\triangle P Q R$ respectively.
$\angle x=\angle y$ in $\triangle A B C$ and $\angle \beta=\angle \alpha$ in $\triangle P Q R$ (given)
Now, $\angle B=\angle Q$ and $\angle A=\angle P$
(corresponding angles of similar triangles)
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} \angle A=\frac{1}{2} \angle P \Rightarrow \angle x=\angle \beta$
Now in $\triangle A B D$ and $\triangle P Q S$,
$\angle B A D=\angle Q P S$ (from eq. i) and $\angle B=\angle Q$
$\therefore \quad \triangle A B D \sim \triangle P Q S$ (By $A A$ Similarity)
$\therefore \quad \frac{A B}{P Q}=\frac{A D}{P S}$

From equations (i) and (ii), $\frac{A B}{P Q}=\frac{B C}{Q R}=\frac{C A}{R P}=\frac{A D}{P S}$
2. In right $\triangle A B D, A D^{2}=A B^{2}+B D^{2}$
$\Rightarrow A D^{2}=A B^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2} B C\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow A D^{2}=A B^{2}+\frac{1}{4} B C^{2}$

Similarly, in right $\triangle E B C, E C^{2}=B C^{2}+\frac{1}{4} A B^{2}$

Adding (i) and (ii), we get $A D^{2}+E C^{2}=\frac{5}{4} A B^{2}+\frac{5}{4} B C^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{5}}{2}\right)^{2}+E C^{2}=\frac{5}{4}(5)^{2} \Rightarrow E C^{2}=\frac{125}{4}-\frac{45}{4}$
$\Rightarrow E C^{2}=\frac{80}{4}=20 \mathrm{~cm} \Rightarrow C E=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~cm}$
3. $\triangle A B C$ is an equilateral with side a , then
$A B=A C=B C=a$. Draw $A D \perp B C$.
In $\triangle A D B$ and $\triangle A D C$,
$A B=A C, \angle A D B=\angle A D C=90^{\circ}$
and $\angle B=\angle C=60^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad \triangle A D B \cong \triangle A D C$ …… (By $A S A$ )
$\therefore B D=D C=\frac{a}{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-185.jpg?height=300&width=270&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=781)

Now, In $\triangle A D B$,

$$
A B^{2}=A D^{2}+B D^{2}(\text { Using Pythagoras Theorem })
$$

$\Rightarrow A D=\sqrt{A B^{2}-B D^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{4}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AD}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a$
Now, area of $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2} \times B C \times A D=\frac{1}{2} \times a \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a$
$=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}$ sq. unit.

$\angle \mathrm{E}=\angle \mathrm{E}$ (common)

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{CD}}=\frac{\mathrm{BE}}{\mathrm{DE}}=\frac{\mathrm{AE}}{\mathrm{CE}}$
(i) (b) Distance $\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{S} \times \mathrm{T}=1.2 \times 4=4.8 \mathrm{~m}$ from (i)

$$
\begin{aligned}

Exercise 2
Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. (i) (a) Pair of equilateral triangles are similar figures.
(b) Pair of squares are similar figures.
(ii) (a) A triangle and a quadrilateral form a pair of non-similar figures.
(b) A square and a rhombus form a pair of nonsimilar figure.
2. The two quadrilateral in figure are not similar because their corresponding angles are not equal.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-185.jpg?height=236&width=199&top_left_y=630&top_left_x=1375)
3. $\frac{P E}{E Q}=\frac{3.9}{3}=1.3, \frac{P F}{F R}=\frac{3.6}{2.4}=\frac{3}{2}=1.5$
$\Rightarrow \frac{P E}{E Q} \neq \frac{P F}{F R} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{EF}$ is not $\|$ to QR
4. In $\triangle \mathrm{ACB}$ (See figure), $\mathrm{LM} \| \mathrm{CB}$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow \frac{A M}{M B}=\frac{A L}{L C}$
(Basic Proportionality Theorem)
In $\triangle \mathrm{ACD}$, $\mathrm{LN} \| \mathrm{CD}$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow \frac{A N}{N D}=\frac{A L}{L C}$
(Basic Proportionality Theorem)
From (i) and (ii), we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{A M}{M B}=\frac{A N}{N D} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{A M}{A M+M B}=\frac{A N}{A N+N D} \Rightarrow \frac{A M}{A B}=\frac{A N}{A D}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. In figure, $D E \| O Q$ and $D F \| O R$, then

By Basic Proportionality Theorem,
We have, $\frac{P E}{E Q}=\frac{P D}{D O}$
and $\frac{P F}{F R}=\frac{P D}{D O}$

From (i) and (ii), $\frac{P E}{E Q}=\frac{P F}{F R} \Rightarrow E F \| Q R$
(By Converse of Basic Proportionality Theorem)
6. From figure,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \angle D O C=180^{\circ}-125^{\circ}=55^{\circ} \\
& \angle D C O+\angle C D O+\angle D O C=180^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Sum of three angles of $\triangle O D C$ )
$\Rightarrow \angle D C O+70^{\circ}+55^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle D C O=180^{\circ}-125^{\circ}=55^{\circ}$
Now, we are given that $\triangle O D C \sim \triangle O B A$

$$
\angle O A B=\angle O C D=55^{\circ}(\mathrm{CPCT})
$$

i.e., $\angle O A B=55^{\circ}$
Hence, we have
$\angle D O C=55^{\circ}, \angle D C O=55^{\circ}, \angle O A B=55^{\circ}$
7. In figure, $\angle 1=\angle 2$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{PR}$
(Sides opposite to equal angles of $\triangle \mathrm{PQR}$ )
We are given that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q R}{Q S} & =\frac{Q T}{P R} \Rightarrow \frac{Q R}{Q S}=\frac{Q T}{P Q}(\because P Q=P R \text { proved }) \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q S}{Q R} & =\frac{P Q}{Q T} \text { (Taking reciprocals) }
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, in $\triangle P Q S$ and $\triangle T Q R$, we have

$$
\angle P Q S=\angle T Q R \quad(\text { Each }=\angle 1)
$$

and $\frac{Q S}{Q R}=\frac{P Q}{Q T}$

Therefore, by SAS similarity criterion, we have

$$
\Delta \mathrm{PQS} \sim \Delta \mathrm{TQR} .
$$

8. In figure, we have
$\angle \mathrm{ABC}=\angle \mathrm{AMP}$
(Each $=90^{\circ}$ )
Because the $\triangle \mathrm{s} \mathrm{ABC}$ and AMP are right angled at B and M, respectively.
Also $\quad \angle B A C=\angle P A M \quad($ Each $=\angle A)$
$\Rightarrow \triangle A B C \sim \triangle A M P$ (By $A A$ similarity criterion)
$\Rightarrow \frac{A C}{A P}=\frac{B C}{M P}$
(Ratio of the corresponding sides of similar triangles)
$\Rightarrow \frac{C A}{P A}=\frac{B C}{M P}$
9. $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle D E F$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{E F^{2}}$
(By Basic Proportionality theorem)
$\Rightarrow \frac{64}{121}=\frac{B C^{2}}{E F^{2}} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{B C}{E F}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{8}{11}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{B C}{E F}=\frac{8}{11} \Rightarrow B C=\frac{8}{11} \times E F$
$\Rightarrow B C=\frac{8}{11} \times 15.4 \mathrm{~cm}=11.2 \mathrm{~cm}$
10. Since, $D$ and $E$ are the mid points of the sides $B C$ and $C A$ respectively of $\triangle A B C$,
$\therefore \quad D E \| B A$

Similarly, $F E \| B C$ and $D F \| C A$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AFDE}$ is a parallelogram and BDEF is a parallelogram, Now in $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,
$\angle \mathrm{FDE}=\angle \mathrm{A} \quad \ldots \ldots($ opposite angles of a $\| \mathrm{gm}$ AFDE $)$
$\angle \mathrm{DEF}=\angle \mathrm{B} \quad \ldots \ldots($ opposite angles of a $\| \mathrm{gm} B D E F)$
$\therefore \quad \triangle D E F \sim \triangle A B C$ …..(By $A A$ rule of similarity)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-186.jpg?height=314&width=303&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1265)
$\therefore \frac{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle A B C)}=\frac{D E^{2}}{A B^{2}}=\frac{(1 / 2 A B)^{2}}{A B^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}$
Hence, area $(\triangle \mathrm{DEF}):$ area $(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=1: 4$
11. Given : $A B C D$ is a given square and BCE is an equilateral triangle described on $B C$, a side of the square and $A F C$ is an equilateral triangle described on the diagonal $A C$ of the square.
To prove : area $(\triangle B C E)=1 / 2$ area $(\triangle \mathrm{A} C F)$
Proof : Since, $\triangle B C E$ and $\triangle A C F$ are equilateral
$\therefore \quad \triangle B C E \sim \triangle A C F \quad\left[\because\right.$ each angle is $\left.60^{\circ}\right]$

$$
\therefore \frac{\operatorname{area}(\triangle B C E)}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle A C F)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{A C^{2}}=\frac{B C^{2}}{2 B C^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-186.jpg?height=309&width=492&top_left_y=1247&top_left_x=1169)
$[\because A B C D$ is a square with side BC ,
diagonal $A C=\sqrt{B C^{2}+B C^{2}}=\sqrt{2 B C^{2}}$ ]
Hence area $(\triangle B C E)=\frac{1}{2}$ area $(\triangle A C F)$.
Hence proved.
12. (i) Let $a=3 \mathrm{~cm}, b=8 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $c=6 \mathrm{~cm}$

Here, the larger side is $b=8 \mathrm{~cm}$
We have, $a^{2}+c^{2}=3^{2}+6^{2}$
$=9+36=45 \neq b^{2}$
So, the triangle with the given sides is not a right triangle.
(ii) Let $a=13 \mathrm{~cm}, b=12 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $c=5 \mathrm{~cm}$

Here, the larger side is $a=13 \mathrm{~cm}$
We have, $b^{2}+c^{2}=12^{2}+5^{2}=144+25=169=a^{2}$
So, the triangle with the given sides is a right triangle.
Its hypotenuse $=13 \mathrm{~cm}$.
13. In $\triangle A B C, \angle A C B=90^{\circ}$. We are given that $\triangle A B C$ is an isosceles triangle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-186.jpg?height=284&width=444&top_left_y=2270&top_left_x=1197)
$\Rightarrow \angle A=\angle B=45^{\circ} \Rightarrow A C=B C$
By Pythagoras Theorem, we have
$A B^{2}=A C^{2}+B C^{2}$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
=A C^{2}+A C^{2} & (\because B C=A C) \\
=2 A C^{2} & (\because B C)
\end{array}
$$

14. In right angle $\triangle O F A$,
$O A^{2}=O F^{2}+A F^{2}$ (Pythagoras Theorem)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-187.jpg?height=275&width=479&top_left_y=630&top_left_x=415)

$$
\Rightarrow O A^{2}-O F^{2}=A F^{2}
$$

Similarly, $O B^{2}-O D^{2}=B D^{2}$
and $O C^{2}-O E^{2}=C E^{2}$

Adding (i), (ii) and (iii), we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
& O A^{2}+O B^{2}+O C^{2}-O D^{2}-O E^{2}-O F^{2} \\
& =A F^{2}+B D^{2}+C E^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

15. [Hint: In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ADB}$,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-187.jpg?height=222&width=364&top_left_y=1197&top_left_x=470)

$$
\mathrm{AB}^{2}=\mathrm{AD}^{2}+\mathrm{BD}^{2}
$$

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}, \mathrm{AC}^{2}=\mathrm{AD}^{2}+\mathrm{DC}^{2}$
$=\mathrm{AD}^{2}+(\mathrm{BC}-\mathrm{BD})^{2}$ and simplify by using (i)]

## Exemplar Questions

1. No, corresponding sides must also be proportional.
2. No, for this, $\angle \mathrm{P}$ should be $90^{\circ}$.
3. Yes by AA criterion.

The given figure,
$\angle \mathrm{A}=\angle \mathrm{A}$ (Common)
$\angle D=\angle C$ (Given).
$\therefore \triangle \mathrm{ADE} \sim \triangle \mathrm{ACB}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-187.jpg?height=241&width=273&top_left_y=1672&top_left_x=762)
4. Consider $\triangle A B P$ and $\triangle C D P$
$\angle A=\angle C$ (given)
$\angle A P B=\angle C P D$ (Vertically opp.)
$\triangle A B P \sim \triangle C D P$ (By AA criterion)
We have,
$\frac{A B}{C D}=\frac{B P}{D P}=\frac{A P}{C P}$
$A B=6 \mathrm{~cm}, B P=15 \mathrm{~cm}, A P=12 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $C P=4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore \quad \frac{6}{C D}=\frac{12}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C D=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
also, $\frac{B P}{D P}=\frac{A P}{C P} \Rightarrow \frac{15}{D P}=\frac{12}{4} \Rightarrow D P=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.

1. (b)
2. (c) As $P Q$ is parallel to $B C \Rightarrow \triangle A B C \sim \triangle A P Q$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \text { Area of } \triangle A B C \\
& \text { Area of } \triangle A P Q \\
& \text { Ratio of sides }=\frac{A B}{A P}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1} \quad \therefore \mathrm{AP}: \mathrm{AB}=1: \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}

3. (a) Since, $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle P Q R$

$$
\therefore \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle P Q R)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}=\frac{P R^{2}}{A C^{2}}=\frac{Q R^{2}}{B C^{2}}=\frac{9}{1}\left[\because \frac{Q R}{B C}=\frac{3}{1}\right]=9
$$

4. (b) Hypotenuse $=270 \mathrm{~m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \text { Hypotenuse }^{2}=\text { Side }^{2}+\text { Side }^{2}=2 \text { Side }^{2} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \begin{array}{l}
\text { Side }^{2}=(270)^{2} / 2=72900 / 2=36450 \\
\text { or Side }^{2}=190.91 \mathrm{~m}
\end{array} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \text { Required area }=1 / 2 \times 190.91 \times 190.91 \\
& \quad=36446.6 / 2=18225 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \text { (approx) }
\end{aligned}
$$

5. (d) $\because \frac{\text { Perimeter of } \triangle A B C}{\text { Perimeter of } \triangle P Q R}=\frac{A B}{P Q}=\frac{B C}{Q R}=\frac{A C}{P R}$
6. (c) Since, $D E \| B C \quad \therefore \triangle \mathrm{ADE} \sim \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
$\therefore \frac{A D}{D B}=\frac{A E}{E C} \Rightarrow \frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1}{E C} \Rightarrow E C=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
7. (b) In $\triangle K P N$ and $\triangle K L M$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \angle K N P=\angle K M L=46^{\circ} \\
& \angle K=\angle K
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore \quad \triangle K N P \sim \triangle K M L$ (By $A$ A criterion of similarity)
$\Rightarrow \frac{K N}{K M}=\frac{N P}{M L} \Rightarrow \frac{c}{b+c}=\frac{x}{a}$
8. (a) Statement given in option (a) is false.
9. (b) Since, $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle A P Q$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A P Q)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{P Q^{2}} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{4 \cdot \operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{P Q^{2}} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{B C}{P Q}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{B C}{P Q}=\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

10. (d) Since, $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle P Q R$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{ar}(\triangle P Q R)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{Q R^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{9}{16}=\frac{(4.5)^{2}}{Q R^{2}} \\
& \Rightarrow Q R^{2}=\frac{16 \times(4.5)^{2}}{9} \Rightarrow Q R=6 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

11. (a) Let side of a square $=x \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ By Pythagoras theorem, $x^{2}+x^{2}=(16)^{2}=256$
$\Rightarrow 2 x^{2}=256 \Rightarrow x^{2}=128 \Rightarrow x=8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$.
12. (b) Given an equilateral triangle $A B C$ in which
$A B=B C=C A=2 p$
and $A D \perp B C$.
$\therefore \quad$ In $\triangle A D B$,

$$
A B^{2}=A D^{2}+B D^{2}
$$

(By Pythagoras theorem)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-188.jpg?height=223&width=168&top_left_y=552&top_left_x=822)
$\Rightarrow(2 p)^{2}=A D^{2}+p^{2} \Rightarrow A D^{2}=\sqrt{3} p$.
13. (c) Given, area of two similar triangles,
$A_{1}=81 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}, A_{2}=49 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Ratio of corresponding medians $=\sqrt{\frac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{81}{49}}=\frac{9}{7}$
14. (c) Let $\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{x} \mathrm{cm}$

Since $\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}$, therefore $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is an isoscele triangle.
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{B}=\angle \mathrm{CAB}=72^{\circ}$
Since AD bisects $\angle \mathrm{A}$
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{DAB}=36^{\circ}$ so, In $\triangle \mathrm{ADB}, \angle \mathrm{ADB}=72^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \triangle \mathrm{ADB}$ is an isoscele triangle
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AD}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
Similarly, $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$ is also an isoscele triangle.
$\therefore \mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{CD} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AD}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-188.jpg?height=435&width=510&top_left_y=1555&top_left_x=338)

Now $\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{CD}}{\mathrm{BD}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1+\mathrm{x}}{1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{(1)^{2}-4(1)(-1)}}{2}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$
$\mathrm{BD}=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}$

18. (c) Let $\triangle P Q R$

Given, $Q R^{2}+P R^{2}=5 P Q^{2}$
Median $P M$ and $Q N$ intersect at $G$.
$\Rightarrow P N=N R=\frac{1}{2} P R$ \&
$Q M=M R=\frac{1}{2} Q R$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-189.jpg?height=266&width=623&top_left_y=1841&top_left_x=342)
$Q G=\frac{2}{3} Q N, G M=\frac{1}{3} P M$
$\Rightarrow Q G^{2}+G M^{2}=\left(\frac{2}{3} Q N\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{3} P M\right)^{2}$
$=\frac{4}{9} Q N^{2}+\frac{1}{9} P M^{2}$
$=\frac{4}{9}\left(\frac{2 P Q^{2}+2 Q R^{2}-P R^{2}}{4}\right)$
$+\frac{1}{9}\left(\frac{2 P Q^{2}+2 P R^{2}-Q R^{2}}{4}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{9}\left[\begin{array}{l}8 P Q^{2}+8 Q R^{2}-4 P R^{2} \\ +2 P Q^{2}+2 P R^{2}-Q R^{2} \\ 4\end{array}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{9}\left[\frac{10 P Q^{2}+7 Q R^{2}-2 P R^{2}}{4}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{9}\left[\frac{2 Q R^{2}+7 Q R^{2}}{4}\right]=\frac{1}{4} Q R^{2}=Q M^{2}$
$Q G^{2}+G M^{2}=Q M^{2} \quad \therefore \angle Q G M=90^{\circ}$

## Passage Based Questions

1. (a) $A D$ is the median, so $D$ is the mid point of $B C$.

So, $B D=D C=\frac{1}{2} B C$

In right angled $\triangle A M C, A C^{2}=A M^{2}+M C^{2}$

In right angled $\triangle A M D, A M^{2}=A D^{2}-M D^{2}$

Putting $A M^{2}$ from (iii) in (ii), we get,
$A C^{2}=A D^{2}-M D^{2}+M C^{2}$
$=A D^{2}-M D^{2}+(M D+D C)^{2}$
$=A D^{2}+2 D M \cdot \frac{B C}{2}+\left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2}$
So, $A C^{2}=A D^{2}+B C . D M+\left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2}$
2. (b) In right angled $\triangle A B M, A B^{2}=A M^{2}+B M^{2}$

From $\triangle A M D, A M^{2}=A D^{2}-M D^{2}$
So, $A B^{2}=A D^{2}-M D^{2}+B M^{2}$
$=A D^{2}-M D^{2}+(B D-M D)^{2}$
$=A D^{2}-M D^{2}+B D^{2}-2 B D \cdot M D+M D^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A B^{2}=A D^{2}-B C \cdot D M+\left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2}$ Proved.
3. (c) From the above two solutions

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A C^{2}=A D^{2}+B C \cdot D M+\left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2} \\
& \text { and } A B^{2}=A D^{2}-B C \cdot D M+\left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding results of (i) \& (ii), we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
A C^{2}+A B^{2}=A D^{2}+B C \cdot D M+ & \left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2} \\
& +A D^{2}-B C \cdot D M+\left(\frac{B C}{2}\right)^{2} \\
\Rightarrow A C^{2}+A B^{2}=2 A D^{2}+ & \frac{1}{2}(B C)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Assertion \& Reason

1. (a) Reason is true. [This is Thale's Theorem]

## For Assertion

Since $D E \| B C \therefore$ by Thale's Theorem
$\frac{A D}{D B}=\frac{A E}{E C} \Rightarrow \frac{D B}{A D}=\frac{E C}{A E}$
$\Rightarrow 1+\frac{D B}{A D}=1+\frac{E C}{A E}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-190.jpg?height=252&width=286&top_left_y=2284&top_left_x=708)
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{A D+D B}{A D}=\frac{A E+E C}{A E}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{A B}{A D}=\frac{A C}{A E} \quad \therefore$ Assertion is true.

Since reason gives Assertion.
2. (d) In right angled $\triangle A B C$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
A B^{2} & =A C^{2}+B C^{2}(\text { By Pythagorus Theorem }) \\
& =A C^{2}+A C^{2}[\because B C=A C] \\
& =2 A C^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore A B^{2}=2 A C^{2}$
∴ Assertion is false.
Again since
$A B^{2}=2 A C^{2}=A C^{2}+A C^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-190.jpg?height=204&width=254&top_left_y=758&top_left_x=1560)
$=A C^{2}+B C^{2}(\because A C=B C$ given $)$
$\therefore \angle C=90^{\circ}$
(By converse of Pythagoras Theorem)
∴ Reason is true.
3. (b) Reason is true
[standard result]
For Assertion, since $\triangle A B C \sim \triangle D E F$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\operatorname{area}(\triangle A B C)}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{B C^{2}}{E F^{2}}=\frac{(4)^{2}}{(5)^{2}}=\frac{16}{25}$
( ∵ ratio of areas of two similar $\Delta s$ is equal to the ratio of the squares of corresponding sides)
$\therefore \quad \frac{64}{\operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)}=\frac{16}{25} \Rightarrow \operatorname{area}(\triangle D E F)=\frac{64 \times 25}{16}$
$=4 \times 25=100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
∴ Assertion is true. But reason is not the correct explanation for assertion.

1. Ans : 7

$\Rightarrow 2 x^{2}-14 x=0 \Rightarrow x=7$

4. Ans: 1

In $\triangle A B C$, we have $D E \| B C$
$\therefore \quad \frac{A D}{D B}=\frac{A E}{E C}$ (By basic proportionality theorem)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-191.jpg?height=410&width=458&top_left_y=561&top_left_x=1249)
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{4 x-3}{3 x-1}=\frac{8 x-7}{5 x-3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 20 x^{2}-15 x-12 x+9=24 x^{2}-21 x-8 x+7$
$\Rightarrow \quad 20 x^{2}-27 x+9=24 x^{2}-29 x+7$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 x^{2}-2 x-2=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x^{2}-x-1=0 \Rightarrow(2 x+1)(x-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=1$ or $x=-1 / 2$
So, the required value of $x$ is 1 .
[ $x=-1 / 2$ is neglected as length cannot be negative]
5. Ans : 2

We have
$\frac{A O}{O C}=\frac{B O}{O D} \Rightarrow \frac{3 x-1}{5 x-3}=\frac{2 x+1}{6 x-5}$
$\therefore(3 x-1)(6 x-5)=(2 x+1)(5 x-3)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 18 x^{2}-21 x+5=10 x^{2}-x-3$
$\Rightarrow \quad 8 x^{2}-20 x+8=0 \Rightarrow(x-2)(2 x-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=2$ or $x=\frac{1}{2}$
When $x=\frac{1}{2}, O C$ becomes negative. Hence, $x=2$.

## Area of a Triangle

The area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ formed by the vertices $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and
$\frac{1}{2}\left|x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right|$
(i) Area of triangle $=\frac{1}{2} \times$ Base × Altitude
(ii) Area of polygon can be calculated by dividing it into the triangular
(iii) If area of triangle is zero, then the points are collinear.

## Mid-point Formula

The coordinates of the mid point P of the line segment joining the points

$$
\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}\right)
$$

The distance between any two points origin is $\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}} m_{1}: m_{2}$ are, $m_{1}: m_{2}$ are,

## Distance Formula

$P\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $Q\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$

$$
P Q=\sqrt{\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)^{2}}
$$

## Section Formula

(i) The coordinates of the point $P(x, y)$ which divides the line segment joining the points $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ internally in the ratio

$$
\left(\frac{m_{1} x_{2}+m_{2} x_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}, \frac{m_{1} y_{2}+m_{2} y_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right)
$$

(ii) The coordinates of the point $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ which divides the line segment joining the points $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ externally in the ratio,

$$
\left(\frac{m_{1} x_{2}-m_{2} x_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}, \frac{m_{1} y_{2}-m_{2} y_{1}}{m_{1}-m_{2}}\right)
$$

(iii) If the ratio in which $P$ divides $A B$ is $\mathrm{K}: 1$, then the coordinates of the point P will be,

$$
\left(\frac{K x_{2}+x_{1}}{K+1}, \frac{K y_{2}+y_{1}}{K+1}\right)
$$

## COORDINATE OF A POINT IN XY-PLANE

The perpendicular distance of any point $P$ from $Y$-axis is called $X$-coordinate (or abscissa) of the point $P$ and the perpendicular distance of the point $P$ from $X$-axis is called $Y$-coordinate (or ordinate) of the point $P$. In the figure, $P Q$ and $P R$ are the perpendicular distances of the point $P$ from $Y$ and $X$-axis.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-193.jpg?height=426&width=437&top_left_y=559&top_left_x=845)

If $P Q=x$ units $(=O R), P R=y$ units $(=O Q)$ the position of point $P$ is represented by $P(x, y)$. Here the ordered pair $(x, y)$ is called the coordinates of point $P$. Hence, $P(x, y)$ is a point in the $X Y$-plane which is $x$ unit distant from $Y$-axis and y unit distant from $X$-axis.

In the ordered pair $(x, y)$, the first entry ' $x$ ' is the $X$-coordinate and the second entry ' $y$ ' is the $Y$-coordinate of the point. $(x, y)$ is called an ordered pair because there is a pair of numbers $x$ and $y$, in which the first entry $x$ is always $X$-coordinate and the second entry $y$ is always $Y$-coordinate of the point.

## SIGN CONVENTIONS IN THE XY-PLANE

(a) All the distances are measured from origin (O).
(b) All the distances measured along or parallel to X -axis and right side of Y -axis are taken as $+v e$.
(c) All the distances measured along or parallel to X -axis and left side of Y -axis are taken as -ve.
(d) All the distances measured along or parallel to Y -axis and above the X -axis are taken as $+v e$.
(e) All the distances measured along or parallel to Y -axis and below the X -axis are taken as -ve.

## According to the Above Sign Conventions

(i) Coordinate of origin is $(0,0)$
(ii) Coordinate of any point on the $X$-axis and right side of origin is of the form $(x, 0)$, where $x>0$.
(iii) Coordinate of any point on the $X$-axis and left side of origin is of the form ( $-x, 0$ ), where $x>0$.
(iv) Coordinate of any point on the $Y$-axis and above the origin is of the form $(0, y)$, where $y>0$.
(v) Coordinate of any point on the $Y$-axis and below the origin is of the form $(0,-y)$, where $y>0$.
(vi) $X$ and $Y$-axis divide the $x o y$ plane in four parts. Each part is called a quadrant.

The four quadrants are written as I-quadrant (xoy), II-quadrant (yox '), III- quadrant ( $x^{\prime} o y$ ') and (IV) quadrant ( $y^{\prime} o x$ ). Each of these quadrants shows the specific quadrant of the xoy plane as shown below :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-193.jpg?height=506&width=521&top_left_y=2037&top_left_x=804)

Note: (a) Any of the four quadrants does not include any part of $X$ or $Y$-axis.
(b) In first quadrant both $X$ and $Y$-coordinates of any point are $+v e$.
(c) In second quadrant, $X$-coordinate is $-v e$ and $Y$-coordinate is $+v e$.
(d) In third quadrant, both $X$ and $Y$-coordinates of any point are-ve.
(e) In fourth quadrant, $X$-coordinate is $+v e$ and $Y$-coordinate is -ve as shown in the diagram.

## PLOTTING OF A POINT WHOSE COORDINATES ARE KNOWN

The point can be plotted by measuring its proper distances from both the axes. Thus, any point $(h, k)$ can be plotted as follows :
(i) Measure $O M$ equal to h along the $X$-axis.
(ii) Now measure $M P$ perpendicular to $O M$ equal to $k$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-194.jpg?height=378&width=399&top_left_y=758&top_left_x=1418)

The method of describing the location of points in a plane was proposed by the great French mathematician Rene' Descartes in 17th century. Rene' Descartes' Latinized name is Renatius Cartesius and from here the terminology "Cartisian plane" and "Cartisian coordinate system" are derived.

## DISTANCE BETWEEN TWO POINTS

Distance between any two points $P\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $Q\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ in the plane is the length of the line segment $P Q$.

From $P$ and $Q$ draw perpendiculars $P L$ and $Q M$ respectively on the $X$-axis and $P R$ perpendicular on $Q M$.

Then, $O L=x_{1}, O M=x_{2}, P L=y_{1}$ and $Q M=y_{2}$
$\therefore P R=L M=O M-O L=x_{2}-x_{1}$
$Q R=Q M-R M=Q M-P L=y_{2}-y_{1}$
Since $P R Q$ is a right angled triangle,
$\therefore \quad P Q^{2}=P R^{2}+Q R^{2}$

$$
=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}
$$

(By the Pythagoras Theorem)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-194.jpg?height=442&width=499&top_left_y=1466&top_left_x=1315)
$\therefore P Q=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}$
i.e., Distance between any two points $=\sqrt{(\text { difference of abscissae })^{2}+(\text { difference of ordinates })^{2}}$
Corollary : The distance of the point $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ from the origin $(0,0)$ is $\sqrt{\left(x_{1}-0\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-0\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}}$

Coordinate geometry is used to manage and regulate the location of air transport. Coordinate system is used in the Global Positioning System (GPS) which provides location and time information in all weather conditions. In a GPS, the longitude and the latitude of a place are its coordinates. The distance formula is used to find the distance between two places in a GPS.

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

Find the distance between each of the following pair of points :
(a) $P(6,8)$ and $Q(-9,-12)$
(b) $A(-6,-1)$ and $B(-6,11)$

SOLUTION :
(a) Here, the points are $P(6,8)$ and $Q(-9,-12)$.

By using distance formula, we have

$$
P Q=\sqrt{(-9-6)^{2}+(-12-8)^{2}}=\sqrt{15^{2}+20^{2}}=\sqrt{225+400}=\sqrt{625}=25
$$

Hence, $P Q=25$ units.
(b) Here, the points are $A(-6,-1)$ and $B(-6,11)$

By using distance formula, we have

$$
A B=\sqrt{(-6-(-6))^{2}+(11-(-1))^{2}}=\sqrt{0^{2}+12^{2}}=12
$$

Hence, $A B=12$ units.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-195.jpg?height=82&width=379&top_left_y=964&top_left_x=865&polygon=256,0,377,18,379,20,379,79,352,82,21,82,5,79,0,77,0,22,3,20,7,18,162,0)

Slope ( $m$ ) of the line $l$ passing through the points $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)=m=\tan \theta=\frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-195.jpg?height=657&width=698&top_left_y=1169&top_left_x=721)

## APPLICATIONS OF DISTANCE FORMULA

(i) For given three points $A, B, C$ to decide whether they are collinear or vertices of a particular triangle. First we find the length of $A B, B C$ and $C A$ then we shall find that the point are
(a) Collinear, if the sum of two shorter distances is equal to the longest distance
(b) Vertices of an equilateral triangle if $A B=B C=C A$
(c) Vertices of an isosceles triangle if $A B=B C$ or $B C=C A$ or $C A=A B$
(d) Vertices of a right angled triangle if $A B^{2}+B C^{2}=C A^{2}$ or $B C^{2}+C A^{2}=A B^{2}$ or $C A^{2}+A B^{2}=B C^{2}$
(ii) For given four points $A, B, C$ and $D$;
(a) $A B=B C=C D=D A ; A C=B D \Rightarrow A B C D$ is a square.
(b) $A B=B C=C D=D A ; \Rightarrow A B C D$ is a rhombus.
(c) $A B=C D, B C=D A ; A C=B D \Rightarrow A B C D$ is a rectangle.
(d) $A B=C D, B C=D A ; \Rightarrow A B C D$ is a parallelogram.
(iii) (a) Diagonal of square, rhombus, rectangle and parallelogram always bisect each other
(b) Diagonal of rhombus and square bisect each other at right angle.
(c) Three given points are collinear if area of the triangle formed from these three points is zero.
(d) Four given points are collinear, if area of quadrilateral formed from these four points is zero.

## SECTION FORMULA

To find the co-ordinates of a point, which divides a line segment joining two points internally in a given ratio :
Let $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ be the two given points and $P$ be a point on $A B$, which divides it internally in the given ratio $\mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}$ i.e., $A P: P B=\mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}$.
We have to find the co-ordinates of P . Let $\mathrm{P}=(x, y)$.
Draw the perpendiculars $A L, P M, B N$ from $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{P}$ and B respectively on $x$-axis and $A K, P T$ from A and P on $P M$ and $B N$ respectively. Then, from similar triangles AKP and PTB, we have, $\frac{A P}{P B}=\frac{A K}{P T}=\frac{P K}{B T}$
Now, $\quad A K=L M=O M-O L=x-x_{1}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P T=M N=O N-O M=x_{2}-x \\
& P K=M P-M K=M P-L A=y-y_{1} \\
& B T=N B-N T=N B-M P=y_{2}-y
\end{aligned}
$$

From (i), we have, $\frac{m}{n}=\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{2}-x}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{2}-y}$
Now, $\frac{m}{n}=\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{2}-x}$
$\Rightarrow m x_{2}-m x=n x-n x_{1}$
$\Rightarrow x(m+n)=m x_{2}+n x_{1}$
$\therefore \quad x=\frac{m x_{2}+n x_{1}}{m+n}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-196.jpg?height=481&width=524&top_left_y=705&top_left_x=1295)

Now, $\frac{m}{n}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{2}-y} \quad \Rightarrow y=\frac{m y_{2}+n y_{1}}{m+n}$
Hence, $\quad x=\frac{m x_{2}+n x_{1}}{m+n}$ and $y=\frac{m y_{2}+n y_{1}}{m+n}$
Therefore, co-ordinates of a point which divides the line segment joining the points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ in the ratio $m: n$ internally is $\left(\frac{m x_{2}+n x_{1}}{m+n}, \frac{m y_{2}+n y_{1}}{m+n}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-196.jpg?height=100&width=387&top_left_y=1516&top_left_x=808)

The coordinates of a point $C$ which divides line segment joining two points $A$ and $B$ externally in the ratio $m: n$ are $\left(\frac{m x_{2}-n x_{1}}{m-n} \cdot \frac{m y_{2}-n y_{1}}{m-n}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-196.jpg?height=68&width=359&top_left_y=1781&top_left_x=836)

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

Find the coordinates of the point which divides the line segment joining the points $(6,3)$ and $(-4,5)$ in the ratio 3: 2 internally.

## SOLUTION :

Let $P(x, y)$ be the required point. Then,
$x=\frac{3 \times(-4)+2 \times 6}{3+2}$ and $y=\frac{3 \times 5+2 \times 3}{3+2}$
$\Rightarrow x=0$ and $y=\frac{21}{5}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-196.jpg?height=104&width=643&top_left_y=2229&top_left_x=685)

So, the coordinates of P are ( $0,21 / 5$ )

## MID-POINT FORMULA

The co-ordinates of the mid-point of the line segment joining two points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ can be obtained by taking $m=n$ in the section formula given above. Putting $m=n$ in the section formula, we have

$$
x=\frac{n x_{2}+n x_{1}}{n+n}=\frac{x_{2}+x_{1}}{2} \text { and } y=\frac{n y_{2}+n y_{1}}{n+n}=\frac{y_{2}+y_{1}}{2}
$$

Hence, co-ordinates of the mid-point joining two points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ is $\left(\frac{x_{2}+x_{1}}{2}, \frac{y_{2}+y_{1}}{2}\right)$

Prove that the points $\mathbf{A}(-\mathbf{5}, 4), \mathbf{B}(-\mathbf{1},-\mathbf{2})$ and $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{5}, 2)$ are the vertices of an isosceles right-angled triangle. Find the coordinates of $D$ so that $A B C D$ is a square.

## SOLUTION :

Consider the given points $\mathrm{A}(-5,4), \mathrm{B}(-1,-2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(5,2)$.
Now, by distance formula, we have

$$
A B=\sqrt{(-1+5)^{2}+(-2-4)^{2}}=\sqrt{16+36}=\sqrt{52}
$$

and $B C=\sqrt{(5+1)^{2}+(2+2)^{2}}=\sqrt{36+16}=\sqrt{52}$
So, $A B=B C$

$$
A C=\sqrt{(5+5)^{2}+(2-4)^{2}}=\sqrt{100+4}=\sqrt{104}
$$

Now, $A B^{2}+B C^{2}=(\sqrt{52})^{2}+(\sqrt{52})^{2}=52+52=104=A C^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \angle A B C=90^{\circ}$
Hence, it is proved that $A(-5,4), B(-1,-2)$ and $C(5,2)$ are the vertices of an isosceles right-angled triangle.
Let $P$ be the midpoint of diagonal $A C$ of the square $A B C D$.
Then the coordinates of the mid point P of diagonal AC is
$=\left(\frac{-5+5}{2}, \frac{4+2}{2}\right)=(0,3)$
Also the diagonals of a square bisect each other. So, $P(0,3)$ is also the mid point of diagonal $B D$. Let the coordinates of the point D be $(h, k)$.
Then we have
$\frac{h-1}{2}=0 \Rightarrow h=1$ and $\frac{k-2}{2}=3 \Rightarrow k=6+2=8$
Hence, the coordinates of D are $(h, k)=(1,8)$.

## POINTS OF TRISECTION

Trisection means a line is dividing into three equal parts.
This can be done by finding two points $C$ and $D$ on the line segment $A B$, such that $A C=C D=D B$
Let us assume $A C=C D=D B=p$
Then $A C=p$ and $C B=C D+D B=p+p=2 p$

$$
\therefore \quad A C: C B=p: 2 p=1: 2
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-197.jpg?height=67&width=579&top_left_y=2161&top_left_x=1292)

Also, $A D=A C+C D=p+p=2 p$ and $D B=p$
$\therefore \quad A D: D B=2 p: p=2: 1$
Hence, we can conclude that, to find points of trisection, we find two points $C$ and $D$ which divides AB in the ratio $1: 2$ and $2: 1$, respectively.

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 3

Find the coordinates of points which trisect the line segment joining $(1,-2)$ and $(-3,4)$.

## SOLUTION :

Let $\mathrm{A}(1,-2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-3,4)$ be the given points. Let the points of trisection be P and Q . Then, $\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{QB}=\lambda$ (say).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-198.jpg?height=117&width=630&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=689)
$\therefore \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PQ}+\mathrm{QB}=2 \lambda$ and $\mathrm{AQ}=\mathrm{AP}+\mathrm{PQ}=2 \lambda$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AP}: \mathrm{PB}=\lambda: 2 \lambda=1: 2$ and $\mathrm{AQ}: \mathrm{QB}=2 \lambda: \lambda=2: 1$
So, P divides AB internally in the ratio $1: 2$ while Q divides internally in the ratio $2: 1$. Thus, the coordinates of P and Q are

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P\left(\frac{1 \times(-3)+2 \times 1}{1+2}, \frac{1 \times 4+2 \times(-2)}{1+2}\right)=P\left(\frac{-1}{3}, 0\right) \\
& Q\left(\frac{2 \times(-3)+1 \times 1}{2+1}, \frac{2 \times 4+1 \times(-2)}{2+1}\right)=Q\left(\frac{-5}{3}, 2\right) \text { respectively }
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the two points of trisection are ( $-1 / 3,0$ ) and ( $-5 / 3,2$ )
Time to
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-198.jpg?height=97&width=106&top_left_y=1014&top_left_x=390)

Check Your
Knowledge
Prove analytically that if in a triangle, two medians are equal in length, then it is an isosceles triangle.

## SOLUTION :

Let $A B C$ be a triangle in which two medians $B E$ and $C F$ are equal in length, where $E$ and $F$ are the mid-points of sides $A C$ and $A B$ respectively. Let $D$ be the mid-point of the side $B C$ and length of $B C$ be $2 a$ where $a>0$. Take $D$ as origin and axes as shown in the figure then

$$
D \equiv(0,0), B \equiv(-a, 0) \text { and } C \equiv(a, 0)
$$

Let $A$ be $(h, k)$, then by mid-point formula,

$$
E \equiv\left(\frac{a+h}{2}, \frac{k+0}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{a+h}{2}, \frac{k}{2}\right)
$$

and $F \equiv\left(\frac{-a+h}{2}, \frac{k+0}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{h-a}{2}, \frac{k}{2}\right)$.
Since, $B E=C F$, therefore, $B E^{2}=C F^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-198.jpg?height=377&width=538&top_left_y=1398&top_left_x=1279)
$\Rightarrow\left\{\frac{a+h}{2}-(-a)\right\}^{2}+\left(\frac{k}{2}-0\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{h-a}{2}-a\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{k}{2}-0\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{a+h+2 a}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{h-a-2 a}{2}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{(3 a+h)^{2}}{4}=\frac{(h-3 a)^{2}}{4}$
$\Rightarrow 9 a^{2}+h^{2}+6 a h=h^{2}+9 a^{2}-6 a h$
$\Rightarrow 6 a h+6 a h=0 \Rightarrow 12 a h=0 \Rightarrow h=0(\because \mathrm{a} \neq 0)$
Hence, the point $A$ is $(0, k)$

$$
\Rightarrow A B=\sqrt{(-a-0)^{2}+(0-k)^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+k^{2}}
$$

$$
\text { and } A C=\sqrt{(a-0)^{2}+(0-k)^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}+k^{2}}
$$

From (i) and (ii), we find that $A B=A C$
$\Rightarrow \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is an isosceles triangle.
Hence, it is proved that, if in a triangle two medians are equal, then it is an isosceles triangle.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## 1. Centroid of a Triangle

The centroid G is the point of intersection of medians $\mathrm{AD}, \mathrm{BE}$ and CF , which are the lines joining a vertex with the mid-point of opposite side.
The coordinates of the centroid G are $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$.
where $\mathrm{A}\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), \mathrm{B}\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\mathrm{C}\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-199.jpg?height=214&width=328&top_left_y=511&top_left_x=1548)

## 2. Incentre of the Triangle

The incentre I is the centre of circle touching the sides of the triangle internally, called INCIRCLE and it is the point of intersection of the internal bisectors. AP, BQ, CR of the vertex angles.
The coordinates of incentre I are given by
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-199.jpg?height=218&width=321&top_left_y=744&top_left_x=1555)
$\left(\frac{a x_{1}+b x_{2}+c x_{3}}{a+b+c}, \frac{a y_{1}+b y_{2}+c y_{3}}{a+b+c}\right)$,
where $a=\mathrm{BC}, b=\mathrm{CA}$ and $c=\mathrm{AB}$ are the sides of the triangle.
3. Circumcentre of the Triangle

The circumcentre $\mathrm{O}^{\prime}$ is the centre of the circle passing through the vertices of triangle and it is the point of intersection of perpendicular bisectors of the sides of triangle.
Thus, we have $\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}$
Circumcentre of a right angled triangle is the mid points of its hypotenuse.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-199.jpg?height=293&width=321&top_left_y=982&top_left_x=1557)
4. Orthocentre: It is the point of intersection of perpendicular drawn from vertices on opposite sides of a triangle.

Remarks: (i) If the triangle is equilateral, the centroid, incentre, orthocentre, circumcentre coincides.
(ii) Orthocentre, centroid and cirumcentre are always collinear.
(iii) In an isosceles triangle, centroid, orthocentre, incentre, circumcentre lies on the same line.

## AREA OF A TRIANGLE

Let $A B C$ be any triangle whose vertices are $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $C\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$. Draw $B L, A M$ and $C N$ perpendiculars from $B, A$ and $C$ respectively on the $X$-axis. $A B L M, A M N C$ and $B L N C$ are all trapeziums.
Area of $\triangle A B C=$ Area of trapezium $A B L M+$ Area of trapezium $A M N C-$ Area of trapezium $B L N C$
We know that, Area of trapezium

$$
=\frac{1}{2} \times(\text { Sum of parallel sides }) \times(\text { Distance between the paralleled sides })
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-199.jpg?height=410&width=499&top_left_y=1827&top_left_x=813)

Therefore, area of $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2}(B L+A M)(L M)+\frac{1}{2}(A M+C N)(M N)-\frac{1}{2}(B L+C N)(L N)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{2}\left(y_{2}+y_{1}\right)\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(y_{1}+y_{3}\right)\left(x_{3}-x_{1}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(y_{2}+y_{3}\right)\left(x_{3}-x_{2}\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since area of a triangle is always positive, therefore we reject the negative sign if appear in the value of area of the triangle.

Note: (i) Area of triangle $=\frac{1}{2} \times$ Base × Altitude
(ii) Area of polygon can be calculated by dividing it into the triangular region.
(iii) If area of triangle formed by joining three points is zero, then points are collinear.

## ILLUSTRATION : 4

Find the area of the triangle formed by the points $\mathbf{A}(5,2), \mathbf{B}(4,7)$ and $\mathbf{C}(7,-4)$.

## SOLUTION :

Here, $x_{1}=5, y_{1}=2, x_{2}=4, y_{2}=7, x_{3}=7$ and $y_{3}=-4$
$\therefore \quad$ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}\left|x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right|$
⇒ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}|5(7+4)+4(-4-2)+7(2-7)|$
⇒ Area of $\left.\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2} \right\rvert\,(5 \times 11+4 \times(-6)+7 \times(-5) \mid$
⇒ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}|(55-24-35)|=\frac{1}{2}|-4|=2$ sq. units

## AREA OF A QUADRILATERAL

Let the vertices of quadrilateral $A B C D$ be $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right), C\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ and $D\left(x_{4}, \mathrm{y}_{4}\right)$
So, area of quadrilateral $A B C D=$ Area of $\triangle A B C+$ Area of $\triangle A C D$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-200.jpg?height=301&width=525&top_left_y=1260&top_left_x=1292)

## Learn More

Area of a quadrilateral with vertices $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right),\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ and $\left(x_{4}, y_{4}\right)$ is given by $\frac{1}{2}\left|\left(x_{1}-x_{3}\right)\left(y_{2}-y_{4}\right)-\left(x_{2}-x_{4}\right)\left(y_{1}-y_{3}\right)\right|$

## CONDITION OF COLLINEARITY OF THREE GIVEN POINTS

Three given points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ will be collinear if $x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)=0$

## ILLUSTRATION : 5

Prove that the points $(2,-2),(-3,8)$ and $(-1,4)$ are collinear.

## SOLUTION :

Area of the triangle

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{2}|2(8-4)+(-3)(4+2)+(-1)(-2-8)| \\
& =\frac{1}{2}|2 \times 4-3 \times 6+10| \\
& =\frac{1}{2}|8-18+10|=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, given three points are collinear.

Coordinates plane is a two dimensional plane formed by the intersection of a horizontal line, called $x$-axis and a vertical line, called $y$-axis.

Axis is the reference line from which distance of any point in the plane passing through $x$ and $y$-axis is measured.

There are many applications of coordinate geometry.
(i) Distance between two point $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{2}\right)^{2}}$
(ii) Mid-pint of two points $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)=\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}\right)$

CASE-I : To find distance between two places on earth by using latitude \& longitude

Latitude : It is a coordinate that specifies the north-south position of a point on Earth's surface.

Longitude : It is coordinate that specifies the east-west position of a point on the Earth's surface.

Illustration: The coordinates of Kolkata is $22.6^{\circ} \mathrm{N}$ and $88.4^{\circ} \mathrm{E}$ and the coordinates of Bangalore is $13^{\circ} N$ and $77.5^{\circ} E$. Find the air distance between Bangalore and Kolkata.
(Consider the length of the latitude between two longitudes of $1^{\circ}$ apart is 110 km and length of longitude between two latitudes of $1^{\circ}$ apart is 104 km .)

Sol.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-201.jpg?height=583&width=557&top_left_y=1707&top_left_x=1136)

Now $22.6^{\circ} \mathrm{N}-13^{\circ} \mathrm{N}=9.6^{\circ} \mathrm{N}$ and $88.4^{\circ} \mathrm{E}-77.5^{\circ} \mathrm{E}=10.9^{\circ} \mathrm{E}$
where, $9.6^{\circ} \mathrm{N}=9.6 \times 104 \mathrm{~km}=998.4 \mathrm{~km}$
and $10.9^{\circ} \mathrm{E}=10.9 \times 110 \mathrm{~km}=1199 \mathrm{~km}$
$\therefore \quad$ Distance between Banglore and Kolkata $=\sqrt{(998.4)^{2}+(1199)^{2}}=1560 \mathrm{~km}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-201.jpg?height=195&width=201&top_left_y=1286&top_left_x=742)

CASE-II : Proof of geometrical theorem by used the formula of coordinate geometry.

Illustration: The line segment joining the middle points of two sides of a triangle is equal to half of its third side.

Sol.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-201.jpg?height=658&width=256&top_left_y=578&top_left_x=594)

Consider a $\triangle O A B$ whose vertex $O$ is the origin and side $O A$ is along + ve direction of $x$-axis. Suppose coordinate of $A$ be ( $a, 0$ ) and coordinate of $B$ be ( $a_{1}, a_{2}$ )

Also suppose $C$ and $D$ are mid-points of $O B$ and $A B$ respectively.

Using mid-point formula
Coordinate of C is $\left(\frac{a_{1}}{2}, \frac{a_{2}}{2}\right)$ and D is $\left(\frac{a+a_{1}}{2}, \frac{a_{2}}{2}\right)$

Using distance formula
$C D=-\mathrm{cm}$ and $O A=a \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore 2 C D=O A$

## Think Out of the Box

Q.1. Find the distance of the point ( $-3,4$ ) from the $x$-axis.
[Hint : Distance from $x$-axis is ordinate of point, Ans. 4]
