the segment joinning the points $(1,3)$ and $(2,7)$.
[Hint : Put the coordinate of intersection point in given equation, Ans. 3 : 4]

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. Find the distance between the points $A(-6,-1)$ and $B(-6,11)$

Sol. Here the points are $A(-6,-1)$ and $B(-6,11)$. By using distance formula, we have
$A B=\sqrt{(-6-(-6))^{2}+(11-(-1))^{2}}=\sqrt{0^{2}+12^{2}}=12$
$\therefore A B=12$ units.

Hence, distance between A and B is 12 units.
2. The distance between two points $(0,0)$ and $(x, 3)$ is 5 . Find $x$.

Sol. By using distance formula, we have the distance between $(0,0)$ and $(x, 3)$ is $\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(3-0)^{2}}=5$
It is given that $\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(3-0)^{2}}=5$ or $\sqrt{x^{2}+3^{2}}=5$
Squaring both sides, $x^{2}+9=25$ or $x^{2}=16$ or $x= \pm 4$
Hence, $\quad x=+4$ or $x=-4$
3. Find the coordinates of a point on $\boldsymbol{Y}$-axis which is equidistant from the points $(\mathbf{1 3 , 2})$ and $(\mathbf{1 2 , - 3})$.

Sol. Let $P(0, y)$ be the required point and the given points be $A(12,-3)$ and $B(13,2)$.
Then $P A=P B$ (given)
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(12-0)^{2}+(-3-y)^{2}}=\sqrt{(13-0)^{2}+(2-y)^{2}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{144+(y+3)^{2}}=\sqrt{169+(2-y)^{2}}$
Taking square on both sides, we get
$144+9+y^{2}+6 y=169+4+y^{2}-4 y \Rightarrow 10 y=20 \Rightarrow y=2$
∴ The required point on $Y$-axis is $(0,2)$.
4. Find the ratio in which the join of $(-4,3)$ and $(5,-2)$ is divided by (i) $\mathbf{X}$-axis (ii) $\mathbf{Y}$-axis.

Sol. (i) X -axis divides the join of $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ in the ratio of $-y_{1}: y_{2}=-3:-2=3: 2$.
(ii) Y -axis divides, in the ratio of $-x_{1}: x_{2}=4: 5$.
5. Find the co-ordinates of a point which divides the line segment joining the points $(-1,4)$ and $(0,-3)$ in the ratio 1:4 internally.
Sol. Let $A(-1,4)$ and $B(0,-3)$ be the given points and let $P(x, y)$ divide $A B$ in the ratio $1: 4$ internally.
Using section formula, we have

$$
x=\frac{1 \times 0+4 \times(-1)}{1+4}=-\frac{4}{5} \text { and } y=\frac{1 \times(-3)+4 \times 4}{1+4}=\frac{13}{5}
$$

$\therefore P\left(-\frac{4}{5}, \frac{13}{5}\right)$ divides AB in the ratio $1: 4$ internally.
6. $A B C$ is a triangle in which $P, Q, R$ are the mid-points of $B C, C A, A B$ respectively. The coordinates are $A(-3,2), Q(1,-2)$ and $P(2,2)$. Find $\overline{R Q}$.
Sol. Let the coordinates of $C$ be $(x, y)$.
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{-3+x}{2}, \frac{2+y}{2}\right)=(1,-2)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{-3+x}{2}=1, \frac{2+y}{2}=-2 \Rightarrow x=5, y=-6$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-202.jpg?height=294&width=311&top_left_y=1950&top_left_x=1503)

Let the coordinates of $B$ be $(a, b)$.
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{a+5}{2}, \frac{b-6}{2}\right)=(2,2) \Rightarrow a=-1, b=10$
Coordinates of $R=\left(\frac{-1-3}{2}, \frac{10+2}{2}\right)=(-2,6)$ and $\overline{R Q}=\sqrt{(1+2)^{2}+(-2-6)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+64}=\sqrt{73}$
7. The coordinates of $A, B$ and $C$ are $(-1,5),(3,1)$ and $(5,7)$ respectively. $D, E$ and $F$ are the mid-points of $B C, C A$ and AB respectively. Calculate the area of the triangle DEF .
Sol. Mid-point $\mathrm{D}\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)=\left(\frac{3+5}{2}, \frac{1+7}{2}\right)=(4,4)$
Mid-point E $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)=\left(\frac{-1+5}{2}, \frac{5+7}{2}\right)=(2,6)$
Mid-point $\mathrm{F}\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)=\left(\frac{-1+3}{2}, \frac{5+1}{2}\right)=(1,3)$
Now, using the formula, area of triangle $=\frac{1}{2}\left|x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right|$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-203.jpg?height=408&width=412&top_left_y=440&top_left_x=1464)

⇒ Area of $\triangle D E F=\frac{1}{2}|4(6-3)+2(3-4)+1(4-6)|=4$ square units.
Hence, the area of $\triangle D E F$ is 4 square units.
8. What kind of triangle is formed by $A(1,2), B(4,3)$ and $C(5,6)$ ?

Sol. $A B^{2}=(4-1)^{2}+(3-2)^{2}=9+1=10$
$B C^{2}=(5-4)^{2}+(6-3)^{2}=1+9=10$
$C A^{2}=(5-1)^{2}+(6-2)^{2}=16+16=32$
$A B^{2}=\mathrm{BC}^{2} \Rightarrow$ it is isosceles.
$C A^{2}>A B^{2}+B C^{2} \Rightarrow 32>10+10 \Rightarrow \angle B$ is obtuse
Hence, $A B C$ is an obtuse isosceles $\triangle$.
9. The area of a triangle is 5 . Two of its vertices are $(2,1)$ and $(3,-2)$. The third vertex lies on $y=x+3$. Find the third vertex.
Sol. Let the third vertex be $\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$, area of triangle $=\frac{1}{2}\left|\left[x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right]\right|$
As $x_{1}=2, y_{1}=1, x_{2}=3, y_{2}=-2$, area of $\Delta=5$
$\Rightarrow 5=\frac{1}{2}\left|2\left(-2-y_{3}\right)+3\left(y_{3}-1\right)+x_{3}(1+2)\right|$
$\Rightarrow 10=\left|3 x_{3}+y_{3}-7\right| \Rightarrow 3 x_{3}+y_{3}= \pm 17$
Taking positive sign, $3 x_{3}+y_{3}-7=10 \Rightarrow 3 x_{3}+y_{3}=17$

Taking negative sign, $3 x_{3}+y_{3}-7=-10 \Rightarrow 3 x_{3}+y_{3}=-3$

Given that $\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ lies on $y=x+3$
So, $-x_{3}+y_{3}=3$

Solving eqs. (i) and (iii), $x_{3}=\frac{7}{2}, y_{3}=\frac{13}{2}$
Solving eqs. (ii) and (iii), $\quad x_{3}=\frac{-3}{2}, \quad y_{3}=\frac{3}{2}$.
So the third vertex is $\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{13}{2}\right)$ or $\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right)$
10. Find the co-ordinates of points which trisect the line segment joining $(1,-2)$ and $(-3,4)$.

Sol. Let $\mathrm{A}(1,-2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-3,4)$ be the given points and $\mathrm{C} \& \mathrm{D}$ be the points of trisection.
Then, $\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{DB}=\mathrm{p}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{CB}=\mathrm{CD}+\mathrm{DB}=\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{p}=2 \mathrm{p}$
and $\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{AC}+\mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{p}=2 \mathrm{p}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-203.jpg?height=136&width=499&top_left_y=2067&top_left_x=1372)
then $\mathrm{AC}: \mathrm{CB}=\mathrm{p}: 2 \mathrm{p}=1: 2$
and $\mathrm{AD}: \mathrm{DB}=2 \mathrm{p}: \mathrm{p}=2: 1$
Let C divides AB internally in the ratio $1: 2$ while D divides it in the ratio $2: 1$.
∴ By section formula, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C=\left[\frac{1(-3)+2(1)}{1+2}, \frac{1(4)+2(-2)}{1+2}\right]=\left(\frac{-1}{3}, 0\right) \text { and } \\
& D=\left[\frac{2(-3)+1(1)}{2+1}, \frac{2(4)+1(-2)}{2+1}\right]=\left(\frac{-5}{3}, 2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the two points of trisection are C and D .
11. Show that the points $(1,1),(3,0)$ and $(-1,2)$ are collinear.

Sol. Let $P(1,1), \mathrm{Q}(3,0)$ and $R(-1,2)$ be the given points

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P Q=\sqrt{(3-1)^{2}+(0-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{4+1}=\sqrt{5} \text { units } \\
& Q R=\sqrt{(-1-3)^{2}+(2-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{16+4}=2 \sqrt{5} \text { units } \\
& R P=\sqrt{(-1-1)^{2}+(2-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{4+1}=\sqrt{5} \text { units }
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, $P Q+R P=(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{5})$ units $=2 \sqrt{5}$ units $=Q R$
$\therefore \quad P, Q$, and $R$ are collinear points.
12. Find the area of the quadrilateral whose vertices, taken in order, are $(-4,-2),(-3,-5),(3,-2)$ and $(2,3)$.

Sol. We have made the rough sketch of the given quadrilateral ABCD as shown in figure.
Join the diagonal AC.
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
$=\frac{1}{2}[-4 \times(-5+2)-3 \times(-2+2)+3 \times(-2+5)]$
$=\frac{21}{2}$ sq. units
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ACD}$
$=\frac{1}{2}[20+15+0]=\frac{35}{2}$ sq. units
Then the area of the quadrilateral ABCD
$=$ area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}+$ area of $\triangle \mathrm{ACD}$
$=\frac{21}{2}+\frac{35}{2}$ sq. units $\Rightarrow 28$ sq. units
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-204.jpg?height=604&width=673&top_left_y=760&top_left_x=1144)

## SOLVED EXAMPLES BASED ON CONNECTING TOPIC

13. The centre and radius of the circumcircle of the triangle whose vertices are $(-2,3),(2,-1)$, and $(4,0)$ are
(a) $(3,5) ; 5 \sqrt{2}$
(b)
$\boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{0} \boldsymbol{,} \mathbf{0} \boldsymbol{)} \boldsymbol{;} \frac{\mathbf{5} \boldsymbol{\mathbf { 2 }}}{\mathbf{2}}$
(c) $\quad\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right) ; \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$
(d) $\quad\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{2}\right) ; \frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{2}$

Sol. (d) Let $\mathrm{A}=(-2,3), \mathrm{B}=(2,-1), \mathrm{C}=(4,0)$
Let the centre of the circumcircle of the $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ be $\mathrm{P}=(\alpha, \beta)$
Then, $\mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PC}$ i.e. $\mathrm{PA}^{2}=\mathrm{PB}^{2}=\mathrm{PC}^{2}$
$(\alpha+2)^{2}+(\beta-3)^{2}=(\alpha-2)^{2}+(\beta+1)^{2}=(\alpha-4)^{2}+(\beta-0)^{2}$
or, $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+4 \alpha-6 \beta+13=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}-4 \alpha+2 \beta+5=\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}-8 \alpha+16$
Subtracting $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ from each, $4 \alpha-6 \beta+13=-4 \alpha+2 \beta+5=-8 \alpha+16$
From the first two, $8 \alpha-8 \beta+8=0 \Rightarrow \alpha-\beta+1=0$

From the last two, $4 \alpha+2 \beta-11=0$

Solving (ii) and (iii) we get $\alpha=\frac{3}{2}$ and $\beta=\frac{5}{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-204.jpg?height=374&width=456&top_left_y=1644&top_left_x=1356)
$\therefore(\alpha, \beta)=\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{2}\right)=$ circumcentre.
Now, radius $=\mathrm{PC}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}-4\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{2}-0\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{-5}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{25}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{25}{4}\right)}=\sqrt{\frac{50}{4}}=\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{2}$
14. If $(0,1),(1,1)$ and $(1,0)$ are middle points of the sides of a triangle, find its incentre.

Sol. Let $A(a, b), B(c, d)$ and $C(e, f)$ be vertices of a triangle, then $a+c=0, c+e=2, e+a=2$,
$b+d=2, d+f=2, f+b=0$,
on solving these, we get $a=0=b, c=0, d=2, e=2, f=0 \Rightarrow A(0,0), B(0,2)$ and $C(2,0)$
Now,

$$
B C=2 \sqrt{2} \quad C A=2, A B=2
$$

∴ Incentre of a $\triangle A B C=(2-\sqrt{2}, 2-\sqrt{2})$
15. Let $A(4,2), B(6,5)$ and $C(1,4)$ be the vertices of $\triangle A B C$.

If $\mathrm{A}\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), \mathrm{B}\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\mathrm{C}\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ are the vertices of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, find the coordinates of the centroid of the triangle.
Sol. Let $\mathrm{A}\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), \mathrm{B}\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\mathrm{C}\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ be the vertices of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ whose medians are $\mathrm{AD}, \mathrm{BE}$ and CF respectively. So, $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}$ and F are respectively the mid-points of $\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CA}$ and AB .

Coordinates of D are $\left(\frac{x_{2}+x_{3}}{2}, \frac{y_{2}+y_{3}}{2}\right)$.
Coordinates of a point dividing AD in the ratio $2: 1$ are
$\left(\frac{1 . x_{1}+2\left(\frac{x_{2}+x_{3}}{2}\right)}{1+2}, \frac{1 . y_{1}+2\left(\frac{y_{2}+y_{3}}{2}\right)}{1+2}\right)$
$=\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-205.jpg?height=237&width=441&top_left_y=488&top_left_x=1430)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-205.jpg?height=267&width=610&top_left_y=715&top_left_x=1265)

The coordinates of E are $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{3}}{2}\right)$. The coordinates of a point dividing BE in the ratio $2: 1$ are $\left(\frac{1 . x_{2}+2\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{3}}{2}\right)}{1+2}, \frac{1 . y_{2}+2\left(\frac{y_{1}+y_{3}}{2}\right)}{1+2}\right)$
$\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$
Similarly, the coordinates of a point dividing CF in the ratio $2: 1$ are
$\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$
Thus, the point $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$ is common to $\mathrm{AD}, \mathrm{BE}$ and CF and divides them in the ratio $2: 1$.
∴ The medians of a triangle are concurrent and the coordinates of the centroid are $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$.

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which only one is correct.

1. If $\mathrm{P}=(2,5), \mathrm{Q}=(\mathrm{x},-7)$ and $\mathrm{PQ}=13$, what is the value of ' $x$ '?
(a) 5
(b 3
(c) $\quad-3$
(d) -5
2. The points $(a, b),\left(a_{1}, b_{1}\right)$ and $\left(a-a_{1}, b-b_{1}\right)$ are collinear if
(a) $a b=a_{1} b_{1}$
(b) $\mathrm{ab}_{1}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{~b}$
(c) $a=b$
(d) $a_{1}=b_{1}$
3. If $\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}, 4\right)$ is the midpoint of the line segment joining $\mathrm{A}(-6,5)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-2,3)$, then what is the value of ' a '?
(a) -4
(b) -12
(c) 12
(d) -6
4. If the mid point of the line joining $(3,4)$ and $(\mathrm{k}, 7)$ is $(x, y)$ and $2 x+2 y+1=0$. Find the value of $k$.
(a) 10
(b) -15
(c) 15
(d) -10
5. In what ratio is the line segment joining the points $(3,5) \&(-4,2)$ divided by y -axis?
(a) $3: 2$
(b) $3: 4$
(c) $2: 3$
(d) $4: 3$
6. In what ratio does the point $(-2,3)$ divide the line-segment joining the points $(-3,5)$ and $(4,-9)$ ?
(a) $2: 3$
(b) $1: 6$
(c) $6: 1$
(d) $2: 1$
7. If $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$ is any point on the line joining the points $\mathrm{A}(a, 0)$ and $\mathrm{B}(0, b)$, then
(a) $\frac{x}{b}+\frac{y}{a}=1$
(b) $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=1$
(c) $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$
(d) $\frac{x}{b}-\frac{y}{a}=1$
8. The perimeter of a triangle with vertices $(0,4)$, $(0,0)$ and $(3,0)$ is
(a) 5
(b) 12
(c) 11
(d) $7+\sqrt{5}$
9. The point P on $x$-axis equidistant from the points $\mathrm{A}(-1,0)$ and $B(5,0)$ is
(a) $(2,0)$
(b) $(0,2)$
(c) $(3,0)$
(d) $(2,2)$
10. The coordinates of the point which is reflection of point $(-3,5)$ in $x$-axis are
(a) $(3,5)$
(b) $(3,-5)$
(c) $(-3,-5)$
(d) $(-3,5)$
11. If the point $P(6,2)$ divides the line segment joining $\mathrm{A}(6,5)$ and $\mathrm{B}(4, y)$ in the ratio $3: 1$, then the value of $y$ is
(a) 4
(b) 3
(c) 2
(d) 1

## True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. The distance between $\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Q}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}, \mathrm{y}_{2}\right)$ is $\sqrt{\left(x_{2}+x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}+y_{1}\right)^{2}}$
2. The coordinates of the point $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ which divides the line segment joining the points $\mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ and $\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}, \mathrm{y}_{2}\right)$ internally in the ratio $\mathrm{m}_{1}: \mathrm{m}_{2}$ are $\left(\frac{m_{1} x_{2}-m_{2} x_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}, \frac{m_{1} y_{2}-m_{2} y_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right)$
3. The mid-point of the line segment joining the points $\mathrm{P}\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Q}\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ is $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}\right)$.
4. The area of the triangle formed by the points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$, $\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ is the numerical value of the expression $\frac{1}{2}\left[x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right]$.
5. Points $(1,7),(4,2),(-1,-1)$ and $(-4,4)$ are the vertices of a square.
6. Coordinates of the point which divides the join of $(-1,7)$ and $(4,-3)$ in the ratio $2: 3$ is $(1,3)$.
7. Ratio in which the line segment joining the points $(-3,10)$ and $(6,-8)$ is divided by $(-1,6)$ is $3: 7$.
8. Area of the triangle formed by the points $P(-1.5,3)$, $Q(6,-2)$ and $R(-3,4)$ is 0 .
9. The ratio in which the point $(3,5)$ divides the join of $(1,3)$ and $(4,6)$ is $2: 1$.
10. The distance of the point $(5,3)$ from the $X$-axis is 5 units
11. The distance of a point $(2,3)$ from $Y$-axis is $y$-units.

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. Find the area of a triangle whose vertices are
$A(-8,-2), B(-4,-6)$ and $C(-1,5)$.
2. Find the radius of the circle whose centre is at $(0,0)$ and which passes through the point $(-6,8)$.
3. $\quad A, B$ and $C$ are three collinear points. The coordinates of $A$ and $B$ are $(3,4)$ and $(7,7)$ respectively and $A C=10$ units. Find the co-ordinates of $C$.
4. Find the ratio in which the line $x+y=4$ divides the line joining the points $(-1,1)$ and $(5,7)$.
5. Find the area of the triangle whose vertices are $(a, a)$, $(a+1, a+1),(a+2, a)$.
6. The point A divides the join of points $(-5,1)$ and $(3,5)$ in the ratio $\mathrm{k}: 1$. Coordinates of points B and C are $(1,5)$ and $(7,-2)$ respectively. If the area of $\triangle A B C$ be 2 units, then find the value of $k$.
7. Distance of the point $(4, a)$ from $x$-axis is double its distance from y -axis, then find the value of ' $a$ '.
8. If the distance between the points ( $4, k$ ) and $(1,0)$ is 5 , then what can be the possible values of k ?
9. Find the ratio in which $\mathrm{P}(4, \mathrm{~m})$ divides the line segment joining the points $\mathrm{A}(2,3)$ and $\mathrm{B}(6,-3)$. Hence find m .
10. Find the ratio in which the segment joining the points $(1,-3)$ and $(4,5)$ is divide by $x$-axis? Also find the coordinates of this point on $x$-axis.

## Short Answer Questions

1. Show that the points $A(5,6), B(1,5), C(2,1)$ and $D(6,2)$ are the vertices of a square.
2. Determine the ratio in which the point $P(m, 6)$ divides the join of $\mathrm{A}(-4,3)$ and $\mathrm{B}(2,8)$. Also, find the value of m .
3. The coordinates of the mid-point of a line segment are $(2,3)$. If coordinate of one of the end points of the line segment are $(6,5)$, find the coordinate of the other end point.
4. If $A(3,5), B(-5,-4), C(7,10)$ are the three vertices of a parallelogram taken in the order, then find the coordinates of the fourth vertex.
5. Find a point on the $X$-axis, which is equidistant from the points $(7,6)$ and $(3,4)$.
6. If $(-2,2),(x, 8)$ and $(6, y)$ are three points on a circle having centre $(2,5)$, then find $x$ and $y$.
7. If $(-3,-1),(a, b),(3,3)$ and $(4,3)$ are the four vertices of a parallelogram taken in order, then, find the value of $a b$.
8. Find the area of the triangle ABC with $\mathrm{A}(1,-4)$ and mid-points of sides through A being $(2,-1)$ and $(0,-1)$.
9. In figure, ABC is a triangle coordinates of whose vertex A are $(0,-1)$. D and E respectively are the mid-points of the sides AB and AC and their coordinates are $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$ respectively. If F is the mid-point of BC , find the areas of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-207.jpg?height=291&width=318&top_left_y=1987&top_left_x=1318)
10. A line intersects the y -axis and x -axis at the points P and $Q$ respectively. If $(2,-5)$ is the mid-point of $P Q$, then find the coordinates of P and Q .
11. If $\mathrm{A}(-2,1), \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{C}(4, \mathrm{~b})$ and $\mathrm{D}(4,5)$ are the vertices of a parallelogram ABCD , find the values of a and b . Hence find the lengths of its sides.
12. The line segment joining the points $a(2,1)$ and $\mathrm{B}(5,-8)$ is trisected at the points P and Q such that P is nearer to A . If P also lies on the line given by $2 x-y+k=0$, find the value of $k$.
13. If the mid-point of the line segment joinning the points $\mathrm{A}(3,4)$ and $\mathrm{B}(k, 6)$ is $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$ and $x+y-10=0$, find the value of $k$.

## OR

Find the area of triangle ABC with $\mathrm{A}(1,-4)$ and the midpoints of sides through A being $(2,-1)$ and $(0,-1)$.

## Long Answer Questions

1. Find the value of m for which the points with coordinates $(3,3),(\mathrm{m}, 6)$ and ( $1 / 2,15 / 2$ ) are collinear.
2. In what ratio is the line segment joining the points ( $-2,-3$ ) and $(3,7)$ divided by the $Y$-axis? Also, find the coordinates of the point of division.
3. Show that the points $(2,0),(-6,-2),(-4,-4)$ and $(4,-2)$ form a parallelogram.
4. Find the values of k so that the area of the triangle with vertices $(1,-1),(-4,2 \mathrm{k})$ and $(-\mathrm{k},-5)$ is 24 sq. units.
5. Prove that the area of a triangle with vertices ( $\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}-2$ ), $(\mathrm{t}+2, \mathrm{t}+2)$ and $(\mathrm{t}+3, \mathrm{t})$ is independent of t .
6. If the points $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{k}+1,2 \mathrm{k}), \mathrm{B}(3 \mathrm{k}, 2 \mathrm{k}+3)$ and $\mathrm{C}(5 \mathrm{k}-1,5 \mathrm{k})$ are collinear, then find the value of k .

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Find the equation of median that is drawn to the side $B C$ of $\triangle A B C$ whose vertices are $A(1,-2), B(3,6)$ and $C(5,0)$.
2. Find the equation of a line which divides the line segment joining the points $(1,-2)$ and $(3,-1)$ in the ratio $3: 1$ perpendicularly.
3. If the points $A(1,-2), B(2,3), C(a, 2)$ and $D(-4,-3)$ form a parallelogram, find the value of $a$ and height of the parallelogram taking $A B$ as base.
4. Find the ratio in which the line joining the points $(a+b, b+a)$ and $(a-b, b-a)$ is divided by the point $(a, b)$.
5. Find the area of a triangle formed by the lines $4 x-y-8=0$, $2 x+y-10=0$ and $y=0$ (in sq. units).

## HOTS Questions

1. $A B C D$ is a quadrilateral formed by the points $A(-1,-1)$, $B(-1,4), C(5,4)$ and $D(5,-1) . P, Q, R$ and $S$ are the midpoint of $A B, B C, C D$ and $D A$ respectively. What type of quadrilateral $P Q R S$ is?
2. The two opposite vertices of a square are $(-1,2)$ and $(3,2)$. Find the coordinates of the other two vertices.
3. The line segment joining $A(2,3)$ and $B(-3,5)$ is extended through each end by a length equal to its original length. Find the coordinates of the new ends.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. Class X students of a secondary school in Krishnagar have been allotted a rectangular plot of a land for gardening activity. Saplings of Gulmohar are planted on the boundary at a distance of 1 m from each other. There is a triangular grassy lawn in the plot as shown in the fig.

The students are to sow seeds of flowering plants on the remaining area of the plot.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-208.jpg?height=309&width=563&top_left_y=466&top_left_x=1133)

## Considering A as origin, answer question (i) to (v)

[CBSE Sample Paper]
(i) Considering A as the origin, what are the coordinates of A?
(a) $(0,1)$
(b) $(1,0)$
(c) $(0,0)$
(d) $(-1,-1)$
(ii) What are the coordinates of P ?
(a) $(4,6)$
(b) $(6,4)$
(c) $(4,5)$
(d) $(5,4)$
(iii) What are the coordinates of R ?
(a) $(6,5)$
(b) $(5,6)$
(c) $(6,0)$
(d) $(7,4)$
(iv) What are the coordinates of D ?
(a) $(16,0)$
(b) $(0,0)$
(c) $(0,16)$
(d) $(16,0)$
(v) What are the coordinate of P if D is taken as the origin?
(a) $(12,2)$
(b) $(-12,2)$
(c) $(12,3)$
(d) $(6,10)$

Then answer the following questions.

(b) 4 unit

(b) unit
(c) 6 unit
(d) 5 unit

(d) $(6,4)$

## Exercise 2

## Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. Find the distance between the following pair of point: $(2,3),(4,1)$
2. Check whether $(5,-2),(6,4)$ and $(7,-2)$ are the vertices of an isosceles triangle.
3. If $Q(0,1)$ is equidistant from $P(5,-3)$ and $R(x, 6)$, find the value of $x$. Also find the distances $Q R$ and $P R$.
4. Find the coordinates of the points of trisection of the line segment joining $(4,-1)$ and $(-2,-3)$.
5. If A and B are $(-2,-2)$ and $(2,-4)$, respectively, find the coordinates of P such that $\mathrm{AP}=\frac{3}{7} \mathrm{AB}$ and P lies on the line segment AB .
6. Find the coordinates of the points which divide the line segment joining $\mathrm{A}(-2,2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(2,8)$ into four equal parts.
7. Find the area of the triangle formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of the triangle whose vertices are $(0,-1),(2,1)$ and $(0,3)$. Find the ratio of this area to the area of the given triangle.
8. You have studied in Class IX (Chapter 9, example 3) that a median of a triangle divides it into two triangles of equal areas. Verify this result for $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ whose vertices are $\mathrm{A}(4,-6), \mathrm{B}(3,-2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(5,2)$.

## Foundation Builder

## Single Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which only one is correct.tt

1. $P, Q, R$ are three collinear points. The coordinates of $P$ and $R$ are $(3,4)$ and $(11,10)$ respectively and $P Q$ is equal to 2.5 units. Coordinates of $Q$ are
(a) $(5,11 / 2)$
(b) $(11,5 / 2)$
(c) $(5,-11 / 2)$
(d) $(-5,11 / 2)$
2. $C$ is the mid-point of $P Q$, if $P$ is $(4, x), C$ is $(y,-1)$ and $Q$ is $(-2,4)$, then $x$ and $y$ respectively are
(a) - 6 and 1
(b) - 6 and 2
(c) 6 and - 1
(d) 6 and - 2
3. The ratio in which the point $(2, y)$ divides the join of $(-4,3)$ and $(6,3)$ and hence the value of $y$ is
(a) $2: 3, y=3$
(b) $3: 2, y=4$
(c) $3: 2, y=3$
(d) $3: 2, y=2$
4. Ratio in which the line $3 x+4 y=7$ divides the line segment joining the points $(1,2)$ and $(-2,1)$ is
(a) $3: 5$
(b) $4: 6$
(c) $4: 9$
(d) None of these
5. Determine the ratio in which the line $2 x+y-4=0$ divides the line segment joining the points $\mathrm{A}(2,-2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(3,7)$.
6. Find the centre of a circle passing through the points $(6,-6),(3,-7)$ and $(3,3)$.
7. ABCD is a rectangle formed by the points $\mathrm{A}(-1,-1)$, $B(-1,4), C(5,4)$ and $D(5,-1) . P, Q, R$ and $S$ are the mid-points of $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CD}$ and DA , respectively. Is the quadrilateral PQRS a square? a rectangle? or a rhombus? Justify your answer.

## Exemplar Questions

1. If the mid-point of the line segment joining the points $A(3,4)$ and $B(k, 6)$ is $P(x, y)$ and $x+y-10=0$, find the value of $k$.
2. Find the area of the triangle $A B C$ with $A(1,-4)$ and the mid-points of sides through $A$ being $(2,-1)$ and $(0,-1)$.
3. Name the type of triangle PQR formed by the points $P(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}), Q(-\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{2})$ and $R(-\sqrt{6}, \sqrt{6})$
4. $A B C D$ is a parallelogram with vertices $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $C\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$. Find the coordinates of the fourth vertex $D$ in terms of $x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, y_{1}, y_{2}$ and $y_{3}$.
5. The point on the $X$-axis which is equidistant from the points $A(-2,3)$ and $B(5,4)$ is
(a) $(0,2)$
(b) $(2,0)$
(c) $(3,0)$
(d) $(-2,0)$
6. The point which divides the line joining the points $A(1,2)$ and $B(-1,1)$ internally in the ratio $1: 2$ is
(a) $\left(\frac{-1}{3}, \frac{5}{3}\right)$
(b) $\left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{5}{3}\right)$
(c) $(-1,5)$
(d) $(1,5)$
7. The centroid of the triangle whose vertices are $(3,-7)$, $(-8,6)$ and $(5,10)$ is
(a) $(0,9)$
(b) $(0,3)$
(c) $(1,3)$
(d) $(3,5)$
8. The points $A(-4,-1), B(-2,-4), C(4,0)$ and $D(2,3)$ are the vertices of a
(a) Parallelogram
(b) Rectangle
(c) Rhombus
(d) Square
9. If the point $P(p, q)$ is equidistant from the points $A(a+b, b-a)$ and $B(a-b, a+b)$, then
(a) $a p=b y$
(b) $b p=a y$
(c) $a p+b q=0$
(d) $b p+a q=0$
10. The distances of a point from the $x$-axis and the $y$-axis are 5 and 4 respectively. The coordinates of the point can be
[JSTSE]
(a) $(5,4)$
(b) $(5,0)$
(c) $(0,4)$
(d) $(4,5)$
11. If the points $(a, 0),(0, b)$ and $(1,1)$ are collinear then which of the following is true :
[JSTSE]
(a) $\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=2$
(b) $\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}}=1$
(c) $\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}}=2$
(d) $\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}}=1$
12. If the vertices of an equilateral triangle have integral coordinates, then
[NTSE]
(a) such a triangle is not possible.
(b) the area of the triangle is irrational.
(c) the area of the triangle is an integer.
(d) the area of the triangle is rational but not an integer.
13. P is a point on the graph of $y=5 x+3$. The coordinates of a point Q are $(3,-2)$. If M is the mid point of PQ , then M must lie on the line represented by
[NTSE]
(a) $y=5 x+1$
(b) $y=5 x-7$
(c) $y=\frac{5}{2} x-\frac{7}{2}$
(d) $y=\frac{5}{2} x+\frac{1}{2}$
14. A line $l$ passing through the origin makes an angle $\theta$ with positive direction of $x$-axis such that $\sin \theta=\frac{3}{5}$. The coordinates of the point, which lies in the fourth quadrant at a unit distance from the origin and on perpendicular to $l$, are
[NTSE]
(a) $\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right)$
(b) $\left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{5}\right)$
(c) $(3,-4)$
(d) $(4,-3)$
15. The centre of the circle passing through the ponts $(6,-6)$, $(3,-7)$ and $(3,3)$ is
[NTSE]
(a) $(3,2)$
(b) $(-3,-2)$
(c) $(3,-2)$
(d) $(-3,2)$
16. A circle passes through the vertices of a triangle ABC . If the vertices are $\mathrm{A}(-2,5), \mathrm{B}(-2,-3), \mathrm{C}(2,-3)$, then the centre of the circle is
[NTSE]
(a) $(0,0)$
(b) $(0,1)$
(c) $(-2,1)$
(d) $(0,-3)$

## More Than One Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which one or more may be correct.

1. The area of a triangle is 5 and its two vertices are $A(2,1)$ and $B(3,-2)$. The third vertex lies on $y=x+3$. Then third vertex is
(a) $\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{13}{2}\right)$
(b) $\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{5}{2}\right)$
(c) $\left(+\frac{3}{2},+\frac{3}{2}\right)$
(d) $(0,0)$
2. Which of the following points is not 10 units from the origin?
(a) $(-6,8)$
(b) $(-4,-6)$
(c) $(-6,-8)$
(d) $(6,4)$
3. The distance between which of the following two points is 2 units?
(a) (-2, -3) and (-2, -4)
(b) $(0,4)$ and $(0,6)$
(c) $(7,2)$ and $(6,2)$
(d) $(4,-3)$ and $(2,-3)$
4. Which of the following is / are correct ?

Three points will form :
(a) an equilateral triangle, if all the three sides are equal.
(b) an isosceles triangle, if any two sides are equal.
(c) a collinear or a line, if sum of two sides is equal to third side.
(d) a rhombus, if all the four sides are equal.

5 Which of the following is / are incorrect?
Four points will form :
(a) a rectangle, if opposite sides and diagonals are not equal.
(b) a parallelogram, if opposite sides are not equal.
(c) a square, if all the four sides and diagonals are equal.
(d) a right angle triangle, if sum of squares of any two sides is equal to square of third largest side.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph (s) and answer the following questions.

## Passege I

If point $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$ divides the line segment joining $A(a, b)$ and $B$ ( $c, d$ ) internally in the ratio $m_{1}: m_{2}$, then coordinates of $P$ are
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-210.jpg?height=117&width=483&top_left_y=1206&top_left_x=1174)

$$
x=\frac{m_{1} c+m_{2} a}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \quad y=\frac{m_{1} d+m_{2} b}{m_{1}+m_{2}}
$$

1. The coordinates of the point which divides the line segment joining the points $(6,3)$ and $(-4,5)$ in the ratio $3: 2$ internally, is
(a) $\left(0, \frac{21}{5}\right)$
(b) $\left(\frac{21}{5}, 0\right)$
(c) $(0,0)$
(d) $\left(\frac{-21}{5}, \frac{5}{21}\right)$
2. In what ratio does the y -axis divide the line segment joining the point $\mathrm{P}(-4,5)$ and $\mathrm{Q}(3,-7)$ ?
(a) $3: 4$
(b) $4: 3$
(c) $13: 7$
(d) $7: 13$
3. Point $Q$ divides the line segment joining the points $A(-2,1)$ and $B(5,8)$ such that $\frac{A Q}{A B}=\frac{1}{3}$. If $Q$ lies on the line $2 x-y+C=0$, then the value of ' $C$ ' is
(a) 4
(b) 2
(c) -4
(d) 6

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.

[^1]1. Assertion : If $A(2 a, 4 a)$ and $B(2 a, 6 a)$ are two vertices of an equilateral triangle $A B C$ then, the vertex $C$ is given by $(2 a+a \sqrt{3}, 5 a)$.
Reason : In equilateral triangle, all the coordinates of three vertices can be rational.
2. Assertion : The points $(k, 2-2 k),(-k+1,2 k)$ and $(-4-k, 6-2 k)$ are collinear if $k=\frac{1}{2}$.
Reason : Three points $A, B$ and $C$ are collinear in same straight line, if $A B+B C=A C$.
3. Assertion : Mid-point of a line segment divides line in the ratio $1: 1$.
Reason : If area of triangle is zero that means points are collinear.

## SOLUTIONS <br> (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions)

## Exercise 1 <br> Master Boards

Multiple Choice Questions

1. (c) $\mathrm{PQ}=13 \Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}^{2}=169$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{x}-2)^{2}+(-7-5)^{2}=169$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}-4 \mathrm{x}+4+144=169$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}-4 \mathrm{x}-21=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}-7 \mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{x}-21=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{x}-7)(\mathrm{x}+3)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{x}=7,-3$
2. (b) We have,
$\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{b}_{1}-\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{b}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{a}_{1}\left(\mathrm{~b}-\mathrm{b}_{1}-\mathrm{b}\right)+\left(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{a}_{1}\right)\left(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}_{1}\right)\left(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{b}_{1}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{ab}_{1}-\mathrm{ab}-\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{~b}_{1}+\mathrm{ab}-\mathrm{ab}_{1}-\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{~b}_{1}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{ab}_{1}-\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{~b}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{ab}_{1}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{~b}$.
3. (b) Coordinates of mid-point are given by
$\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{x}_{2}}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{y}_{1}+\mathrm{y}_{2}}{2}\right)$
Here, coordinates of mid-point are $\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}, 4\right)$
So, $\frac{a}{3}=\frac{-6-2}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}=-12$
4. (b) Since ( $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}$ ) is midpoint of ( 3,4 ) and ( $\mathrm{k}, 7$ )
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{3+\mathrm{k}}{2}$ and $\mathrm{y}=\frac{4+7}{2}$
Also $2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+1=0$ putting values we get
$3+\mathrm{k}+4+7+1=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}+15=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=-15$
5. (b) Let the required ratio be $\mathrm{K}: 1$
∴ The coordinates of the required point on the y -axis is
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{K}(-4)+3(1)}{\mathrm{K}+1} ; \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{K}(2)+5(1)}{\mathrm{K}+1}$
Since, it lies on y -axis
∴ Its x -cordinates $=0$
$\therefore \frac{-4 K+3}{K+1}=0 \Rightarrow-4 K+3=0$
$\Rightarrow K=\frac{3}{4}$
⇒ Required ratio $=\frac{3}{4}: 1$
∴ ratio $=3: 4$
6. (b) Suppose the required ratio is $\mathrm{m}_{1}: \mathrm{m}_{2}$

Then, using the section formula, we get
$-2=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}(4)+\mathrm{m}_{2}(-3)}{\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow-2 \mathrm{~m}_{1}-2 \mathrm{~m}_{2}=4 \mathrm{~m}_{1}-3 \mathrm{~m}_{2}$

1. right angle
2. $2 \sqrt{2}$
3. isosceles
4. $2: 7$
5. 24. sq. units
1. $x+3 y=7$
2. $x+3 y=7$
3. $x+3 y=7$
4. False
5. True

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. Using the formula, area of triangle

$$
=\frac{1}{2}\left|x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right|
$$

Area $=28$ sq. units.
2. Let $A(0,0)$ and $B(-6,8)$ be the given points.

Now, radius of the cirlce is same as the distance of the line segment $A B$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-213.jpg?height=266&width=362&top_left_y=699&top_left_x=470)
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AB}=\sqrt{(-6-0)^{2}+(8-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{36+64}=\sqrt{100}=10$
Hence, radius of the circle is 10 units.
3. $A B=\sqrt{(7-3)^{2}+(7-4)^{2}}=5$
$A C=10 \quad \stackrel{A(3,4)}{\rightarrow} \quad B(7,7) \quad C(x, y)$
Since $A, B$ and $C$ are collinear
$B C=A C-A B=5$
$\Rightarrow \quad B$ is the mid-point of $A C$.
If the coordinate of $C$ are $(x, y)$, then
$\frac{x+3}{2}=7$ and $\frac{y+4}{2}=7$
$\Rightarrow x=11$ and $y=10$
Hence, the coordinate of $C$ is $(11,10)$.
4. Ratio $=-\left(\frac{-1+1-4}{5+7-4}\right)=\frac{1}{2}$
5. To make it easy, assume $a=1$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-213.jpg?height=483&width=474&top_left_y=1633&top_left_x=415)

Therefore, the vertices are $(1,1),(2,2),(3,1)$
Area of $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2} \times A D \times B C=\frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 2=1$
6. $\mathrm{A} \equiv\left(\frac{3 k-5}{k+1}, \frac{5 k+1}{k+1}\right)$, Area of $\triangle A B C=2$ units
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{3 k-5}{k+1}\right)(5+2)+1\left(-2-\frac{5 k+1}{k+1}\right)+7\left(\frac{5 k+1}{k+1}-5\right)\right]$
$= \pm 2$
$\Rightarrow 14 k-66= \pm 4(k+1) \Rightarrow k=7$ or $31 / 9$
7. For point (4, a) distance from x -axis $=a$ and distance from $y$-axis $=4$
According to the question
$a=2(4)=8$
8. Let $\mathrm{A}(4, \mathrm{k})$ and $\mathrm{B}(1,0)$ are two points.

Given that, $\mathrm{AB}=5$ units
By Distance formula
$\mathrm{AB}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}-\mathrm{x}_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{1}-\mathrm{y}_{2}\right)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow 5=\sqrt{(4-1)^{2}+(\mathrm{k}-0)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow 25=9+\mathrm{k}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}^{2}=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}= \pm 4$
9.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-213.jpg?height=136&width=545&top_left_y=854&top_left_x=1151)

Let $\mathrm{P}(4, \mathrm{~m})$ divide AB in the ratio of $\mathrm{K}: 1$
By section formula, $4=\frac{\mathrm{K}(6)+1(2)}{\mathrm{K}+1}$
$\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{~K}+4=6 \mathrm{~K}+2$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2=2 \mathrm{~K}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=1$
So, $\mathrm{P}(4, \mathrm{~m})$ is midpoint of AB as it divides AB in the ratio of $1: 1$.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}=\frac{1(-3)+1(3)}{1+1}=\frac{-3+3}{2}=\frac{0}{2}=0$
Hence the value of $\mathrm{m}=0$
10. Let the ratio be $\mathrm{k}: 1$.

Then by the section formula, the coordinates of the point which divides AB in the ratio $\mathrm{k}: 1$ are $\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{k}+1}{\mathrm{k}+1}, \frac{5 \mathrm{k}-3}{\mathrm{k}+1}\right)$.
This point lies on the x -axis, and we know that on the x -axis, $\mathrm{y}=0$
$\therefore \frac{5 \mathrm{k}-3}{\mathrm{k}+1}=0 \Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{k}-3=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{3}{5}$.
That is, the ratio is $(3 / 5): 1$ that is $3: 5$.
Putting $\mathrm{k}=\frac{3}{5}$, we get the point of intersection as $\left(\frac{17}{8}, 0\right)$.

## Short Answer Questions

1. $A B=B C=C D=A D$
$\therefore A C^{2}=A B^{2}+B C^{2} \Rightarrow \angle A B C=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore A B C D$ is a square.
2. $A(-4,3), B(2,8)$ and $P(m, 6)$
$\underset{\mathrm{A}(-4,3)}{\mathrm{k}: 1} \underset{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{m}, 6)}{\circ} \mathrm{B}(2,8)$
Let P divides the join of AB in the ratio of $\mathrm{k}: 1$
$\therefore y$ coordinate of $\mathrm{P}=\frac{k \times 8+1 \times 3}{k+1}$
$\Rightarrow 6=\frac{8 k+3}{k+1} \Rightarrow 6 k+6=8 k+3$
$\Rightarrow 2 k=3 \Rightarrow k=3 / 2$
$\therefore \mathrm{P}$ divides the join of $A B$ in the ratio of $3: 2$.
$x$ coordinate of $P=\frac{3 \times 2+2 \times(-4)}{3+2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad m=\frac{6-8}{5} \Rightarrow m=-2 / 5$
3. $(-2,1)$ is the co-ordinate of the other end point.
4. Co-ordinates of fourth vertex $D=(15,19)$.
5. Let the point on the $X$-axis be ( $x_{1}, 0$ ). The other two points $A$ and $B$ are $A(7,6), B(3,4)$ we have $P A=P B$ or $P A^{2}=P B^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-214.jpg?height=268&width=392&top_left_y=733&top_left_x=397)
or $\left(x_{1}-7\right)^{2}+6^{2}=\left(x_{1}-3\right)^{2}+4^{2}$
or $x_{1}{ }^{2}-14 x_{1}+49+36=x_{1}{ }^{2}-6 x_{1}+9+16$
or $-14 x_{1}+85=-6 x_{1}+25$
or $8 x_{1}=60 \quad$ or $\quad x_{1}=\frac{60}{8}$
$\therefore \quad x_{1}=\frac{15}{2}$
∴ The point $P$ on $X$-axis equidistant from $A$ and $B$ is $\left(\frac{15}{2}, 0\right)$
6. Here $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{OC}$ (= radius)
$\Rightarrow O A^{2}=O B^{2}=O C^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16+9=(x-2)^{2}+9=16+y^{2}-10 y+25$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-214.jpg?height=287&width=472&top_left_y=1580&top_left_x=358)
$\Rightarrow \quad 25=x^{2}+4+4 x+9=16+y^{2}-10 y+25$
$\Rightarrow 0=x^{2}-4 x-12=y^{2}-10 y+16$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x-6)(x+2)=0$ and $(y-8)(y-2)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=6,-2$ and $y=8,2$
7. As diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other, so
$\frac{-3+3}{2}=\frac{a+4}{2}$ and $\frac{-1+3}{2}=\frac{b+3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=-4$ and $b=-1 \therefore a b=4$.
8.

P is the mid-point of AB
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}+1=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=3$ [By Mid-point formula]
$y-4=-2 \Rightarrow y=2 \Rightarrow B(3,2)$
Similarly :

$$
z+1=0 \Rightarrow z=-1
$$

and $\mathrm{t}-4=-2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}(-1,2)$
$\therefore \quad$ Area $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
$=\frac{1}{2}[1(2-2)+3(2+4)-1(-4-2)]$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 24=12$ sq units
9. Let the coordinates of B and C be $\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}, \mathrm{y}_{2}\right)$ and $\left(\mathrm{x}_{3}, \mathrm{y}_{3}\right)$, respectively.

D is the mid-point of AB .
Then, $(1,0)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}_{2}+0}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{y}_{2}-1}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad 1=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{2}}{2}$ and $0=\frac{\mathrm{y}_{2}-1}{2}$
So, $\mathrm{x}_{2}=2$ and $\mathrm{y}_{2}=1$
Then, the coordinates of B are $(2,1)$.
As, E is the mid-point of AC .
Therefore, $(0,1)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}_{3}+0}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{y}_{3}-1}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad 0=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{3}}{2}$ and $1=\frac{\mathrm{y}_{3}-1}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}_{3}=0$ and $\mathrm{y}_{3}=3$
Then, the coordinates of C are $(0,3)$.
Now, F is the mid-point of BC . So, its coordinates
$=\left(\frac{2+0}{2}, \frac{1+3}{2}\right)=(1,2)$
Therefore, area of a triangle
$=\frac{1}{2}\left|\mathrm{x}_{1}\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{3}\right)+\mathrm{x}_{2}\left(\mathrm{y}_{3}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{x}_{3}\left(\mathrm{y}_{1}-\mathrm{y}_{2}\right)\right|$
Here, the area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
$=\frac{1}{2}|0(1-3)+2(3+1)+0(-1-1)|$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 8=4$ square units
Hence, the area of $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}$
$=\frac{1}{2}|1(1-2)+0(2-0)+1(0-1)|$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times|(-2)|=1$ square units
10.

Let the coordinates of P and Q are $(0,-\mathrm{k})$ and $(\mathrm{h}, 0)$ respectively.
So, coordinate of mid-point $\mathrm{R} \equiv(2,-5)$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}+0}{2}, \frac{-\mathrm{k}+0}{2}\right) \equiv(2,-5)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=4, \mathrm{k}=10$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-215.jpg?height=264&width=476&top_left_y=694&top_left_x=326)

As ABCD is a parallelogram (given)
⇒ Diagonals of parallelogram ABCD bisect each other So, mid - point of $\mathrm{AC}=$ mid - point of BD
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{-2+4}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{~b}+1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{4+\mathrm{a}}{2}, \frac{5+0}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{2}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{~b}+1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{4+\mathrm{a}}{2}, \frac{5}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{2}=\frac{4+\mathrm{a}}{2} \quad$ and $\quad \frac{\mathrm{b}+1}{2}=\frac{5}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2=4+\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{b}+1=5$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=2-4=-2$ and $\mathrm{b}=5-1=4$
Hence $\mathrm{a}=-2$ and $\mathrm{b}=4$
Now, $\mathrm{AB}=\sqrt{(-2+2)^{2}+(1-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{1}=1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{CD}=1$ unit as opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal.
Also,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{AD} & =\sqrt{(4+2)^{2}+(5-1)^{2}} \\
& =\sqrt{36+16}=\sqrt{52}=2 \sqrt{13} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{BC}=2 \sqrt{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

12. Given P and Q be the points of trisection of AB i.e., $\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{QB}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-215.jpg?height=110&width=474&top_left_y=1914&top_left_x=415)

Therefore, P divides AB internally in the ratio $1: 2$.
Therefore, the coordinates of P , by applying the section formula, are
$\left(\frac{1(5)+2(2)}{1+2}, \frac{1(-8)+2(1)}{1+2}\right)$, i.e., $(3,-2)$
Since, P also lies on the line $2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{k}=0$.
Hence, $(3,-2)$ satisfy $2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{k}=0$
$\therefore \quad 2(3)-(-2)+\mathrm{k}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=-8$.
Hence, the value of k is -8 .
13. Since, mid-point of $\mathrm{A}(3,4)$ and $\mathrm{B}(k, 6)$ is $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}(x, y)=\left(\frac{3+k}{2}, \frac{4+6}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{3+k}{2}, 5\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{3+k}{2}, y=5$
Putting values of $x$ and $y$ in

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y-10=0 \\
& \frac{3+k}{2}+5-10=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{3+k}{2}=5 \Rightarrow 3+k=10 \\
\Rightarrow & k=10-3 \Rightarrow k=7
\end{aligned}
$$

OR

\mathrm{D}(2,-1)=\left(\frac{x_{1}+1}{2}, \frac{y_{1}-4}{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow & 2=\frac{x_{1}+1}{2} \\
& 4=x_{1}+1 \\
& x_{1}=3
\end{array} \right\rvert\, \begin{aligned}
& -1=\frac{y_{1}-4}{2} \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{~B}(3,2)
\end{aligned} \quad \begin{aligned}
& -2=y_{1}-4 \\
& y_{1}=2
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-215.jpg?height=250&width=469&top_left_y=1105&top_left_x=1240)

\quad \begin{array}{l}
x_{2}+1=0 \\
x_{2}=-1
\end{array} & \begin{array}{l}
-2=y_{2}-4 \\
y_{2}=2

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}(-1,2)$
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ [where $\mathrm{A}(1,-4), \mathrm{B}(3,2), \mathrm{C}(-1,2)$ ]

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{2}|[1(2-2)+3(2+4)-1(-4-2)]| \\
& =\frac{1}{2}|0+18+6|=\frac{1}{2} \times 24=12 \\
& =12 \text { sq unit. }
\end{aligned}
$$

## Long Answer Questions

1. If points are collinear, then one point divides the other two in some ratio.
Let point ( $m, 6$ ) divides the joint of ( 3,3 ) and ( $\frac{1}{2}, \frac{15}{2}$ ) in the ratio $k: 1$. Then,

$$
(m, 6)=\left(\frac{\frac{k}{2}+3}{k+1}, \frac{\frac{15 k}{2}+3}{k+1}\right) \Rightarrow m=\frac{\frac{k}{2}+3}{k+1}
$$

$$
\text { and } 6=\frac{\frac{15 k}{2}+3}{k+1}
$$

From (ii), we get
$6 k+6=\frac{15 k}{2}+3 \Rightarrow 6 k-\frac{15 k}{2}=3-6 \Rightarrow k=2$
Substituting, $k=2$ in (i), we get
$m=\frac{\frac{2}{2}+3}{2+1}=\frac{4}{3}$
$\therefore \quad$ For $m=\frac{4}{3}$ points are collinear.
2. Let $Y$-axis divides the join of $(-2,-3)$ and $(3,7)$ in the ratio $k: 1$.
$\therefore \quad$ Point of division is $\left(\frac{3 k-2}{k+1}, \frac{7 k-3}{k+1}\right)$

As this point lies on $Y$-axis, therefore, $X$-coordinates $=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 k-2}{k+1}=0 \Rightarrow 3 k-2=0 \Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{3}$
⇒ Ratio is $\frac{2}{3}: 1$, i.e. $2: 3$
Substituting in (i), we get
Point of division is $\left(0, \frac{\frac{14}{3}-3}{\frac{2}{3}+1}\right)=(0,1)$
3. Let the given points be $A(2,0), B(-6,-2), C(-4,-4)$ and $D(4,-2)$
Then, $A B=\sqrt{(-6-2)^{2}+(-2-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{68}$ units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B C=\sqrt{(-4+6)^{2}+(-4+2)^{2}}=\sqrt{8} \text { units. } \\
& C D=\sqrt{(4+4)^{2}+(-2+4)^{2}}=\sqrt{68} \text { units. } \\
& D A=\sqrt{(4-2)^{2}+(-2-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{8} \text { units. } \\
& A C=\sqrt{(-4-2)^{2}+(4-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{52} \text { units. } \\
& B D=\sqrt{(4+6)^{2}+(-2+2)^{2}}=10 \text { units. }
\end{aligned}
$$

Clearly,
$A B=C D, B C=D A$ and $A C \neq B D$.
i.e., the opposite sides of the quadrilateral are equal and diagonals are not equal. Hence, the given points form a parallelogram.
4. $\quad$ Area of the triangle $=24$ sq. units
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}[1(2 \mathrm{k}+5)-4(-5+1)-\mathrm{k}(-1-2 \mathrm{k})]=24$
So, $2 \mathrm{k}^{2}+3 \mathrm{k}-27=0 \Rightarrow(2 \mathrm{k}+9)(\mathrm{k}-3)=0$
After solving we get $\mathrm{k}=3,-\frac{9}{2}$
5. Let $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}-2), \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{t}+2, \mathrm{t}+2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{t}+3, \mathrm{t})$ be the vertices of the given triangle.
Since the area of the triangle having vertices $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$, $\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}, \mathrm{y}_{2}\right)$ and $\mathrm{x}_{3}, \mathrm{y}_{3}$ )
$=\frac{1}{2}\left|\mathrm{x}_{1}\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{3}\right)+\mathrm{x}_{2}\left(\mathrm{y}_{3}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{x}_{3}\left(\mathrm{y}_{1}-\mathrm{y}_{2}\right)\right|$
So, area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\left|\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}_{1}\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{3}\right)+\mathrm{x}_{2}\left(\mathrm{y}_{3}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{x}_{3}\left(\mathrm{y}_{1}-\mathrm{y}_{2}\right)\right]\right| \\
& =\left|\frac{1}{2}(2 \mathrm{t}+2 \mathrm{t}+4-4 \mathrm{t}-12)\right| \\
& =|-4|=4 \text { square units }
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the area of the triangle with specified vertices is independent of t .
6. Since, $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{k}+1,2 \mathrm{k}), \mathrm{B}(3 \mathrm{k}, 2 \mathrm{k}+3)$ and $\mathrm{C}(5 \mathrm{k}-1,5 \mathrm{k})$ are collinear.
So, the slope of AB and BC will be same.
Slope of $\mathrm{AB}=$ Slope of BC
$\frac{2 \mathrm{k}+3-2 \mathrm{k}}{3 \mathrm{k}-(\mathrm{k}+1)}=\frac{5 \mathrm{k}-(2 \mathrm{k}+3)}{(5 \mathrm{k}-1)-3 \mathrm{k}}$
$\frac{3}{2 \mathrm{k}-1}=\frac{3 \mathrm{k}-3}{2 \mathrm{k}-1}$
$(2 \mathrm{k}-1)(3 \mathrm{k}-3-3)=0 \Rightarrow(2 \mathrm{k}-1)(3 \mathrm{k}-6)=0$
$\mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2}, 2$
So, the value of k is 2 or $\frac{1}{2}$.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Let $D$ be the mid-point of side $B C$ and $A D$ be the median from vertex $A$ to the side $B C$.
Then, coordinates of mid-point $D$ are
$=\left(\frac{3+5}{2}, \frac{0+6}{2}\right)=(4,3)$
Slope of line $A D$ is,
$m=\frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}}=\frac{3-(-2)}{4-1}=\frac{5}{3}$.
Then, equation of median passing through the point $A(1,-2)$ with slope $\frac{5}{3}$ is
$y-(-2)=\frac{5}{3}(x-1)$
$3 y+6=5 x-5 \Rightarrow 5 x-3 y-11=0$
Hence, the required equation of median is $5 x-3 y-11=0$.
2. Since, the line divides the segment joinning the point $A(1,-2)$ and $B(3,-1)$ in the ratio $3: 1$.
Then, the coordinates of the point which divides in the given ratio is
$\left(\frac{m x_{2}+n x_{1}}{m+n}, \frac{m y_{2}+n y_{1}}{m+n}\right)=\left(\frac{3 \times 3+1 \times 1}{3+1}, \frac{3 \times(-1)+1 \times(-2)}{3+1}\right)$
$=\left(\frac{10}{4}, \frac{-5}{4}\right)=\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{-5}{4}\right)$.
Slope $m$ of the line segment joining the points $(1,-2)$ and $(3,-1)$ is,
$m=\frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}}=\frac{-1-(-2)}{3-1}=\frac{1}{2}$
Now, slope of the line perpendicular to the line segment joining the points $A$ and $B$ is
$m^{\prime}=-\frac{1}{m}=-2$
Since, the equation of line passing through the point $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ with slope $m$ is
$\left(y-y_{1}\right)=m\left(x-x_{1}\right)$
Then, the equation of line passing through the point $\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{-5}{4}\right)$ with slope $(-2)$ is
$y-\left(\frac{-5}{4}\right)=(-2)\left(x-\frac{5}{2}\right)$
$y+\frac{5}{4}=-2 x+5$
$4 y+5=-8 x+20$
$8 x+4 y-15=0$
Hence, the required equation of line is
$8 x+4 y-15=0$
3. Let $A B C D$ be a parallelogram. Since, diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other. Then, the segments $A C$ and $B D$ have the same mid-point.
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{1+a}{2}, \frac{-2+2}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{-4+2}{2}, \frac{-3+3}{2}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-217.jpg?height=351&width=485&top_left_y=1265&top_left_x=406)
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a+1}{2}=\frac{-2}{2} \Rightarrow a+1=-2$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=-2-1 \Rightarrow a=-3$
∴ The point $C$ is $(-3,2)$.
Let $C K \perp A B$, then length of $C K$ is the height of the parallelogram taking $A B$ as base.
Now, area $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2}(A B \times C K)$
$\Rightarrow C K=\frac{2 \text { area } \triangle A B C}{A B}$

By distance formula $A B=\sqrt{(2-1)^{2}+(3+2)^{2}}=\sqrt{26}$
and area $(\triangle A B C)=\frac{1}{2}\{1(3-2)+2(2-(-2))$
$+(-3)(-2-3)\}$ sq. units
$=\frac{1}{2}\{1+8+15\}=12$ sq. units
From eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we find that

$$
C K=\frac{2 \times 12}{\sqrt{26}}=\frac{24 \sqrt{26}}{\sqrt{26} \sqrt{26}}=\frac{12}{13} \sqrt{26}
$$

Hence, required height of parallelogram is $\frac{12}{13} \sqrt{26}$.
4. Let the line joining the point $A(a+b, b+a)$ and
$B(a-b, b-a)$ is divided by the point $P(a, b)$ in the ratio
$k: 1$, then $a=\frac{k(a-b)+1 \cdot(a+b)}{k+1}$
$\Rightarrow a(k+1)=a k-b k+a+b$
$\Rightarrow \quad a k+a=a k-b k+a+b$
$\Rightarrow b k=b \Rightarrow k=1$
Hence, the required ratio is $1: 1$.
5. Consider the given equation of lines are

$$
\begin{array}{r}
4 x-y-8=0 \\
2 x+y-10=0 \\
y=0
\end{array}
$$

From eqn. (i) + (ii),

$$
6 x-18=0 \Rightarrow x=3
$$

Put $x=3$ in eqn. (i),

$$
4 \times 3-y-8=0 \Rightarrow y=4
$$

So, point of intersection is $(3,4)$.
From eqns. (ii) and (iii), the point of intersection is $(5,0)$
From eqns. (i) and (iii), the point of intersection is $(2,0)$.
So, the vertices of triangles are $(3,4),(5,0)$ and $(2,0)$.
Then, the area of triangle is
$\frac{1}{2}\left|x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right|$
$=\frac{1}{2}|3(0-0)+5(0-4)+2(4-0)|=\frac{1}{2}|0-20+8|$
$=\frac{1}{2}|-12|=\frac{1}{2} \times 12=6$ sq. unit

## HOTS Questions

1. $P$ is the mid point of $A B$
∴ Coordinates of $P$ are $\left(\frac{-1-1}{2}, \frac{-1+4}{2}\right)=\left(-1, \frac{3}{2}\right)$
$Q$ is the mid-point of $B C$
∴ Coordinates of $Q$ are $\left(\frac{-1+5}{2}, \frac{4+4}{2}\right)=(2,4)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-217.jpg?height=246&width=556&top_left_y=1758&top_left_x=1197)
$R$ is the mid-point of $C D$
∴ Co-ordinate of $R$ are $\left(\frac{5+5}{2}, \frac{-1+4}{2}\right)=\left(5, \frac{3}{2}\right)$
$S$ is the mid-point of $D A$
∴ Co-ordinate of $S$ are $\left(\frac{5-1}{2}, \frac{-1-1}{2}\right)=(2,-1)$
$\therefore P\left(-1, \frac{3}{2}\right), Q(2,4), R\left(5, \frac{3}{2}\right)$ and $S(2,-1)$ are the mid-points of $A B, B C, C D$ and $D A$ respectively.
Now,
$P Q=\sqrt{(2+1)^{2}+\left(4-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+\frac{25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{61}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}$
$Q R=\sqrt{(5-2)^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}-4\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+\frac{25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{61}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{61}}{2}$
2. Let the line segment joining $\mathrm{A}(2,3)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-3,5)$ is extended through each end by a length equal to its original length AB as shown in fig.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-218.jpg?height=95&width=616&top_left_y=488&top_left_x=1091)

Let $\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Q}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}, \mathrm{y}_{2}\right)$ be the new end points
Now, $\mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{BQ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{A}$ is the mid-point of PB .
$\Rightarrow 2=\frac{x_{1}+(-3)}{2}$ and $3=\frac{y_{1}+5}{2}$

Also, diagonal
$P R=\sqrt{(5+1)^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{36+0}=\sqrt{36}=6$
and diagonal $S Q=\sqrt{(2-2)^{2}+(4+1)^{2}}$

$$
=\sqrt{0+(5)^{2}}=\sqrt{25}=5
$$

∴ Diagonal $P R \neq$ Diagonal $Q S$
$\Rightarrow P Q R S$ is a rhombus
2. Let the vertices of a square be $A, B, C, D$ and $A=(-1,2)$ and $C=(3,2)$. Let $B=\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ and $D=\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$
In a square, all sides are equal, so, $A B=B C=C D=D A$ and both the diagonals are equal, so, $A C=B D$.
Diagonals of a square bisect each other .
Let diagonals bisect each other at P . So, P is the mid point of AC, where coordinates of $P=\left(\frac{3-1}{2}, \frac{2+2}{2}\right)=(1,2)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-218.jpg?height=302&width=362&top_left_y=1400&top_left_x=413)
$A B=B C \Rightarrow A B^{2}=B C^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(x_{1}+1\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-2\right)^{2}=\left(x_{1}-3\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-2\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow x_{1}^{2}+2 x_{1}+1=x_{1}^{2}-6 x_{1}+9 \Rightarrow 8 x_{1}=8 \Rightarrow x_{1}=1$
Since, $P$ is also the mid point of $B D ; \frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}=1$
$\Rightarrow x_{1}+x_{2}=2$ or $1+x_{2}=2 \Rightarrow x_{2}=1$
As, $A C^{2}=A D^{2}+D C^{2}$
$(3+1)^{2}+(2-2)^{2}=\left(x_{2}+1\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-2\right)^{2} +\left(x_{2}-3\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-2\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2\left(y_{2}-2\right)^{2}+(1+1)^{2}+(1-3)^{2}=4^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2\left(y_{2}-2\right)^{2}+4+4=16$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2\left(y_{2}-2\right)^{2}=8 \Rightarrow\left(y_{2}-2\right)^{2}=4 \Rightarrow y_{2}-2= \pm 2$
$\Rightarrow y_{2}=4$ or 0
Since, $P$ is mid point of BD
$\Rightarrow \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}=2 \Rightarrow y_{1}+y_{2}=4$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ if $y_{2}=0, y_{1}=4$ and if $y_{2}=4, y_{1}=0$
So, $y_{1}=0$ and $y_{2}=4$ or $y_{1}=4$ and $y_{2}=0$
Hence, coordinates of the other two vertices are $(1,0)$ and ( 1,4 ).
$\Rightarrow \quad 4=x_{1}-3$ and $6=y_{1}+5$
which gives $x_{1}=7$ and $y_{1}=1$
Thus, the coordinate of the point P are $(7,1)$
Again, B is the mid-point of AQ
$\therefore \frac{2+\mathrm{x}_{2}}{2}=-3$ and $\frac{3}{y} \quad \Rightarrow 2+\mathrm{x}_{2}=-6$
and $3+y_{2}=10 \Rightarrow x_{2}=-8$ and $y_{2}=7$
∴ Therefore, the coordiantes of Q are ( $-8,7$ ).
Hence the coordinates of the new ends are $(7,1)$ and $(-8,7)$.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. (i) (c) $(0,0)$
(ii) (a) $(4,6)$
(iii) (a) $(6,5)$
(iv) (a) $(16,0)$
(v) (b) $(-12,6)$
2. (i) (b) Coordinate of $\mathrm{P}=(3,4)$
(ii) (a) $\mathrm{P}(3,4) \& \mathrm{Q}(6,7)$
$P Q=\sqrt{(6-3)^{2}+(7-4)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+9}=3 \sqrt{2}$ unit
(iii) (c) $\mathrm{P}(3,4)$ \& $\mathrm{R}(9,4)$
$\operatorname{PR}=\sqrt{(9-3)^{2}+(4-4)^{2}}=\sqrt{6^{2}+0}$
$=6$ unit
(iv) (a) $\mathrm{P}(3,4), \mathrm{Q}(6,7), \mathrm{R}(9,4)$ and $\mathrm{S}(6,1)$
$\mathrm{QR}=\sqrt{(9-6)^{2}+(4-7)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+9}=3 \sqrt{2}$ unit
$\mathrm{RS}=\sqrt{(9-6)^{2}+(4-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+9}=3 \sqrt{2}$ unit
$\mathrm{PS}=\sqrt{(6-3)^{2}+(1-4)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+9}=3 \sqrt{2}$ unit
$\mathrm{PQ}=3 \sqrt{2}$ unit
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{QR}=\mathrm{RS}=\mathrm{PS}$
All sides equal
$\mathrm{PR}=6$ unit
$\mathrm{QS}=\sqrt{(6-6)^{2}+(7-1)^{2}}=6$ unit
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{PR}=\mathrm{QR}$
Diagonals is also equal
So, ABCD is square.
(v) (d) Mid-point of $\mathrm{QS}=\left(\frac{6+6}{2}, \frac{7+1}{2}\right)$
$=(6,4)$

Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. $x_{1}=2, y_{1}=3 ; x_{2}=4, y_{2}=1$

Distance between the two given points
$=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{(4-2)^{2}+(1-3)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{2}$ units
2. The three points are $A(5,-2), B(6,4)$ and $C(7,-2)$
$A B=\sqrt{(6-5)^{2}+(4+2)^{2}}=\sqrt{37}$
$B C=\sqrt{(7-6)^{2}+(-2-4)^{2}}=\sqrt{37}$
$C A=\sqrt{(7-5)^{2}+(-2+2)^{2}}=\sqrt{4+0}=2$
In $\triangle A B C$, we have $A B=B C$.
Therefore, $\triangle A B C$ is isosceles.
3. Since $Q(0,1)$ is equidistant from $P(5,-3)$ and $R(x, 6)$, then $Q P=Q R$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(5-0)^{2}+(-3-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(6-1)^{2}}$
Squaring both sides, we get
$25+16=x^{2}+25 \Rightarrow x^{2}=16 ; x= \pm 4$
When $x=4, R$ is $(4,6)$
$\therefore Q R=\sqrt{(4-0)^{2}+(6-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{16+25}=\sqrt{41}$
and $P R=\sqrt{(4-5)^{2}+(6+3)^{2}}=\sqrt{1+81}=\sqrt{82}$
Again when $x=-4, R$ is $(-4,6)$.
$Q R=\sqrt{(-4-0)^{2}+(6-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{16+25}=\sqrt{41}$
and $P R=\sqrt{(-4-5)^{2}+(6+3)^{2}}=\sqrt{81+81}$
$=\sqrt{162}=\sqrt{81 \times 2}=9 \sqrt{2}$
Hence $x= \pm 4, Q R=\sqrt{41}, P R=\sqrt{82}$ and $9 \sqrt{2}$
4.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-219.jpg?height=138&width=604&top_left_y=1740&top_left_x=324)

Point P and Q trisect the line segment joining the points $\mathrm{A}(4,-1)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-2,-3)$,
i.e., $\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{QB}$.
Here, P divides AB in the ratio $1: 2$ and Q divides AB in the ratio $2: 1$.
$x$-coordinate of $\mathrm{P}=\frac{1 \times(-2)+2 \times(4)}{1+2}=\frac{6}{3}=2$;
$y$-coordinate of $\mathrm{P}=\frac{1 \times(-3)+2 \times(-1)}{1+2}=\frac{-5}{3}$
Thus, the coordinates of P are $\left(2, \frac{-5}{3}\right)$.
Now, $x$-coordinate of $\mathrm{Q}=\frac{2 \times(-2)+1 \times(4)}{2+1}=0$
$y$-coordinate of $\mathrm{Q}=\frac{2 \times(-3)+1 \times(-1)}{2+1}=-\frac{7}{3}$

Thus, the coordinates of Q are $\left(0,-\frac{7}{3}\right)$.
Hence, the points of trisection are $\mathrm{P}\left(2,-\frac{5}{3}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Q}\left(0,-\frac{7}{3}\right)$.
5. $\mathrm{AP}=\frac{3}{7} \mathrm{AB}$
$\mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{AB}-\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{AB}-\frac{3}{7} \mathrm{AB}=\frac{4}{7} \mathrm{AB}$
$\frac{\mathrm{AP}}{\mathrm{BP}}=\frac{\frac{3}{7} \mathrm{AB}}{\frac{4}{7} \mathrm{AB}}=\frac{3}{4}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-219.jpg?height=136&width=602&top_left_y=881&top_left_x=1151)

Thus, P divides AB in the ratio $3: 4$.
$x$-coordinate of $\mathrm{P}=\frac{3 \times(2)+4 \times(-2)}{3+4}=-\frac{2}{7}$
$y$-coordinate of $\mathrm{P}=\frac{3 \times(-4)+4 \times(-2)}{3+4}=-\frac{20}{7}$
Hence, the coordinates of P are $\left(-\frac{2}{7},-\frac{20}{7}\right)$.
6. $(-2,2) \mathrm{A} \times-\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{R}(2,8)$

Points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ divide AB into four equal parts.
$\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{QR}=\mathrm{RB}$
Now, $Q$ is mid-point of $A B$.
By mid-point formula, the coordinates of the point Q are $\left(\frac{-2+2}{2}, \frac{2+8}{2}\right)$, i.e., $(0,5)$.
$P$ is mid-point of $A Q$
Then, the coordinates of P are $\left(\frac{-2+0}{2}, \frac{2+5}{2}\right)$ i.e., $\left(-1, \frac{7}{2}\right)$
R is mid-point of QB
Then, the coordinates of R are $\left(\frac{0+2}{2}, \frac{5+8}{2}\right)$ i.e., $\left(1, \frac{13}{2}\right)$
Hence, the required points are $\mathrm{P}\left(-1, \frac{7}{2}\right), \mathrm{Q}(0,5)$ and $\mathrm{R}\left(1, \frac{13}{2}\right)$.
7. $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}$ are the mid-points of the sides $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}$ and CA respectively of the $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-219.jpg?height=303&width=458&top_left_y=2151&top_left_x=1247)
$\mathrm{D}=\left(\frac{0+2}{2}, \frac{-1+1}{2}\right)=(1,0) ; \mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{2+0}{2}, \frac{1+3}{2}\right)=(1,2)$
$\mathrm{F}=\left(\frac{0+0}{2}, \frac{3-1}{2}\right)=(0,1)$
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}=1$ sq. unit
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=4$ sq. units
Hence, the area of the $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}=1$ sq. unit
∴ The ratio of the area of the $\triangle \mathrm{DEF}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is $1: 4$.
8. Let AD be the median of a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.

Co-ordinates of D which is the mid-point of BC are
$\left(\frac{3+5}{2}, \frac{-2+2}{2}\right)=(4,0)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-220.jpg?height=287&width=593&top_left_y=794&top_left_x=296)

Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)+x_{2}\left(y_{3}-y_{1}\right)+x_{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}[4(-2-0)+3(0+6)+4(-6+2)]$
$=\frac{1}{2}[-8+18-16]=\frac{1}{2}(-8+2)$
$=\frac{1}{2}(-6)=-3=3$ sq units. $\quad[\quad$ In magnitude $]$
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}=\frac{1}{2}[4(0-2)+4(2+6)+5(-6-0)]$
$=\frac{1}{2}(-8+32-30)$
$=\frac{1}{2}(-6)=-3=3$ sq units. [ In magnitude]
Hence, median AD divides $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ into two equal triangles i.e. $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$, each of area 3 sq. units.
9. Suppose, the line $2 x+y-4=0$ divides the line segment joining $\mathrm{A}(2,-2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(3,7)$ in the ratio $k: 1$ at point C .
Then, the coordinates of C are $\left(\frac{3 k+2}{k+1}, \frac{7 k-2}{k+1}\right)$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-220.jpg?height=268&width=512&top_left_y=1987&top_left_x=338)

But, C lies on $2 x+y-4=0$. Therefore,
$2\left(\frac{3 k+2}{k+1}\right)+\left(\frac{7 k-2}{k+1}\right)-4=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 6 k+4+7 k-2-4 k-4=0 \Rightarrow 9 k-2=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 9 k=2 \Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{9}$
So, the required ratio is $2: 9$ internally.
10. Let $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ be the centre of the circle passing through the points $\mathrm{A}(6,-6), \mathrm{B}(3,-7)$ and $\mathrm{C}(3,3)$. Then, $\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{CP}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-220.jpg?height=332&width=446&top_left_y=479&top_left_x=1192)

Now, $\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{BP} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AP}^{2}=\mathrm{BP}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x-6)^{2}+(y+6)^{2}=(x-3)^{2}+(y+7)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-12 x+36+y^{2}+12 y+36$
$=x^{2}-6 x+9+y^{2}+14 y+49$
$\Rightarrow-12 x+6 x+12 y-14 y+72-58=0$
$\Rightarrow-6 x-2 y+14=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x+y-7=0$
and, $\mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{CP} \Rightarrow \mathrm{BP}^{2}=\mathrm{CP}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow(x-3)^{2}+(y+7)^{2}=(x-3)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-6 x+9+y^{2}+14 y+49$
$=x^{2}-6 x+9+y^{2}-6 y+9$
$\Rightarrow-6 x+6 x+14 y+6 y+58-18=0$
$\Rightarrow 20 y+40=0$
$\Rightarrow y=\frac{-40}{20}=-2$

Putting $y=-2$ in equation (i), we get
$3 x-2-7=0$
$\Rightarrow 3 x=9 \Rightarrow x=3$
Thus, the centre of the circle is $(3,-2)$.
11. Various points are marked in the adjoining figure.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-220.jpg?height=323&width=469&top_left_y=1603&top_left_x=1183)
$P Q=\sqrt{(2+1)^{2}+\left(4-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{3^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{9+\frac{25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{36+25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{61}{4}}$
$\mathrm{QR}=\sqrt{(5-2)^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}-4\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{3^{2}+\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}} \sqrt{9+\frac{25}{4}}$
$=\sqrt{\frac{36+25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{61}{4}}$
$\mathrm{RS}=\sqrt{(2-5)^{2}+\left(-1-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+\left(\frac{-5}{2}\right)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{9+\frac{25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{36+25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{61}{4}}$
and, $\mathrm{SP}=\sqrt{(-1-2)^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}+1\right)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{9+\frac{25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{36+25}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{61}{4}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{QR}=\mathrm{RS}=\mathrm{SP}$
Now, $P R=\sqrt{(5+1)^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{36}=6$
and, $\mathrm{SQ}=\sqrt{(2-2)^{2}+(4+1)^{2}}=\sqrt{25}=5$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{PR} \neq \mathrm{SQ}$
Since all the sides are equal but the diagonals are not equal,
$\therefore \mathrm{PQRS}$ is a rhombus.

## Exemplar Questions

1. Mid-point of the line segment joining $A(3,4)$ and $B(\mathrm{k}, 6)$

$$
=\left(\frac{3+k}{2}, \frac{4+6}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{3+k}{2}, 5\right)
$$

Then, $\left(\frac{3+k}{2}, 5\right)=(x, y)$
Therefore, $\frac{3+k}{2}=x$ and $5=y$.
Since $x+y-10=0$, we have

$$
\frac{3+k}{2}+5-10=0 \text { i.e., } 3+k=10
$$

Therefore, $k=7$.
2. Let the coordinates of $B$ and $C$ be $(a, b)$ and $(x, y)$, respectively.
Then, $\left(\frac{1+a}{2}, \frac{-4+b}{2}\right)=(2,-1)$
Therefore, $1+a=4,-4+b=-2$

$$
a=3, b=2
$$

Also, $\left(\frac{1+x}{2}, \frac{-4+y}{2}\right)=(0,-1)$
Therefore, $1+x=0,-4+y=-2$
i.e., $x=-1$ and $y=2$
The coordinates of the vertices of $\triangle A B C$ are
$A(1,-4), B(3,2)$ and $C(-1,2)$.
Area of $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2}[1(2-2)+3(2+4)-1(-4-2)]$
$=\frac{1}{2}[18+6]=12$ sq. units
3. Using distance formula,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P Q=\sqrt{(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2})^{2}+(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2})^{2}} \\
& =\sqrt{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}+(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}=\sqrt{16}=4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P R=\sqrt{(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{6})^{2}+(\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{6})^{2}} \\
& =\sqrt{2+6+2 \sqrt{12}+2+6-2 \sqrt{12}}=\sqrt{16}=4 \\
& R Q=\sqrt{(-\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{6})^{2}+(-\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{6})^{2}} \\
& =\sqrt{2+6-2 \sqrt{12}+2+6+2 \sqrt{12}}=\sqrt{16}=4
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{PR}=\mathrm{RQ}=4$; points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ form an equilateral triangle.
4. Let the coordinates of $D$ be $(x, y)$. We know that diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-221.jpg?height=342&width=515&top_left_y=840&top_left_x=1217)

Therefore, mid-point of $A C=$ mid-point of $B D$
$\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{3}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{3}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{x_{2}+x}{2}, \frac{y_{2}+y}{2}\right)$
i.e., $x_{1}+x_{3}=x_{2}+x$ and $y_{1}+y_{3}=y_{2}+y$
i.e, $x_{1}+x_{3}-x_{2}=x$ and $y_{1}+y_{3}-y_{2}=y$

Thus, the coordinates of $D$ are
$\left(x_{1}+x_{3}-x_{2}, y_{1}+y_{3}-y_{2}\right)$

## Exercise 3

Foundation Builder

## Single Option Correct

1. (a)
2. (a) Since, $C(y,-1)$ is the mid-point of $P(4, x)$ and $Q(-2,4)$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-221.jpg?height=120&width=419&top_left_y=1685&top_left_x=1265)

We have, $\frac{4-2}{2}=y$ and $\frac{4+x}{2}=-1$
$\therefore y=1$ and $x=-6$
3. (c) Let the required ratio be $k: 1$

Then, $2=\frac{6 k-4(1)}{k+1}$ or $k=\frac{3}{2}$
$\therefore \quad$ The required ratio is $\frac{3}{2}: 1$ or $3: 2$
Also, $y=\frac{3(3)+2(3)}{3+2}=3$
4. (c) $-\frac{3(1)+4(2)-7}{3(-2)+4(1)-7}=-\frac{4}{-9}=\frac{4}{9}$
5. (b) Let $P(x, 0)$ be a point on X -axis such that $A P=B P$
$\Rightarrow A P^{2}=B P^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x+2)^{2}+(0-3)^{2}=(x-5)^{2}+(0+4)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+4 x+4+9=x^{2}-10 x+25+16$
$\Rightarrow \quad 14 x=28 \Rightarrow x=2$
Hence, required point is $(2,0)$.
6. (b)
7. (b) Centroid is $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}}{3}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}}{3}\right)$
i.e. $\left(\frac{3+(-8)+5}{3}, \frac{-7+6+10}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{0}{3}, \frac{9}{3}\right)=(0,3)$
8. (b) 9. (b)
10. (d)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-222.jpg?height=182&width=222&top_left_y=655&top_left_x=331)
11. (d) As $(a, 0),(0, b)$ and $(1,1)$ are collinear
$\therefore \quad a(b-1)+0(1-0)+1(0-b)=0$
$a b-a-b=0$
$a b=a+b$
$1=\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}$
12. (a) Let the vertex be $(x r, y r), r=1,2,3$, where both $x r$ and $y r$ are integers. Hence its area

$$
=\frac{1}{2} \Sigma x_{1}\left(y_{2}-y_{3}\right)=\text { rational number }
$$

Also if a be the side of equilateral triangle, then
$a^{2}=\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)^{2}=a$ positive integer.
But the area of an equilateral triangle $=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}$.

$$
\therefore \quad \text { Area }=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) a^{2}, \text { irrational number },
$$

[ $a^{2}$ is a positive integer.]
Thus the two statements (i) and (ii) for area are contradictory.

Hence, such type of an equilateral triangle is not possible.
13. (b) Let coordinate of point $p$ be $(h, 5 h+3)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-222.jpg?height=213&width=467&top_left_y=1722&top_left_x=360)

Since, M is the mid-point of PQ , therefore by mid-point formula, we have $\mathrm{M}=\left(\frac{h+3}{2}, \frac{5 h+3-2}{2}\right)$.
Clearly by observing the options, we can say that M must lie on the line

$$
y=5 x-7
$$

14. (a)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-222.jpg?height=304&width=323&top_left_y=2193&top_left_x=333)
$\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\theta$
$\mathrm{CO} \perp \mathrm{OA}$
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{BOC}=\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)$
$\sin \theta=\frac{3}{5} ; \quad \cos \theta=\frac{4}{5}\left[\because \cos \theta=\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}\right]$
Now, $\sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{-y}{1}$
$\Rightarrow y=-\cos \theta \Rightarrow y=\frac{-4}{5}$
$\cos \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{x}{1} \Rightarrow x=\sin \theta, x=\frac{3}{5}$
15. (c) $(x-6)^{2}+(y+6)^{2}=(x-3)^{2}+(y+7)^{2}$

Also, $(x-3)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}=(x-3)^{2}+(y+7)^{2}$
$y^{2}-6 y+9=y^{2}+14 y+49$
$-20 y=40 \Rightarrow y=-2$
Putting $y=-2$ in equation (i), we have
$(x-6)^{2}+(4)^{2}=(x-3)^{2}+(5)^{2}$
$x^{2}-12 x+36+16=x^{2}-6 x+9+25$
$-6 x=-18 \Rightarrow x=3$
16. (b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-222.jpg?height=410&width=475&top_left_y=1122&top_left_x=1149)

Let $\mathrm{O}(x, y)$ is the centre of the given circle.
Join $\mathrm{OA}, \mathrm{OB}$ \& OC .

$$
\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{OC}
$$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{OA}^{2}=\mathrm{OB}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(x+2)^{2}+(y-5)^{2}}=\sqrt{(x+2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+4+4 x+y^{2}+25-10 y=x^{2}+4+4 x+y^{2}+9+6 x$
$\Rightarrow 16 y=16 \Rightarrow y=1$
Again: $\mathrm{OB}^{2}=\mathrm{OC}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(x+2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}}=\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+4+4 x+(y+3)^{2}=x^{2}+4-4 x+(y+3)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 8 x=0 \Rightarrow x=0$
$\therefore \quad$ centre of the circle is $(0,1)$.

2. (b, d)
3. (b, d)
4. (a, b, c)

All the statements given in option 'a', 'b' and 'c' are correct.
5. (a, b, d)

Passage Based Questions

1. (a) $a=6, b=3$
$c=-4, d=5$
$m_{1}=3, m_{2}=2$

$$
\begin{array}{ll} 
& \underset{\mathrm{A}(6,3)}{\square} \\
\therefore & x=\frac{3(-4)+2(6)}{3+2}=0 ; y=\frac{3(5)+2(3)}{3+2}=\frac{21}{5} \\
\therefore & P(x, y)=\left(0, \frac{21}{5}\right)
\end{array}
$$

2. (b) Let $x$-axis divide $P \mathrm{Q}$ in the ratio $k: 1$. Then, the coordinates of the point of division are

$$
A=\left(\frac{3 k-4}{k+1}, \frac{-7 k+5}{k+1}\right) \text { (By Section formula) }
$$

Since, $A$ lies on $y$-axis
therefore, $\frac{3 k-4}{k+1}=0 \Rightarrow 3 k-4=0 \Rightarrow k=\frac{4}{3}$
Hence, the required ratio is $\frac{4}{3}$ i.e. $4: 3$
3. (c) $\frac{A Q}{A B}=\frac{1}{3}$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{A Q}{A Q+Q B}=\frac{1}{3}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-223.jpg?height=104&width=437&top_left_y=1256&top_left_x=434)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad 3 A Q=A Q+Q B \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 A Q=Q B \Rightarrow \frac{A Q}{Q B}=\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $Q$ divides $A B$ in the ratio $1: 2$.

$$
\therefore \quad Q=\left[\frac{1(5)+2(2)}{1+2}, \frac{1(8)-2(1)}{1+2}\right]=(3,2)
$$

Since, $Q(3,2)$ lies on the line

$$
2 x-y+C=0 \therefore 2(3)-2+C=0 \Rightarrow C=-4
$$

## Assertion \& Reason

1. (c) Let $A\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right), B\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right) \& C\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ are all rational coordinates of a triangle $A B C$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC}) & =\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll}
x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\
x_{2} & y_{2} & 1 \\
x_{3} & y_{3} & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
= & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\left[\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

LHS = rational, RHS = irrational
Hence, $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right) \&\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ cannot be all rational
2. (a) Both assertion and reason are correct. Reason is correct explanation of assertion.
3. (b) Both statements are individually correct.

## Integer Type Questions

1. Ans : 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A B=\sqrt{25}=5 \text { units } \\
& B C=\sqrt{25}=5 \text { units, } \mathrm{CA}=5 \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(By Distance formula)P

Also, By Pythagoras Theorem
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-223.jpg?height=476&width=481&top_left_y=413&top_left_x=1233)
$A B^{2}+B C^{2}=C A^{2}$
$\therefore \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is a right angled isosceles triangle.
Now, area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}$ (base) $\times$ (height)

$$
\frac{25}{a}=\frac{1}{2}(A B) \times(B C)=\frac{1}{2}(5) \times(5) \Rightarrow \frac{25}{a}=\frac{25}{2}
$$

$\Rightarrow a=2$
2. Ans : 2

Let B be $(-a,-5)$, since C is mid-point of AB .

$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}=\left(\frac{-a+4}{2}, \frac{-5-1}{2}\right)
$$

But $\mathrm{C}=(1,-3) \therefore \frac{-a+4}{2}=1, \frac{-5-1}{2}=-3$
$\Rightarrow-a=-2$
Hence, the coordinates of B are $(-2,-5)$, where $a=2$.
3. Ans: 4

Since, given points are collinear
∴ $\quad$ Area of the triangle formed by these points $=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 7+10+5 k-3-6-7 k=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-2 k+8=0 \Rightarrow k=4$
(By using the formula of area of $\Delta$ )
4. Ans : 0

Area of $\Delta$ formed by these given points

$$
\begin{aligned}
=\frac{1}{2}[a\{c+a-(a+b)\}+b\{(a+b) & -(b+c)\} \\
& +c\{(b+c)-(c+a)\}]
\end{aligned}
$$

$=\frac{1}{2}[a c-a b+b(a+b)-b(b+c)+c(b+c)-c(c+a)]$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[a c-a b+a b+b^{2}-b^{2}-b c+b c+c^{2}-c^{2}-c a\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}[0]=0$
5. Ans: 4

Since, diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-224.jpg?height=414&width=465&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=360)
∴ mid-point of $A C=$ mid-point of $B D$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{4-2}{2}=\frac{a+1}{2}$ and $\frac{-1+b}{2}=\frac{0+2}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=2-1=1$ and $b=2+1=3$
$\therefore \quad a+b=1+3=4$
6. Ans: 4

Given, $A B=10 \Rightarrow A B^{2}=100$
$\Rightarrow(x-2)^{2}+(5+3)^{2}=100$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+4-4 x+64=100$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}-4 x+4=36$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-4 x-32=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x-8)(x+4)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=8$ or -4
So, sum $=8-4=4$
7. Ans : $\mathbf{6}$
$2 a=\frac{2 a+1+5}{2}, \frac{4+b-1}{2}=b$
$\Rightarrow 4 a=2 a+6,3+b=2 b$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 a=6,3=b$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=3$ and $b=3$
$\therefore \quad a+b=6$.

## Exercise 4

1. (a) Let $\mathrm{S}(x, y)$ be the circumcentre of $\triangle P Q R$.
$\Rightarrow S P=S Q=S R \Rightarrow S P^{2}=S Q^{2}=S R^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-224.jpg?height=346&width=456&top_left_y=1809&top_left_x=365)

Now, $S P^{2}=S Q^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x+2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}=(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2 x+6 y+12=0 \Rightarrow x+3 y+6=0$

Consider $S Q^{2}=S R^{2}$
$\Rightarrow(x+1)^{2}+y^{2}=(x-7)^{2}+(y+6)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 16 x-12 y-84=0$
$\Rightarrow 4 x-3 y-21=0$

On solving (i) and (ii), we get
$x=3, y=-3$
$\therefore \quad$ Circumcentre $=(3,-3)$.
2. (a) Given, vertices $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C are the vertices of a right angled triangle.
Since, $\left(2, \frac{-1}{2}\right)$ is the vertex at the right angle, therefore, its orthocentre is $\left(2, \frac{-1}{2}\right)$.
3. (d) Circumcentre $=$ mid-point of AB

$$
=\left(\frac{12+0}{2}, \frac{0+12}{2}\right)=(6,6)
$$

Centroid $=\left(\frac{12+0+12}{3}, \frac{0+12+12}{3}\right)=(8,8)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-224.jpg?height=368&width=419&top_left_y=877&top_left_x=1206)

Distance between the circumcentre and centroid

$$
=\sqrt{(8-6)^{2}+(8-6)^{2}}=\sqrt{4+4}=\sqrt{8}=2 \sqrt{2}
$$

4. (a, b, d)

Area $=\frac{1}{2}[0(0-b)+a(b-0)+0(0)]=\frac{a b}{2}$

Centroid $=\left(\frac{0+0+a}{3}, \frac{0+0+b}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{a}{3}, \frac{b}{3}\right)$
Circumcentre is mid-point of the hypotenuse of the triangle
$\therefore \quad$ Circumcentre $=\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{b}{2}\right)$
5. Ans : 8

Let ABC be the triangle whose vertices are $\mathrm{A}(0,0)$, $\mathrm{B}(5,3), \mathrm{C}(3,5)$ and $\mathrm{D}(x, y)$ be the circumcentre.
Then, $D A=D B=D C$
$\Rightarrow D A^{2}=D B^{2}=D C^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-224.jpg?height=284&width=328&top_left_y=2259&top_left_x=1251)

Consider $D A^{2}=D B^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=(x-5)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}=x^{2}+25-10 x+y^{2}+9-6 y$
$\Rightarrow \quad 10 x+6 y=34 \Rightarrow 5 x+3 y=17$

Consider $D B^{2}=D C^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(x-5)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}=(x-3)^{2}+(y-5)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x+5 y=17$

On solving (i) and (ii), we get
$x=y=\frac{17}{8}$
Hence ' $a$ ' $=8$
6. Ans : 0

Let $A(-36,7), B(20,7)$ and $C(0,-8)$ be the vertices of $\triangle A B C$. Then
$B C=\sqrt{(20-0)^{2}+(7+8)^{2}}=25$
$C A=\sqrt{(-36-0)^{2}+(7+8)^{2}}=39$
$A B=\sqrt{(20+36)^{2}+(7-7)^{2}}=56$
$\therefore \quad x=\frac{25(-36)+39(20)+56(0)}{25+39+56}=\frac{-120}{120}=-1$
$\therefore y=\frac{25(7)+39(7)+56(-8)}{25+39+56}=\frac{0}{120}=0$
Hence, incentre $=(-1,0) \equiv(-1, a)$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=0$
7. Ans: $\mathbf{3}$

Let $A B C$ be a triangle whose vertices are
$A=(6,3), \quad B=(-1,7), \quad C=(x, y)$
and centroid $G=(1,5)$
Then, using the formula, for coordinates of centroid, we get
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-225.jpg?height=291&width=463&top_left_y=607&top_left_x=1242)
$1=\frac{6+(-1)+x}{3}$ and $5=\frac{3+7+y}{3}$
$\Rightarrow x=-2$ and $y=5$
Hence, the third vertex is $C=(-2,5)$
8. Ans : 3
$x=\frac{2+5+3}{3}=\frac{10}{3}$ and $y=\frac{1+2+4}{3}=\frac{7}{3}$
