(C) $\frac{1+\cos x}{\sin x}=\frac{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}=\cot \frac{x}{2}$
(D) $\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}=\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x+2 \sin } =\sqrt{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}}=|\sin x+\cos x|$
2. (a) We have, $3 A=2 A+A \Rightarrow \tan 3 A=\tan \left(2{ }^{\circ}\right.$
$=\frac{(\tan 2 A+\tan A)}{(1-\tan 2 A \tan A)}$
$\Rightarrow \tan 3 A-\tan 3 A \tan 2 A \tan A=\tan 2 A+\tan$
$\Rightarrow \tan 3 A-\tan 2 A-\tan A=\tan 3 A \tan 2 A \tan$
3. (d) Consider,
$\cos \theta \cdot \cos \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)-\sin \theta \sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)$
$=\cos \theta . \sin \theta-\sin \theta . \cos \theta=0$
4. (c) Given : $\tan ^{2} \mathrm{~A}=2 \tan ^{2} \mathrm{~B}+1$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1+\tan ^{2} \mathrm{~A}=2 \tan ^{2} \mathrm{~B}+1+1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sec ^{2} \mathrm{~A}=2 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{~B}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~B}=2 \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~A}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~B}=1+\cos 2 \mathrm{~A}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~B}-1=\cos 2 \mathrm{~A}$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~B}=\cos 2 \mathrm{~A}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos 2 \mathrm{~A}+\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~B}=0$.
5. (c) Consider $\left(\frac{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+x\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+x\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)-\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}\right)^{2}$
$=\left(\frac{-\sin x+\cos x}{\sin x-\cos x}\right)^{2}=\left\{-\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sin x-\cos x}\right)\right\}$
$=(-1)^{2}=1$
6. (d) The given expression is :
$\sin 20^{\circ}\left(\tan 10^{\circ}+\cot 10^{\circ}\right)$
$=\sin 20^{\circ}\left(\tan 10^{\circ}+\frac{1}{\tan 10^{\circ}}\right)$
$=2 \sin 10^{\circ} \cos 10^{\circ}\left(\frac{\tan ^{2} 10^{\circ}+1}{\tan 10^{\circ}}\right)$
$=2 \sin 10^{\circ} \cos 10^{\circ}\left(\frac{\sec ^{2} 10^{\circ}}{\tan 10^{\circ}}\right)$
$=2 \sin 10^{\circ} \cos 10^{\circ} \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} 10^{\circ} \cdot \sin 10^{\circ}} \cos 10^{\circ}$
7. (c) Given values are $\tan \mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2}$ and $\tan \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\tan (2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B})=\frac{\tan 2 \mathrm{~A}+\tan \mathrm{B}}{1-\tan 2 \mathrm{~A} \tan \mathrm{~B}}$
and, $\tan 2 \mathrm{~A}=\frac{2 \tan \mathrm{~A}}{1-\tan ^{2} \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{2 \frac{1}{2}}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{4}{3}$
So, $\tan (2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B})=\frac{\frac{4}{3}+\frac{1}{3}}{1-\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{3}}=\frac{\frac{5}{3}}{\frac{5}{9}}=3$
8. (c) Given that $\tan \theta=m$ and $\tan 2 \theta=n$
$\tan 3 \theta=\frac{\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta}{1-\tan \theta \tan 2 \theta}$
Since, $\tan 3 \theta=\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta \ldots .$. (as given)
$\Rightarrow \tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta=\frac{\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta}{1-\tan \theta \tan 2 \theta}$
$\Rightarrow(\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta)(1-\tan \theta \tan 2 \theta)-(\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta)=0$
$\Rightarrow(\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta)(1-\tan \theta \tan 2 \theta-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow(\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta)(\tan \theta \tan 2 \theta)=0$
$\Rightarrow(m+n)(m n)=0 \Rightarrow(m+n)=0$
[since, $m \neq 0$ and $n \neq 0$ ]
9. (b) $3(\sin \theta-\cos \theta)^{4}+6(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)^{2}+4 \sin ^{6} \theta$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =3(1-2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)^{2}+6(1+2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)+4 \sin ^{6} \theta \\
& =3\left(1+4 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta-4 \sin \theta \cos \theta\right)+6 \\
& \quad+12 \sin \theta \cos \theta+4 \sin ^{6} \theta \\
& =9+12 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta+4 \sin ^{6} \theta \\
& =9+12 \cos ^{2} \theta\left(1-\cos ^{2} \theta\right)+4\left(1-\cos ^{2} \theta\right)^{3} \\
& =9+12 \cos ^{2} \theta-12 \cos ^{4} \theta \\
& \quad+4\left(1-\cos ^{6} \theta-3 \cos ^{2} \theta+3 \cos ^{4} \theta\right) \\
& =9+4-4 \cos ^{6} \theta \\
& =13-4 \cos ^{6} \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

10. (b) $\because \alpha+\beta$ and $\alpha-\beta$ both are acute angles.
$\cos (\alpha+\beta)=\frac{3}{5}$, then $\sin (\alpha+\beta)=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4}{5}$
$\Rightarrow \tan (\alpha+\beta)=\frac{4}{3}$
and $\sin (\alpha-\beta)=\frac{5}{13}$, then
$\cos (\alpha-\beta)=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^{2}}=\frac{12}{13}$
$\Rightarrow \tan (\alpha-\beta)=\frac{5}{12}$
Now, $\tan 2 \alpha=\tan ((\alpha+\beta)+(\alpha-\beta))$
$=\frac{\tan (\alpha+\beta)+\tan (\alpha-\beta)}{1-\tan (\alpha+\beta) \cdot \tan (\alpha-\beta)}=\frac{\frac{4}{3}+\frac{5}{12}}{1-\frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{5}{12}}=\frac{63}{16}$

=\frac{\tan 30^{\circ}+\tan 45^{\circ}}{1-\tan 30^{\circ} \cdot \tan 45^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}+1}{1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}
$$

## 9. Some Applications of Trigonometry

Foundation
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-256.jpg?height=1686&width=1589&top_left_y=860&top_left_x=196)

## ANGLE OF ELEVATION AND ANGLE OF DEPRESSION

Let an observer at the point O is observing an object at the point P . The line OP is called the LINE OF SIGHT of the point P . Let OA be the horizontal line in the vertical plane passing through OP . If object P be above the horizontal line OA , then the acute angle AOP, between the line of sight and the horizontal line is known as ANGLE OF ELEVATION of object P. If the object P is below the horizontal line OA , then the acute angle AOP , between the line of sight and horizontal line is known as $\mathbf{A N G L E}$ OF DEPRESSION of object P.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-257.jpg?height=255&width=447&top_left_y=639&top_left_x=616)
Fig. (i)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-257.jpg?height=252&width=440&top_left_y=639&top_left_x=1073)
Fig. (ii)

In fig. (i), $\angle \mathrm{AOP}=$ Angle of elevation.
In fig. (ii), $\angle \mathrm{AOP}=$ Angle of depression.

Did You
Know?
Theodolite is an instrument which is used in measuring the angle of elevation and angle of depression.

## TO FIND THE HEIGHT AND THE DISTANCE OF TOWER STANDING ON A HORIZONTAL PLANE

Let AB be a tower and B be its foot. On the horizontal line through B , take two points P and Q . Measure the length PQ .
Let $\mathrm{PQ}=a$.
Let the angles of elevation of the top A of the tower as seen from P and Q be respectively $\alpha$ and $\beta(\beta>\alpha)$, then $\angle \mathrm{APB}=\alpha$, $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=\beta$.
Let $A B=x, B Q=y$.
From right angled $\triangle \mathrm{ABP}, \tan \alpha=\frac{A B}{P B}=\frac{x}{a+y}$
$\therefore a+y=x \cot \alpha$

From right angled $\triangle A B Q, \tan \beta=\frac{A B}{B Q}=\frac{x}{y}$
$\therefore y=x \cot \beta$

From equations (i) and (ii),
$\therefore a=x \cot \alpha-x \cot \beta \Rightarrow x=\frac{a}{\cot \alpha-\cot \beta}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-257.jpg?height=305&width=446&top_left_y=1621&top_left_x=1425)

Also, $y=x \cot \alpha-a$
$\Rightarrow y=\frac{a \cot \alpha}{\cot \alpha-\cot \beta}-a \Rightarrow y=\frac{a \cot \alpha-a(\cot \alpha-\cot \beta)}{\cot \alpha-\cot \beta} \Rightarrow y=\frac{a \cot \beta}{\cot \alpha-\cot \beta}$
In the above case, $P$ and $Q$ are on the same side of the tower.
If the two points are on the opposite sides of the tower then from the adjoining figure, we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \tan \alpha=\frac{x}{P B} \text { or } P B=x \cot \alpha \text { and } \tan \beta=\frac{x}{B Q} \text { or } B Q=x \cot \beta . \\
& \therefore a=P B+B Q=x(\cot \alpha+\cot \beta), \therefore x=\frac{a}{\cot \alpha+\cot \beta} \text { and } y=B Q=x \cot \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-257.jpg?height=309&width=441&top_left_y=2083&top_left_x=1430)

Note : Here, all the lines $A P, A Q, A B$ are in the same plane.

The angle of elevation of a cliff from a fixed point is $\theta$. After going up a distance of $\boldsymbol{k}$ meters towards the top of the cliff at an angle of $\phi$, it is found that the angle of elevation is $\alpha$. Show that the height of the cliff is

$$
\frac{k(\cos \phi-\sin \phi \cot \alpha)}{\cot \theta-\cot \alpha} \text { meters }
$$

## SOLUTION :

$\mathrm{MP}=h$ meters be the height of the cliff
$\angle \mathrm{MAP}=\theta$ is the angle of elevation of the top P as observed from the fixed point A .
$\angle \mathrm{PCD}=\alpha$, where $\mathrm{AC}=k$ meters and $\angle \mathrm{CAB}=\phi$
Draw $\quad \mathrm{CD} \perp \mathrm{MP}$ and $\mathrm{CB} \perp \mathrm{AM}$
P(Top of
Here, $\quad \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{BM}$ and $\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{MD}$.
From, rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, we have
$\frac{B C}{A C}=\sin \phi$ and $\frac{A B}{A C}=\cos \phi \Rightarrow \frac{B C}{k}=\sin \phi$ and $\frac{A B}{k}=\cos \phi$
$\Rightarrow \quad B C=k \sin \phi$ and $A B=k \cos \phi$

From, rt. $\triangle \mathrm{AMP}$,

$$
\frac{M P}{A M}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \frac{h}{A M}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow A M=h \cot \theta
$$

From, rt. $\triangle \mathrm{CDP}$,

$$
\frac{D P}{C D}=\tan \alpha \Rightarrow \tan \alpha=\frac{M P-M D}{B M}=\frac{M P-B C}{A M-A B}
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad \tan \alpha=\frac{h-k \sin \phi}{h \cot \theta-k \cos \phi} \quad$ (From (1), (2) and (3))
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\cot \alpha}=\frac{h-k \sin \phi}{h \cot \theta-k \cos \phi}$
$\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{k(\cos \phi-\cot \alpha \sin \phi)}{\cot \theta-\cot \alpha} \Rightarrow h=\frac{k(\cos \phi-\sin \phi \cot \alpha)}{\cot \theta-\cot \alpha}$.

## CONNECTING TOPIC

Bearing : If the observer and the object are on same plane (level), then bearing is defined. A bearing is an angle , measured clockwise or anticlockwise from the any one of four standard direction East, West, North and South.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-259.jpg?height=648&width=664&top_left_y=529&top_left_x=769)

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

If the bearing from $A$ to $B$ is $E 30^{\circ} \mathrm{S}$, then find the bearing from $B$ to $A$ from North to West direction.

## SOLUTION :

Since, the bearing from A to B is $\mathrm{E} 30^{\circ} \mathrm{S}$ means $30^{\circ}$ from East to South.
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{EAB}=30^{\circ}$
Now, we have to find the bearing from B to A from North to West direction.
i.e. $\angle \mathrm{ABN}=$ ?
Let $\angle \mathrm{ABN}=x$.
$\angle \mathrm{EAB}=\angle \mathrm{ABW}=30^{\circ}$ [Alternate interior angles]
$\because \angle \mathrm{NBA}+\angle \mathrm{ABW}=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore x+30^{\circ}=90^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-259.jpg?height=433&width=627&top_left_y=1370&top_left_x=1244)
$\square \Rightarrow x=90^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$
Thus, the bearing from B to A from North to West direction is $60^{\circ}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-259.jpg?height=58&width=90&top_left_y=1928&top_left_x=1041)

## Learn More

## Compass bearing:

Compass bearing always begin with North ( N ) or South ( S ) in clockwise or anti clockwise direction, which ever is smallest angle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-259.jpg?height=332&width=127&top_left_y=2161&top_left_x=776)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-259.jpg?height=332&width=124&top_left_y=2165&top_left_x=1217)

Two stations due south of a leaning tower which leans towards the north are at distances $\boldsymbol{a}$ metres and $\boldsymbol{b}$ metres from its foot. If $\alpha, \beta$ be the elevations of the top of the tower from these stations, prove that inclination $\theta$ of the leaning tower to the horizontal is given by $\cot \theta=\frac{b \cot \alpha-a \cot \beta}{b-a}$.

## SOLUTION :

MP is the tower which is at a inclination $\theta$ to the horizontal.
Let NP $=h$ meters be the vertical height of the top of the tower from the level ground.
A and B are two stations such that $\mathrm{AM}=a$ metres and $\mathrm{BM}=b$ metres
Let $\mathrm{MN}=x$ metres.
In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{MNP}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{P N}{M N}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \frac{h}{x}=\tan \theta \\
\Rightarrow & x=\frac{h}{\tan \theta} \Rightarrow x=h \cot \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ANP}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{P N}{A N}=\tan \alpha \Rightarrow \frac{h}{x+a}=\tan \alpha \\
\Rightarrow & x+a=h \cot \alpha \Rightarrow x=h \cot \alpha-a
\end{aligned}
$$

In rt. $\triangle B N P$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{P N}{B N}=\tan \beta \Rightarrow \frac{h}{b+x}=\tan \beta \\
\Rightarrow & b+x=\frac{h}{\tan \beta} \Rightarrow x=h \cot \beta-b
\end{aligned}
$$

From (1) and (2),

$$
\begin{aligned}
\quad h \cot \theta=h \cot \alpha-a & \Rightarrow a=h \cot \alpha-h \cot \theta \\
\Rightarrow \quad h(\cot \alpha-\cot \theta)=a & \Rightarrow h=\frac{a}{(\cot \alpha-\cot \theta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-260.jpg?height=714&width=771&top_left_y=888&top_left_x=1009)

From (1) and (3), we get
$h \cot \theta=h \cot \beta-b \Rightarrow b=h \cot \beta-h \cot \theta$
$\Rightarrow h=\frac{b}{\cot \beta-\cot \theta}$

From (4) and (5), we get

$$
\frac{a}{\cot \alpha-\cot \theta}=\frac{b}{\cot \beta-\cot \theta} \Rightarrow a(\cot \beta-\cot \theta)=b(\cot \alpha-\cot \theta) \Rightarrow \cot \theta=\frac{b \cot \alpha-a \cot \beta}{b-a}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-260.jpg?height=200&width=330&top_left_y=1996&top_left_x=194)

If the figure is formed in only one plane is known as two dimensional figure, whereas, if the figure is formed in more than one plane is known as three dimensional figure.
$08=\theta \Leftarrow$ og ur? $=\frac{\varepsilon \rho}{I}=\theta$ ue? $\Leftarrow I=\frac{\varepsilon \sim 8}{t}=\theta$ ue ± $\frac{\partial \mathcal{I}}{\partial \mathcal{V}}=\theta_{\text {ич }}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-261.jpg?height=532&width=37&top_left_y=1999&top_left_x=441)

## Think Out of the Box

Q. 1. A bird is sitting on the top of a vertical pole 20 m high and its elevation from a point O on the ground is $45^{\circ}$. It flies off horizontally straight away from the point O . After one second, the elevaion of the bird from O is reduced to $30^{\circ}$. Then, find the speed (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of bird.
Ans. Speed $=20(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Q. 2. A man is walking towards a vertical pillar in a straight path, at a uniform speed. At a certain point A on the path, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is $30^{\circ}$. After walking for 10 minutes from A in the same direction, at a point B , he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is $60^{\circ}$. Then find the time taken (in minutes) by him, from B to reach the pillar.
Ans. Time taken by $\operatorname{man} \frac{10}{2}=5$ minutes. opposite sides, If $\mathrm{AB}=50$ units, then the height of the tower is:
Q. 3. A tower standing at right angles to the ground subtends angles $\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}$ and $\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$ and at two points A and B situated in a line through the foot of the tower and on the
opposite sides, If $\mathrm{AB}=50$ units, then the height of the tower is:
Ans. Height $=25(\sqrt{2}-1)$ units
Some Applications of Trigonometry

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. The angles of elevation of the top of a tower from the top and the foot of a pole of height $\mathbf{1 0 ~ m}$ are $\mathbf{3 0}^{\circ}$ and $\mathbf{6 0}^{\circ}$ respectively. Find the height of the tower.
Sol. Let AB and CD be the pole and tower respectively.
Let $C D=h \mathrm{~m}$
Then, $\angle \mathrm{DAC}=60^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{DBE}=30^{\circ}$
Now, $\frac{C D}{C A}=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}, \therefore C D=\sqrt{3} C A \Rightarrow \frac{h}{\sqrt{3}}=C A$
Again, $\frac{D E}{B E}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\therefore(h-10)=\frac{B E}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{C A}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h / \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h}{3} \quad[\because B E=C A]$
$\Rightarrow 3 h-30=h \Rightarrow 2 h=30 \Rightarrow h=15$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-263.jpg?height=405&width=563&top_left_y=662&top_left_x=1288)

Hence, height of the tower $=15 \mathrm{~m}$.
2. A man is standing on the deck of a ship, which is $\mathbf{8 ~ m}$ above water level. He observes the angle of elevation of the top of a hill as $60^{\circ}$ and the angle of depression of the base of the hill as $30^{\circ}$. Calculate the distance of the hill from the ship and the height of the hill.
Sol. Let $x m$ be the distance of hill from man and $(h+8) m$ be the height of hill which is required.
In rt. $\triangle A C B$,
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{A C}{B C}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow h=\sqrt{3} x[\because \mathrm{ED}=\mathrm{BC}=x]$
In rt. $\triangle B C D$,
$\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{C D}{B C}=\frac{8}{x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{8}{x} \Rightarrow x=8 \sqrt{3}$
Distance of ship from hill $=x=8 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
∴ Height of hill $=h+8=\sqrt{3} x+8=(\sqrt{3})(8 \sqrt{3})+8=32 \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-263.jpg?height=439&width=368&top_left_y=1199&top_left_x=1503)
3. A vertical tower stands on a horizontal plane and is surmounted by a vertical flag staff of height 5 meters. At point on the plane, the angle of elevation of the bottom and the top of the flag staff are respectively $\mathbf{3 0}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$ and $\mathbf{6 0}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$. Find the height of the tower.
Sol. Let $A B$ be the tower of height $h$ meter and $B C$ be the height of flag staff surmounted on the tower.
Let the point of the plane be $D$ at a distance $x$ meter from the foot of the tower.
In rt. $\triangle A B D$,
$\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{A D} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{3} h$

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$,
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{A C}{A D} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{5+h}{x} \Rightarrow x=\frac{5+h}{\sqrt{3}}$

From (i) and (ii), $\sqrt{3} h=\frac{5+h}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow 3 h=5+h \Rightarrow 2 h=5 \Rightarrow h=\frac{5}{2}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$.
So, the height of tower $=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-263.jpg?height=492&width=444&top_left_y=1731&top_left_x=1434)
4. The angle of elevation of an aeroplane from a point on the ground is $45^{\circ}$. After a flight of 15 sec , the elevation changes to $\mathbf{3 0}^{\circ}$. If the aeroplane is flying at a height of $\mathbf{3 0 0 0}$ metres, find the speed of the aeroplane.
Sol. Let the point on the ground is E which is y metres from point B and let after 15 sec ., flight covers $x$ metres distance
In rt. $\triangle A E B, \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{E B} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{3000}{y} \Rightarrow y=3000 \mathrm{~m}$

In rt. $\triangle C E D, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{C D}{E D} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{3000}{x+y} \quad(\because A B=C D)$
$\Rightarrow x+y=3000 \sqrt{3}$

From eqs. (i) and (ii)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+3000=3000 \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x=3000(\sqrt{3}-1) \Rightarrow x=3000 \times(1.732-1) \\
\Rightarrow & x=3000 \times 0.732 \Rightarrow x=2196 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speed of aeroplane $=\frac{\text { Distance covered }}{\text { Time taken }}$
$=\frac{2196}{15} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}=146.4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}=\frac{2196}{15} \times \frac{18}{5} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=527.04 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.
Hence, the speed of aeroplane is $527.04 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-264.jpg?height=432&width=480&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1302)
5. There is a small island in the middle of a 100 m wide river and a tall tree stands on the island. $P$ and $Q$ are points directly opposite to each other on the two banks, and in line with the tree. If the angles of elevation of the top of the tree from $P$ and $Q$ are respectively $30^{\circ}$ and $45^{\circ}$, find the height of the tree.
Sol. Let $T A$ be the tree.

Let the height of the tree be $h$ metres, i.e., $\quad \mathrm{AT}=h \mathrm{~m}$
In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{QAT}, \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow h=x\left(\because \tan 45^{\circ}=1\right)$
In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{PAT}, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{h}{100-x}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h}{100-x}[\because x=h]$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-264.jpg?height=576&width=691&top_left_y=1005&top_left_x=1123)
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} h=100-h \Rightarrow(\sqrt{3}+1) h=100$
$\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}+1} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{\sqrt{3}-1}=50(\sqrt{3}-1)=36.6 \mathrm{~m}$.
∴ The height of the tree is 36.6 m
6. In figure, ABCD is a rectangle in which segments AP and AQ are drawn as shown. Find the length of $(\mathrm{AP}+\mathrm{AQ})$.

Sol. In rt. $\triangle A D Q$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{A D}{A Q}=\sin 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{A D}{A Q}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow A Q=2 A D \\
\Rightarrow & A Q=2 \times 30=60 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

In rt. $\triangle A B P, \frac{A B}{A P}=\sin 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{A B}{A P}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow A P=2 A B \Rightarrow A P=2 \times 60=120 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore A P+A Q=120+60=180 \mathrm{~cm}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-264.jpg?height=369&width=565&top_left_y=1731&top_left_x=1238)
7. The shadow of a tower, when the angle of elevation of the sun is $\mathbf{4 5}^{\circ}$, is found to be $\mathbf{1 0}$ metres longer than when it is $60^{\circ}$. Find the height of the tower.
Sol. Let length of tower $A B=x \mathrm{~m}$.
Angle of elevation at point $C=60^{\circ}$. Shadow of tower, $B C=y \mathrm{~m}, C D=10 \mathrm{~m}$
Now in rt. $\triangle A B C, \frac{A B}{B C}=\tan 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}$

In rt. $\triangle A B D, \frac{A B}{B D}=\tan 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{10+y}=1 \Rightarrow x=10+y$

Putting the value of $y$ in (ii), we get
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-264.jpg?height=410&width=441&top_left_y=2133&top_left_x=1366)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x=10+\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x=10 \sqrt{3}+x \\
\Rightarrow & x(\sqrt{3}-1)=10 \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x=\frac{10 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}-1} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}+1} \Rightarrow x=\frac{10 \sqrt{3}(\sqrt{3}+1)}{2} \\
\Rightarrow & x=5 \sqrt{3}(\sqrt{3}+1) \Rightarrow x=15+5 \sqrt{3} \\
\Rightarrow & x=15+5(1.73) \Rightarrow x=15+8.65=23.65 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

8. A person observed the angle of elevation of the top of a tower as $30^{\circ}$. He walked 50 m towards the foot of the tower along level ground and found the angle of elevation of the top of the tower as $\mathbf{6 0}^{\circ}$. Find the height of the tower.
Sol. Suppose, height of the tower $A B=x m$ and distance $B C=y \mathrm{~m}$.

In rt. $\triangle A B C, \frac{A B}{B C}=\tan 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}$

In rt. $\triangle A B D, \frac{A B}{D B}=\tan 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{50+y}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x=50+y$

Putting the value of $y$ in (ii), we get, $\sqrt{3} x=50+\frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x \times \sqrt{3}=50 \sqrt{3}+x \Rightarrow 3 x=50 \sqrt{3}+x \Rightarrow x=25 \sqrt{3}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-265.jpg?height=378&width=432&top_left_y=808&top_left_x=1439)

Hence, height of the tower $=25 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.

## Multiple Choice Questions

## DIRECTIONS (Qs. 1-10) : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which only one is correct.

1. From a point on the ground, the angles of elevation of the bottom and the top of a transmission tower fixed at the top of a 20 m high building are $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively. Find the height of the tower.
(a) $20(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{m}$
(b) $20 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(c) 20 m
(d) 40 m
2. From the figure, find the value of $\frac{\tan \theta}{\tan \phi}$ and it is given that ' $D$ ' be the mid point of $B C$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-266.jpg?height=355&width=396&top_left_y=1151&top_left_x=397)
(a) $\frac{3}{4}$
(b) $\frac{1}{2}$
(c) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
(d) $\frac{4}{7}$
3. The angles of elevation of the top of a tower, as seen from two points A and B situated in the same line and at distance $x$ and $y$ respectively. from the foot of the tower, are complementary. Find the height of the tower.
(a) $\sqrt{x+y}$
(b) $\sqrt{x y}$
(c) $x y$
(d) $\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}$
4. A 15 m long ladder placed vertically along a wall broke in such a way that its top touches the ground making an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with it. At what height from the ground did the ladder break?
(a) 7.34 m (b)
(b) 8 m
(c) 9.46 m
(d) 6.96 m
5. A tree 6 m tall casts a 4 cm long shadow. At the same time, a flag pole casts a shadow 50 m long. How long is the flag pole?
(a) 75 m
(b) 100 m
(c) 150 m
(d) 50 m
6. The height of a tower is $h$ and the angle of elevation of the top of the twoer is $\alpha$. On moving a distance $\mathrm{h} / 2$ towards the tower, the angle of elevation becomes $\beta$. What is the value of $\cot \alpha-\cot \beta$ ?
(a) $\frac{1}{2}$
(b) $\frac{2}{3}$
(c) 1
(d) 2
7. A bridge across a river makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the river bank. If the length of the bridge across the river is 150 m , width of the river is
(a) $75 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}$
(b) 150 m
(c) $\frac{75}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$
(d) 75 m
8. A professor standing on one end of a football field observes the elevation of the top of a flood light tower at an angle of $\alpha$. He then walks a distance equal to twice the height of the tower and finds that the elevation of the top is now at an angle of $90^{\circ}-\alpha$. What is the value of $\tan \alpha$ ?
(a) $1-\sqrt{2}$
(b) $\sqrt{2}-1$
(c) $\sqrt{2}$
(d) 1 .
9. An electrician has a repair an electric fault on a pole of height 5 cm . He has to reach a point 1.3 m below the top of the pole to undertake the repair work. What should be the length of the ladder that he should use which, when inclined at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal, would enable him to reach the required position? Also, how far from the foot of the pole should he place the foot of the ladder? [Take $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ ].
(a) $4.28 \mathrm{~m}, 2.14 \mathrm{~m}$
(b) $4.28 \mathrm{~m}, 2.83 \mathrm{~m}$
(c) $4.67 \mathrm{~m}, 2.14 \mathrm{~m}$
(d) $3.67 \mathrm{~m}, 2.14 \mathrm{~m}$
10. An aeroplane when flying at a height of 3125 m from the ground passes vertically below another plane at an instant when the angles of elevation of the two planes from the same point on the ground are $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively. Find the distance between the two planes at that instant.
(a) 3920 m
(b) 6250 m
(c) 5125 m
(d) 4444 m

## Fill in the Blanks

DIRECTIONS : Complete the following statements with an appropriate word / term to be filled in the blank space(s).

1. The $\_\_\_\_$ is the line drawn from the eye of an observer to the point in the object viewed by the observer.
2. The $\_\_\_\_$ of an object viewed, is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when it is above the horizontal level, i.e., the case when we raise our head to look at the object.
3. The $\_\_\_\_$ of an object viewed, is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when it is below the horizontal level, i.e., the case when we lower our head to look at the object.
4. In the adjoining figure, the positions of observer and object are marked. The angle of depression is $\_\_\_\_$

## True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

A straight highway leads to the foot of a tower of height 50 m . From the top of the tower, the angles of depression of two cars standing on the highway are $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$. [ $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ ]

Now, based on the above information, mark the given statements as true or false.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-267.jpg?height=291&width=232&top_left_y=1466&top_left_x=534)

1. Distance between the cars is 57.67 m .
2. First car is at a distance of 38.90 m from the tower.
3. Second car is at a distance of 86.50 m . from the tower.
4. Car at point C is at a distance of 200 m away from the top of the tower.

## Match the Following

DIRECTIONS : Each question contains statements given in two columns which have to be matched. Statements ( $A, B, C, D$ ) in column-I have to be matched with statements ( $p, q, r, s$ ) in column-II.

1. From a window, $h$ metres high above the ground, of a house in a street, the angles of elevation and depression of the top and bottom of another house on the opposite side of the street are $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively, then match the column.

## Column-I

(A) $D B$
(B) $D E$
(C) $C E$
(D) $A D$

## Column-II

(p) $h(1+\tan \alpha \cot \beta)$
(q) $h / \sin \beta$
(r) $h \tan \alpha \cot \beta$
(s) $h \cot \beta$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-267.jpg?height=469&width=401&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1274)

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. The string of a kite is 250 m long it makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Find the height of the kite, assuming that there is no slackness in the string.
2. The horizontal distance between two towers is 140 m . The angle of elevation of the top of the first tower when seen from the top of the second tower is $30^{\circ}$. If the height of the second tower is 60 m , find the height of the first tower.
3. The angle of elevation of the top of a tower, from a point on the ground and at a distance of 30 m from its foot, is $30^{\circ}$. Find the height of the tower.
4. An electric pole is $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$ high. If its shadow is $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$ in length, find the angle of elevation of the sun.
5. The tops of two towers of height $x$ and $y$, standing on level ground, subtend angles of $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively at the centre of the line joining their feet, then find $x: y$
6. In figure, AB is a 6 m high pole and CD is a ladder inclined at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal and reaches up to a point D of pole. If $\mathrm{AD}=2.54 \mathrm{~m}$, find the length of the ladder. (use $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-267.jpg?height=469&width=385&top_left_y=1884&top_left_x=1281)
7. If a tower 30 m high, casts a shadow $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$ long on the ground, then what is the angle of elevation of the sun?

## Short Answer Questions

1. The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point $A$ on the ground is $30^{\circ}$. On moving a distance of 20 metres towards the foot of the tower to a point $B$, the angle of elevation increases to $60^{\circ}$. Find the height of the tower and distance of the tower from the point $A$.
2. An observer 1.5 m tall is 28.5 m away from a chimney. The angle of elevation of the top of the chimney from his eye is $45^{\circ}$. What is the height of the chimney?
3. From a point on the ground, the angles of elevation of the bottom and the top of a transmission tower fixed at the top of a 20 m high building are $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively. Find the height of the tower.
4. Two pillars of equal height stand on either side of a roadway which is 80 m wide. At a point on the road between pillars, the elevations of the pillars are $60^{\circ}$ and $30^{\circ}$. Find the height of the pillars and the position of the point.
5. Two persons are ' $a$ ' metres apart and the height of one is double that of the other. If from the middle point of the line joining their feet, an observer finds the angular elevations of their tops to be complementary, then find the height of the shortest person.
6. The angle of elevation of the top of a building from the foot of the tower is $30^{\circ}$ and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the foot of the building is $45^{\circ}$. If the tower is 30 m high, find the height of the building.
7. A man standing on the deck of a ship, which is 10 m above water level, observes the angle of elevation of the top of a hill as $60^{\circ}$ and the angle of depression of the base of hill as $30^{\circ}$. Find the distance of the hill from the ship and the height of the hill.
8. On a straight line passing through the foot of a tower, two points C and D are at distances of 4 m and 16 m from the foot respectively. If the angles of elevation from C and D of the top of the tower are complementary, then find the height of the tower.

## Long Answer Questions

1. A 1.5 m tall boy is standing at some distance from a 30 m tall building. The angle of elevation from his eyes to the top of the building increases from $30^{\circ}$ to $60^{\circ}$ as he walks towards the building. Find the distance he walked towards the building.
2. The height of a house subtends a right angle at the opposite window. The angle of elevation of the window from the base of the house is $60^{\circ}$. If the width of the road is 6 m , find the height of the house.
3. At a point A, 20 metres above the level of water in a lake, the angle of elevation of a cloud is $30^{\circ}$. The angle of depression of the reflection of the cloud in the lake, at A is $60^{\circ}$. Find the distance of the cloud from A .
4. The angle of elevation of the top Q of a vertical tower PQ from a point X on the ground is $60^{\circ}$. From a point Y , 40 m vertically above $X$, the angle of elevation of the top Q of tower is $45^{\circ}$. Find the height of the tower PQ and the distance PX. (Use $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ ).
5. An aeroplane is flying at a height of 300 m above the ground. Flying at this height, the angles of depression from the aeroplane of two points on both banks of a river in opposite directions are $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively. Find the width of the river. [Use $\sqrt{3}=1.732$ ]
6. As observed from the top of a 100 m high light house from the sea-level, the angles of depression of two ships are $30^{\circ}$ and $45^{\circ}$. If one ship is exactly behind the other on the same side of the light house, find the distance between the two ships.
7. A man in a boat rowing away from a light house 100 m high takes 2 minutes to change the angle of elevation of the top of the light house from $60^{\circ}$ to $30^{\circ}$. Find the speed of the boat in metres per minute.
[Use $\sqrt{3}=1.732$ ]
8. From a point on the ground, the angles of elevation of the bottom and the top of a tower fixed at the top of a 20 m high building are $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively. Find the height of the tower.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. The angle of elevation of the top of a tower at a point is $45^{\circ}$. After moving a distance P m towards the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is found to be $\theta$. Prove that the height of the tower is $\frac{\mathrm{P} \tan \theta}{\tan \theta-1}$
2. A ladder rests against a wall at an angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. Its foot is pulled away from the wall through a distance $a \mathrm{~m}$, so that it slides a distance $b \mathrm{~m}$ down the wall making an angle $\beta$ with the horizontal. Show that $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{\cos \beta-\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha-\sin \beta}$.
3. As observed from the foot of a mountain the angle of elevation of the summit of the mountain is $45^{\circ}$, after ascending 1000 m towards the mountain up a slope of $30^{\circ}$ inclination, the anlge of elevation is observed as $60^{\circ}$. Find the height of the summit of the mountain from the level ground.
4. A window of a house is $h$ metre above the ground. From the window, the angles of elevation and depression of the top and bottom of another house situated on the opposite side of the lane are found to be $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively. Prove that the height of the other house is $h(1+\tan \alpha \cot \beta)$ metre.
5. In figure, height of the platform AB above the base XY is $4 b \mathrm{~m}$. The steps $\mathrm{CD}, \mathrm{EF}, \mathrm{GH}$ and XY are evenly spaced at distance $b \mathrm{~m}$, from each other. Length of $A B=a \mathrm{~m}$. Show that CD = $a+2 b \cot \theta$ and GH = $a+6 b \cot \theta$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-269.jpg?height=373&width=460&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=420)

## HOTS Questions

1. A boy on horizontal plane finds bird flying at a distance of 100 m from him at an elevation of $30^{\circ}$. A girl standing on the roof of 20 metre high building, finds the angle of elevation of the same bird to be $45^{\circ}$. Both the boy and the girl are on opposite sides of the bird. Find the distance of bird from the girl.
2. Two ships are sailing in the sea on the either side of the light house, the angles of the depression of two ships as observed from the top of the light-house are $60^{\circ}$ and $45^{\circ}$ respectively. If the distance between the ships is $200\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$, find the height of the light-house.
3. (i) The angle of elevation of a bird from a point 50 metres above a lake is $30^{\circ}$ and the angle of depression of its reflection in the lake is $60^{\circ}$. Find the height of the bird above the lake.

## Exercise 2 Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. A statue, 1.6 m tall, stands on the top of a pedestal. From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top of the statue is $60^{\circ}$ and from the same point the angle of elevation of the top of the pedestal is $45^{\circ}$. Find the height of the pedestal.
2. The angle of elevation of the top of a building from the foot of the tower is $30^{\circ}$ and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the foot of the building is $60^{\circ}$. If the tower is 50 m high, find the height of the building.
3. A TV tower stands vertically on a bank of a canal. From a point on the other bank directly opposite to the tower, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is $60^{\circ}$. From another point 20 m away from this point on the line joining this point to the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is $30^{\circ}$ (see Fig.). Find the height of the tower and the width of the canal.
(ii) The angle of elevation of a cloud from a point 200 metres above a lake is $30^{\circ}$ and the angle of depression of its reflection in the lake is $60^{\circ}$. Find the height of the cloud.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. An electrician has to repair an electric fault on a pole of height 5 m . He needs to reach a point 1.3 m below the top of the pole to under take the repair work. He place the ladder of length 7.4 m at that position.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-269.jpg?height=330&width=259&top_left_y=708&top_left_x=1329)

Then answer the following questions.
(i) Measure of $\angle \mathrm{C}$ is :
(a) $45^{\circ}$
(b) $60^{\circ}$
(ii) Measure of $\angle \mathrm{B}$ is:
(a) $60^{\circ}$
(b) $30^{\circ}$
(c) $30^{\circ}$
(c) $45^{\circ}$
(d) $90^{\circ}$
(d) $90^{\circ}$
(iii) Distance between foot of ladder and pole is:
(a) 3.7 m
(b)
$3.7 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(c) $3.7 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}$
(d) $7.4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(iv) The value of $\sin \mathrm{B}$ is:
(a) $\frac{1}{2}$
(b) 0
(c) 1
(d) $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
(v) The value of $\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~B}+\sin ^{2} \mathrm{C}$ is
(a) 1
(b) 0
(c) $\frac{1}{2}$
(d) $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
4. A 1.2 m tall girl spots a balloon moving with the wind in a horizontal line at a height of 88.2 m from the ground. The angle of elevation of the balloon from the eyes of the girl at any instant is $60^{\circ}$. After some time, the angle of elevation reduces to $30^{\circ}$ (see fig.) Find the distance travelled by the balloon during the interval.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-269.jpg?height=341&width=560&top_left_y=2211&top_left_x=1197)
5. A straight highway leads to the foot of a tower. A man standing at the top of the tower observes a car at an angle of depression of $30^{\circ}$, which is approaching the foot of the tower with a uniform speed. Six seconds later, the angle of depression of the car is found to be $60^{\circ}$. Find the time taken by the car to reach the foot of the tower from this point.
6. The angles of elevation of the top of a tower from two points at a distance of 4 m and 9 m from the base of the tower and in the same straight line with it are complementary. Prove that the height of the tower is 6 m .

## Exemplar Questions

1. A spherical balloon of radius $r$ subtends an angle $\theta$ at the eye of an observer. If the angle of elevation of its centre is $\phi$, find the height of the centre of the balloon.

## DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.tt

1. In the adjoining figure, the length of $B C$ is
(a) $2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
(b) $3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
(c) $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
(d) 3 cm
2. If the angle of depression of an object from a 75 m high tower is $30^{\circ}$, then the distance of the object from the tower is
(a) $25 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(b) $50 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(c) $75 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(d) 150 m
3. The angles of elevation of the top of a tower from two points at distances $m$ and $n$ metres are complementary. If the two points and the base of the tower are on the same straight line, then the height of the tower is
(a) $\sqrt{m n}$
(b) $m n$
(c) $\frac{m}{n}$
(d) None of these
4. If the height and length of the shadow of a man are the same, then the angle of elevation of the sun is
(a) $45^{\circ}$
(b) $60^{\circ}$
(c) $90^{\circ}$
(d) $120^{\circ}$
5. The height of a tree, if it casts a shadow 15 m long on the level of ground, when the angle of elevation of the sun is $45^{\circ}$, is
(a) 10 m
(b) 14 m
(c) 8 m
(d) 15 m
6. In the given figure, the positions of the observer and the object are mentioned, the angle of depression is
7. From a balloon vertically above a straight road, the angles of depression of two cars at an instant are found to be $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$. If the cars are 100 m apart, find the height of the balloon.
8. The angle of elevation of a cloud from a point $h$ metres above the surface of a lake is $\theta$ and the angle of depression of its reflection in the lake is $\phi$.
Prove that the height of the cloud above the lake is $h\left(\frac{\tan \phi+\tan \theta}{\tan \phi-\tan \theta}\right)$.
9. The lower window of a house is at a height of 2 m above the ground and its upper window is 4 m vertically above the lower window. At certain instant the angles of elevation of a balloon from these windows are observed to be $60^{\circ}$ and $30^{\circ}$, respectively. Find the height of the ballon above the ground.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-270.jpg?height=76&width=95&top_left_y=2556&top_left_x=1822)
10. From the top of a building of height ' $h$ ' meter, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is ' $\alpha$ ' and angle of depression of the foot of the tower is ' $\beta$ '. The height of the tower is
[NTSE]
(a) $\frac{h+(\tan \alpha+\tan \beta)}{\tan \beta}$
(b) $\frac{h(\tan \alpha+\tan \beta)}{\tan \beta}$
(c) $\frac{h \tan (\alpha+\beta)}{\tan \beta}$
(d) $\frac{h+\tan (\alpha+\beta)}{\tan \beta}$

## More Than One Option Correct

## DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.

1. Which of the following is/are correct?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-271.jpg?height=328&width=492&top_left_y=1009&top_left_x=406)
(a) $\theta_{1}$ is the angle of elevation.
(b) $\theta_{2}$ is the angle of depression.
(c) The angle of elevation or depression is always measured from horizontal line through the point of observation.
(d) $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ are always equal.
2. Which of the following statements are correct?
(a) Line of sight is the line drawn from the eyes of the observer to a point in the object where the person is viewing.
(b) Angle of elevation is the angle formed by the line of sight with horizontal through the eyes of observer when the object is above the horizontal level.
(c) Angle of depression is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when the object is below the horizontal level.
(d) None of these

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph (s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage-I

From the top of a tower, the angles of depression of two objects on the same side of the tower are found to be $\alpha$ and $\beta$ where $\alpha>\beta$.

1. If the distance between the objects is ' $p$ ' metres, then the height ' $h$ ' of the tower is
(a) $\frac{p \tan \alpha \tan \beta}{\tan \alpha-\tan \beta}$
(b) $\frac{\tan \alpha \tan \beta}{\tan \alpha-\tan \beta}$
(c) $\frac{p(\tan \alpha-\tan \beta)}{\tan \alpha \tan \beta}$
(d) None of these
2. The height of the tower if $p=150 \mathrm{~m}, \alpha=60^{\circ}$ and $\beta=30^{\circ}$, is
(a) 120 m
(b) 130 m
(c) 140 m
(d) None of these
3. The distance of the extreme object from the top of the tower is
(a) 65 m
(b) 130 m
(c) 260 m
(d) None of these

4. the horizontal distance between $A B$ and $C D$ is
(a) $20 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion :

In the figure,
if $B C=20 \mathrm{~m}$, then
height $A B$ is 11.56 m .

Reason : $\tan \theta=\frac{A B}{B C}=\frac{\text { perpendicular }}{\text { base }}$,
where $\theta$ is the angle $\angle A C B$.
2. Assertion : If the length of shadow of a vertical pole is equal to its height, then the angle of elevation of the sun is $45^{\circ}$.
Reason : According to pythagoras theorem, $h^{2}=l^{2}+b^{2}$, where $h=$ hypotenuse, $l=$ length and $b=$ base

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

1. If the angle of elevation of a cloud from a point P which is 25 m above a lake be $30^{\circ}$ and the angle of depression of reflection of the cloud in the lake from P be $60^{\circ}$, then the height of the cloud (in meters) from the surface of the lake is:
[NTSE]
(a) 60
(b) 50
(c) 45
(d) 42
2. Two poles standing on a horizontal ground are of heights 5 m and 10 m respectively. The line joining their tops makes an angle of $15^{\circ}$ with the ground. Then the distance (in m ) between the poles, is:
[NTSE]
(a) $5(2+\sqrt{3})$
(b) $5(\sqrt{3}+1)$
(c) $\frac{5}{2}(2+\sqrt{3})$
(d) $10(\sqrt{3}-1)$
3. The angle of elevation of the top of a vertical tower standing on a horizontal plane is observed to be $45^{\circ}$ from a point A on the plane. Let B be the point 30 m vertically above the point A . If the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from B be $30^{\circ}$, then the distance (in m ) of the foot of the tower from the point A is:
[NTSE]
(a) $15(3+\sqrt{3})$
(b) $15(5-\sqrt{3})$
(c) $15(3-\sqrt{3)}$
(d) $15(1+\sqrt{3})$

## SOLUTIONS

## (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions)

## Exercise 1

Master Boards

## Multiple Choice Questions

1. (a) Let $A B$ be the building of height 20 m and $B C$ be the transmission tower of height $h$ metres.
In $\triangle O A B, \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{O A} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{20}{O A}$
$\Rightarrow O A=20 \mathrm{~m}$
$\Rightarrow O A=20 \mathrm{~m}$
In $\triangle O A C$,
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{h+20}{20}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h+20}{20}$
$\Rightarrow h=20(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{m}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-273.jpg?height=351&width=330&top_left_y=865&top_left_x=717)
2. (b) $\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{DC}$ given as D is the mid point of BC .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-273.jpg?height=355&width=392&top_left_y=1274&top_left_x=456)

In $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}, \cot \theta=\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{CD}}$

In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \cot \phi=\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{BC}}$
$\therefore \frac{\cot \theta}{\cot \phi}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{CD}}}{\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{BC}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{CD}}}{\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{2 \mathrm{CD}}} \quad[\because 2 \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{BC}]$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\cot \theta}{\cot \phi}=\frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow \frac{\tan \theta}{\tan \phi}=\frac{1}{2}$
3. (b) Let DC be the tower of height ' $h$ ' metres.

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ACD}, \tan \theta=\frac{h}{x}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-273.jpg?height=345&width=350&top_left_y=2193&top_left_x=475)

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{BDC}, \tan (90-\theta)=\frac{h}{y} \Rightarrow \cot \theta=\frac{h}{y}$
Multiplying (i) by (ii), we get
$\tan \theta \times \cot \theta=\frac{h}{x} \times \frac{h}{y} \Rightarrow \tan \theta \times \frac{1}{\tan \theta}=\frac{h^{2}}{x y}$
$\Rightarrow 1=\frac{h^{2}}{x y} \Rightarrow h^{2}=x y \Rightarrow h=\sqrt{x y}$
4. (d)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-273.jpg?height=387&width=424&top_left_y=918&top_left_x=1201)

Let the broken part of the ladder, $\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{x}$ metres
$\therefore \quad$ length of ladder $=\mathrm{AC}+\mathrm{AB}=15 \mathrm{~m}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{AB}=15 \Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=(15-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{m}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,
$\sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{15-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{15-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3} x=30-2 x \Rightarrow x(\sqrt{3}+2)=30$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{30}{\sqrt{3}+2}=\frac{30(2-\sqrt{3})}{(2)^{2}-(\sqrt{3})^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=30(2-\sqrt{3})=8.04 \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, $\mathrm{AB}=15-x=(15-8.04) \mathrm{m}=6.96 \mathrm{~m}$
5. (a) Let $h$ be the length of the pole.

By the given condition $\frac{6}{4}=\frac{h}{50}$
$\Rightarrow h=\frac{6 \times 50}{4}=75 \mathrm{~m}$
6. (a) Here, In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$,
$\cot \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{BD}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2}+\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{h}}$
also, In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,
$\cot \beta=\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{h}}$
Now, $\cot \alpha-\cot \beta$
$=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2}+\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{h}}-\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{h}}=\frac{1}{2}$
Multiplying (i) by (ii), we get
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-273.jpg?height=429&width=332&top_left_y=2023&top_left_x=1544)
7. (a)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-274.jpg?height=410&width=613&top_left_y=406&top_left_x=322)
8. (b) In the following figure,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-274.jpg?height=218&width=248&top_left_y=909&top_left_x=470)

Let $\mathrm{OB}=x \mathrm{~m}$.
$\tan \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~h}+\mathrm{x}}$
$\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\alpha\right)=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\tan \alpha$

From (i) and (ii)
$\tan ^{2} \alpha+2 \tan \alpha-1=0 \quad \therefore \tan \alpha=\sqrt{2}-1$
9. (a) In figure, the electrician is requried to reach the point

B on the pole AD .
So, $\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{AD}-\mathrm{AB}=(5-1.3)$
$=3.7 \mathrm{~m}$
So, $\sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{BD}}{\mathrm{BC}} \Rightarrow \frac{3.7}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{BC}=\frac{3.7^{\prime} 2}{\sqrt{3}}=4.28 \mathrm{~m}$ (Approx.)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-274.jpg?height=350&width=234&top_left_y=1521&top_left_x=751)
i.e., the length of the ladder should be 4.28 m
Now, $\frac{\mathrm{DC}}{\mathrm{BD}}=\cot 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
i.e., $\mathrm{DC}=\frac{3.7}{\sqrt{3}}=2.14 \mathrm{~m}($ Approx $)$
Therefore, he should place the foot of the ladder at a distance of 2.14 m from the pole.
10. (b) Let $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ be the position of the two aeroplanes.
and $\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=x \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{OM}=y \mathrm{~m}$

$$
\frac{y}{3125}=\cot 30^{\circ}=\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{3} \times 3125
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-274.jpg?height=442&width=382&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1224)

Also, $\frac{y}{3125+x}=\cot 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \frac{(3125) \sqrt{3}}{3125+x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow 3125+x=(3125)(3) \Rightarrow x=6250 \mathrm{~m}$.

Very Short Answer Questions

1. Let $K$ be the position of the kite at a height $h$ meters above the ground $O A$.

The length of the string $=O K=250 \mathrm{~m}$ such that $\angle K O A=60^{\circ}$.

In rt. $\triangle K O A$, we have
$\frac{K A}{K O}=\sin 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow K A=K O \sin 60^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow K A=250 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=125 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-274.jpg?height=362&width=195&top_left_y=2140&top_left_x=1612)
2. Let the height of the first tower be $h$ meters (In figure).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-275.jpg?height=321&width=378&top_left_y=422&top_left_x=456)
i.e., $A B=h$ and $A P=h-60$
In rt. $\triangle A C P, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{A P}{C P} \Rightarrow h=60+\frac{140}{\sqrt{3}}=140.83 \mathrm{~m}$
3. $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
4. $45^{\circ}$
5.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-275.jpg?height=246&width=396&top_left_y=995&top_left_x=324)

Given : $\mathrm{BE}=\mathrm{EC}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{ABE}, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{y}{B E} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} B E=y$

In $\triangle \mathrm{DCE}, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{x}{E C} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{x}{B E}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{B E}{\sqrt{3}}=x$
$x: y=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}: \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x: y=1: 3$
[From (i) and (ii)]
6. $\because \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AD}+\mathrm{DB}=6 \mathrm{~m}$

Since, $\mathrm{AD}=2.54 \mathrm{~m}$
So, $2.54 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{DB}=6 \mathrm{~m} \therefore \mathrm{DB}=3.46 \mathrm{~m}$
For the right angled triangle $\mathrm{CBD}, \frac{\mathrm{BD}}{\mathrm{CD}}=\sin 60^{\circ}$
$\square \frac{3.46 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{CD}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{CD}=\frac{2 \times 3.46 \mathrm{~m}}{1.73}(\because \sqrt{3}=1.73)$
Therefore, $\mathrm{CD}=4 \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, the length of the ladder CD is 4 m .
7. Let AB be a tower where $\mathrm{AB}=30 \mathrm{~m}$ and its shadow be BC where $\mathrm{BC}=10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-275.jpg?height=336&width=410&top_left_y=2097&top_left_x=447)

Suppose, the angle of elevation of the sun be $\theta$.
Now, In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,

$$
\tan \theta=\frac{30}{10 \sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3}
$$

So, $\tan \theta=\sqrt{3}=\tan 60^{\circ}$
Therefore, $\theta=60^{\circ}$
Hence, the angle of elevation of the sun be $60^{\circ}$.

## Short Answer Questions

1. Height of tower is 17.32 m and distance of tower from point $A$ is 10 m .
2. Let $C$ be the eye of observer.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-275.jpg?height=377&width=444&top_left_y=852&top_left_x=1254)

Let $A B$ be the chimney.
$\tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A E}{C E}$
$\Rightarrow A E=C E$
Now, $A B=B E+A E=(1.5+28.5) \mathrm{m}=30 \mathrm{~m}$.
3. Let $A B$ be the building of height 20 m and $B C$ be the transmission tower of height $h$ metres.
In rt. $\triangle O A B, \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{O A} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{20}{O A}$
$\Rightarrow O A=20 \mathrm{~m}$
In rt. $\triangle O A C$,
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{h+20}{20}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h+20}{20}$
$\Rightarrow h=20(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{m}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-275.jpg?height=352&width=337&top_left_y=1599&top_left_x=1537)
4. Height of each pillar $=20 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$ and 20 m from the pillar, whose angle of elevation is $60^{\circ}$.
5. In $\triangle \mathrm{CDE}$,
$\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{h}{a / 2}=\frac{2 h}{a}$
$\Rightarrow \cot \theta=\frac{2 h}{a}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-275.jpg?height=330&width=437&top_left_y=2224&top_left_x=1256)
6.

$$
\text { In } \triangle \mathrm{ABE}, \tan \theta=\frac{4 h}{a}
$$

Multiply (i) and (ii), we get

$$
\cot \theta \times \tan \theta=\frac{2 h}{a} \times \frac{4 h}{a}=\frac{8 h^{2}}{a^{2}}=1 \Rightarrow h=\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{2}} .
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-276.jpg?height=332&width=485&top_left_y=612&top_left_x=267)

In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \frac{30}{y}=\tan 45^{\circ}=1 \Rightarrow y=30$
In $\triangle \mathrm{BCD}$
$\frac{x}{y}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow x=\frac{y}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{30}{\sqrt{3}}=10 \sqrt{3}$
7.
∴ Height of building is $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-276.jpg?height=426&width=382&top_left_y=1231&top_left_x=274)

A man is standing on the deck of the ship at point A .
CE be the hill with base at C .
$\angle \mathrm{DAE}=60^{\circ}, \angle \mathrm{CAD}=30^{\circ}$
Now,
$\angle \mathrm{CAD}=\angle \mathrm{ACB}=30^{\circ} \quad$ [Alternate angles]
$\mathrm{AB}=10 \mathrm{~m}$
Let $\mathrm{ED}=h \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{BC}=x \mathrm{~m}$.
In $\triangle \mathrm{EAD}$,

$$
\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{E D}{A D} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow h=x \sqrt{3}
$$

In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{BC}}$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{10}{x} \Rightarrow x=10 \sqrt{3}
$$

So, distance of the hill from the ship $=10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
$h=10 \sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}=30 \mathrm{~m} \quad$ [From (i) and (ii)]
Hence, height of the hill $=h+10=30+10=40 \mathrm{~m}$.
8. Suppose $\mathrm{A} B$ be a tower and there are two points C and D at the distances of 4 m and 16 m from the foot of the tower respectively.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-276.jpg?height=314&width=446&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1192)

Let $\angle \mathrm{ADB}=\theta^{\circ}$
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{ACB}=90^{\circ}-\theta^{\circ}$
Let the height of the tower be $h$.
$\tan \theta=\frac{h}{16}$
$\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{h}{4} \Rightarrow \cot \theta=\frac{h}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\tan \theta}=\frac{h}{4}$
[From (i) and (ii)
$\Rightarrow \frac{h}{4} \times \frac{h}{16}=1 \Rightarrow h^{2}=64$
$\Rightarrow h=8 \mathrm{~m} \quad(\because$ height cannot be negative)
Hence, the height of the tower is 8 m .

## Long Answer Questions

1. Let eye of the boy be at $P, 1.5$ meter above the ground shown by line $A D B$. From point $P$, angle of elevation of top of building is $30^{\circ}$. He moves towards building and comes to a point $D$, where angle of elevation is $60^{\circ}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-276.jpg?height=337&width=525&top_left_y=1537&top_left_x=1155)
$A P=D R=\mathrm{BQ}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$.
So, $A D=P R, D B=R Q$
$C Q=C B-B Q=30-1.5=28.5 \mathrm{~m}$.
Let $A D=x \mathrm{~m}$
In $\triangle P C Q, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{C Q}{P Q}=\frac{28.5}{x+D B}$
In $\triangle R Q C, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{28.5}{R Q}=\frac{28.5}{D B}$
$\Rightarrow D B=28.5 \cot 60^{\circ}=\frac{28.5}{\sqrt{3}}$
So, $x+\frac{28.5}{\sqrt{3}}=28.5 \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x=(28.5) \sqrt{3}-(28.5) \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.
Hence, the distance walked by the boy towards the building $=19 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
2. Let $B A$ be the house and $D$ be the window on opposite side of the other house $C D$ such that, $\angle A D B =90^{\circ}$. Here, $B C$ is width of road.
In $\triangle D B C, \frac{B C}{D C}=60^{\circ}$

$$
D C=6 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}=E B
$$

In $\triangle A D E$,

$$
\angle A D E=\angle A D B-\angle E D B=30^{\circ}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-277.jpg?height=396&width=260&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=794)
$\therefore \frac{E D}{A E}=\cot 30^{\circ}$

$$
A E=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}
$$

From equations (i) and (ii), we get
$A B=A E+E B=6 \sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{3}=8 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, height of the house is $8 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
3.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-277.jpg?height=406&width=558&top_left_y=1018&top_left_x=324)

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\because \quad \frac{h}{x} & =\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
& x=\sqrt{3} h .
\end{aligned}
$$

Now In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}, \frac{40+h}{x}=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$

$$
x=\frac{40+h^{x}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

So, $\sqrt{3} h=\frac{40+h}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \therefore h=20 \mathrm{~m}$.
Therefore, $x=20 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, the distance of the cloud from A
i.e., $\mathrm{AC}=\sqrt{(20)^{2}+(20 \sqrt{3})^{2}}=40 \mathrm{~m}$
4. Suppose the height of the tower PQ be $h \mathrm{~m}$ and the distance PX be $x \mathrm{~m}$.
Since, $\mathrm{YX}=\mathrm{PM}=40 \mathrm{~m}$,
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{QM}=(h-40) \mathrm{m}$
In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{QPX}$,
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{h}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h}{x}$
$\Rightarrow h=\sqrt{3} x$

In rt. $\Delta \mathrm{QMY}$,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-277.jpg?height=424&width=314&top_left_y=2023&top_left_x=735)

$$
\tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{h-40}{x} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{h-40}{x} \Rightarrow h=x+40 \ldots(\mathrm{ii})
$$

From (i) and (ii), we get ; $\sqrt{3} x=x+40$
$\Rightarrow \quad x(\sqrt{3}-1)=40 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x(1.73-1)=40$
$\Rightarrow 0.73 x=40 \Rightarrow x=54.79 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \quad h=54.79+40=94.79 \mathrm{~m} \quad$ [from (ii)]
Hence, the height of the tower is 94.79 m and the distance PX is 54.79 m .
5.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-277.jpg?height=250&width=565&top_left_y=667&top_left_x=1146)

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{BDC}$,
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{300}{\mathrm{BD}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{300}{\mathrm{BD}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{BD}=\frac{300 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3} \sqrt{3}}$

$$
=100 \sqrt{3}=100 \times 1.732=173.2 \mathrm{~m}
$$

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}$,
$\tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{300}{\mathrm{AD}}$
$\Rightarrow 1=\frac{300}{\mathrm{AD}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AD}=300 \mathrm{~m}$
Therefore, width of the river $=\mathrm{BD}+\mathrm{AD}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =173.2+300 \\
& =473.20 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the width of the river is 473.20 m .
6.
(light house)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-277.jpg?height=374&width=520&top_left_y=1589&top_left_x=1315)

The height of the light house $A B=100 \mathrm{~m}$
Here $\angle \mathrm{ADB}=45^{\circ}$ and $\angle A C B=30^{\circ}$ (Given)
Now, In right $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$,
$\frac{A B}{B D}=\tan 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow x=100$
also, In right $\triangle A B C$,
$\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B C}=\frac{100}{x+y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{100}{100+y}$
$\Rightarrow 1.732 \times 100=100+y$
$\Rightarrow y=173.2-100=73.2 \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, the distance between two ships $C D=73.2 \mathrm{~m}$
7.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-278.jpg?height=268&width=408&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=319)

Given, height of the light house is 100 m .
According to the question the man takes 2 minutes to rowing the boat from C to D .
Let $\mathrm{AC}=x_{1}$ meter and $\mathrm{CD}=x_{2}$ meter
In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,

$$
\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{100}{x_{1}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{100}{x_{1}} \Rightarrow x_{1}=\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$,

$$
\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{100}{\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{100}{\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow x_{1}+x_{2}=100 \sqrt{3}
$$

Using equation (i) in above equation,

$$
\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}+x_{2}=100 \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x_{2}=100 \sqrt{3}-\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \text { or, } x_{2}=\frac{200}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Since, speed $=\frac{\text { distance }}{\text { time }}=\frac{x_{2}}{2}=\frac{200}{2 \sqrt{3}}$

$$
\text { ∴ speed }=\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{100}{1.732}=57.737
$$

Therefore, the speed of the boat is 57.737 meters per minute.
8. AB be the building of height 20 m

Let height of tower AC is $h \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-278.jpg?height=369&width=454&top_left_y=1681&top_left_x=367)

In right $\triangle \mathrm{APB}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{~PB}} \quad\left[\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{~B}}\right] \\
& 1=\frac{20}{\mathrm{~PB}} \\
& \mathrm{~PB}=20 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

In right $\triangle \mathrm{PCB}$

$$
\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{~PB}}, \quad \sqrt{3}=\frac{20+h}{20}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 20 \sqrt{3}=20+h \\
& h=20 \sqrt{3}-20=20(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Note

The angle of elevation of a point viewed, is the angle formed by the line of sight with horizontal when it is above horizontal level.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Let $A B=h \mathrm{~m}$, height of tower. and $B C=x \mathrm{~m} ; C D=P \mathrm{~m}$.
In rt. $\triangle A B C$,
$\tan \theta=\frac{A B}{B C}=\frac{h}{x}$

$$
h=x \tan \theta
$$

In rt. $\triangle A B D$,
$\tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B D} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{h}{x+P}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-278.jpg?height=362&width=378&top_left_y=861&top_left_x=1434)

$$
\Rightarrow x+P=h \Rightarrow x=h-P
$$

from (i) and (ii)

$$
h=(h-P) \tan \theta \Rightarrow h=\frac{P \tan \theta}{\tan \theta-1}
$$

2. $O P=l \mathrm{~m}$ be the length of the ladder.
$\angle P O Q=\alpha$
$\frac{P Q}{l}=\sin \alpha \Rightarrow P Q=l \sin \alpha$
$\frac{O Q}{l}=\cos \alpha \Rightarrow O Q=l \cos \alpha$
Now, $O A=a \mathrm{~m}$ and $P B=b \mathrm{~m}$
Then, $A Q=a+l \cos \alpha$
and $B Q=l \sin \alpha-b$

$$
A B=O P=l .
$$

In rt. $\triangle A Q P$,
$\frac{B Q}{l}=\sin \beta \Rightarrow \frac{l \sin \alpha-b}{l}=\sin \beta$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-278.jpg?height=472&width=588&top_left_y=2053&top_left_x=1123)
$\Rightarrow \sin \alpha-\frac{b}{l}=\sin \beta \quad \Rightarrow \frac{b}{l}=\sin \alpha-\sin \beta$
$\Rightarrow b=l(\sin \alpha-\sin \beta)$
$\frac{A Q}{l}=\cos \beta \quad \Rightarrow \frac{a+l \cos \alpha}{l}=\cos \beta$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{l}+\cos \alpha=\cos \beta \quad \Rightarrow \frac{a}{l}=\cos \beta-\cos \alpha$
$\Rightarrow a=l(\cos \beta-\cos \alpha)$

Dividing (ii) by (i), we get
$\frac{a}{b}=\frac{l(\cos \beta-\cos \alpha)}{l(\sin \alpha-\sin \beta)}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{\cos \beta-\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha-\sin \beta}$.
3. Let $O P=h \mathrm{~m}$ height of mountain.
$A C=1000 \mathrm{~m}$ and $C D=O B=x \mathrm{~m}$.
In rt. $\triangle A C B$
$\sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{B C}{A C} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{B C}{1000}$
$B C=500=O D$
$\cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{A C}$
$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{A B}{1000}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-279.jpg?height=284&width=547&top_left_y=1176&top_left_x=502)
$A B=500 \sqrt{3}$.
In rt. $\triangle P C D ; \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{P D}{C D} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h-500}{x}$
$x=\frac{h-500}{\sqrt{3}}$

In rt. $\triangle A O P ; \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{O P}{O A} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{h}{500 \sqrt{3}+x}$
$500 \sqrt{3}+x=h$
from (i) and (ii)
$h=1366$
Thus, height of mountain $=1366 \mathrm{~m}$ (approx.)
4. Let $A B=H \mathrm{~m}$, the height of another house.
$Q C \perp A B, P Q=B C=h \mathrm{~m}$
In rt. $\triangle A Q C$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-279.jpg?height=453&width=384&top_left_y=2101&top_left_x=459)
$\tan \alpha=\frac{A C}{Q C} \Rightarrow A C=Q C \tan \alpha$

In rt. $\triangle Q C B ; \cot \beta=\frac{Q C}{B C}$
$Q C=B C \cot \beta=h \cot \beta$
from (i) and (ii)
$A C=h \tan \alpha . \cot \beta$.
Height of another house $=A B=A C+B C$
$=h(1+\tan \alpha \cdot \cot \beta) \mathrm{m}$.
5. $\therefore \angle A C P=\angle A X Y=\theta$ [corresponding]
$\angle B D Q=\angle B Y X=\theta ; \angle A G R=\angle B S H=\theta$,
$C P=Q D$ and $G R=S H$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-279.jpg?height=351&width=463&top_left_y=1016&top_left_x=1242)

In rt. $\triangle A C P, \cot \theta=\frac{C P}{A P}=\frac{C P}{b}$
$C P=b \cot \theta=Q D$
$\because C D=C P+P Q=Q D=b \cot \theta+a+b \cot \theta$
$=a+2 b \cot \theta$
In rt. $\triangle A G R, \cot \theta=\frac{G R}{A R}=\frac{G R}{3 b}$
$G R=3 b \cot \theta=S H$
$\because G H=G R+R S+S H=9+6 b \cot \theta$.

## HOTS Questions

1. Let $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{B}$ and A be the positions of the bird, boy and girl respectively.
Given, $\mathrm{PB}=100 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{AC}=20 \mathrm{~m}$. Let $\mathrm{PM}=x \mathrm{~m}$.
In right-angled $\triangle \mathrm{PBO}$, we have,
$\sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{OP}}{\mathrm{BP}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-279.jpg?height=296&width=547&top_left_y=2231&top_left_x=1201)
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{OP}}{100} \Rightarrow \mathrm{OP}=\frac{100}{2}=50 \mathrm{~m}$
Now, $\mathrm{OP}=\mathrm{OM}+\mathrm{MP}$
$\Rightarrow 50=20+x[\because O M=A C=20 m]$
$\Rightarrow x=30 \mathrm{~m}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{PMA}, \sin 45^{\circ}=\frac{x}{P A} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{30}{P A}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{PA}=30 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}=30 \times 1.41=42.3 \mathrm{~m}$.
Hence, required distance is 42.3 m .
2. Let height of the light house $\mathrm{AB}=h \mathrm{~m}$. Let C and D be the position of two ships, such that $\mathrm{CD}=200\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-280.jpg?height=419&width=433&top_left_y=1192&top_left_x=376)

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{C B}$ or $\sqrt{3}=\frac{h}{\mathrm{CB}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{CB}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{3}}$.

In rt. $\triangle \mathrm{ABD}, \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B D}$ or $1=\frac{h}{B D}$
$\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{BD}$.

Now, $\mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{CB}+\mathrm{BD}$
By eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$C D=\frac{h}{\sqrt{3}}+h \Rightarrow 200\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=h\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}+1\right)$
$\Rightarrow 200\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\mathrm{h}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}+1\right)$
$\Rightarrow h=200$ metres
3. (i) 100 m (ii) 400 m
[Hint. (i) Let $A$ be the reflection of the bird $B$ in the lake, then $C B=C A=h$ metres (say)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-280.jpg?height=472&width=311&top_left_y=543&top_left_x=1263)
$\therefore N B=(h-50) \mathrm{m}$ and $A N=(h+50) \mathrm{m}$.

Let ON be $d$ metres.
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{h+50}{d} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h+50}{d}$ and
$\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{h-50}{d} \Rightarrow d=(h-50) \sqrt{3}$
Thus, $3=\frac{h+50}{h-50} \Rightarrow h=100 \mathrm{~m}$ ]
Case Study Based MCQs

1. $\mathrm{BD}=5-1.3=3.7 \mathrm{~m}$
(i) (c) $\quad \sin \mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{BD}}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{3.7}{7.4}=\frac{1}{2}$

$$
\sin \mathrm{C}=\sin 30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=30^{\circ}
$$

(ii) (a) $\mathrm{D}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=180^{\circ}$
$90^{\circ}+\mathrm{B}+30^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$
B $=60^{\circ}$
(iii) (b) $\quad \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{BD}}{\mathrm{CD}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{3.7}{\mathrm{CD}}$

$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{CD}=3.7 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}
$$

(iv) (d) $\sin \mathrm{B}=\sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
(v) (a) $\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~B}+\sin ^{2} \mathrm{C}=\sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}+\sin ^{2} 30^{\circ}$
$=\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}=1$

## Exercise 2 <br> Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. Let $\mathrm{PQ}=h$ metres be the height of the pedestal.
$\mathrm{PR}=1.6$ metres is the height of the statue.
$\angle \mathrm{POQ}=45^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ROQ}=60^{\circ}$
From $\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}, \quad \frac{\mathrm{PQ}}{\mathrm{OQ}}=\tan 45^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{OQ}=h$ metres
From $\triangle \mathrm{ORQ}, \frac{\mathrm{RQ}}{\mathrm{OQ}}=\tan 60^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-281.jpg?height=679&width=675&top_left_y=973&top_left_x=315)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow \quad h & =\frac{1.6}{\sqrt{3}-1}=\frac{1.6 \times(\sqrt{3}+1)}{(\sqrt{3}-1)(\sqrt{3}+1)} \\
& =0.8 \times(\sqrt{3}+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $h=\frac{4}{5}(\sqrt{3}+1) \mathrm{m}$ is the height of the pedestal.
2. Let $\mathrm{PQ}=50$ metres is the height of the tower and $\mathrm{AB}=h$ metres be the height of the building. Angle of elevation of the top of the building from the foot of the

From $\triangle \mathrm{AQB}$,
$\frac{h}{\mathrm{BQ}}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{BQ}=h \sqrt{3}$
..(i) ⇒ $\mathrm{BQ}=\frac{50}{\sqrt{3}}$ tower $=30^{\circ}$, i.e., $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=30^{\circ}$.

Angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the foot of the building $=60^{\circ}$, i.e., $\angle \mathrm{PBQ}=60^{\circ}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-281.jpg?height=627&width=574&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1190)

From (i) and (ii), we have $h \sqrt{3}=\frac{50}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow h=16 \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
3. Let $\mathrm{PQ}=h$ metres be the height of the tower and $\mathrm{BQ}=x$ metres be the width of the canal such that $\angle \mathrm{PBQ}=60^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-281.jpg?height=423&width=700&top_left_y=1311&top_left_x=1123)

Now, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the point $\mathrm{A}=30^{\circ}$,
i.e., $\angle \mathrm{PAQ}=30^{\circ}$, where $\mathrm{AB}=20$ metres.
From $\triangle \mathrm{PBQ}, \frac{h}{x}=\tan 60^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow h=x \sqrt{3}$

From $\triangle \mathrm{PAQ}, \quad \frac{h}{20+x}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\frac{20+x}{\sqrt{3}}$

From (i) and (ii), we have
$\mathrm{x}=10 \mathrm{~m}$
From (i), $h=10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, the height of the tower is $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$ and the width of the canal is 10 m .
4. In $\triangle \mathrm{EOA}, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{AO}}{\mathrm{EA}}$

$$
\sqrt{3}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow h=\sqrt{3} x
$$

In $\triangle \mathrm{E} O^{\prime} B$,
$\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{O^{\prime} B}{E B}=\frac{h}{x+y}$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h}{x+y}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow x+y=h \sqrt{3}
$$

From (i), $h=\sqrt{3} x \Rightarrow 87=\sqrt{3} x[\because h=88.2-1.2]$

$$
\Rightarrow x=\frac{87 \sqrt{3}}{3}=29 \sqrt{3}
$$

From (ii), $y=h \sqrt{3}-x=87 \sqrt{3}-29 \sqrt{3}=58 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$.
5. Let $P Q=h$ metres be the height of the tower. $P$ is the top of the tower. $P X$ is horizontal line through $P$. The first and second position of the car are at $A$ and $B$, respectively.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-282.jpg?height=421&width=613&top_left_y=1402&top_left_x=285)
$\angle A P X=30^{\circ}$
(Angle of depression of the car when observed at $A$ ) and $\angle B P X=60^{\circ}$
(Angle of depression of the car when observed at $B$ )
Then, $\angle P A Q=30^{\circ}$ and $\angle P B Q=60^{\circ}$
Let the speed of the car be $x \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{second}$
Then, distance $A B=6 x$ metres.

Let the time taken from $B$ to $Q$ be $n$ seconds.
Then, distance $B Q=n x$ metres

In right $\triangle \mathrm{PAQ}, \quad \frac{h}{6 x+n x}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{(n+6) x}{\sqrt{3}} .
$$

In right $\triangle P B Q, \frac{h}{n x}=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad h=n x \sqrt{3}
$$

From (i) and (ii), we have

$$
\frac{(n+6)}{\sqrt{3}} x=n x \sqrt{3}
$$

$\Rightarrow n+6=n \sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow n=3$.

Hence, the time taken by the car from $B$ to $Q=3$ seconds.
6. Let $P Q=h \mathrm{~m}$ be the height of the tower
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-282.jpg?height=415&width=652&top_left_y=1155&top_left_x=1091)
$A Q=4 \mathrm{~m}$ and $\angle P A Q=\theta, B Q=9 \mathrm{~m}$
and $\angle P B Q=\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right.$, (i.e., complementary angle of $\left.\theta\right)$

In right triangle $P A Q$,

$$
\frac{h}{4}=\tan \theta .
$$

In right triangle $P B Q$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{h}{9} & =\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\cot \theta \\
\Rightarrow \frac{h}{9} & =\frac{1}{\tan \theta} .
\end{aligned}
$$

From (i) and (ii),

$$
\frac{h}{4} \times \frac{h}{9}=\tan \theta \times \frac{1}{\tan \theta}=1 \Rightarrow h=6
$$

Hence, the height of the tower is 6 m .

## Exemplar Questions

1. In fig. O is the centre of balloon, whose radius $O P=r$ and $\angle P A Q=\theta$. Also, $\angle O A B=\phi$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-283.jpg?height=481&width=643&top_left_y=536&top_left_x=328)

Let the height of the centre of the balloon be $h \mathrm{~m}$.
Thus $O B=h \mathrm{~m}$.
Now, from rt. $\triangle O A P, \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{r}{d}$, where $O A=d$

Also, from $\triangle O A B, \sin \phi=\frac{h}{d}$.

From (i) and (ii), we get $\frac{\sin \phi}{\sin \frac{\theta}{2}}=\frac{\frac{h}{d}}{\frac{r}{d}}=\frac{h}{r}$
or $h=r \sin \phi \operatorname{cosec} \frac{\theta}{2}$.
2. Let the height of the balloon at P be $h$ meters (see Fig.) Let $A$ and $B$ be the two cars. Thus, $A B=100 \mathrm{~m}$. From $\triangle P A Q, A Q=P Q=h$

$$
\left[\because \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{P Q}{A Q}\right]
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-283.jpg?height=563&width=556&top_left_y=1790&top_left_x=372)

Now, from $\triangle P B Q, \frac{P Q}{B Q}=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{ }$ or $\frac{h}{h-100}=\sqrt{3}$ or $\quad h=\sqrt{3}(h-100)$

Therefore, $h=\frac{100 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}-1}=50(3+\sqrt{3})$
i.e., the height of the balloon is $50(3+\sqrt{3}) \mathrm{m}$.
3. Let $P$ be the cloud and Q be its reflection in the lake (see Fig.). Let $A$ be the point of observation such that $A B=h \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-283.jpg?height=506&width=670&top_left_y=685&top_left_x=1137)

Let the height of the cloud above the lake be $x \mathrm{~m}$.
Let $A L=d \mathrm{~m}$.
Now, from $\triangle P A L, \frac{x-h}{d}=\tan \theta$

From $\triangle Q A L, \frac{x+h}{d}=\tan \phi$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$
\frac{x+h}{x-h}=\frac{\tan \phi}{\tan \theta}
$$

(Using componendo \& Dividendo rule)
or $\frac{2 x}{2 h}=\frac{\tan \phi+\tan \theta}{\tan \phi-\tan \theta}$
Therefore, $x=h\left(\frac{\tan \phi+\tan \theta}{\tan \phi-\tan \theta}\right)$.
4. $\quad P D$ is height of ballon.

Let $P F=h \mathrm{~m}$ and $A F=B E=x \mathrm{~m}$.
$A B=E F=4 \mathrm{~m}$
$B C=E D=2 \mathrm{~m}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-283.jpg?height=414&width=337&top_left_y=2019&top_left_x=1306)

In rt. $\triangle A P F ; \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{P F}{A F}$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{3} h
$$

In rt. $\triangle P B E ; \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{P E}{B E} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{h+4}{x}$
$\Rightarrow h+4=x \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow h+4=3 h$ [from (i)]
$h=2 \mathrm{~m}$.
Thus height of ballon $=2+4+2=8 \mathrm{~m}$.
