$\Rightarrow y+20=3 \mathrm{y} \Rightarrow 2 y=20 \Rightarrow y=10 \mathrm{~m}$
8. (b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-284.jpg?height=281&width=337&top_left_y=1695&top_left_x=1151)

Taking $\triangle A R C$ and $\triangle B Q C$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-284.jpg?height=250&width=743&top_left_y=2055&top_left_x=1046)
$\tan (90-\theta)=\cot \theta$
$\frac{20}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{45}($ let $\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{AB}=x \mathrm{~m})$
$x^{2}=900 \Rightarrow x=30$

Now, in $\triangle A R D$ and $B Q D$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{45}{A D}=\frac{20}{B D}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \frac{A D}{B D}=\frac{9}{4} \\
& A D=\frac{9}{13} \times 30=20\left(\frac{10}{13}\right) \mathrm{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

9. (a) Let $P Q R S$ be the rectangular field with length $l$ and width $b$ and $R T$ be the vertical pole.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-285.jpg?height=355&width=316&top_left_y=781&top_left_x=493)

In $\triangle P R T, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{PR}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{10}{\mathrm{PR}}$
$P R=\sqrt{l^{2}+b^{2}}=10 \sqrt{3}$

$$
l^{2}+b^{2}=100 \times 3=300
$$

In $\triangle S R T, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{10}{S R} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{10}{S R}$

$$
S R=\frac{10}{\sqrt{3}}=l \Rightarrow l^{2}=\frac{100}{3}
$$

Use equation (ii) in (i), then
$b^{2}=300-\frac{100}{3}=\frac{800}{3}$
$l^{2} b^{2}=\frac{100}{3} \times \frac{800}{3}=\frac{80000}{9} \Rightarrow l b=\frac{200 \sqrt{2}}{3}$
Hence, the required area of rectangular field is $\frac{200 \sqrt{2}}{3}$.
10. (b) In figure,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-285.jpg?height=447&width=287&top_left_y=1950&top_left_x=767)
$\tan \alpha=\frac{y}{x}$ and
$\tan \beta=\frac{h}{x}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan \beta}=\frac{y}{h} \\
& h+y=h\left(1+\frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan \beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

## Passage Based Questions

1. (a) Height of the tower $(A B)=h \mathrm{~m}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-285.jpg?height=483&width=524&top_left_y=717&top_left_x=1210)
distance $(C D)=p \mathrm{~m}$
Let distance $(B C)=x \mathrm{~m}$
$\angle A C B=\alpha$ and $\angle A D B=\beta$
In right $\triangle A B C, \frac{A B}{B C}=\tan \alpha$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{h}{x}=\tan \alpha \\
& \Rightarrow \quad h=x \tan \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

In right $\triangle A B D, \frac{A B}{B D}=\tan \beta$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{h}{B C+C D}=\tan \beta \Rightarrow h=(x+p) \tan \beta
$$

From (i), we get $x=\frac{h}{\tan \alpha}$
Hence, $h=\frac{p \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot \tan \beta}{\tan \alpha-\tan \beta}$
2. (b) Putting $p=150 \mathrm{~m}, \alpha=60^{\circ}$ and $\beta=30^{\circ}$, we get

$$
h=\frac{150 \times \tan 30^{\circ} \times \tan 60^{\circ}}{\tan 60^{\circ}-\tan 30^{\circ}}=129.9 \mathrm{~m}
$$

Hence, the height of the tower $=129.9 \mathrm{~m}=130 \mathrm{~m}$.
3. (c) $\sin \beta=\frac{h}{y} \Rightarrow y=\frac{h}{\sin \beta}=\frac{130}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=260 \mathrm{~m}$.
4. (a) $B D=\frac{A B}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{60}{\sqrt{3}}=20 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
5. (c) $C D=E B=40 \mathrm{~m} . \quad[\because \mathrm{EB}=60-20]$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-286.jpg?height=458&width=291&top_left_y=422&top_left_x=447)
(Sol. 6-8) : Let AB and CD be two pillars of equal height $x$ metre.
Let E be the point on road such that $\mathrm{BE}=y$ metre and $\mathrm{DE}=(100-\mathrm{y})$ metre
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-286.jpg?height=333&width=414&top_left_y=1082&top_left_x=443)

In $\triangle \mathrm{ABE}, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{x}{y} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{3} y$

In $\Delta \mathrm{CDE}, \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{x}{100-y} \Rightarrow x=\frac{100-y}{\sqrt{3}}$

From (i) and (ii)
$y=25$ and $x=\sqrt{3}(25)=43.3 \mathrm{~m}$
6. (b)
7. (a)
8. (c)

## Assertion \& Reason

1. (a) Both the assertion and reason are correct, reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B C}=\frac{A B}{20} \\
& A B=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \times 20=\frac{20}{1.73}=11.56 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Multiple Matching Questions

1. (A) $\rightarrow q ;(\mathrm{B}) \rightarrow s ;(\mathrm{C}) \rightarrow p ;(\mathrm{D}) \rightarrow r$
(A) $\tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B C} \Rightarrow B C=10$
(B) $\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B C}=\frac{A B}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow A B=\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}=3$
(C) $\cos \theta=\frac{20}{40}=\frac{1}{2}=\cos 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}$
(D) In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{A B}{B C} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=2$, and $\tan \theta=\frac{A B}{B D}=\frac{2}{10}=\frac{1}{5}$.

## Exercise 4 Foundation Builder +

1. (b) Let height of the cloud from the surface of the lake be $h$ meters.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-286.jpg?height=408&width=565&top_left_y=847&top_left_x=1133)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{h-25}{P R} \\
\therefore \quad & P R=(h-25) \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

and in $\triangle P R S: \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{h+25}{P R}$

$$
P R=\frac{h+25}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Then, from eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (h-25) \sqrt{3}=\frac{h+25}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\therefore \quad & h=50 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

2. (a)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-286.jpg?height=394&width=661&top_left_y=1861&top_left_x=1089)

3. (a) Let the height of the tower be $h$ and distance of the foot of the tower from the point $A$ is $d$.
By the diagram,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-287.jpg?height=396&width=355&top_left_y=507&top_left_x=475)

## 10. Circles

Foundation

## (CONCEPT MAP)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-288.jpg?height=1702&width=1385&top_left_y=849&top_left_x=411)

## CIRCLE

A circle is the set of all points in a plane at a fixed distance from a fixed point in the plane. The fixed point is called the centre of the circle. The fixed distance is called the radius of the circle.

Usually a circle is named by its centre and radius. A circle with centre ' $O$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' is named as circle ( $O, r$ ).
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-289.jpg?height=241&width=245&top_left_y=456&top_left_x=1626)

OR
A circle is the locus of a point which moves in a plane in such a way that its distance from a given fixed point in the plane is always constant.

## CONGRUENT AND CONCENTRIC CIRCLES

We say that two circles are congruent if they have the same radius.
Two or more circles with the same centre are called concentric circles.
In the figure, circles $(O, p),(O, q),(O, r)$ are three concentric circles.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-289.jpg?height=332&width=342&top_left_y=918&top_left_x=1507)

## CYCLIC QUADRILATERAL

If all vertices of a quadrilateral lie on a circle, then it is called a cyclic quadrilateral.
Remark : Sum of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is $180^{\circ}$.
In cyclic quadrilateral $A B C D, \angle A+\angle C=180^{\circ}$ and $\angle B+\angle D=180^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-289.jpg?height=61&width=113&top_left_y=1484&top_left_x=1018)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-289.jpg?height=236&width=241&top_left_y=1302&top_left_x=1603)

## Learn More

An exterior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is equal to its opposite interior angle. In cyclic quadrilateral $A B C D, \angle C B E=\angle A D C$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-289.jpg?height=346&width=460&top_left_y=1644&top_left_x=831)

## TERMS RELATED TO A CIRCLE

- A line segment that connects two distinct points on a circle is called a chord.
- A chord that passes through the centre of a circle is called a diameter. Diameter is the longest chord of a circle.
- A line that intersects the circle at exactly two points is called a secant (line).
- A tangent to a circle is a straight line which touches the circle at only one point. The point where the tangent touches the circle is called the point of contact of the tangent to the circle. In the figure, $P$ is the point of contact of the tangent to the circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-289.jpg?height=387&width=467&top_left_y=2142&top_left_x=1377)


## Learn More

Segments : The chord of a circle divide it into two parts. Each part is called a segment. Smaller part is called minor segment and larger part is called major segment.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-290.jpg?height=332&width=319&top_left_y=429&top_left_x=1466)

Alternate segment and its angle: The segment of a circle opposite to the angle formed by a tangent and a chord through the point of contact is called the alternate segment for that angle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-290.jpg?height=254&width=332&top_left_y=772&top_left_x=1462)

Theorem: An angle between a tangent and a chord through the point of contact is equal to the angle in the alternate segment.
In the figure, $\angle \mathrm{APC}=\angle \mathrm{CDP}$ and $\angle \mathrm{BPD}=\angle \mathrm{DCP}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-290.jpg?height=410&width=259&top_left_y=1046&top_left_x=1535)

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge
In the given figure, two circles $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ intersect at $S$ and $Q . P Q N$ and $R Q M$ are tangents drawn to $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ respectively at $Q . M A B$ and $A B N$ are the chords of the circles $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$. If $\angle N Q R=85^{\circ}$, then find $\angle A Q B$.

## SOLUTION

It is given that $\angle N Q R=85^{\circ}$
But $\angle N Q R=\angle M Q P$ ( ∵ Vertically opposite angles)
$\therefore \angle M Q P=85^{\circ}$
By alternate segment theorem, $\angle N Q R=\angle Q A B$
$\therefore \angle Q A B=85^{\circ}$ and $\angle M Q P=\angle Q B A$
$\therefore \angle Q B A=85^{\circ}$
In $\triangle A Q B, \angle A Q B=180^{\circ}-\left(85^{\circ}+85^{\circ}\right)=10^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-290.jpg?height=372&width=458&top_left_y=1790&top_left_x=1308)

## NUMBER OF TANGENTS FROM A POINT 'P' TO A CIRCLE

## Case I : When point ' $\boldsymbol{P}$ ' lies outside the circle

Only two tangents can be drawn from an exterior point to a circle. In the figure, $P Q$ and $P R$ are the two tangents from an exterior point $P$ to a circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-290.jpg?height=248&width=362&top_left_y=2284&top_left_x=1455)

## Case II : When point ' $P$ ' lies on the circle

Only one tangent can be drawn from a point lying on the circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-291.jpg?height=202&width=341&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1530)

## Case III : When point ' $\mathbf{P}$ ' lies inside the circle

No tangent can be drawn from a point lying inside the circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-291.jpg?height=211&width=214&top_left_y=603&top_left_x=1653)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-291.jpg?height=356&width=1619&top_left_y=773&top_left_x=255)

## LENGTH OF A TANGENT

Length of the segment of the tangent from the external point ' P ' and the point of contact with the circle is called the length of the tangent from the point ' P ' to the circle.

## Theorem 1:

Statement : The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
Given : PQ is a tangent of a circle at a point A and O is the centre of the circle.
To prove : $\mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{PQ}$
Construction : Take any point B on PQ . Join OB .
Proof : Distance of centre from any point on tangent other than the point of contact is greater than its distance from point of contact. Since, point B is outside the circle. Therefore, $\mathrm{OB}>\mathrm{OA}$
Hence, out of all the line segments joining the centre O to the points on the tangent, OA is the least.

Since perpendicular from a point O on the tangent PQ is least of all line segments joining the centre to the points on the tangent. Hence $\mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{PQ}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-291.jpg?height=339&width=472&top_left_y=1537&top_left_x=1377)

Note : Converse of Theorem-1 is also true.

## Remarks :

The line containing the radius through the point of contact is called the 'normal' to the circle at the point. Normal to a circle at the point of contact of a tangent to the circle is perpendicular to the tangent.

## Theorem 2:

Statement : The lengths of two tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.
Given : O is the centre of the circle. RP and RQ are two tangents drawn from external point R to the circle.
To prove: $\mathrm{RP}=\mathrm{RQ}$
Construction : Join OP, OQ and OR.
Proof: We know that the tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-291.jpg?height=369&width=473&top_left_y=2151&top_left_x=1398)

$$
\angle \mathrm{OPR}=\angle \mathrm{OQR}=90^{\circ}
$$

In $\triangle \mathrm{OPR}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{OQR}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \angle \mathrm{OPR}=\angle \mathrm{OQR}=90^{\circ}[\mathrm{By}(\mathrm{i})] \\
& \mathrm{OR}=\mathrm{OR} \quad(\text { Common sides }) \\
& \mathrm{OP}=\mathrm{OQ} \quad(\text { Radii of the same circle })
\end{aligned}
$$

So, from right angle-hypotenuse-side congruency

$$
\Delta \mathrm{OPR} \cong \triangle \mathrm{OQR}
$$

Since corresponding sides of two congruent triangles are equal.
$\therefore \mathrm{RP}=\mathrm{RQ}$
Corollary : Centre of the circle lies on the bisector of the angle between the two tangent drawn from an exterior point.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-292.jpg?height=191&width=323&top_left_y=758&top_left_x=196)

When two chords intersect each other inside a circle, the products of their segments are equal i.e. $\boldsymbol{A P} \times \boldsymbol{P B}=\boldsymbol{C P} \times \boldsymbol{P D}$. It is known as intersecting chord theorem.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-292.jpg?height=204&width=211&top_left_y=909&top_left_x=1053)

The product of the intercepts of two secants from an external point are equal. i.e. $P A \times P B=P C \times P D$. It is known as intersecting secants theorem.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-292.jpg?height=257&width=490&top_left_y=1228&top_left_x=913)

Note : (i) If AB is a tangent to a circle at B and ACD is a secant, then $\mathrm{AB}^{2}=\mathrm{AC} \times \mathrm{AD}$. [Shown in Fig.-1]
(ii) The distance between two parallel tangents drawn to a circle is equal to the diameter of the circle.
[Shown in Fig.-2]

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-292.jpg?height=286&width=406&top_left_y=1745&top_left_x=543)
Fig-1

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-292.jpg?height=319&width=392&top_left_y=1740&top_left_x=1192)
Fig-2

Time to
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-292.jpg?height=85&width=95&top_left_y=2156&top_left_x=397)

Check Your
Knowledge
Two circles with centres $O$ and $O^{\prime}$ and of radii 5 cm and 12 cm respectively, intersect at two points $P$ and $Q$ such that OP and $\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}$ are tangents of the two circles. Find the length of the common chord PQ .

## SOLUTION

Join $\mathrm{OO}^{\prime}$
$\angle \mathrm{OPO}^{\prime}=90^{\circ}\left[\because \mathrm{OP}\right.$ and $\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}$ are radii and tangents to other circle $]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad\left(\mathrm{OO}^{\prime}\right)^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{PO}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \\
&=25+144=169
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{OO}^{\prime}=13 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Area of $\triangle \mathrm{OPO}^{\prime}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OO}^{\prime} \times \mathrm{PM}=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OP} \times \mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}\left[\because \mathrm{OO}^{\prime} \perp \mathrm{PQ}\right] \\
& 13 \times \mathrm{PM}=5 \times 12 \\
& \mathrm{PM}=\frac{5 \times 12}{13}=\frac{60}{13} \\
& \mathrm{PQ}=2 \mathrm{PM}\left[\because \mathrm{OO}^{\prime} \text { bisect } \mathrm{PQ}\right] 9.23 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& =2 \times \frac{60}{13}=\frac{120}{13}=9.23 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-293.jpg?height=232&width=368&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1503)

## COMMON TANGENTS

- If a line is tangent to two circles in the same plane, then the line is called a common tangent to the circles.
- If the two circles are on the same side of the tangent, then the common tangent is called Direct common tangent.
- If the two circles are on the opposite side of the tangent, then the common tangent is called Indirect (or transverse) common tangent.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-293.jpg?height=526&width=990&top_left_y=1144&top_left_x=568)

In the figure (i), AB is the Direct common tangent and in figure (ii) AB is the Indirect (or transverse) common tangent.

## IMPORTANT POINTS TO BE REMEMBER

| 1 | The number of common tangents to the circles one lying inside the other is zero. | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-293.jpg?height=152&width=160&top_left_y=1822&top_left_x=1464) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | The number of common tangents to two circles touching internally is one. i.e. one direct common tangent. | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-293.jpg?height=224&width=388&top_left_y=1995&top_left_x=1351) |
| 3 | The number of common tangents to two intersecting circles is two, i.e., two direct common tangents. | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-293.jpg?height=278&width=452&top_left_y=2237&top_left_x=1318) |


| 4 | The number of common tangents to two circles touching externally is three, out of which two are direct common tangents and one is indirect or transverse common tangent. | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-294.jpg?height=390&width=577&top_left_y=394&top_left_x=1197) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 5 | The number of common tangents to two non-intersecting circles is four, out of which 2 are direct common tangents and 2 are indirect (or transverse) common tangents. | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-294.jpg?height=263&width=410&top_left_y=810&top_left_x=1281) |
| 6 | When two circles touch each other internally or externally, then the line joining the centres is perpendicular to the tangent drawn at the point of contact of the two circles. <br> Case (I) <br> Two circles with centres $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ touch each other internally at $\mathrm{P} . \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{P}$ is the line drawn through the centres and XY is the common tangent drawn at P <br> $\therefore \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{P}$ is perpendicular to XY <br> Case (II) <br> Two circles with centres $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ touch each other externally at $\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{PC}_{2}$ is the line joining the centres of the circles and XY is the common tangent to the two circles at P . | ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-294.jpg?height=321&width=434&top_left_y=1092&top_left_x=1266)![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-294.jpg?height=299&width=290&top_left_y=1410&top_left_x=1341) <br> Y |

## CONNECTING TOPIC

Consider two circles with centres $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ and radii $\mathrm{r}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{r}_{2}$.
Case I: If $C_{1} C_{2}>r_{1}+r_{2}$, i.e. the distance between centres $>$ the sum of radii.
Then, the circles neither touch nor intersect and one circle is out of other.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-295.jpg?height=245&width=522&top_left_y=557&top_left_x=838)

Case II : If $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}=\mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}$. Then, the circles touch one another externally.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-295.jpg?height=234&width=330&top_left_y=842&top_left_x=936)

Case III : If $r_{2}-r_{1}<\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}<r_{1}+r_{2}$. Then, the circles intersect at two distinct points. [Assuming $r_{2}>r_{1}$ ].
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-295.jpg?height=190&width=300&top_left_y=1119&top_left_x=941)

$$
r_{2}-r_{1}<C_{1} C_{2}<r_{1}+r_{2}
$$

Case IV : If $C_{1} C_{2}=r_{2}-r_{1}$. Then, the circle touch one another internally.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-295.jpg?height=241&width=215&top_left_y=1402&top_left_x=989)

Case V : If $C_{1} C_{2}<r_{2}-r_{1}$. Then, the smaller circle completely lies inside the bigger circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-295.jpg?height=236&width=207&top_left_y=1690&top_left_x=993)

## Incircle :

The largest circle that will fit inside the triangle i.e., the circle which touches all sides of a triangle is called incircle or inscribed circle and centre is called the triangle's incentre. Clearly, each side of the triangle is a tangent to the circle. Thus $A B, B C$ and $C A$ are tangents to the circle. Radius through the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent.
$\therefore O D \perp A B, O E \perp B C$ and $O F \perp A C$.
Also, lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.
$\therefore A D=A F, B D=B E$ and $C E=C F$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-296.jpg?height=222&width=339&top_left_y=1297&top_left_x=304)

Let us understand the above concepts in a better way in different cases through the illustrative examples.

CASE-I : When radius of the incircle and two parts of one side (like $A D$ and $D B$ of the side $A B$ ) of a triangle are given and we have to find other two sides of a triangle.

Illustration: The radius of the incircle of a triangle is 3cm and the segments into which one side is divided by the point of contact are 5 cm and 9 cm . Determine the other two sides of the triangle.

Sol. A circle with centre ' O ' and radius 3 cm inscribed in a triangle ABC such that $\mathrm{BE}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{EC}=9 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore B D=B E=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ [tangents from external points] $C F=E C=9 \mathrm{~cm}$. Let $A D=A F=x \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore \quad c=A B=(5+x) \mathrm{cm}, a=B C=5+9=14 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $b=A C=(9+x) \mathrm{cm}$.
$s=\frac{A B+B C+A C}{2}=\frac{5+x+14+9+x}{2}=14+x$.
Area of $\triangle A B C=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}=\sqrt{(14+x) \times x \times 5 \times 9}$
We know that $\operatorname{Ar} \triangle A B C=A r \triangle A O B+A r \triangle B O C+A r \triangle A O C$
$4 x=14 \Rightarrow x=3.5 \therefore A B=5+3.5=8.5 \mathrm{~cm} ; A C=9+3.5=12.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-296.jpg?height=252&width=312&top_left_y=1304&top_left_x=1008)
$=\sqrt{(14+x) \times x \times 5 \times 9}=\frac{1}{2} \times 14 \times 3+\frac{1}{2} \times(5+x) \times 3+\frac{1}{2} \times(9+x) \times 3 \Rightarrow 5 x=14+x$
Q. 1 A circle in a triangle with sides 9, 12 and 15. Then find the radius of the circle. [Hint : $9^{2}+12^{2}=15^{2} \Rightarrow$ right angled triangle. Ans. Radius $=3$ units]
Q. 2 A circle is inscribed in the triangle $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with sides of measure $\mathrm{AB}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$, $\mathrm{BC}=12 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AC}=14 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find the measure of AD .

CASE-II : When all three sides of triangle are given and we have to find radius of the incircle.

Illustration: $A$ circle is inscribed in a triangle $A B C$ such that $A B=13 \mathrm{~cm}, B C=14 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $A C=15 \mathrm{~cm}$. Find the radius of circle.

Sol. Let radius of circle be r cm .
$\because \quad a=13 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~b}=14 \mathrm{~cm}$, and $c=15 \mathrm{~cm}$. $s=\frac{13+14+15}{2}=2$

Ar $\triangle A B C=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$
$=\sqrt{21 \times 8 \times 7 \times 6}=84 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$\because A r \triangle A B C=A r \triangle A O B+A r \triangle B O C+A r \triangle A O C$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-296.jpg?height=229&width=222&top_left_y=401&top_left_x=474)
$84=\frac{1}{2} \times 13 \times r+\frac{1}{2} \times 14 \times r+\frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times r \Rightarrow r=\frac{84}{21}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
CASE-III : When a circle inscribed in a triangle whose all sides are given and we have to find the portion of a side (like AD or BD of AB ) of a triangle.

Illustration: $A$ circle inscribed in a $\triangle A B C$ having sides $A B=8 \mathrm{~cm}, B C=14 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $A C=16 \mathrm{~cm}$. Sides, $A B, B C$ and $A C$ touches the circle at point $D, E$ and $F$ respectively. Find $A D$, $B E$ and $C F$.

Sol. Let $A D=A F=x \mathrm{~cm}, B D=B E=y \mathrm{~cm}$ and

Now,

$$
C E=C F=z \mathrm{~cm} .
$$

$A B=x+y=8 \mathrm{~cm}$
$B C=y+z=14 \mathrm{~cm}$
$A C=x+z=16 \mathrm{~cm}$

Solving equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-296.jpg?height=266&width=204&top_left_y=392&top_left_x=1204)
$A D=x=5 \mathrm{~cm} ; B E=y=3 \mathrm{~cm} ; C F=z=11 \mathrm{~cm}$.
[Hint : $\mathrm{AF}=\mathrm{AD}$. Ans. $\mathrm{AD}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ ]
Q. 3 Find the length of sides of an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle of radius $2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
[Hint : Side $=2 r \sin 60^{\circ}$. Ans. Length of side $=6 \mathrm{~cm}$.]

## CASE STUDY-2 : Based on tangents from external points

Let us consider a circle with centre $O$ and a point $P$ outside the circle. $P A$ and $P B$ are two tangents from the external point $\mathrm{P} . \mathrm{AB}$ is a secant joining point of contacts $A$ and $B$ of tangents and circle.
$A O$ and $B O$ are radii from point of contacts $A$ and $B$ of tangents and circle. In the figure:

- $P A=P B$
- $P O$ is the perpendicular bisector of $A B$
- $\angle 3=\angle 4$
- $\angle 3=\angle 4=\angle 5=\angle 6=\frac{1}{2}(\angle 3+\angle 4)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-297.jpg?height=361&width=192&top_left_y=1441&top_left_x=512)

Let us understand the above concepts in a better way in different cases through illustrative examples.

CASE-I : When radius of a circle and length of chord joinning point of contacts of tangents and circle are given and we have to find length of tangents.

Illustration: In the figure, $A B$ is a chord of length 8 cm of a circle of radius 5 cm . The tangents at $A$ and $B$ to the circle intersects at a point $P$.

Find the length $P A$.
Sol. $\mathrm{AM}=\mathrm{BM}=\frac{1}{2} A B=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. In right $\triangle O M A$, we have
$O M=\sqrt{O A^{2}-A M^{2}}=\sqrt{25-16}=\sqrt{9}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$.

Now $\angle P A O=90^{\circ}$

- $\angle 1=\angle 2$
- $\angle 5=\angle 6$
$\therefore \quad$ Right $\triangle P M A \sim$ Right $\triangle A M O$ [BY AA similarity criteria]
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{P A}{5}=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow P A=\frac{4 \times 5}{3}=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$. Hence $\quad P A=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-297.jpg?height=247&width=240&top_left_y=1458&top_left_x=1009)
$\therefore \quad \frac{P A}{A O}=\frac{M A}{M O}$ [The corresponding sides of similar $\Delta$ s are proportional]


## Think Out of the Box

with centre O and radii 8 cm and 5 cm respectively. If $\mathrm{PA}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$ then find the length of PB .
[Hint : $\mathrm{PA}^{2}+\mathrm{OA}^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{OP}=17 \mathrm{~cm} ; \mathrm{PB}^{2}+\mathrm{OB}^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}$. Ans. $\mathrm{PB}=2 \sqrt{66} \mathrm{~cm}$ ]
[Hint : $\mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PA}=11 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{RB}=\mathrm{RS}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. Ans. $\mathrm{PR}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.]

CASE-II : When radius of circle and length of tangents are given and we have to find length of chord joining the point of contacts of the tangents and circle.

Illustration: The tangents of length 4 cm at two points $A$ and $B$ of a circle with radius 3 cm intersect at $P$. Find the length of chord $A B$.

Sol. $\because \angle P A O=90^{\circ}[O A \perp A P]$
$\therefore \quad O P^{2}=A P^{2}+O A^{2}=16+9=25$
$\mathrm{OP}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Area of right triangle PAO.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-297.jpg?height=385&width=196&top_left_y=407&top_left_x=569)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} \times O A \times A P=\frac{1}{2} \times O P \times A D \Rightarrow 3 \times 4=5 \times A D \\
& A D=\frac{3 \times 4}{5}=\frac{12}{5}=2.4 \Rightarrow A B=2 A D=2 \times 2.4=4.8 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

CASE-III : When angle between two tangents drawn from an external point and length of chord joining point of contacts of two tangents and circle are given and we have to find length of tangents and radius.

Illustration: $P A$ and $P B$ are tangents to a circle from an external point $P$ such that $A B=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle A P B=60^{\circ}$, then find length of tangents and radius.

Sol. $A D=\frac{1}{2} A B=3 \mathrm{~cm}$. In rt. $\triangle A D P$,

$$
A P=6 \mathrm{~cm} \text { and } P D=3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-297.jpg?height=386&width=197&top_left_y=412&top_left_x=1161)

$$
\because \triangle A P D \sim \triangle O A D(\text { By } A A) \therefore \frac{A P}{O A}=\frac{P D}{A D} \Rightarrow \frac{6}{O A}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{3} \Rightarrow O A=2 \sqrt{3}
$$

Thus tangents $=\mathrm{AP}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and radius $=\mathrm{OA}=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
Q. 1 Let PA is the tangent to the circle with larger radius and PB is the tangent to the circle with smaller radius drawn from an external point P to two concentric circles
Q. $2 P A$ and $P B$ are tangents from external point $P$ to a circle with centre $O$. $Q R$ is another tangent which touches the circle at $S$. If $P A=11 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $R S=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ then find the length of PR .

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. PQ is a chord of length 8 cm of a circle of radius 5 cm . The tangents at P and Q intersect at a point T (see Fig.). Find the length of TP.
Sol. Join OT . Let it intersect PQ at a point R . Then, $\triangle \mathrm{TPQ}$ is isosceles triangle and TO is the angle bisector of $\angle \mathrm{PTQ}$.
So, $\mathrm{OT} \perp \mathrm{PQ}$. Therefore, OT bisects PQ which gives
$\mathrm{PR}=\mathrm{RQ}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Also, $\quad \mathrm{OR}=\sqrt{\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{PR}^{2}}=\sqrt{5^{2}-4^{2}}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Now, $\quad \angle \mathrm{TPR}+\angle \mathrm{RPO}=90^{\circ}=\angle \mathrm{TPR}+\angle \mathrm{PTR}$
So, $\angle \mathrm{RPO}=\angle \mathrm{PTR}$
Therefore, right triangle TRP is similar to the right triangle
PRO (by AA similarity).
This gives, $\frac{\mathrm{TP}}{\mathrm{PO}}=\frac{\mathrm{RP}}{\mathrm{RO}}$ i.e. $\frac{\mathrm{TP}}{5}=\frac{4}{3}$ or $\mathrm{TP}=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-298.jpg?height=517&width=490&top_left_y=605&top_left_x=1311)
2. Find the length of tangent drawn from a point whose distance from the centre of a circle is 5 cm and the radius of the circle is $\mathbf{3 c m}$.

Sol. In figure, O is the centre of the circle and $\mathrm{OA}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{OP}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$.
∵ The tangent is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact. Hence, in right angled triangle OPA,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{OA}^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}+\mathrm{PA}^{2} \\
& \mathrm{PA}^{2}=\mathrm{OA}^{2}-\mathrm{OP}^{2}=(5)^{2}-(3)^{2}=25-9 \\
& \mathrm{PA}^{2}=16 \text { or } \mathrm{PA}=4 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-298.jpg?height=295&width=456&top_left_y=1197&top_left_x=1361)

Hence, the length of tangent is 4 cm .
3. Two concentric circles with centre $O$ have $A, B, C, D$ as the points of intersection with the line $\ell$ as shown in the figure . If $\mathbf{A D}=\mathbf{1 2 ~ c m}$ and $\mathbf{B C}=\mathbf{8 ~ c m}$, find the length of $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{CD}, \mathrm{AC}$ and BD .
Sol. BC is the chord of inner circle. Since, $\mathrm{OM} \perp \mathrm{BC}$
∴ It bisects BC .
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{BM}=\mathrm{CM}=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{BC})=\frac{1}{2}(8)=4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-298.jpg?height=314&width=415&top_left_y=1493&top_left_x=1402)

Similarly, AD is the chord of outer circle. Since, $\mathrm{OM} \perp \mathrm{AD}$
∴ It bisects AD .
$\therefore \mathrm{AM}=\mathrm{MD}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{AD}=\frac{1}{2}(12)=6 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Now, $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AM}-\mathrm{BM}=6-4=2 \mathrm{~cm} ; \quad \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{MD}-\mathrm{MC}=6-4=2 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{AM}+\mathrm{MC}=6+4=10 \mathrm{~cm} ; \quad \mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{BM}+\mathrm{MD}=4+6=10 \mathrm{~cm} .
$$

4. A circle touches the side $B C$ of a $\triangle A B C$ at $P$ and touches $A B$ and $A C$ when produced at $Q$ and $R$, respectively as shown in figure. Show that $\mathrm{AQ}=\frac{\mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{2}}$ (Perimeter of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ ).
Sol. Given : A circle is touching the side BC of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ at P and touching AB and AC when produced at Q and R respectively.
To prove : $\mathrm{AQ}=\frac{1}{2}$ (Perimeter of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ )
Proof :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{AQ}=\mathrm{AR} \\
& \mathrm{BQ}=\mathrm{BP} \\
& \mathrm{CP}=\mathrm{CR}
\end{aligned}
$$

[Tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal]
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-298.jpg?height=376&width=371&top_left_y=2142&top_left_x=1443)

Now, Perimeter of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BC}+\mathrm{CA}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BP}+\mathrm{PC}+\mathrm{CA} \\
& =(\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BQ})+(\mathrm{CR}+\mathrm{CA}) \\
& =\mathrm{AQ}+\mathrm{AR}=\mathrm{AQ}+\mathrm{AQ}
\end{aligned}
$$

[From (ii) and (iii)]
[From (i)]
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AQ}=\frac{1}{2}$ (Perimeter of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ )
5. In a right triangle ABC , the perpendicular BD on the hypotenuse AC is drawn. Prove that
(i) $\mathbf{A C} \times \mathbf{A D}=\mathbf{A B}^{\mathbf{2}}$
(ii) $\mathbf{A C} \times \mathbf{C D}=\mathbf{B C}^{\mathbf{2}}$

Sol. We draw a circle with BC as diameter. Since, $\angle \mathrm{BDC}=90^{\circ}$.
$\therefore \quad$ The circle on BC as diameter will pass through D .
$\therefore \mathrm{AB} \perp \mathrm{BC}$
Also, AB is a tangent to the circle at B
Since, AB is a tangent and ADC is a secant to the circle.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AC} \times \mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}$. This proves (i)
Again,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{AC} \times \mathrm{CD} & =\mathrm{AC} \times(\mathrm{AC}-\mathrm{AD})=\quad \mathrm{AC}^{2}-\mathrm{AC} \times \mathrm{AD} \\
& =\mathrm{AC}^{2}-\mathrm{AB}^{2} \quad[\mathrm{Using}(\mathrm{i})] \\
& =\mathrm{BC}^{2} \quad[\Delta \mathrm{ABC} \text { is a right triangle }]
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-299.jpg?height=342&width=330&top_left_y=625&top_left_x=1546)

Hence, $\quad \mathrm{AC} \times \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{BC}^{2}$. This proves (ii)
6. Two circles touch externally at $\mathbf{P}$ and a common tangent touches them at $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$. Prove that
(i) the common tangent at P bisects AB .
(ii) AB subtends a right angle at P .

Sol. Let PT be the common tangent at any point P . Since, the tangent to a circle from an external point are equal.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{TA}=\mathrm{TP}, \mathrm{TB}=\mathrm{TP} \Rightarrow \mathrm{TA}=\mathrm{TB}$
i.e. $\quad \mathrm{PT}$ bisects AB at T

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{TA}=\mathrm{TP} \text { gives } \angle \mathrm{TAP}=\angle \mathrm{TPA} & (\text { from } \triangle \mathrm{PAT}) \\
\mathrm{TB}=\mathrm{TP} \text { gives } \angle \mathrm{TBP}=\angle \mathrm{TPB} & (\text { from } \triangle \mathrm{PBT})
\end{array}
$$

$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{TAP}+\angle \mathrm{TBP}=\angle \mathrm{TPA}+\angle \mathrm{TPB}=\angle \mathrm{APB}$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{TAP}+\angle \mathrm{TBP}+\angle \mathrm{APB}=2 \angle \mathrm{APB}=180^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-299.jpg?height=330&width=563&top_left_y=1190&top_left_x=1311)
[Angle sum property of a triangle]

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{APB}=90^{\circ}
$$

7. In the given figure, if $\mathbf{A B}=\mathbf{A C}$, then prove that $\mathbf{B C}=\mathbf{2 C E}$.

Sol. We know that tangents from an external point to a circle are equal in length.

$$
\begin{array}{llr}
\therefore & \mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{AF} & \ldots . .(\mathrm{i}) \\
\text { and } & \mathrm{CE}=\mathrm{CF} & \ldots \mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{BE} \\
\text { Now, } & \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AC} & \ldots .(\text { iii }) \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{AB}-\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{AC}-\mathrm{AD} & \text { (given) }
\end{array}
$$

(subtracting AD from both sides)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-299.jpg?height=307&width=321&top_left_y=1603&top_left_x=1553)
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AB}-\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{AC}-\mathrm{AF} \quad$ [using eq. (i)]
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{CF}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{BE}=\mathrm{CF}$
[using eq. (ii)]
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{BE}=\mathrm{CE}$

Now, $\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{CE}+\mathrm{BE}=\mathrm{CE}+\mathrm{CE}[$ from eq. (iv)]

$$
\Rightarrow \quad B C=2 C E
$$

8. In the given figure, $\angle A D C=90^{\circ}, B C=38 \mathrm{~cm}, C D=28 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $B P=25 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find the radius of the circle.
Sol. Join OR and OS and let $r$ be the radius of circle.

$$
\text { Given, } \mathrm{BC}=38 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{CD}=28 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

$$
\mathrm{BP}=25 \mathrm{~cm}=\mathrm{BQ} \quad(\because \text { tangent drawn from external point } \mathrm{B} \text { to the circle })
$$

Then, $\quad \mathrm{CQ}=\mathrm{BC}-\mathrm{BQ}=38-25=13 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{CR}=\mathrm{CQ}=13 \mathrm{~cm}$
Then, $\mathrm{RD}=\mathrm{CD}-\mathrm{CR}=28-13=15 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-299.jpg?height=291&width=330&top_left_y=2147&top_left_x=1541)

Clearly, quadrilateral DROS is a square. ( $\therefore$ Three angles are $90^{\circ}$ )

$$
\therefore \quad r=\mathrm{OR}=\mathrm{RD}=15 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

9. In the given figure, PT and PS are tangents to a circle from a point P such that $\mathrm{PT}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{TPS}=60^{\circ}$, then find the length of chord TS.
Sol. We know that length of tangents drawn from an external point contact to the circle are equal.
$\therefore \mathrm{PS}=\mathrm{PT}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Then, $\angle \mathrm{PTS}=\angle \mathrm{PST}$
( ∵ angles opposite to equal sides are equal)
In $\triangle \mathrm{PTS}$, we have

$$
\angle \mathrm{PTS}+\angle \mathrm{PST}+\angle \mathrm{TPS}=180^{\circ} \quad\left(\because \text { sum of three angles of a triangle is } 180^{\circ}\right)
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{PTS}+\angle \mathrm{PTS}+60^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \angle \mathrm{PTS}=120^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PTS}=60^{\circ}$
Thus, $\angle \mathrm{PTS}=\angle \mathrm{PST}=\angle \mathrm{TPS}=60^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad \triangle \mathrm{PTS}$ is an equilateral triangle.
Hence, TS = 5 cm
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-300.jpg?height=455&width=296&top_left_y=434&top_left_x=1521)
10. In the given figure, from an external point $P$, a tangent $P Q$ and a line segment $P R S$ drawn to a circle with centre $O$. $O T \perp R S$. Prove that
(i) $\boldsymbol{P R . P S}=\boldsymbol{P T ^ { 2 }}-\boldsymbol{R T ^ { 2 }}$
(ii) $\quad P T^{2}-R T^{2}=O P^{2}-O Q^{2}$

Sol. (i) $P R . P S=(P T-R T)(P T+T S)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(P T-R T)(P T+T R)(\because T S=T R \text { as } O T \perp R S) \\
& =P T^{2}-R T^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(By Pythagoras in $\triangle P T O$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-300.jpg?height=214&width=330&top_left_y=952&top_left_x=1457)
(ii) $P T^{2}-R T^{2}=\left(O P^{2}-O T^{2}\right)-R T^{2}$
$=O P^{2}-\left(O T^{2}+R T^{2}\right)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =O P^{2}-O R^{2} \\
& =O P^{2}-O O^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
(\because O R=O Q=\text { radii })
$$

11. In the given figure, $\boldsymbol{A B}$ is a tangent at a point $\boldsymbol{C}$ of the circle with centre $\mathbf{O}$.

If $\angle T C B=30^{\circ}$, find $\angle A C S$.
Sol. Since, $S T$ is a diameter.
$\therefore \angle S C T=90^{\circ}$
Now, $\angle A C S+\angle S C T+\angle T C B=180^{\circ}$ (Linear pair)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \angle A C S+90^{\circ}+30^{\circ}=180^{\circ} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \angle A C S=60^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-300.jpg?height=282&width=392&top_left_y=1425&top_left_x=1425)

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which only one is correct.

1. Two circles with centres O and P , and radii 8 cm and 4 cm touch each other externally. Find the length of their common tangent QR .
(a) 8 cm
(b) 7 cm
(c) $8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-301.jpg?height=260&width=394&top_left_y=794&top_left_x=657)
(d) $7 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
2. Two chords AB and CD of a circle intersect each other at P outside the circle. If $\mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$, $\mathrm{BP}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{PD}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$, find CD .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-301.jpg?height=234&width=389&top_left_y=1107&top_left_x=660)
(a) 4 cm
(b) 5 cm
(c) 8 cm
(d) 10 cm
3. A ball is in the rest position against a step PQ . If $\mathrm{PQ}=10$ cm and $\mathrm{QR}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find diameter of the ball.
(a) 16 cm
(b) 32.5 cm
(c) 28 cm
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-301.jpg?height=266&width=337&top_left_y=1430&top_left_x=712)
(d) 42 cm
4. In the given figure, PA and PB are two tangents to the circle with centre O . If $\angle \mathrm{APB}=40^{\circ}$, find $\angle \mathrm{AQB}$ and $\angle \mathrm{AMB}$.
(a) $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=70^{\circ}$,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-301.jpg?height=241&width=380&top_left_y=1731&top_left_x=671)
$\angle \mathrm{AMB}=110^{\circ}$
(b) $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=110^{\circ}, \angle \mathrm{AMB}=70^{\circ}$
(c) $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=100^{\circ}, \angle \mathrm{AMB}=50^{\circ}$
(d) $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=60^{\circ} \angle \mathrm{AMB}=40^{\circ}$
5. A tangent CQ touches a circle with centre O at P . Diameter AB is produced to meet the tangent at C . If $\angle \mathrm{ACP}=\mathrm{a}^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{BPC}=\mathrm{b}^{\circ}$, the relation connecting a and b is
(a) $\mathrm{a}^{\circ}+\mathrm{b}^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-301.jpg?height=256&width=309&top_left_y=2129&top_left_x=742)
(b) $\mathrm{a}^{\circ}+2 \mathrm{~b}^{\circ}=90^{\circ}$
(c) $\mathrm{a}^{\circ}-\mathrm{b}^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{a}^{\circ}+\mathrm{b}^{\circ}=100^{\circ}$
6. PQ is a tangent drawn from a point P to a circle with centre O and QOR is a diameter of the circle such that $\angle \mathrm{POR}=120^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{OPQ}$ is
(a) $60^{\circ}$
(b) $45^{\circ}$
(c) $30^{\circ}$
(d) $90^{\circ}$

## Fill in the Blanks

DIRECTIONS : Complete the following statements with an appropriate word / term to be filled in the blank space(s).

1. A tangent to a circle touches it at $\_\_\_\_$ point (s).
2. A line intersecting a circle at two points is called a $\_\_\_\_$
3. A circle can have $\_\_\_\_$ parallel tangents at the most.
4. The common point of a tangent to a circle and the circle is called $\_\_\_\_$ .
5. There is no tangent to a circle passing through a point lying $\_\_\_\_$ the circle.
6. The tangent to a circle is $\_\_\_\_$ to the radius through the point of contact.
7. There are exactly two tangents to a circle passing through a point lying $\_\_\_\_$ the circle.
8. The lengths of the two tangents from an external point to a circle are $\_\_\_\_$ .
9. The tangents drawn at the ends of a diameter of a circle are. $\_\_\_\_$ .
10. In given Fig., the length $\mathrm{PB}=$ $\_\_\_\_$ cm.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-301.jpg?height=259&width=263&top_left_y=1439&top_left_x=1343)
True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. The tangent to a circle is a special case of the secant.
2. The perpendicular at the point of contact to the tangent to a circle does not pass through the centre.
3. A circle can have at the most two parallel tangents.
4. If P is a point on a circle with centre C , then the line drawn through P and perpendicular to CP is the tangent to the circle at the point P .
5. The centre of the circle lies on the bisector of the angle between the two tangents.
6. A tangent to a circle is a line that intersects the circle at only one point.
7. Two equal chords of a circle are always parallel.
8. A line drawn from the centre of a circle to a chord always bisects it.
9. Line joining the centers of two intersecting circles always bisect their common chord.
10. In a circle, two chords PQ and RS bisect each other. Then PRQS is a rectangle.

## Long Answer Questions

1. In the diagram, O is the centre of the circle ABCD . The straight lines AC and BD intersect at O .
The tangent at A meets
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=223&width=426&top_left_y=438&top_left_x=566)
CD produced at E and $\angle \mathrm{AED}=32^{\circ}$. Calculate
(a) $\angle \mathrm{ABD}$
(b) $\angle \mathrm{AOD}$
2. In the given figure, find $\angle \mathrm{RTQ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{RQT}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=252&width=351&top_left_y=758&top_left_x=415)
3. In the given figure, PT is a tangent to the circle at T . If $\mathrm{PA}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$, find PT .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=193&width=214&top_left_y=1091&top_left_x=488)
4. In the adjoining figure, PA and PB are tangents to a circle with centre O . If OP is equal to the diameter of the circle, prove that $\triangle \mathrm{ABP}$ is an equilateral triangle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=218&width=296&top_left_y=1297&top_left_x=694)
5. Prove that the tangent drawn at the mid-point of an arc of a circle is parallel to the chord joining the end points of the arc.
6. In figure, two equal circles, with centres O and $\mathrm{O}^{\prime}$, touch each other at X . $\mathrm{OO}^{\prime}$ produced meets the circle with centre $\mathrm{O}^{\prime}$ at A. AC is tangent to
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=241&width=428&top_left_y=1635&top_left_x=566)
the circle with centre O , at the point C . $\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}$ is perpendicular to AC . Find the value of $\frac{\mathrm{DO}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{CO}}$.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. The radii of two concentric circles are 16 cm and 10 cm . AB is a diameter of the bigger circle. BD is tangent to the smaller circle touching it at D . Find the length of AD .
2. From external point $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{a}$ tangent PT and secant PAB is drawn to a circle with centre O . ON is perpendicular in the chord AB (shown in
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=229&width=387&top_left_y=2188&top_left_x=603)
figure). Prove that
(i) $\mathrm{PA} \times \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PN}^{2}-\mathrm{AN}^{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{PN}^{2}-\mathrm{AN}^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OT}^{2}$
(iii) $\mathrm{PA} \times \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PT}^{2}$
3. Tangents $P Q$ and $P R$ are drawn to a circle such that $\angle \mathrm{RPQ}=30^{\circ}$, A chord RS is drawn parallel to the tangent PQ (shown in figure). Find $\angle \mathrm{RQS}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=222&width=342&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1475)
4. In a right triangle ABC , in which $\angle \mathrm{B}=90^{\circ}$, a circle is drawn with AB as diameter intersecting the hypotenuse AC at P . Prove that the tangents to the circle at P bisects BC .
5. It was required to plant trees along the sides of a quadrilateral touching a circle as shown in figure. Distance between consecutive trees was to be kept same throughout. Two gardeners Ram and Rahim were appointed by the contractor to complete the job. Ram was alloted the sides PS and QR and Rahim, the other two sides. When the job was completed Ram was paid $10 \%$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=343&width=223&top_left_y=733&top_left_x=1589)
more than what was paid to Rahim.
(i) Who planted more trees, Ram or Rahim?
(ii) Comment on the dealings of the contractor.

## HOTS Questions

1. The radii of two concentric circles are 13 cm and 8 cm . PQ is a diameter of the bigger circle. QR is a tangent to the smaller circle touching it at R . Find the length PR .
2. A, B and C are three points on a circle. The tangent at C meets BA produced at T. Given that $\angle \mathrm{ATC}=36^{\circ}$ and that the $\angle \mathrm{ACT}=48^{\circ}$, calculate the angle
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=239&width=387&top_left_y=1354&top_left_x=1425)
subtended by AB at the centre of the circle.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. The class X students are given a mathematical problem to discuss the properties of tangent to a circle and to evaluate some unknown angles of the figure shown below. In the given figure O is the centre of the circum-circle of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. Tangents A and B
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-304.jpg?height=335&width=382&top_left_y=1726&top_left_x=1430)
intersect at P. If it is given that $\angle \mathrm{APB}=80^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{AOC}=140^{\circ}$. Then answer the following questions.
(i) The number of possible tangents to a circle from a given point outside of the circle.
(a) 0
(b) 1
(c) 2
(d) infinite
(ii) Find $\angle \mathrm{PAB}$
(a) $50^{\circ}$
(b) $60^{\circ}$
(c) $70^{\circ}$
(d) $90^{\circ}$
(iii) Find $\angle \mathrm{OAB}$
(a) $30^{\circ}$
(b) $40^{\circ}$
(c) $50^{\circ}$
(d) $60^{\circ}$
(iv) Find $\angle \mathrm{OCA}$
(a) $20^{\circ}$
(b) $30^{\circ}$
(c) $40^{\circ}$
(d) $80^{\circ}$
(v) Find $\angle \mathrm{CAB}$
(a) $30^{\circ}$
(b) $60^{\circ}$
(c) $90^{\circ}$
(d) $120^{\circ}$

## Text-book Questions

1. A tangent PQ at a point P of a circle of radius 5 cm meets a line through the centre O at a point Q so that $\mathrm{OQ}=12 \mathrm{~cm}$. Length PQ is :
(a) 12 cm
(b) 13 cm
(c) 8.5 cm
(d) $\sqrt{119} \mathrm{~cm}$
2. In fig. if TP and TQ are the two tangents to a circle with centre O so that $\angle \mathrm{POQ}=110^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{PTQ}$ is equal to
(a) $60^{\circ}$
(b) $70^{\circ}$
(c) $80^{\circ}$
(d) $90^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-305.jpg?height=292&width=243&top_left_y=810&top_left_x=808)
3. If tangents PA and PB from a point P to a circle with centre O are inclined to each other at an angle of $80^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{POA}$ is equal to
(a) $50^{\circ}$
(b) $60^{\circ}$
(c) $70^{\circ}$
(d) $80^{\circ}$
4. Prove that the tangents drawn at the ends of a diameter of a circle are parallel.
5. Two concentric circles are of radii 5 cm and 3 cm . Find the length of the chord of the larger circle which touches the smaller circle.
6. Prove that the parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a rhombus.
7. PQ and RS are two parallel tangents to a circle with centre O and another tangent XY with point of contact C intersect PQ at A and RS at B . Prove that $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=90^{\circ}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-305.jpg?height=292&width=373&top_left_y=1653&top_left_x=466)
8. Prove that the angle between the two tangents drawn from an external point to a circle is supplementary to the angle subtended by the line-segment joining the points of contact at the centre.
9. The radius of the incircle of a triangle is 4 cm and the segment into which one side is divided by the point of contact are 6 cm and 8 cm . Find the other two sides of the triangle.
10. A circle touches all the four sides of a quadrilateral $A B C D$. Prove that the angles subtended at the centre of the circle by the opposite sides are supplementary.

## Exemplar Questions

1. If $d_{1}, d_{2}\left(d_{2}>d_{1}\right)$ be the diameters of two concentric circles and $c$ be the length of a chord of a circle which is tangent to the other circle, prove that $d_{2}^{2}=c^{2}+d_{1}^{2}$.
2. If $a, b, c$ are the sides of a right triangle where $c$ is the hypotenuse, prove that the radius $r$ of the circle which touches the sides of the triangle is given by $r=\frac{a+b-c}{2}$.
3. If a circle touches the side $B C$ of a triangle $A B C$ at $P$ and extended sides $A B$ and $A C$ at $Q$ and $R$, respectively, prove that $A Q=\frac{1}{2}(B C+C A+A B)$
4. If angle between two radii of a circle is $130^{\circ}$, the angle between the tangents at the ends of the radii is :
(a) $90^{\circ}$
(b) $50^{\circ}$
(c) $70^{\circ}$
(d) $40^{\circ}$
5. In fig. the pair of tangents $A P$ and $A Q$ drawn from an external point $A$ to a circle with centre $O$ are perpendicular to each other and length of each tangent is 5 cm .
Then, the radius of the circle is
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-305.jpg?height=264&width=385&top_left_y=1160&top_left_x=1283)
(a) 10 cm
(b)
7.5 cm
(c) 5 cm
(d) 2.5 cm
6. In fig., BOA is a diameter of a circle and the tangent at a point P meets BA extended at $T$. If $\angle \mathrm{PBO}=30^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{PTA}$ is equal to $30^{\circ}$. Is this statement true ? Justify your answer.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-305.jpg?height=286&width=515&top_left_y=1663&top_left_x=1215)
7. In fig., PQL and PRM are tangents to the circle with centre O at the points Q and R , respectively and S is a point on the circle such that $\angle \mathrm{SQL}=50^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{SRM}=60^{\circ}$. Then $\angle \mathrm{QSR}$ is equal to $40^{\circ}$. Is this statement true? Justify your answer.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-305.jpg?height=392&width=567&top_left_y=2158&top_left_x=1192)

## Single Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

(a) $3 r$
(b) $6 r$
(c) $9 r$
(d) $12 r$
2. AB and CD are two chords of a circle intersecting at the point P outside the circle. If $\mathrm{PA}=12 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{CD}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{PC}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$, then AB is equal to
(a) 15.5 cm
(b) 4 cm
(c) 8 cm
(d) 10 cm

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-306.jpg?height=229&width=355&top_left_y=1151&top_left_x=415)

If $\angle \mathrm{BAC}=70^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{P}$ is
(a) $30^{\circ}$
(b) $40^{\circ}$
(c) $45^{\circ}$
(d) $50^{\circ}$

(b) $70^{\circ}$
(c) $80^{\circ}$
(d) $90^{\circ}$

(a) $45^{\circ}$
(b) $30^{\circ}$
(c) $60^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-306.jpg?height=353&width=392&top_left_y=1820&top_left_x=598)
(d) $90^{\circ}$
6. In the diagram, O is the centre of the circle and D , E and F are mid points of $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BO}$ and OA respectively. If $\angle \mathrm{DEF}=30^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{ACB}$ is
(a) $30^{\circ}$
(b) $60^{\circ}$
(c) $90^{\circ}$

(a) $30^{\circ}$
(b) $\quad 150^{\circ}$

8. In two concentric circles, if chords are drawn in the outer circle which touch the inner circle, then
(a) all chords are of different lengths.
(b) all chords are of same length.
(c) only parallel chords are of same length.
(d) only perpendicular chords are of same length.
9. Number of tangents to a circle which are parallel to a secant, is
(a) 3
(b) 2
(c) 1
(d) infinite
10. AB and $C D$ are two common tangents to circles which touch each other at a point $C$. If $D$ lies on $A B$ such that $C D=4 \mathrm{~cm}$, then $A B$ is
(a) 12 cm
(b) 8 cm
(c) 4 cm
(d) 6 cm
11. In the figure, $\triangle \mathrm{APB}$ is formed by three tangents to the circle with centre O . If $\angle \mathrm{APB}=40^{\circ}$, then the measure of $\angle \mathrm{BOA}$ is
[NTSE]
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-306.jpg?height=271&width=385&top_left_y=1530&top_left_x=1226)
(a) $50^{\circ}$
(b) $55^{\circ}$
(c) $60^{\circ}$
(d) $70^{\circ}$
12. Three circles with radii $\mathrm{R}_{1}, \mathrm{R}_{2}$ and $r$ touch each other externally as shown in the adjoining figure. If PQ is their common tangent and $R_{1}>R_{2}$, then which of the following relations is correct?
[NTSE]
(a) $\mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{r}$
(b) $\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}=2 \mathrm{r}$
(c) $\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}$
(d) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{r}}}$

13. Two circles, both of radii $a$ touch each other and each of them touches internally a circle of radius $2 a$. Then the radius of the circle which touches all the three circles is
[NTSE]
(a) $\frac{1}{2} a$
(b) $\frac{2}{3} a$
(c) $\frac{3}{4} a$
(d) $a$
14. In the figure, $O$ is the centre of the circle and $O A=C D$, then $\angle C P D$ is
[JSTSE]
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-307.jpg?height=270&width=220&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=543)
(a) $45^{\circ}$
(b) $30^{\circ}$
(c) $70^{\circ}$
(d) $60^{\circ}$

[JSTSE]
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-307.jpg?height=300&width=312&top_left_y=840&top_left_x=495)
(a) $60^{\circ}$
(b) $130^{\circ}$
(c) $50^{\circ}$
(d) $40^{\circ}$
16. In the given figure, AC is the diameter of the circle. $\mathrm{ED} \| \mathrm{AC}$, $\angle \mathrm{CBE}=65^{\circ}$, then $\angle \mathrm{DEC}$ is
[JSTSE]
(a) $35^{\circ}$
(b) $25^{\circ}$
(c) $65^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-307.jpg?height=259&width=278&top_left_y=1192&top_left_x=776)
(d) $30^{\circ}$
17. Let $A B C D$ be a square of side length 1 , and $\Gamma$ a circle passing through $B$ and $C$, and touching $A D$. The radius of $\Gamma$ is
[KVPY]
(a) $\frac{3}{8}$
(b) $\frac{1}{2}$
(c) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
(d) $\frac{5}{8}$
18. Three circles of radii 1,2 and 3 units respectively touch each other externally in the plane. The circumradius of the triangle formed by joining the centers of the circles is
[KVPY]
(a) 1.5
(b) 2
(c) 2.5
(d) 3
19. Circles A, B and C are externally tangent to each other and internally tangent to circle D. Circles A and $B$ are congruent. Circle $C$ has radius 1 unit and passes through the centre of circle $D$. Then are radius of circle $B$ is $\_\_\_\_$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-307.jpg?height=286&width=257&top_left_y=1923&top_left_x=797)
units.
[NMTC]
(a) $\frac{3}{7}$
(b) $\frac{6}{9}$
(c) $\frac{8}{9}$
(d) $\frac{9}{8}$
20. The length of tangent drawn from a point Q to a circle is 24 cm and distance of Q from the centre of circle is 25 cm . The radius of circle is
[NTSE]
(a) 7 cm
(b) 12 cm
(c) 15 cm
(d) 24.5 cm

## More Than One Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.

1. Which of the following is a not cyclic quadrilateral?
(a) Rhombus
(b) Rectangle
(c) Parallelogram
(d) Trapezium
2. Which of the following is/are not correct?
(a) A secant is a line that intersects a circle in four distinct points.
(b) In a circle, the perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects the chord.
(c) The point common to a circle and its tangent is called the point of contact.
(d) Adjacent angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.
3. Which of the following statement(s) is / are correct ?
(a) The length of tangent from an external point $P$ on circle with centre $O$ is always less than $O P$.
(b) The tangent to the circumcircle of an isosceles triangle ABC at $A$, in which $A B=A C$, is parallel to $B C$.
(c) If angle between two tangents drawn from a point $P$ to a circle of radius ' a ' and centre ' O ' is $90^{\circ}$, then $O P=a \sqrt{2}$.
(d) None of these
4. Which of the following statement(s) is/are false?
(a) If a chord $A B$ subtends an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the centre of a circle, then angle between the tangents at A and B is also $60^{\circ}$.
(b) The length of tangent from an external point on a circle is always greater than the radius of the circle.
(c) If a number of circle touch a given line segment PQ at a point A , then their centres lie on the perpendicular bisector of $P Q$.
(d) None of these
5. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
(a) Angle between the tangent line and the radius at the point of contact is $90^{\circ}$.
(b) A circle can have two parallel tangents atmost.
(c) The distance between two parallel tangents drawn to a circle is equal to radius.
(d) A line intersecting a circle in two points is called a chord.

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS: Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage - I

The lengths of two parallel chords of a circle are 6 cm and 8 cm . The smaller chord is at a distance of 4 cm from the centre

1. The radius of the circle is
(a) 10 cm
(b) 5 cm
(c) 3 cm
(d) None of these
2. The distance of the other chord from the centre is
(a) 3 cm
(b) 6 cm
(c) 4 cm
(d) None of these

Two roads starting from $P$ are touching a circular path at $Q$ and $R$, Sagar runs from $P$ to $Q, 20 \mathrm{~km}$ and $Q$ to $O, 15 \mathrm{~km}$. Gagan runs from $P$ to $O$ directly, then
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-308.jpg?height=213&width=298&top_left_y=685&top_left_x=445)

(a) 20 km
(b) 15 km
(c) 25 km
(d) 35 km

(a) 20 km
(b) 15 km
(c) 25 km
(d) 35 km
5. Who will win the race ?
(a) Sagar
(b) Gagan
(c) Both are winner
(d) Data insufficient

6. Length of $R Q$ is
(a) 4 m
(b) 8 m
(c) 3 m
(d) 5 m

(a) 4 m
(b) 8 m
(c) 3 m
(d) 5 m

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-308.jpg?height=319&width=392&top_left_y=2142&top_left_x=397)

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion: If in a circle, the radius of the circle is 3 cm and distance of a point from the centre of a circle is 5 cm , then length of the tangent will be 4 cm .
Reason: $(\text { hypotenuse })^{2}=(\text { base })^{2}+(\text { height })^{2}$
2. Assertion: If in a cyclic quadrilateral, one angle is $40^{\circ}$, then the opposite angle is $140^{\circ}$
Reason: Sum of opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral is equal to $360^{\circ}$
3. Assertion: If length of a tangent from an external point to a circle is 8 cm , then length of the other tangent from the same point is 8 cm .
Reason: length of the tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.

## SOLUTIONS

## (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions)

## Multiple Choice Questions

1. (c) Join O to P and Q . Join P to R . Draw $\mathrm{SP} \perp \mathrm{OQ}$. Now $\mathrm{SP}=\mathrm{QR}$, as they are opposite sides of rectangle PRQS.
$\mathrm{OP}=8 \mathrm{~cm}+4 \mathrm{~cm}=12 \mathrm{~cm} ; \mathrm{OS}=8 \mathrm{~cm}-4 \mathrm{~cm}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ In right triangle POS,
$\mathrm{SP}=\sqrt{\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OS}^{2}}=\sqrt{12^{2}-4^{2}}=8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \mathrm{QR}=8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$
2. (d) Since, two chords AB and CD of the circle are intersecting at P , when produced.
$\therefore \mathrm{PA} . \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PC} . \mathrm{PD}\left[\mathrm{Each}=(\text { length of the tangent from } \mathrm{P})^{2}\right]$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{PB}) .(\mathrm{PB})=(\mathrm{PD}+\mathrm{DC}) \cdot \mathrm{PD}$
$\Rightarrow(5+3)(3)=(2+x) 2 \Rightarrow 24=(2+x)(2) \Rightarrow 12=2+x$
$\Rightarrow x=10 \Rightarrow \mathrm{CD}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
3. (b) In right $\triangle \mathrm{OSP}$,
$\mathrm{OP}^{2}=\mathrm{PS}^{2}+\mathrm{OS}^{2}$
$r^{2}=225+(r-10)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad r^{2}=225+r^{2}-20 \mathrm{r}+100$
$\Rightarrow \quad 20 r=325 \Rightarrow 2 r=32.5$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-309.jpg?height=218&width=278&top_left_y=1338&top_left_x=776)

Hence, diameter $=32.5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
4. (a) Since, $\mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{PA}$ and $\mathrm{OB} \perp \mathrm{PB}$,
∴ In quadrilateral AOBP ,
$\Rightarrow 40^{\circ}+90^{\circ}+90^{\circ}+\angle \mathrm{AOB}=360^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}=140^{\circ}$
Also, $\angle \mathrm{AQB}=\frac{1}{2}$ of $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=70^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{AMB}=\frac{1}{2}$
of refle $x \angle \mathrm{AOB}=\frac{1}{2} \times\left(360^{\circ}-140^{\circ}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \times 220=110^{\circ}$
$[\because$ The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by the arc at any point on the remaining part of the cirlce.]
5. (b) Given, $\angle \mathrm{BPC}=\mathrm{b}^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ACP}=\mathrm{a}^{\circ}$.

Also, $\angle \mathrm{OPA}=\angle \mathrm{OAP}=\mathrm{b}^{\circ}$ (Angles in an isosceles triangle OAP, angle in alternate segment.)
$\angle \mathrm{CPO}=90^{\circ} \therefore \angle \mathrm{CPA}=90^{\circ}+\mathrm{b}^{\circ}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{ACP}, \angle \mathrm{ACP}=180^{\circ}-\left[\left(\mathrm{b}^{\circ}+90^{\circ}\right)+\mathrm{b}^{\circ}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^{\circ}+2 \mathrm{~b}^{\circ}=90^{\circ}$
6. (c) Since, PQ is tangent from a point P
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{OQP}=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{POR}=120^{\circ}$
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{POR}+\angle \mathrm{POQ}=180^{\circ}$
[linear pair]
$\angle \mathrm{POQ}=180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{OPQ}+\angle \mathrm{OQP}+\angle \mathrm{POQ}=180^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-309.jpg?height=235&width=283&top_left_y=2263&top_left_x=769)
[ ∵ Sum of angles of $\Delta=180^{\circ}$ ]
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{OPQ}+90^{\circ}+60^{\circ}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{OPQ}=30^{\circ}$
