## Match the Following

1. (A) → (s); (B) $\rightarrow(\mathbf{p}) ;(\mathbf{C}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{q}) ;(\mathbf{D}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{r})$
2. $(\mathrm{A}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{r})$; (B) $\rightarrow(\mathrm{p})$; (C) $\rightarrow(\mathrm{q})$; (D) $\rightarrow(\mathrm{s})$
3. (A) → (r); (B) $\rightarrow$ (q); (C) $\rightarrow$ (s); (D) $\rightarrow$ (p)
$O P \perp B C$, Let $A P=x \mathrm{~cm}$ and $P B=C P=y \mathrm{~cm}$
On applying Pythagoras in $\triangle A P B$ and $\triangle O P B$, we have $36=y^{2}+x^{2}$ and $81=(9-x)^{2}+y^{2}$
On solving these, we get, $x=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $y=4 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$.
Area of $\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2}(B C \times A P)=\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \sqrt{2} \times 2=8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
4. (A) → (s); (B) $\rightarrow(\mathbf{r}) ;(\mathbf{C}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{q}) ;(\mathbf{D}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{p})$

$$
A D=A F=x \mathrm{~cm}, B D=B E=y \mathrm{~cm}, C E=C F=z \mathrm{~cm}
$$

( ∵ tangents drawn from an exterior point to a circle are equal in length).

$$
A B=8 \mathrm{~cm} \Rightarrow A D+B D=8 \Rightarrow x+y=8
$$

Similarly, $B E+C E=10$
$\Rightarrow \quad y+z=10 \ldots$ (ii) and $z+x=12$

Adding equations (i) + (ii) + (iii),
$\Rightarrow \quad x+y+z=15$

Thus, on solving (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv)
we get, $A D=x \mathrm{~cm}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B E=y \mathrm{~cm}=3 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& C F=z \mathrm{~cm}=7 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. $(\mathbf{A}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{r}) ;(\mathbf{B}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{p}) ;(\mathbf{C}) \rightarrow(\mathbf{q})$

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. The diameter of a circle is twice as long as the radius. So, $\mathrm{DB}=5$ inches $\div 2=2.5$ inches
2. $\quad \angle \mathrm{PTQ}=70^{\circ}$
3. $\mathrm{PQ}^{2}=119 \Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{119}$
4. $8 \mathrm{~cm} .[\because$ For two intersecting chord ST.TR $=$ PT.TQ $]$
5. $\angle \mathrm{RPQ}=60^{\circ}$
6. $\frac{A T}{O T}=\cos 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} O T=A T \Rightarrow A T=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(4)=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
7. Since, $O P \perp A B \quad \therefore \quad O P$ bisects $A B$.
$\therefore A P=\frac{1}{2}(A B)=\frac{1}{2}(6)=3 \mathrm{~cm}$
In $\triangle O A P, O A^{2}=O P^{2}+A P^{2} \Rightarrow O P^{2}=25-9 \Rightarrow O P=4 \mathrm{~cm}$
8. $\because \quad \angle \mathrm{OPQ}=90^{\circ}-\angle \mathrm{QPT}=90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$
$(\because \mathrm{OP} \perp \mathrm{PT})$
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{POQ}=180^{\circ}-30^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=120^{\circ} \quad(\because \mathrm{OP}=\mathrm{OQ})$
Hence, $\angle \mathrm{PRQ}=\frac{360^{\circ}-120^{\circ}}{2}=\frac{240^{\circ}}{2}=120^{\circ}$
[Angle subtended at circumference]
9. Since, $\angle \mathrm{CAB}=30^{\circ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{AB}$ is a diameter to the circle with centre O .
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{ACB}=90^{\circ}$
Join OC.
So, $\mathrm{OC}=\mathrm{OA}$ (Radii)
$\angle \mathrm{CAB}=\angle \mathrm{ACO}=30^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-310.jpg?height=262&width=318&top_left_y=1027&top_left_x=676)

As the tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{OCP}=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PCA}+\angle \mathrm{OCA}=90^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{PCA}=90^{\circ}-30^{\circ} \quad \therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{PCA}=60^{\circ}$
10. Since, $\angle \mathrm{APB}=60^{\circ}$

But, $\angle \mathrm{APO}=\angle \mathrm{BPO}$
$(\because \triangle \mathrm{APO} \cong \triangle \mathrm{BPO})$
So, $\angle \mathrm{APO}+\angle \mathrm{BPO}=60^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-310.jpg?height=172&width=305&top_left_y=1466&top_left_x=689)
$\therefore \quad 2 \angle \mathrm{APO}=60^{\circ}(\because \angle \mathrm{APO}=\angle \mathrm{BPO}) \angle \mathrm{APO}=30^{\circ}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{APO}$,
$\sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{OP}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{OP}=2 \mathrm{a} \quad\left(\because \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}\right)$

## Short Answer Questions

1. Since, OABC is a rhombus
$\therefore \mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{OC}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
Now, $\mathrm{OD} \perp \mathrm{BC} \Rightarrow \mathrm{CD}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BC}=\frac{1}{2}(10)=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore \quad$ By pythagoras theorem, $\mathrm{OC}^{2}=\mathrm{OD}^{2}+\mathrm{DC}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{OD}^{2}=\mathrm{OC}^{2}-\mathrm{DC}^{2}=(10)^{2}-(5)^{2}=100-25=75$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{OD}=\sqrt{75}=5 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \quad$ area $(\triangle \mathrm{OBC})=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BC} \times \mathrm{OD}=\frac{1}{2}(10) \times 5 \sqrt{3}$
$=25 \sqrt{3}$ sq. cm.
So, Area of rhombus $=2$ (Area of $\Delta$ OBC)
$=2(25 \sqrt{3})=50 \sqrt{3}$ sq.cm.
2. $\mathrm{AP}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{AB}$
3. $r=2 \mathrm{~cm}$.
4. $\mathrm{AD}=7 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{BE}=5 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{CF}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$
5. Join $\mathrm{OA}, \mathrm{OB}$ and $\mathrm{OP} . \mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{AP}, \mathrm{OB} \perp \mathrm{BP}$.

In $\triangle \mathrm{OAP}, \angle \mathrm{OAP}=90^{\circ}, \mathrm{OP}^{2}=\mathrm{OA}^{2}+\mathrm{AP}^{2}=5^{2}+12^{2}=169$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{OP}=13 \mathrm{~cm}$.
In $\triangle \mathrm{OBP}, \angle \mathrm{OBP}=90^{\circ}, \mathrm{BP}^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OB}^{2}=13^{2}-3^{2}=160$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{BP}=4 \sqrt{10} \mathrm{~cm}$
6. Let the centre of the circle be ' $O$ ' and $A B$ is the given chord.
$A B=10 \mathrm{~cm}, O A=13 \mathrm{~cm}$
$O M \perp A B, O M=$ distance of chord $A B$ from centre.
As $O M \perp A B$, so, $M$ is the mid point of $A B$.
$\therefore A M=M B=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Now, in $\triangle A O M$
$A O^{2}=A M^{2}+O M^{2}$
$\mathrm{OM}^{2}=A O^{2}-A M^{2}$
$O M^{2}=13^{2}-5^{2}=169-25=144 \mathrm{~cm}$
7. Here, $\angle \mathrm{POR}=90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$
$(\because \mathrm{OP} \perp \mathrm{PR})$
As, $\frac{\mathrm{PR}}{\mathrm{OR}}=\sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{OR}=2 \mathrm{PR}=\mathrm{PR}+\mathrm{RQ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-310.jpg?height=205&width=308&top_left_y=1178&top_left_x=1509)

Hence, Proved
8. Since, O is the centre and $r$ is the radius of the circle.

And, PT and PS are tangents to the circle.
$\because \quad \mathrm{OP}=2 r$
To prove :
$\angle \mathrm{OTS}=\angle \mathrm{OST}=30^{\circ}$
Proof:
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-310.jpg?height=177&width=310&top_left_y=1493&top_left_x=1507)

As, PT and PS are tangents drawn to the circle.
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{OTP}=\angle \mathrm{OSP}=90^{\circ}$ [Tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.] In $\triangle \mathrm{OTP}$,
$\sin \angle \mathrm{OPT}=\frac{\mathrm{OT}}{\mathrm{OP}}=\frac{r}{2 r} \Rightarrow \sin \angle \mathrm{OPT}=\frac{1}{2}$
So, $\angle \mathrm{OPT}=30^{\circ}$
Then, $\angle \mathrm{OTP}+\angle \mathrm{OPT}+\angle \mathrm{TOP}=180^{\circ}$
(By angle sum property)
$\therefore \quad 90^{\circ}+30^{\circ}+\angle \mathrm{TOP}=180^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{TOP}=180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$
Now, $\triangle \mathrm{PTS}$ is an isosceles triangle and OP is the angle bisector of $\angle \mathrm{TPS}$.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{TS} \perp \mathrm{OP} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{OQT}=\angle \mathrm{OQS}=90^{\circ}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{OTQ}, \angle \mathrm{OQT}+\angle \mathrm{OTQ}+\angle \mathrm{TOQ}=180^{\circ}$
(By angle sum property)
$90^{\circ}+\angle \mathrm{OTQ}+60^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{OTQ}=180^{\circ}-150^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$
Similarly, $\angle \mathrm{OSQ}=30^{\circ}$
Hence, $\angle \mathrm{OTS}=\angle \mathrm{OST}=30^{\circ}$
9. Given : TP and TQ are tangents from an external point T .

Prove : $\angle \mathrm{PTQ}=2 \angle \mathrm{OPQ}$
We know that tangents from an external point to a circle are equal in length.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{TP}=\mathrm{TQ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \triangle \mathrm{TPQ}$ is an isosceles triangle.
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{TQP}=\angle \mathrm{TPQ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-311.jpg?height=186&width=364&top_left_y=488&top_left_x=685)
$=\frac{1}{2}\left(180^{\circ}-\angle \mathrm{PTQ}\right)=90^{\circ}-\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{PTQ}$

Now, $\angle \mathrm{OPT}=90^{\circ}$
[ ∵ The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact]
$\angle \mathrm{OPQ}=\angle \mathrm{OPT}-\angle \mathrm{TPQ}$
$=90^{\circ}-\left(90^{\circ}-\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{PTQ}\right) \quad$ [From eq. (i)]
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{OPQ}=\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{PTQ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{PTQ}=2 \angle \mathrm{OPQ} \quad$ Hence, proved.
10. Given: $A$ circle touches the side $B C$ of a $\triangle A B C$ at $P$ and extended sides AB and AC at Q and R .
To prove: $\mathrm{AQ}=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{BC}+\mathrm{CA}+\mathrm{AB})$
Proof: Lengths of tangents drawn to a circle from an external point are equal.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AQ}=\mathrm{AR}$
...(i) [Tangents from A]
$\mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{BQ}$
...(ii) [Tangents from B]
$\mathrm{CP}=\mathrm{CR}$
...(iii) [Tangents from C]
R.H.S. $=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{BC}+\mathrm{CA}+\mathrm{AB})$
$=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{BP}+\mathrm{PC}+\mathrm{CA}+\mathrm{AB})$
$=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BQ}+\mathrm{AC}+\mathrm{CR})$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-311.jpg?height=236&width=264&top_left_y=1530&top_left_x=790)
[from (ii) \& (iii)]
$=\frac{1}{2}[\mathrm{AQ}+\mathrm{AR}]=\frac{1}{2}[\mathrm{AQ}+\mathrm{AQ}][$ from (i) $]$
$=\frac{1}{2}[2 \mathrm{AQ}]=\mathrm{AQ}=$ L.H.S. Hence, proved.

## Long Answer Questions

1. (a) Since A, B, C, D are four points on circle having centre O and AC and BD intersect at O .
So, $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{OC}=\mathrm{OD}$.
Since EA is tangent at $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{EA}$.
In $\triangle \mathrm{AOB}, \mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{BAO}=\angle \mathrm{ABO}$. So, it is isosceles.
Similarly, $\triangle \mathrm{DOC}$ is also isosceles
$\angle \mathrm{ABD}=\angle \mathrm{CAB}=\angle \mathrm{BDC}=\angle \mathrm{ACD}$.
From right angle $\triangle \mathrm{EAC}$
$\angle \mathrm{ACE}=90^{\circ}-32^{\circ}=58^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{ACE}$ is $\angle \mathrm{ACD}=\angle \mathrm{ABD}$.
So, $\angle \mathrm{ABD}=58^{\circ}$
(b) $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\angle \mathrm{DOC}$ and $\angle \mathrm{AOB}+\angle \mathrm{OAB}+\angle \mathrm{OBA}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}+2 \times 58^{\circ}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}=180^{\circ}-116^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}=64^{\circ}=\angle \mathrm{DOC}$
$\angle \mathrm{AOD}=180^{\circ}-\angle \mathrm{DOC}=180^{\circ}-64^{\circ}$
So, $\angle \mathrm{AOD}=116^{\circ}$.
2. $\quad \angle \mathrm{PST}=\frac{1}{2}($ Reflex $\angle \mathrm{POT})$
[Angle at the circumference is half of angle at the centre]
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PST}=\frac{1}{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-311.jpg?height=255&width=352&top_left_y=598&top_left_x=1519)
$\left(360^{\circ}-140^{\circ}\right)=110^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{TOQ}=180^{\circ}-140^{\circ}=40^{\circ}$
[linear pair]
In $\triangle \mathrm{SPR}, \angle \mathrm{SRP}=180^{\circ}-\left(110^{\circ}+45^{\circ}\right)=25^{\circ}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{OTR}, \angle \mathrm{OTR}=180^{\circ}-\left(40^{\circ}+25^{\circ}\right)=115^{\circ}$
Quadrilateral PSTQ is a cyclic quadrilateral
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{TQP}=180^{\circ}-110^{\circ}=70^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{RQT}=180^{\circ}-70^{\circ}=110^{\circ} \quad$ [linear pair]
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{RTQ}=180^{\circ}-\left(25^{\circ}+110^{\circ}\right)=45^{\circ}$
3. $\mathrm{PB}=5+4=9$
$\mathrm{PT}^{2}=\mathrm{PA} . \mathrm{PB}=4 \times 9=36$
$\therefore \mathrm{PT}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
4. Let OP meet the circle at Q . Join AQ . As OP is equal to the diameter of the circle and OQ is radius, so $\mathrm{OQ}=\mathrm{QP}$ i.e. Q is mid-point of OP . Since PA is tangent to the circle at A and OA is its radius, $\mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{AP}$ i.e. $\angle \mathrm{OAP}=90^{\circ}$
In right triangle $\mathrm{OAP}, \mathrm{Q}$ is mid-point of hypotenuse,
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{AQ}=\mathrm{OQ}=\mathrm{QP}$
Also $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OQ}$
(radii of same circle)
$\Rightarrow \quad O A=O Q=A Q$
$\Rightarrow \quad \triangle \mathrm{OAQ}$ is equilateral
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOQ}=60^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-311.jpg?height=209&width=364&top_left_y=1530&top_left_x=1512)
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{AOP}=60^{\circ}$.
In $\triangle \mathrm{OAP}, \angle \mathrm{OPA}+\angle \mathrm{AOP}+\angle \mathrm{OAP}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{OPA}+60^{\circ}+90^{\circ}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{OPA}=30^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{APB}=60^{\circ} \quad(\therefore \mathrm{OP}$ is bisector of $\angle \mathrm{APB})$
Also $\mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{PB} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PAB}=\angle \mathrm{PBA}$.
In $\triangle \mathrm{PAB}, \angle \mathrm{PAB}+\angle \mathrm{PBA}+\angle \mathrm{APB}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \angle \mathrm{PAB}+60^{\circ}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{PAB}=60^{\circ}$
⇒ triangle ABP is equilateral.
5. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-311.jpg?height=254&width=476&top_left_y=2097&top_left_x=1144)

Since, B is mid-point of $\operatorname{arc}(\mathrm{ABC})$
So, $\angle 1=\angle 2$
[ ∵ Equal arcs subtend equal angles at centre]
OA $=$ OC $($ radii of circle $)$

OF = OF (common side)
$\Rightarrow \triangle \mathrm{OAF} \cong \triangle \mathrm{OCF}$
Therefore, $\angle \mathrm{AFO}=\angle \mathrm{CFO}=90^{\circ}$
Now, $\angle \mathrm{AFO}=\angle \mathrm{DBO}=90^{\circ}$
$(\because \mathrm{OB} \perp \mathrm{DE})$
As, these are corresponding angles
Hence, $\mathrm{AC} \| \mathrm{DE}$
6. $\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{X}=\mathrm{OX}$ (Radius of two equal circles)
$\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{D} \perp \mathrm{AC}$
Radius is perpendicular to the tangent.
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{OC} \perp \mathrm{AC}$
$\angle \mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{DA}=\angle \mathrm{ACO}=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad$ line $\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}$ is parallel to line OC .
(Corresponding angles)
In $\triangle \mathrm{ADO}^{\prime}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{ACO}^{\prime}, \angle \mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{DA}=\angle \mathrm{ACO}=90^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{DAO}^{\prime}=\angle \mathrm{CAO}$
(Common)
$\Delta \mathrm{ADO}^{\prime} \sim \Delta \mathrm{ACO}$
By AA similarity,
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{AD}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\mathrm{AO}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AO}}=\frac{\mathrm{DO}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{CO}}$

And, $\mathrm{AO}^{\prime}=\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{X}=\mathrm{XO}$ (Radius)
So, $\mathrm{AO}=\mathrm{AO}^{\prime}+\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{XO}=3 \mathrm{AO}^{\prime}$
$\frac{\mathrm{AO}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AO}}=\frac{\mathrm{AO}^{\prime}}{3 \mathrm{AO}^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{3}$

From (i) and (ii), $\frac{\mathrm{DO}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{CO}}=13$

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. $\because \mathrm{OD} \perp \mathrm{BE}$ and $\mathrm{AE} \perp \mathrm{BE}$
$\therefore \triangle \mathrm{BOD} \sim \triangle \mathrm{BAE}$
$\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{OB}}=\frac{\mathrm{AE}}{\mathrm{OD}}=\frac{\mathrm{BE}}{\mathrm{BD}}$
$\frac{32}{16}=\frac{\mathrm{AE}}{10}=\frac{\mathrm{BE}}{\mathrm{BD}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-312.jpg?height=236&width=257&top_left_y=1475&top_left_x=735)
$\mathrm{AE}=\frac{32}{16} \times 10=20 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\mathrm{BE}=2 \mathrm{BD}$
$\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{DE}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BD}$

In $\triangle \mathrm{BOD}$,
$\mathrm{BD}=\sqrt{\mathrm{OB}^{2}-\mathrm{OD}^{2}}=\sqrt{256-100}=\sqrt{156}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{ADE}, \mathrm{AD}^{2}=\sqrt{\mathrm{AE}^{2}+\mathrm{DE}^{2}}=\sqrt{400+156}$
$=\sqrt{556}=2 \sqrt{139} \mathrm{~cm}$.
2. (i) $\mathrm{PA} \times \mathrm{PB}=(\mathrm{PN}-\mathrm{AN})(\mathrm{PN}+\mathrm{BN})$
$=(P N-A N)(P N-A N)(\because B N=A N)$
$=\mathrm{PN}^{2}-\mathrm{AN}^{2}$
(ii) $\quad \mathrm{PN}^{2}-\mathrm{AN}^{2}=\left(\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{ON}^{2}\right)-\mathrm{AN}^{2}\left(\because \angle \mathrm{ONP}=90^{\circ}\right)$
$=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\left(\mathrm{ON}^{2}+\mathrm{AN}^{2}\right)=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OA}^{2}\left(\because \angle \mathrm{ONA}=90^{\circ}\right)$
$=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OT}^{2}(\because \mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OT}=$ radius $)$
(iii) From (i) and (ii),
$\mathrm{PA} \times \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OT}^{2}=\mathrm{PT}^{2} \quad\left(\because \angle \mathrm{OTP}=90^{\circ}\right)$
$\mathrm{PA} \times \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PT}^{2}$
3. $\because \mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{PR}, \triangle \mathrm{PQR}$ is isosceles
$\therefore \angle 1=\angle 2$, where $\angle 1=\angle \mathrm{PQR}$ and $\angle 2=\angle \mathrm{PRQ}$
But $\angle 1+\angle 2=180^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=150^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle 1=\frac{1}{2}\left(150^{\circ}\right)=75^{\circ}=\angle 2$
As $\angle 3=\angle 1 \quad$ (alternate angles as $\mathrm{SR} \| \mathrm{PQ}$ )
$\Rightarrow \angle 3=75^{\circ}$, where $\angle 3=\angle \mathrm{QRS}$

Also $\angle 4=\angle 1 \quad$ (angle in the alternate segment)
$\angle 4=75^{\circ}$, where $\angle 4=\angle \mathrm{RSQ}$

Since, $\angle \mathrm{RQS}+\angle 3+\angle 4=180^{\circ}$,
therefore, $\angle \mathrm{RQS}=180^{\circ}-\angle 3-\angle 4$
$=180^{\circ}-75^{\circ}-75^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$
4. ABC is a right angled triangle with $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=90^{\circ}$. Circle described on $A B$ as diameter cuts $A C$ at $P$ and tangent to this circle at $P$ intersects $B C$ in $D$.
We shall prove that $D$ is the mid-point of $B C$.
Here, $\angle 1+\angle 2=90^{\circ}$

$$
\left(\because \angle \mathrm{APB}=90^{\circ}, \text { angle in a semi-circle }\right)
$$

Also $\angle 1+\angle 3=90^{\circ} \quad\left(\because \angle \mathrm{ABC}=90^{\circ}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \angle 1+\angle 2=\angle 1+\angle 3$
$\Rightarrow \angle 2=\angle 3$

Also, $\angle 2=\angle 5$
(angle in the alternate segment)
and $\angle 5=\angle 4$
(vertically opposite angles)
$\Rightarrow \angle 2=\angle 4$

From (i) and (ii), $\angle 3=\angle 4$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{PD}$

But $\mathrm{PD}=\mathrm{BD}$ (Tangents from an external point are equal)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-312.jpg?height=464&width=305&top_left_y=1101&top_left_x=1516)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{BD} \Rightarrow \mathrm{D}$ is the mid-point of BC
⇒ The tangent at P bisects BC .
5. (i) Both Ram and Rahim planted equal number of trees as $\mathrm{PS}+\mathrm{QR}=\mathrm{PQ}+\mathrm{SR}$
(ii) Behaviour of contractor is not justified. Both should have been paid the same amount. Contracter is partial.

## HOTS Questions

1. Let the line QR intersects the bigger circle at S . Join PS . O is the mid-point of PQ .
$[\because \mathrm{PQ}$ is a diameter of the bigger circle] QR is a tangent to the smaller circle and OR is a radius through the point of contact R .
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{OR} \perp \mathrm{QR} \Rightarrow \mathrm{OR} \perp \mathrm{QS}$
Since OR is perpendicular to a chord
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-312.jpg?height=202&width=225&top_left_y=2055&top_left_x=1594)

QS of the bigger circle
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{QR}=\mathrm{RS}$
[ ∵ perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects the chord]
$\Rightarrow \quad R$ is the mid-point of QS.
∴ In $\triangle \mathrm{QSP}, \mathrm{O}$ is the mid-point of PQ and R is the mid-point of QS.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{OR}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{PS}$
[Since segment joining the mid-points of any two sides of a triangle is half of the third side]
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{PS}=2 \mathrm{OR}=2 \times 8 \mathrm{~cm}=16 \mathrm{~cm}$.
From right $\triangle \mathrm{OQR}, \mathrm{OR}^{2}+\mathrm{QR}^{2}=\mathrm{OQ}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AR}=\sqrt{105} \mathrm{~cm} \quad \therefore \quad \mathrm{RS}=\mathrm{QR}=\sqrt{105}$
$\mathrm{PR}^{2}=\mathrm{RS}^{2}+\mathrm{PS}^{2}=16^{2}+(\sqrt{105})^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{PR}=19 \mathrm{~cm}$
2. Let O be the centre of the circle. Join OC .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-313.jpg?height=272&width=451&top_left_y=836&top_left_x=424)

Since angle between the radius and the tangent is $90^{\circ}$, i.e.

$$
\angle \mathrm{OCT}=90^{\circ}
$$

$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{OCA}=\angle \mathrm{OCT}-\angle \mathrm{ACT}=90^{\circ}-48^{\circ}=42^{\circ}$
and $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OC}=\mathrm{OB}$ [Radii of the same circle]
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{OAC}=\angle \mathrm{OCA}=42^{\circ}$
[Angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
In $\triangle \mathrm{ACT}$, exterior $\angle \mathrm{CAB}=\angle \mathrm{ACT}+\angle \mathrm{CTA}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 48^{\circ}+36^{\circ}=84^{\circ} \quad \therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{OAB}=\angle \mathrm{CAB}-\angle \mathrm{OAC}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{OAB}=84^{\circ}-42^{\circ}=42^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{OBA}=\angle \mathrm{OAB}=42^{\circ}$
In isosceles $\triangle \mathrm{AOB}, \angle \mathrm{OBA}+\angle \mathrm{OAB}+\angle \mathrm{AOB}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow 42^{\circ}+42^{\circ}+\angle \mathrm{AOB}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{AOB}=180^{\circ}-84^{\circ}=96^{\circ}$.
Hence, the angle subtended by AB at centre is $96^{\circ}$.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. (i) (c) 2
(ii) (a) Since, PA and PB are tangent to the circle from the external point P .
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{PB}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{APB}, \mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{PB} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PAB}=\angle \mathrm{PBA}$
[angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
By angle sum property in $\triangle \mathrm{APB}$
$\angle \mathrm{PAB}+\angle \mathrm{PBA}+\angle \mathrm{APB}=180^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{PAB}+\angle \mathrm{PBA}+80=180^{\circ}[\because \angle \mathrm{PBA}=\angle \mathrm{PAB}]$
$2 \angle \mathrm{PAB}=180-80=100^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{PAB}=\frac{100^{\circ}}{2}=50^{\circ}$
(iii) (b) Since, tangents are perpendicular to radius of the circle
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{AP}$
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{OAP}=90^{\circ}$
Then, $\angle \mathrm{OAB}=\angle \mathrm{OAP}-\angle \mathrm{PAB}$
$=90^{\circ}-50^{\circ}=40^{\circ}$
$\left(\because \angle \mathrm{PAB}=50^{\circ}\right)$
(iv) (a) In $\triangle \mathrm{OAC}$
$\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OC}$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{OAC}=\angle \mathrm{OCA}$
[ ∵ angle opposite to equal sides are equal] By angle sum property in $\triangle \mathrm{OAC}$
$\angle \mathrm{OAC}+\angle \mathrm{OCA}+\angle \mathrm{AOC}=180^{\circ}$
$\angle \mathrm{OAC}+\angle \mathrm{OCA}+140=180^{\circ}$
$2 \angle \mathrm{OCA}=180^{\circ}-140^{\circ}=40$
$\angle \mathrm{OCA}=20^{\circ}$
(v) (b) $\angle \mathrm{OAC}=\angle \mathrm{OCA}=20^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{CAB}=\angle \mathrm{OAC}+\angle \mathrm{OAB}$
$=20^{\circ}+40^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$

Exercise 2
Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. (d) O is the centre of the circle. The radius of the circle is 5 cm .
PQ is tangent to the circle at
P. Then,
$\mathrm{OP}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{OPQ}=90^{\circ}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-313.jpg?height=221&width=330&top_left_y=1034&top_left_x=1544)

We are given that $\mathrm{OQ}=12 \mathrm{~cm}$.
By Pythagoras Theorem, we have
$\mathrm{PQ}^{2}=\mathrm{OQ}^{2}-\mathrm{OP}^{2}=(12)^{2}-(5)^{2}=144-25=119$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{119} \mathrm{~cm}$.
2. (b) In figure, TPOQ is a quadrilateral.

Here, $\angle \mathrm{OPT}=\angle \mathrm{OQT}=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PTQ}+\angle \mathrm{POQ}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{PTQ}+\angle 110^{\circ}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PTQ}=70^{\circ}$.
3. (a) In figure
$\Delta \mathrm{OAP} \cong \triangle \mathrm{OBP}$ (SSS congruence)
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{POA}=\angle \mathrm{POB}=\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{AOB}$
(i)

Also, $\angle \mathrm{AOB}+\angle \mathrm{APB}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{AOB}+80^{\circ}=180^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-313.jpg?height=291&width=208&top_left_y=1580&top_left_x=1663)
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}=100^{\circ}$

Then, from (i) and (ii), $\angle \mathrm{POA}=\frac{1}{2} \times 100^{\circ}=50^{\circ}$.
4. In figure AB is a diameter of the circle having centre at O . $\ell$ and $m$ are two tangents drawn to the circle at A and B respectively.
We have, $\mathrm{OA} \perp \ell$ and $\mathrm{OB} \perp m$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-313.jpg?height=227&width=291&top_left_y=2010&top_left_x=1580)

Here, $\angle 1=\angle 2$
(each $=90^{\circ}$ )
$\Rightarrow \ell \| m$ because the transversal AB makes equal interior alternate angles.
5. In figure, the two concentric circles have their centre at O . The radius of the larger circle is 5 cm and that of the smaller circle is $3 \mathrm{~cm} . \mathrm{AB}$ is a chord of the larger circle
and it touches the smaller circle at P . Join $\mathrm{OA}, \mathrm{OB}$ and OP .
Now, $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{OP}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{OP} \perp \mathrm{AB}$,
i.e., $\angle \mathrm{OPA}=\angle \mathrm{OPB}=90^{\circ}$
∵ Perpendicular from centre to a
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-314.jpg?height=218&width=222&top_left_y=406&top_left_x=772)
chord bisect the chord.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{BP}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{AB}$ or $\mathrm{AB}=2 \mathrm{AP}$.
By Pythagoras theorem,

$$
\mathrm{OA}^{2}=\mathrm{AP}^{2}+\mathrm{OP}^{2} \Rightarrow(5)^{2}=\mathrm{AP}^{2}+(3)^{2}
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AP}^{2}=25-9=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{AP}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AB}=2 \times 4 \mathrm{~cm}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$
6. Given : A parallelogram ABCD circumscribes a circle with centre O.
To prove : ABCD is rhombus
Proof : Since, tangents drawn to a circle from an exterior point are
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-314.jpg?height=248&width=250&top_left_y=895&top_left_x=744)
equal
$\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{AS}$
$\mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{BQ}$
$\mathrm{CR}=\mathrm{CQ}$
$\mathrm{DR}=\mathrm{DS}$

By adding (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv), we get
$\mathrm{AP}+\mathrm{BP}+\mathrm{CR}+\mathrm{DR}=\mathrm{AS}+\mathrm{BQ}+\mathrm{CQ}+\mathrm{DS}$
$(A P+B P)+(C R+D R)=(A S+D S)+(B Q+C Q)$
$\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{AD}+\mathrm{BC}$

Now, $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{CD}$ and $\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{BC}$
from (v) $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{BC}$. So, ABCD is rhombus.
Hence, proved.
7. In $\triangle \mathrm{LAO}$ and $\triangle \mathrm{AOC}, \mathrm{AL}=\mathrm{AC}$ (tangent from A are equal),

$$
\mathrm{AO}=\mathrm{AO}
$$

OL $=$ OC (each = radius)
$\Delta \mathrm{LAO} \cong \triangle \mathrm{CAO}$ (SSS)
$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{LAO}=\angle \mathrm{CAO}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{LAC}=2 \angle \mathrm{CAO}$

Similarly, $\angle \mathrm{MBO}=\angle \mathrm{CBO}$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{CBM}=2 \angle \mathrm{CBO}
$$

Now, $\angle \mathrm{LAC}+\angle \mathrm{CBM}=180^{\circ}$
( $\because \mathrm{PQ} \| \mathrm{RS}$ and ACB is a transversal line)
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \angle \mathrm{CAO}+2 \angle \mathrm{CBO}=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{CAO}+\angle \mathrm{CBO}=90^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{AOB}=180^{\circ}-90^{\circ}=90^{\circ}$
(sum of the angle of a triangle $=180^{\circ}$ )

$$
\therefore \angle \mathrm{AOB}=90^{\circ}
$$

8. In figure, PA and PB are the two tangents drawn to the circle from a point P outside the circle. O is the centre of the circle. A and B are the points of contact of the tangents. AB is the line segment
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-314.jpg?height=241&width=277&top_left_y=2261&top_left_x=717)
joining A and $\mathrm{B} . \angle \mathrm{AOB}$ is
subtended by the segment AB at the centre O .
Here, $\mathrm{OA} \perp \mathrm{AP}$ and $\mathrm{OB} \perp \mathrm{BP}$.
Now, in quadrilateral OAPB,

$$
\angle \mathrm{OAP}=\angle \mathrm{OBP}=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}+\angle \mathrm{APB}=180^{\circ}
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{APB}$ and $\angle$ aob are supplementary.
9. Let $\mathrm{AQ}=x$
$\therefore \mathrm{AP}=x$ (Tangent drawn from external point)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{BC}=6+8=14 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AC}=8+x, \\
& \begin{aligned}
\mathrm{AB}= & 6+x \\
\therefore \quad & 2 s=\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BC}+\mathrm{CA} \\
= & 6+x+8+x+14 \\
= & 28+2 x \Rightarrow s=14+x
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-314.jpg?height=246&width=269&top_left_y=575&top_left_x=1548)

Area of $\triangle A B C=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$
where $a=\mathrm{BC}, b=\mathrm{AC}, c=\mathrm{AB}$

$$
=\sqrt{(14+x)(x)(6)(8)}=\sqrt{48 x(14+x)}
$$

Now, area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{OBC})+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{OCA})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{14 \times 4}{2}+\frac{(8+x) \times 4}{2}+\frac{(6+x) \times 4}{2}=56+4 x \\
& \therefore \sqrt{48 x(14+x)}=56+4 x \\
& \text { Squaring both sides of eqn. (i), we get } \\
& 48 x(14+x)=16(14+x)^{2} \Rightarrow 3 x(14+x)=(14+x)^{2} \\
& \Rightarrow 3 x=14+x \Rightarrow 2 x=14 \Rightarrow x=7 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{AC}=8+7=15 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AB}=6+7=13 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

10. Given : A circle with centre O touches the sides $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}$,

CD and DA of a quadrilateral ABCD at $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{S}$.
T.P. : $\angle \mathrm{BOC}+\angle \mathrm{AOD}=180^{\circ}$

Const : Join OA, OB, OC and OD
P.F. : $\angle 1=\angle 2, \angle 3=\angle 4$,
$\angle 5=\angle 6, \angle 7=\angle 8$
But $\angle 1+\angle 2+\angle 3+\angle 4$
$+\angle 5+\angle 6+\angle 7+\angle 8=360^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-314.jpg?height=275&width=323&top_left_y=1512&top_left_x=1489)
( $\because$ sum of all angles around a point $\mathrm{O}=360^{\circ}$ )
$\therefore \quad 2(\angle 2+\angle 3+\angle 6+\angle 7)=360^{\circ}$
and $2(\angle 4+\angle 5+\angle 8+\angle 1)=360^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\angle 2+\angle 3)+(\angle 6+\angle 7)=180^{\circ}$
and $(\angle 4+\angle 5)+(\angle 8+\angle 1)=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{AOB}+\angle \mathrm{COD}=180^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{BOC}+\angle \mathrm{AOD}=180^{\circ}$

## Exemplar Questions

1. Let $A B$ be a chord of a circle which touches the other circle at $C$. Then $\triangle O C B$ is right triangle (see figure).
By Pythagoras theorem,
$O C^{2}+C B^{2}=O B^{2}$.
i.e., $\left(\frac{1}{2} d_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2} c\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2} d_{2}\right)^{2}$
(As C bisects AB )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-314.jpg?height=222&width=238&top_left_y=2234&top_left_x=1576)

Therefore, $d_{2}{ }^{2}=c^{2}+d_{1}{ }^{2}$
2. Let the circle touches the sides $B C, C A$ and $A B$ of the right triangle $A B C$ at $D, E$ and $F$ respectively, where $B C=a, C A=b$ and $A B=c$ (see figure). Then,
$A E=A F$ and $B D=B F$.
Also, $C E=C D=r$.
i.e., $\quad b-r=A F=A E$, $a-r=B F=B D$
or

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A B=c=A F+B F \\
& =b-r+a-r
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-315.jpg?height=296&width=268&top_left_y=488&top_left_x=781)

This gives $r=\frac{a+b-c}{2}$.
3. See Figure

By Theorem
$B Q=B P, C P=C R$
and $A Q=A R$
Now, $2 A Q=A Q+A R$
$=(A B+B Q)+(A C+C R)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-315.jpg?height=239&width=387&top_left_y=824&top_left_x=662)
$=A B+B P+A C+C P$
$=(B P+C P)+A C+A B=B C+C A+A B$
i.e., $A Q=\frac{1}{2}(B C+C A+A B)$

6. True. As $\angle B P A=90^{\circ}, \angle P A B=\angle O P A=60^{\circ}$.

Also, $O P \perp P T$.
Therefore, $\angle A P T=30^{\circ}$ and $\angle P T A=60^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$.

$\angle R S O=\angle S R O=90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$.
Therefore, $\angle Q S R=40^{\circ}+30^{\circ}=70^{\circ}$.

12. (d) Here, PQ is the common tangent to the three circles

So, $P R=\sqrt{\left(R_{1}+r\right)^{2}-\left(R_{1}-r\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{4 R_{1} r}$
$\mathrm{RQ}=\sqrt{4 \mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{r}}$

$$
\mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{4 \mathrm{R}_{1} \mathrm{R}_{2}}
$$

We know that $P Q=P R+R Q$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{4 \mathrm{R}_{1} \mathrm{R}_{2}}=\sqrt{4 \mathrm{R}_{1} \mathrm{r}}+\sqrt{4 \mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{r}}(\because \operatorname{From}(\mathrm{i}),(\mathrm{ii})$ and (iii))
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{1} \mathrm{R}_{2}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{1} \mathrm{r}}+\sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{r}}$
$\therefore \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{r}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}_{1}}}$
13. (b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-315.jpg?height=291&width=527&top_left_y=1297&top_left_x=1219)

Since, in figure, $\triangle A O B$ is a right angled triangle
$\therefore \quad O A^{2}=A B^{2}+O B^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(a+r)^{2}=(2 a-r)^{2}+a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+2 a r+r^{2}=4 a^{2}+r^{2}-4 a r+a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 6 a r=4 a^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{2 a}{3}$

$\angle A P B=180^{\circ}-(x+y)=60^{\circ}$

+\angle \mathrm{CBE}=90^{\circ}

17. (d) $A B C D$ is a square of sides
$A B=B C=C D=A D=1$ unit
A circle $\Gamma$ passing through $B$ and $C$ and touching $A D$ at $N$, where $B C$ is chord of circle.
$\therefore \quad O M$ bisects the chord $B C$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-316.jpg?height=257&width=284&top_left_y=774&top_left_x=708)
$\therefore C M=M B=\frac{1}{2} B C=\frac{1}{2}$

$$
(\because B C=1)
$$

$\Rightarrow \quad O M=M N-O N=1-r$
In $\triangle O M C, O C^{2}=O M^{2}+C M^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad r^{2}=(1-r)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad r^{2}=1-2 r+r^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow r=\frac{5}{8}$
18. (c) Since, radii of circles are 1, 2 and 3 units.
$\therefore \quad$ Side of $\triangle A B C$ are $A B=5, B C=3, A C=4$
$\therefore \triangle A B C$ is formed a right angled triangle, where $A B$ is hypotenuse of triangle. Since, circumradius of a
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-316.jpg?height=314&width=369&top_left_y=1407&top_left_x=625)
right angled triangle is the half of the hypotenuse.
$\therefore$ Circumradius $=\frac{1}{2} \times A B=\frac{1}{2} \times 5=2.5$
19. (c) In $\triangle M A N$
$(2-r)^{2}=x^{2}+r^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4+r^{2}-4 r=x^{2}+r^{2}$
$4(1-r)=x^{2} \Rightarrow 4-4 r=x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow r=\frac{4-x^{2}}{4}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{CAN},(1+x)^{2}+r^{2}=(1+r)^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-316.jpg?height=284&width=282&top_left_y=1896&top_left_x=712)
$1+x^{2}+2 x+r^{2}=1+r^{2}=2 r$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+2 x=2 r \Rightarrow x^{2}=2 r-2 x$
$\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}=2\left(\frac{4-x^{2}}{4}\right)-2 x \Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{4-x^{2}}{2}-2 x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x^{2}=4-x^{2}-4 x \Rightarrow 3 x^{2}+4 x-4=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 x^{2}+6 x-2 x-4=0 \Rightarrow 3 x(x+2)-2(x+2)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{2}{3}, x=-2$.

$$
r=4-\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}}{4}=\frac{4-\frac{4}{9}}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{36-4}{36}=\frac{32}{36} \Rightarrow \frac{8}{9} .
$$

20. (a) Here, O is the centre of circle.
Let r be the radius of circle

$$
\begin{aligned}
r & =\sqrt{(25)^{2}-(24)^{2}} \\
& =\sqrt{625-576}=\sqrt{49} \\
& =7 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-316.jpg?height=236&width=364&top_left_y=534&top_left_x=1443)

## More Than One Option Correct

1. (a, c, d) 2. (a, d)
2. (a, b, c) All the three statements are correct.
3. (a, b, c) All the three statements are false.
4. (a, b) Only statements given in option ' $a$ ' and ' $b$ ' are true.

$\mathrm{AM}=\frac{1}{2}(A B)=\frac{1}{2}(6)=3 \mathrm{~cm}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{OAM}$,
By Pythagoras theorem,
$\mathrm{OA}^{2}=\mathrm{OM}^{2}+\mathrm{AM}^{2}$
$\mathrm{OA}=\sqrt{16+9}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore \quad$ radius $=\mathrm{OA}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.

$(\mathrm{ON})^{2}=(\mathrm{OC})^{2}-(\mathrm{CN})^{2}$
$\mathrm{ON}=\sqrt{(5)^{2}-(4)^{2}}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$

$\therefore \angle B A D=90^{\circ}$
Now, $\angle B C D=90^{\circ}$ (angle in a semi-circle)
In $\triangle \mathrm{A} B D, \angle B A D+\angle A B D+\angle B D A=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow 90^{\circ}+30^{\circ}+\angle B D A=180^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle B D A=60^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \angle D B C=30^{\circ}$
Now, $\angle T A D=\angle A B D$
$\Rightarrow \angle T A D=30^{\circ}$
Also, $\angle P A B=\angle A D B$
$\Rightarrow \angle P A B=60^{\circ}$

## Integer Type Questions

1. Ans: 8

Given : $A P=4 \mathrm{~cm}, B P=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $A C=12 \mathrm{~cm}$
Since, the length of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal
$\therefore A P=A R \Rightarrow A R=4 \mathrm{~cm}$
$C R=A C-A R .=12-4=8 \mathrm{~cm}$
2. Ans: 3

In $\triangle \mathrm{A} B C$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
A C & =\sqrt{A B^{2}+B C^{2}}=\sqrt{289} \\
& =17 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad & B P=B Q=O P \\
& =O Q=r \mathrm{~cm} \\
A R= & A P=A B-B P \\
= & (8-r) \mathrm{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

( ∵ Lengths of tangents drawn from an external point are same.)
Also, $C R=C Q=C B-B Q=(15-r) \mathrm{cm}$
Now, $C R+A R=A C \Rightarrow r=3 \mathrm{~cm}$
3. Ans : 1

Given, $A B$ and $C D$ are two perpendicular diameters.
$T_{1}, T_{2}$ are parallel tangents at $C$ and $D$ respectively.
$T_{3}, T_{4}$ are parallel tangents at $A$ and $B$ respectively.

Since, $P R \| Q S$, therefore $A B \| R P$ and $A B \| Q S$
Since, $P R=2 \mathrm{~cm}$, therefore $A B=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
So, radius $=\frac{A B}{2}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-318.jpg?height=1684&width=104&top_left_y=855&top_left_x=194)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-318.jpg?height=1687&width=1339&top_left_y=857&top_left_x=442)

## DIVISION OF A LINE SEGMENT

In this concept, we will learn the method of dividing a line segment internally in a given ratio.
You can easily understand this concept through the following illustration.

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

Divide a line segment of length 12 cm internally in the ratio $3: 2$.

## SOLUTION :

Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line segment AB of length 12 cm by using a ruler.
(ii) Draw a ray AX making a suitable acute angle $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$ with AB .
(iii) Along AX , draw $5(=3+2)$ arcs intersecting the ray AX at $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}$, $A_{3}, A_{4}$ and $A_{5}$ such that $A A_{1}=A_{1} A_{2}=A_{2} A_{3}=A_{3} A_{4}=A_{4} A_{5}$
(iv) Join B to $\mathrm{A}_{5}$.
(v) Through $\mathrm{A}_{3}$ draw a line $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{P}$ parallel to $\mathrm{A}_{5} \mathrm{~B}$ by making $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{3} \mathrm{P}$ equal
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-319.jpg?height=439&width=570&top_left_y=541&top_left_x=1306)
to $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{5} \mathrm{~B}$, which intersects AB at point P .
The point P so obtained is the required point, which divides AB internally in the ratio $3: 2$.

## Alternative Method for Division of a Line Segment Internally in a Given Ratio

Use the following steps to divide a given line segment AB internally in a given ratio $m: n$, where $m$ and $n$ are natural numbers.

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment AB of given length.
(ii) Draw a ray AX making a suitable acute angle $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$ with AB .
(iii) Draw a ray $B Y$, on opposite side of $A X$ with respect to $A B$, parallel to $A X$ by making an angle $\angle \mathrm{ABY}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$.
(iv) Draw arcs intersecting the ray AX at $A_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots ., A_{m}$, and ray BY at $B_{1}$, $B_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots ., B_{n}$ such that
$A A_{1}=A_{1} A_{2}=\ldots . .=A_{m-1} A_{m}=B B_{1}=B_{1} B_{2}=\ldots . .=B_{n-1} B_{n}$
(v) Join $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{n}}$. Suppose it intersects AB at P .

The point P is the required point, which divide AB in the ratio $\mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}$ internally.

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-319.jpg?height=716&width=506&top_left_y=1091&top_left_x=1370)

Divide a line segment of length 6 cm internally in the ratio $3: 4$.

## SOLUTION :

Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line segment AB of length 6 cm .
(ii) Draw a ray AX making a suitable acute angle $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$ with AB .
(iii) Draw a ray BY parallel to AX by making $\angle \mathrm{ABY}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$.
(iv) Draw three arcs intersecting the ray AX at $A_{1}, A_{2}, A_{3}$ and 4 arcs intersecting the ray BY at $B_{1}, B_{2}, B_{3}, B_{4}$ such that
$A A_{1}=A_{1} A_{2}=A_{2} A_{3}=B B_{1}=B_{1} B_{2}=B_{2} B_{3}=B_{3} B_{4}$.
(v) Join $A_{3} B_{4}$. Suppose it intersects AB at a point P .

Then, P is the point dividing AB internally in the ratio $3: 4$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-319.jpg?height=189&width=234&top_left_y=2320&top_left_x=255)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-319.jpg?height=184&width=97&top_left_y=2325&top_left_x=479)

The concept of 'construction' is used in structural engineering and architecture engineering to build and design the houses, furniture instruments etc.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-319.jpg?height=732&width=568&top_left_y=1816&top_left_x=1306)

## CONSTRUCTION OF A TRIANGLE SIMILAR TO A GIVEN TRIANGLE (AS PER GIVEN SCALE FACTOR)

## Scale Factor

The ratio $m$ : $n$ of the side of the triangle to be constructed and the corresponding side of the given triangle is known as their scale factor.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-320.jpg?height=995&width=833&top_left_y=591&top_left_x=977)

In this construction, there are two different situations:
(I) Steps of construction when $\boldsymbol{m}<\boldsymbol{n}$ :
(i) Construct the given triangle ABC by using the given data.
(ii) Take any one of the three side of the given triangle as base. Let AB be the base of the given triangle ABC .
(iii) At one end, say A , of the base AB draw a ray AX below AB making a suitable acute angle with AB .
(iv) Along AX mark n points $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}, \ldots \ldots \ldots, \mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{n}}$ such that

$$
\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=\ldots \ldots \ldots=\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}-1} \mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{n}} .
$$

(v) Join $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}}$ to B .
(vi) Draw $A_{m} B^{\prime}$ parallel to $A_{n} B$ by making $\angle \mathrm{A} \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{B}$, which meets AB at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
(vii) From $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$, draw $\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to BC by making $\angle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{ABC}$, which meets AC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$. Triangle $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle, each of whose sides is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}\right)^{\text {th }}$ of the corresponding side of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

Construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in which $\mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{BC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AC}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$. Now, construct a triangle similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ such that each of its sides is two-third of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.

## SOLUTION :

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) With A as centre and radius $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw an arc above AB .
(iii) With B as centre and radius $=6 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw another arc, intersecting the arc drawn in step (ii) at C.
(iv) Join A to C and B to C to obtain $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
(v) Below AB , draw a ray AX making a suitable acute angle with AB on opposite side of C with respect to AB .
(vi) Draw three arcs intersecting the ray AX at $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}$ such that $\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}= \mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{3}$.
(vii) Join $\mathrm{A}_{3}$ to B .
(viii) Draw $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}^{\prime}$ parallel to $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}^{\prime}$ by making $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{2} \mathrm{~B}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{3} \mathrm{~B}$, which meets AB at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
(ix) From $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$, draw $\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to BC by making $\angle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{ABC}$, which meets AC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
$\Delta \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle, each of the whose sides is two-third of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-320.jpg?height=762&width=442&top_left_y=1676&top_left_x=1375)
(II) Steps of construction when $\boldsymbol{m}>\boldsymbol{n}$ :
(i) Construct the given triangle by using the given data.
(ii) Take any one of the three sides of the given triangle and consider it as the base. Let AB be the base of the given triangle ABC .
(iii) At one end, say A , of base AB draw a ray AX making a suitable acute angle with base AB on the opposite side of the vertex C with respect to AB .
(iv) Draw m arcs intersecting the ray AX at $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}, \ldots \ldots \ldots, \mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{m}}$ such that $\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=\ldots \ldots \ldots=\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}-1} \mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{m}}$.
(v) Join $A_{n}$ to $B$
(vi) Draw a line through $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}}$ parallel to $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{B}$ by making $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{AA}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{B}$, which intersects the extended line segment AB at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
(vii) Draw a line through $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ parallel to BC by making $\angle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{ABC}$, which intersects the extended line segment AC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$
(viii) $\Delta \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ so obtained is the required triangle.

## ILLUSTRATION : 4

Draw a triangle ABC with side $\mathrm{BC}=7 \mathrm{~cm} . \angle \mathrm{B}=45^{\circ}, \angle \mathrm{A}=105^{\circ}$. Construct a triangle whose sides are (4/3) times the corresponding side of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.

## SOLUTION :

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw $\mathrm{BC}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw a ray BX and CY such that $\angle \mathrm{CBX}=45^{\circ}$ and

$$
\angle \mathrm{BCY}=180^{\circ}-\left(45^{\circ}+105^{\circ}\right)=30^{\circ}
$$

Suppose BX and CY intersect each other at A .
$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ so obtained is the given triangle.
(iii) Draw a ray BZ making a suitable acute angle with BC on opposite side of vertex A with respect to BC .
(iv) Draw four arcs intersecting the ray BZ at $\mathrm{B}_{1}, \mathrm{~B}_{2}, \mathrm{~B}_{3}, \mathrm{~B}_{4}$ such that $\mathrm{BB}_{1} =\mathrm{B}_{1} \mathrm{~B}_{2}=\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{3}=\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{~B}_{4}$.
(v) Join $\mathrm{B}_{3}$ to C and draw a line through $\mathrm{B}_{4}$ parallel to $\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{C}$ by making $\angle \mathrm{BB}_{4} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{BB}_{3} \mathrm{C}$, which intersects the extended line segment BC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
(vi) Draw a line through $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to CA by making $\angle \mathrm{BC}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ equal to
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-321.jpg?height=1439&width=599&top_left_y=375&top_left_x=1277)
$\angle \mathrm{BCA}\left(=30^{\circ}\right)$, which intersects the extended line segment BA at $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$. Triangle $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ so obtained is the required triangle.

## Check Your Knowledge

Time to

Construct a triangle ABC in which $\mathrm{BC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}, \angle \mathrm{~A}=60^{\circ}$ and altitude through A is 4 cm .
Step 1: Draw a line segment, $\mathrm{BC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Step 2: Draw BL such that $\angle \mathrm{CBL}=60^{\circ}$.
Step 3: Draw BM such that $\angle \mathrm{MBL}=90^{\circ}$.
Step 4: Draw a perpendicular bisector $(\mathrm{XY})$ of BC which intersects BC at the point P .
Step 5: Mark the intersecting point of XY and BM as O .
Step 6: With O as centre and OB as radius draw a circle.
Step 7: Mark the point N on XY such that $\mathrm{PN}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Step 8: Through N, draw a line parallel to BC which intersects the circle at points A and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$.
Step 9: Join $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{AC}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}$.
Step 10: Now, ABC or $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}$ is required triangle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-321.jpg?height=626&width=414&top_left_y=1928&top_left_x=1464)

## To construct an equilateral triangle circumscribed over a circle of radius $\boldsymbol{r} \mathbf{~ c m}$.

## Steps of Construction

Step 1: Draw a circle of radius $r \mathrm{~cm}$ with centre O .
Step 2: Draw radii OP, OQ and OR such that

$$
\angle \mathrm{POR}=\angle \mathrm{ROQ}=120^{\circ} .\left(\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{QOP}=120^{\circ}\right)
$$

Step 3: At $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and R draw perpendiculars to $\mathrm{OP}, \mathrm{OQ}$ and OR respectively to from $\Delta \mathrm{ABC} . \Delta \mathrm{ABC}$ is the required circumscribing equilateral triangle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-322.jpg?height=437&width=419&top_left_y=470&top_left_x=1366)

## CONSTRUCTION OF A TANGENT TO A CIRCLE (AT A GIVEN POINT)

Case (a): To draw the tangent to a circle at a given point on it, when the centre of the circle is known :
Required : To draw the tangent to the circle at P .
Steps of construction :
(i) Take a point O as centre and draw a circle of given radius.
(ii) Take a point P on the circle.
(iii) Join OP.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-322.jpg?height=282&width=616&top_left_y=1014&top_left_x=1201)
(iv) Draw a line AB perpendicular to OP at the point P . Line APB is the required tangent at P .

Circle can have at most 2 parallel tangents, one at a point on it and the other at a point diametrically ?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-322.jpg?height=159&width=95&top_left_y=1420&top_left_x=424)
opposite to it.

## ILLUSTRATION : 5

Draw a circle of diameter $\mathbf{A B}=\mathbf{6 ~ c m}$ with centre $\mathbf{O}$. Through $\mathbf{B}$, draw tangent to the circle.

## SOLUTION :

Given : A circle of diameter AB with centre O .
Required : To draw tangent to the circle at B .
Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a circle with O as centre and radius equal to $\left(\frac{6}{2}=3\right) \mathrm{cm}$.
(ii) Draw a diameter AOB .
(iii) Draw $\mathrm{CD} \perp \mathrm{AB}$ such that $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=90^{\circ}$

So, CD is the required tangent.
Case (b) : To draw the tangent to a circle at a given point on it, when the centre of the circle is
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-322.jpg?height=401&width=307&top_left_y=1585&top_left_x=1500)
not known.
Given : A circle and a point P on it.
Required : To draw the tangent to the circle at P .
Steps of construction :
(i) Draw any chord PQ through P and join P and Q to a point R in major $\operatorname{arc} \overparen{\mathrm{PQ}}$ (or minor $\operatorname{arc} \overparen{\mathrm{PQ}}$ ).
(ii) Construct $\angle \mathrm{QPB}$ equal to $\angle \mathrm{PRQ}$ and on opposite side of R with respect to chord PQ .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-322.jpg?height=356&width=752&top_left_y=2138&top_left_x=1074)

Produce BP to A to get the required tangent APB

## To draw the circum-circle of a given triangle.

## Steps of Construction

Step 1: Draw $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with the given measurements.
Step 2: Draw perpendicular bisectors of the two of the sides, say AB and AC to intersect at O .

Step 3: Taking O as centre, and the radius equal to AO or BO or CO , draw a circle. The circle passes through all the vertices $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C of the triangle. ∴ The circle drawn is the required circum-circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-323.jpg?height=429&width=398&top_left_y=497&top_left_x=1457)

## CONSTRUCTION OF TANGENTS TO A CIRCLE FROM A POINT OUTSIDE THE CIRCLE

In this situation, when the point lies outside the circle, there will be two tangents to the circle from this point.
In this construction, again we have two different situations:
Case (a) : To draw the tangents to a circle from a point outside it (external point), when its centre is known.
Given : A circle with centre O and a point P outside it.
Required : To construct the tangents to the circle from point P .
Steps of construction :
(i) Join OP and bisect it. Let M be the mid-point of OP .
(ii) Taking M as centre and MO as radius, draw a circle which intersect the given circle in two points, say A and B .
(iii) Join P to A and P to $\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{PA}$ and PB are the required tangents from P to given circle .

## ILLUSTRATION : $\mathbf{6}$

Draw a circle of radius 2.5 cm . From a point $\mathrm{P}, 6 \mathrm{~cm}$. apart from the centre of the circle, draw two tangents to the circle.

## SOLUTION :

Given : A point P is at a distance of 6 cm . from the centre of a circle with radius 2.5 cm .

Required : To draw two tangents to the circle from the given point P .
Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line segment OP of length 6 cm .
(ii) With centre O and radius equal to 2.5 cm , draw a circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-323.jpg?height=908&width=552&top_left_y=1082&top_left_x=1322)
(iii) Draw the right bisector of OP , which intersects OP at M .
(iv) Taking M as centre and MO as radius, draw a circle, which intersect the given circle in two points, say A and B .
(v) Join PA and PB . These are the required tangents from P to the given circle.

Case (b): To draw the tangents to a circle from a point outside it (external point), when its centre is not known.
Given : A circle and a point P outside it.
Required : To construct the tangents to the circle from point P .
Step of construction :
(i) Through $P$ draw a secant $P A B$, which intersects the circle at points $A$ and $B$ (say).
(ii) Produce $A P$ to a point C such that $A P=P C$ i.e., $P$ is the mid-point of $A C$.
(iii) Draw a semi-circle with $B C$ as diameter.
(iv) Draw $P D \perp C B$, which intersects the semi-circle at $D$.
(v) With $P$ as centre and $P D$ as radius draw arcs to intersect the given circle at $T$ and $T^{\prime}$.
(vi) Join P to T and $\mathrm{T}^{\prime}$. Then PT and $\mathrm{PT}^{\prime}$ are the required tangents.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-323.jpg?height=394&width=432&top_left_y=2149&top_left_x=1439)

## ILLUSTRATION : 7

Draw a circle of radius 4 cm . Take a point $P$ outside the circle. Without using the centre of the circle, draw two tangents to the circle from point $\boldsymbol{P}$.

## SOLUTION :

Given : A circle with radius 4 cm and a point P outside it.
Required : To draw two tangents to the circle from the given point P .

## Step of construction :

(i) Draw a circle of radius 4 cm .
(ii) Take a point $P$ outside the circle and draw a secant $P A B$, which intersects the circle at $A$ and $B$.
(iii) Produce $A P$ to $C$ such that $A P=C P$.
(iv) Draw a semi-circle with $C B$ as diameter.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-324.jpg?height=407&width=458&top_left_y=484&top_left_x=1356)
(v) Draw $P D \perp C B$, which intersects the semi-circle at $D$.
(vi) With $P$ as centre and $P D$ as radius draw arcs to intersect the given circle at $T$ and $T^{\prime}$.
(vii) Join $P$ to $T$ and $T^{\prime}$. Then, $P T$ and $P T^{\prime}$ are the required tangents.

To construct the direct common tangents to the circles of radii 3.5 cm and 1.5 cm whose centres are 6.5 cm apart.

Step 1: Draw line segment, $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}=6.5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Step 2: Draw circles with radii 3.5 cm and 1.5 cm respectively with $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ as centres.
Step 3: Bisect $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}$ at P and draw a circle taking P as centre and radius equal to $\mathrm{PC}_{1}$ or $\mathrm{PC}_{2}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-324.jpg?height=329&width=680&top_left_y=1213&top_left_x=1137)

Step 4: Draw another circle with radius equal to $(3.5-1.5)=2 \mathrm{~cm}$, taking the centre of larger circle $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ intersecting the circle drawn in step 3 at A and B .
Step 5: Join $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{~A}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{~B}$ and produce $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{~A}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{~B}$ to meet the outer circle at S and T respectively.
Step 6: Draw line segments $C_{2} U$ and $C_{2} V$, such that $C_{2} U \| C_{1} S$ and $C_{2} V \| C_{1} T$, where $U, V$ arc points on the circle with centre $\mathrm{C}_{2}$.
Step 7: Join SU and TV. These are the required direct common tangents.

## Learn <br> ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-324.jpg?height=88&width=104&top_left_y=1811&top_left_x=968) More

Locute the centre of a circular disk.
Hint: Perpendicular bisectors of two non parallel chords of circle passes through the centre of the circle.

## Steps of construction:

(i) Draw two non parallel chords of any length AB and PQ .
(ii) Draw perpendicular bisectors XY and LM of AB and PQ respectively, which intersect at point O .
So, O is the required centre of circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-324.jpg?height=351&width=556&top_left_y=1950&top_left_x=1231)

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. Draw a triangle ABC with side $\mathrm{BC}=8 \mathrm{~cm}, \angle \mathrm{~B}=45^{\circ}, \angle \mathrm{A}=75^{\circ}$. Construct a triangle whose sides are (4/3) times the corresponding side of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
Sol. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw $\mathrm{BC}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw a ray BX and CY such that $\angle \mathrm{CBX}=45^{\circ}$ and

$$
\angle \mathrm{BCY}=180^{\circ}-\left(45^{\circ}+75^{\circ}\right)=60^{\circ}
$$

Suppose BX and CY intersect at $\mathrm{A} . \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, so obtained is the given triangle.
(iii) Draw a ray BZ making an acute angle with BC on opposite side of vertex A with respect to BC .
(iv) Draw four (greater of 4 and 3 in 4/3) arcs intersecting the rays BZ at $\mathrm{B}_{1}, \mathrm{~B}_{2}$, $\mathrm{B}_{3}, \mathrm{~B}_{4}$ such that $\mathrm{BB}_{1}=\mathrm{B}_{1} \mathrm{~B}_{2}=\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{3}=\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{~B}_{4}$.
(v) Join $\mathrm{B}_{3}$ to C and draw a line through $\mathrm{B}_{4}$ parallel to $\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{C}$, intersecting the extended line segment BC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
(vi) Draw a line through $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to CA intersecting the extended line segment BA at $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$.
Triangle $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ so obtained is the required triangle.
2. Construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in which $\mathrm{AB}=6 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{BC}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AC}=7.5 \mathrm{~cm}$. Now construct a triangle similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ such that each of its sides is third-fourth
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-325.jpg?height=659&width=465&top_left_y=646&top_left_x=1411)
of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. Also prove your assertion.
Sol. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{BC}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) With B as centre and radius $=\mathrm{AB}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw an arc.
(iii) With C as centre and radius $=\mathrm{AC}=7.5 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw another arc, intersecting the arc drawn in step (ii) at the point A .
(iv) Join A to B and C to obtain $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
(v) Below BC draw ray BX , making an acute angle $\angle \mathrm{CBX}$.
(vi) Draw four arcs intersecting the ray BX at $\mathrm{B}_{1}, \mathrm{~B}_{2}, \mathrm{~B}_{3}, \mathrm{~B}_{4}$ such that $\mathrm{BB}_{1}=\mathrm{B}_{1} \mathrm{~B}_{2}=\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{3}=\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{~B}_{4}$
(vii) Join $\mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{C}$.
(viii) Since we have to construct a triangle each of whose sides is third-fourth of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. Hence draw $\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{C}$.
(ix) From $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$, draw $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{CA}$, intersecting BA at point $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$. Then $\triangle \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
3. Construct a triangle similar to a given equilateral $\triangle \mathrm{PQR}$ with side 5 cm such that each
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-325.jpg?height=493&width=376&top_left_y=1420&top_left_x=1500)
of its sides is $\frac{\mathbf{6}}{\mathbf{7}}$ th of the corresponding side of $\triangle \mathrm{PQR}$.
Sol. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{QR}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
(ii) With Q as centre and radius $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw an arc.
(iii) With R as centre and radius $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw another arc intersecting the arc drawn in step (ii) at the point P .
(iv) Join PQ and PR to obtain $\triangle \mathrm{PQR}$.
(v) Below QR , draw ray QX making an acute angle $\angle \mathrm{RQX}$.
(vi) Draw seven arcs intersecting the ray QX at $\mathrm{Q}_{1}, \mathrm{Q}_{2}, \ldots . \mathrm{Q}_{7}$; such that
$\mathrm{QQ}_{1}=\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{Q}_{2}=\mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{Q}_{3}=\ldots . .=\mathrm{Q}_{6} \mathrm{Q}_{7}$
(vii) Join $\mathrm{Q}_{7} \mathrm{R}$.
(viii) Draw $\mathrm{Q}_{6} \mathrm{R}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{Q}_{7} \mathrm{R}$, intersecting QR at $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$
(ix) From $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ draw $\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{RP}$, intersecting the QP at $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}$.

We get required $\triangle P Q R^{\prime}$ similar to $\triangle P Q R$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-325.jpg?height=477&width=364&top_left_y=2039&top_left_x=1507)
4. Construct a triangle similar to a given triangle ABC with its sides equal to $\frac{\mathbf{7}}{\mathbf{5}}$ th of the corresponding sides of triangle ABC .
Sol. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a ray BX making a suitable acute angle with BC on opposite side of A.
(ii) Draw seven arcs intersecting the ray BX at $\mathrm{X}_{1}, \mathrm{X}_{2}, \ldots \ldots, \mathrm{X}_{7}$ such that $\mathrm{BX}_{1}=\mathrm{X}_{1} \mathrm{X}_{2}=\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}=\mathrm{X}_{3} \mathrm{X}_{4}=\mathrm{X}_{4} \mathrm{X}_{5}=\mathrm{X}_{5} \mathrm{X}_{6}=\mathrm{X}_{6} \mathrm{X}_{7}$
(iii) Join $\mathrm{X}_{5}$ to C and draw a line $\mathrm{X}_{7} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to $\mathrm{X}_{5} \mathrm{C}$, which intersects BC producted at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
(iv) Draw a line $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ parallel to CA to intersect BA producted at $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$. Then $\Delta \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ will be the required triangle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-326.jpg?height=538&width=618&top_left_y=470&top_left_x=1199)
5. Construct a triangle ABC whose sides are $7.5 \mathrm{~cm}, 7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 6.5 cm . Construct a triangle similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with sides $\frac{\mathbf{2}}{\mathbf{3}} \mathbf{r d}$ of the corresponding sides of triangle $\triangle \mathbf{A B C}$.
Sol. Steps of construction :
(i) Take $\mathrm{BC}=7.5 \mathrm{~cm}$. With B as centre and radius 7 cm draw an arc.
(ii) With C as centre and radius 6.5 cm , draw arc intersecting the arc drawn in step (i) at point A . Then $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is the required triangle.
(iii) Draw a ray BX making an acute angle with BC opposite side of A with respect to BC .
(iv) Draw three arcs intersecting the ray BX at $\mathrm{X}_{1}, \mathrm{X}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{X}_{3}$ such that $\mathrm{BX}_{1}=\mathrm{X}_{1} \mathrm{X}_{2}= \mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}$.
(v) Join $\mathrm{X}_{3} \mathrm{C}$.
(vi) Draw $\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{X}_{3} \mathrm{C}$, which intersects BC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-326.jpg?height=506&width=398&top_left_y=1123&top_left_x=1416)
(vii) Draw $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{CA}$, which intersects BA at $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$. Then $\triangle \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
6. Let $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ be a right triangle in which $\mathrm{AB}=3 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{BC}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{B}=\mathbf{9 0}^{\circ}$. BD is the perpendicular from B to AC . The circle through $B, C, D$ is drawn. Construct the tangents from $A$ to this circle.
Sol. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw line segments $\mathrm{BC}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AB}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$ perpendicular to BC . Join $\mathrm{AC} . \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is the right triangle.
(ii) Draw $\mathrm{BD} \perp \mathrm{AC}$
(iii) Draw perpendicular bisector of BC , which intersects BC at E .
(iv) Draw a circle with centre E and radius EB (or EC ) clearly if passes through the point $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D . Now AB is the tangent to the circle drawn at B
(v) With centre A and radius equal to AB , draw an arc intersecting the circle at F (other than the point B ).
(vi) Draw ray AF .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-326.jpg?height=497&width=479&top_left_y=1813&top_left_x=1338)

Thus AB and AF are two required tangents.

## Multiple Choice Questions

1. To draw tangent from an exterior point ' P ' to a circle using the centre ' O ' of the circle, first we
(a) join P to O .
(b) draw tangent from P to the circle.
(c) draw secant from P which intersects the circle at two points.
(d) draw perpendicular at P to PO .
2. To divide a line segment AB of any length in the ratio 2 : 5, first draw a ray AX (or BX ) making an acute angle with AB . Then, draw 7 arcs intersecting the ray at $\mathrm{X}_{1}, \mathrm{X}_{2}$, $\mathrm{X}_{3}, \mathrm{X}_{4}, \mathrm{X}_{5}, \mathrm{X}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{X}_{7}$ such that $\mathrm{AX}_{1}=\mathrm{X}_{1} \mathrm{X}_{2}=\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}= X_{3} X_{4}=X_{4} X_{5}=X_{5} X_{6}=X_{6} X_{7}$. Then join $X_{7} B$, then after
(a) join $X_{2} B$
(b) draw $\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{~B}^{\prime}$ parallel to $\mathrm{X}_{7} \mathrm{~B}$, which intersects AB at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
(c) join $\mathrm{X}_{5} \mathrm{~B}$
(d) draw perpendicular from $X_{2}$ to $A B$.
3. To draw a pair of tangents to a circle which are inclined to each other at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ it is required to draw tangents at end points of those two radii of the circle, the angle between them should be-
(a) $60^{\circ}$
(b) $\quad 90^{\circ}$
(c) $45^{\circ}$
(d) $135^{\circ}$
4. Arrange the following steps of construction to construct a triangle whose sides are $\frac{3}{4}$ of the corresponding sides of an isosceles triangle of base 9 cm and altitude 5 cm .

## Steps:

(A) Mark four equal points $\mathrm{M}_{1}, \mathrm{M}_{2}, \mathrm{M}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{4}$ on BM such that $B M_{1}=M_{1} M_{2}=M_{2} M_{3}=M_{3} M_{4}$.
(B) Construct a triangle ABC with $\mathrm{BC}=9 \mathrm{~cm}$, altitude $\mathrm{AD}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AC}$.
(C) Join $\mathrm{M}_{4}$ and C .
(D) Through B , draw a ray BM such that $\angle \mathrm{CBM}$ is less than $90^{\circ}$.
(E) Through $\mathrm{M}_{3}$, draw $\mathrm{M}_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{M}_{4} \mathrm{C}$ intersecting BC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
(F) Through $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ draw $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{CA}$ intersecting AB in $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$.
(a) $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}$
(b) $\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{F}$
(c) $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}$
(d) $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}$
5. Which is true?

In order to divide a line segment $\mathrm{AB}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$, in the radio $3: 5$, we draw a ray AX making an acute angle $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$. Along $A X$ mark off.... points $A_{1}, A_{2}, A_{3}$ etc. such that $A A_{1} =A_{1} A_{2}=$ etc., The number of points is
(a) 3
(b) 5
(c) 8
(d) 2
6. To draw a triangle similar to a given triangle, first we
(a) draw incircle of the triangle
(b) draw perpendicular bisector of the base
(c) draw a median of the triangle.
(d) draw a ray making an acute angle preferably with base side and below it.

## True / False

## DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. We can not draw the tangent to a circle at a given point on it, when the centre of the circle is not known.
2. We can draw the tangents to a circle from a point outside it (external point), only when its centre is known.
3. Construction of a triangle is not possible if $\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BC}<\mathrm{AC}$.
4. To draw the perpendicular bisector of line segment PQ , we open the compass more than $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{PQ}$.

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. Divide a line segment of length 10 cm in the ratio of 3 : 5 internally.
2. Divide a line segment of 7 cm length externally in the ratio of $3: 5$
3. Take a point P at a distance of 8 cm from the centre of a circle of radius 3 cm and from P draw two tangents PA and PB to the circle. Measure the lengths of each tangent.
4. Draw a pair of tangents to a circle of radius 2.8 cm , which are inclined to each other at $60^{\circ}$.
5. Draw a circle with centre O and radius 2.2 cm . Take a point P on it. Draw a tangent to the circle at the point P .

## Short Answer Questions

1. Construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in which $\mathrm{BC}=6.5 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AB}=4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=60^{\circ}$. Construct a triangle similar to this triangle whose sides are $\frac{3}{4}$ of the corresponding sides of the triangle ABC .
2. Draw a pair of tangents to a circle of radius 5 cm which are inclined to each other at an angle of $30^{\circ}$.
3. Draw a circle of radius 3 cm . Take two points P and Q on one of its both sides extended diameter each at a distance of 7 cm from the centre. Draw tangents to the circle from these two points P and Q .
4. Draw a line segment of length 8 cm and divide it internally in the ratio $4: 5$.

## Long Answer Questions

1. Draw a line segment AB of length 8 cm . Taking A as centre, draw a circle of radius 4 cm and taking $B$ as centre, draw another circle of radius 3 cm . Construct tangents to each circle from the centre of the other circle.
(v) With centre O and radius OB (or OR ), draw a semi-circle.
(w) With centre P and radius PQ draw arcs intersecting the circle drawn using the bangle at S and T .
2. Construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in which $\mathrm{AB}=4 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{BC}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$. Now construct a triangle similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ such that each of its sides is two-third of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$. Also, prove your assertion.
3. Construct an isosceles triangle whose base is 8 cm and altitude 4 cm and then another triangle whose sides are 3/2 of the corresponding sides of the isosceles triangle.
4. Construct two tangents to a circle of radius 4 cm from a point on the concentric circle of radius 6 cm and measure its length. Also verify the measurement by actual calculation.
5. Given a triangle ABC in which $\mathrm{AB}=2.8 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{BC}=4.3$ cm and $\angle \mathrm{B}=90^{\circ} . \mathrm{BD}$ is the perpendicular from B to AC . A circle is passing through points $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D . Construct the tangents from A to the circle.
6. Draw right triangle in which the sides (other than hypotenuse) are of lengths 3 cm and 4 cm , then construct another similar triangle whose sides are $\frac{5}{3}$ times the corresponding sides of the given triangle.
7. Construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in which $\mathrm{AB}=6 \mathrm{~cm}, \angle \mathrm{~A}=30^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{B}=60^{\circ}$. Construct another $\triangle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with base $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$.
8. Draw two concentric circles of radii 3 cm and 5 cm . Construct a tangent to smaller circle from a point on the larger circle. Also measure its length.
9. Construct an isosceles triangle with base 8 cm and altitude 4 cm . Construct another triangle whose sides are $\frac{2}{3}$ time the corresponding sides of the isosceles triangle.
10. Draw a triangle ABC with $\mathrm{BC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=60^{\circ}$. Then construct a triangle whose sides are $\frac{3}{4}$ of the corresponding sides of the $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
11. Construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in which $\mathrm{CA}=6 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{BAC}=45^{\circ}$. Then construct a triangle whose sides are $3 / 5$ of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Construct a triangle ABC in which $\mathrm{BC}=7 \mathrm{~cm}, \angle \mathrm{~A}=65^{\circ}$ and median AN is 5 cm .
2. Construct a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD in which $\mathrm{AB}=3 \mathrm{cm}, \mathrm{AC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AD}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{D}=70^{\circ}$.
3. Draw the mean proportional of the given segments $a \mathrm{~cm}$ and $b \mathrm{~cm}$.

## HOTS Questions

1. Draw two tangent from a point 9 cm away from the centre of an incircle to the incircle of a triangle length of whose three sides are $7 \mathrm{~cm}, 6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 5 cm .
2. Divide the largest side of a triangle in the ratio $1: 2$, whose base is of the length is 5 cm and one of the base angle is $30^{\circ}$ and the difference of two other sides is 3 cm .
3. Divide the hypotenuse of a right triangle in the ratio $2: 3$, length of whose base is 6 cm and sum of length of hypotenuse and third side is 10 cm .

## Exercise 2

Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. Draw a line segment of length 7.6 cm and divide it in the ratio $5: 8$. Measure the two parts.
2. Construct a triangle with sides $5 \mathrm{~cm}, 6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 7 cm and then another triangle whose sides are $\frac{7}{5}$ of the corresponding sides of the first triangle.
3. Let ABC be a right triangle in which $\mathrm{AB}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$, $\mathrm{BC}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{B}=90^{\circ} . \mathrm{BD}$ is the perpendicular from B on AC . The circle through $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}$ is drawn. Construct the tangents from A to this circle.
4. Draw a circle with the help of a bangle. Take a point outside the circle. Construct the pair of tangents from this point to the circle.

## Exemplar Questions

1. By geometrical construction, it is possible to divide a line segment in the ratio $2+\sqrt{3}: 2-\sqrt{3}$ ? Give answer in True or False.
2. Draw an equilateral triangle ABC of each side 4 cm . Construct a triangle similar to it and of scale factor $\frac{3}{5}$. Is the new triangle also an equilateral ?
3. Given a rhombus ABCD in which $\mathrm{AB}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=60^{\circ}$, divide it into two triangles say, ABC and ADC . Construct the triangle $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with scale factor $\frac{2}{3}$. Draw a line segment $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}^{\prime}$ parallel to CD where $\mathrm{D}^{\prime}$ lies on AD . Is $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}^{\prime}$ a rhombus? Give reasons.
4. To divide a line segment $A B$ in the ratio $p: q(p, q$ are positive integers), draw a ray AX so that $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$ is an acute angle and then mark points on ray AX at equal distances such that the minimum number of the points is
(a) greater of $p$ and $q$
(b) $p+q$
(c) $p+q-1$
(d) $p q$
5. Draw a pair of tangents to a circle which are inclined to each other at an angle of $35^{\circ}$, it is required to draw tangents at the end points of those two radii of the circle, the angle between which is
(a) $105^{\circ}$
(b) $70^{\circ}$
(c) $140^{\circ}$
(d) $145^{\circ}$
6. To divide a line segment AB in the ratio 4 : 7, a ray AX is drawn first such that $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$ is an acute angle and then points $\mathrm{A} 1, \mathrm{~A} 2, \mathrm{~A} 3, \ldots$. are located at equal distance on the ray AX and the point B is joined to
(a) $\mathrm{A}_{12}$
(b) $\mathrm{A}_{11}$
(c) $\mathrm{A}_{10}$
(d) $\mathrm{A}_{9}$

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.

(a) 2
(b) 5
(c) 4
(d) 7

## Multiple Choice Questions

1. (a) Join P to O
2. (b) draw $X_{2} B^{\prime}$ parallel to $X_{7} B$, which intersects $A B$ at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
3. (d) Here PA and PB are two tangents inclined at an angle $45^{\circ}$
Then, $\mathrm{x}+90^{\circ}+90^{\circ}+45^{\circ}=360^{\circ}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+225^{\circ}=360^{\circ} \\
& x=360^{\circ}-225^{\circ} \\
& x=135^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$
