## Very Short Answer Questions

1. Steps of construction :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-331.jpg?height=428&width=556&top_left_y=2065&top_left_x=374)
(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{AB}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw a ray AX making an acute $\angle \mathrm{BAX}$.
(iii) Draw $8(=3+5)$ arcs intersecting the ray AX at points $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}, \mathrm{~A}_{4}, \mathrm{~A}_{5}, \mathrm{~A}_{6}, \mathrm{~A}_{7}$ and $\mathrm{A}_{8}$ such that $\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=\ldots .=\mathrm{A}_{7} \mathrm{~A}_{8}$
(iv) Join $\mathrm{A}_{8} \mathrm{~B}$.
(v) Through $\mathrm{A}_{3}$, draw a line $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{P} \| \mathrm{A}_{8} \mathrm{~B}$, intersecting AB at P .
The point $P$ so obtained is the required point, which divides AB internally into the ratio $3: 5$

## 2. Steps of construction :

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-331.jpg?height=328&width=426&top_left_y=1064&top_left_x=1263)
(i) Draw the line segment $\mathrm{AB}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw a ray BX making an acute $\angle \mathrm{ABX}$.
(iii) Draw 5 arcs intersecting the rays BX at points $\mathrm{B}_{1}$, $\mathrm{B}_{2}, \mathrm{~B}_{3}, \mathrm{~B}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{5}$ such that $\mathrm{BB}_{1}=\mathrm{B}_{1} \mathrm{~B}_{2}=\ldots=\mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{~B}_{5}$
(iv) Join $\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~A}$
(v) Through $\mathrm{B}_{5}$, draw a line $\mathrm{B}_{5} \mathrm{P} \| \mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~A}$, intersecting BA produced at P . The point P so obtained is the required point, which divides AB externally in the ratio 3 : 5

## 3. Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a circle with radius 3 cm and centre O. Take a point P outside the circle such that $\mathrm{OP}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Bisect OP at Q .
(iii) With Q as centre and QP as radius, draw a circle which intersects the given circle at two points A and $B$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-331.jpg?height=336&width=409&top_left_y=1937&top_left_x=1462)
(iv) Join PA and PB .

PA and PB are the required tangents. On measurement $\mathrm{PA}=7.4 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~PB}=7.4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
4. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a circle with centre O and radius $=2.8 \mathrm{~cm}$
(ii) Draw any diameter AOC.
(iii) Construct $60^{\circ}$, $\angle \mathrm{BOC}=$ meeting
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-332.jpg?height=348&width=389&top_left_y=372&top_left_x=603)
the circle at B .
(iv) At A and B , draw perpendiculars to OA and OB respectively intersecting each other at point P . PA and PB are the required tangents.
5. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a circle with centre O and radius 2.2 cm . Take a point P on it.
(ii) Join OP.
(iii) Draw $\mathrm{TT}^{\prime}$ perpendicular to OP at P .
Then TPT' is the desired tangent to the circle at
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-332.jpg?height=367&width=335&top_left_y=872&top_left_x=657)
the point P .

## Short Answer Questions

1. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw $\mathrm{BC}=6.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
(ii) Draw a ray BX making an angle $60^{\circ}$ with BC
(iii) With centre B and radius 4.5 cm draw an arc intersecting the ray BX at A .
(iv) Join AC . Then $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is the given triangle.
(v) Draw a ray BY making a suitable acute angle with BC on opposite side of ray AX with respect to BC
(vi) Draw four arcs intersecting the ray BY at $\mathrm{Y}_{1}, \mathrm{Y}_{2}$, $Y_{3}$ and $Y_{4}$ such that $B Y_{1}=Y_{1} Y_{2}=Y_{2} Y_{3}=Y_{3} Y_{4}$
(vii) Join $Y_{4} C$.
(viii) Draw $\mathrm{Y}_{3} \mathrm{D}$ parallel to $\mathrm{Y}_{4} \mathrm{C}$ intersecting the BC at D . (ix) Draw a line $\mathrm{DE} \| \mathrm{AC}$ intersecting BA at E . Then required triangle is $\triangle \mathrm{BDE}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-332.jpg?height=632&width=465&top_left_y=1893&top_left_x=360)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-332.jpg?height=440&width=712&top_left_y=390&top_left_x=1059)
(i) Draw a circle with centre O and radius 5 cm .
(ii) Draw any radius OA .
(iii) Draw a radius OB such that $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=180^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=150^{\circ}$.
(iv) Draw $\mathrm{AP} \perp \mathrm{OA}$ and $\mathrm{BP} \perp \mathrm{OB}$, Which intersect each other at point P . Thus PA and PB are the required tangents such that $\angle \mathrm{APB}=30^{\circ}$
2. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-332.jpg?height=337&width=725&top_left_y=1231&top_left_x=1055)

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a circle with O as centre and of radius 3 cm .
(ii) Draw a diameter ST and extend it to both the sides to $P$ and $Q$ such that $P O=O Q=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(iii) Draw perpendicular bisectors of PO and OQ . Let M and N be the mid-points of PO and OQ respectively.
(iv) Taking M as centre and MP as radius, draw a circle which meets the given circle at A and B. Similarly taking N as centre and NQ as radius, draw a circle which meets the given circle at C and D .
(v) Join $\mathrm{PA}, \mathrm{PB}, \mathrm{QC}$ and QD .
$\mathrm{PA}, \mathrm{PB}, \mathrm{QC}$ and QD are the required tangents.
4.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-332.jpg?height=232&width=582&top_left_y=2151&top_left_x=1091)

## Long Answer Questions

1. Steps of construction :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-333.jpg?height=601&width=670&top_left_y=466&top_left_x=315)
(i) Draw a line segment AB of length 8 cm .
(ii) With A as centre, draw a circle of radius 4 cm .
(iii) With B as centre, draw another circle of radius 3 cm .
(iv) Draw the perpendicular bisector of AB . Let P be the mid-point of AB .
(v) With P as centre and PA as radius, draw a circle which intersects the circle with centre $A$ at $M$ and N ; and the circle with centre B at R and S .
(vi) Join $\mathrm{BM}, \mathrm{BN}, \mathrm{AR}$ and AS .

These rays $\mathrm{BM}, \mathrm{BN}, \mathrm{AR}$ and AS are required tangents.

## 2. Steps of construction :

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-333.jpg?height=785&width=522&top_left_y=1626&top_left_x=388)
(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{AB}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) With A as centre and radius $=6 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw an arc above AB .
(iii) With B as centre and radius $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$, draw another arc, intersect the arc drawn in step (ii) at C .
(iv) Join AC and BC to obtain $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
(v) Below AB , draw a ray AX making an acute angle with AB .
(vi) Draw three arcs intersecting the ray AX at points $\mathrm{A}_{1}$, $A_{2}, A_{3}$ such that $A A_{1}=A_{1} A_{2}=A_{2} A_{3}$
(vii) Join $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}$.
(viii) From point $A_{2}$, draw $A_{2} B^{\prime} \| A_{3} B$ meeting $A B$ at $B^{\prime}$.
(ix) From $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$, draw $\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{BC}$, meeting AC atC'. $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
Justification : In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{BC} \| \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$, $\angle \mathrm{BAC}^{\prime}=\angle \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{AC}^{\prime}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=\angle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ So, $\triangle \mathrm{ABC} \sim \Delta \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$

$$
\therefore \frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{2}{3} .
$$

## 3. Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{AB}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw perpendicular bisector $\mathrm{PP}^{\prime}$ of AB , which intersects AB at D .
(iii) Taking centre as D and radius of 4 cm , draw an arc, intersecting the ray DP at C .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-333.jpg?height=713&width=746&top_left_y=1382&top_left_x=1103)
(iv) Join AC and $\mathrm{BC} . \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is the required triangle.
(v) Draw a ray AX making an acute angle with AB below $A B$. Draw three arcs intersecting the ray $A X$ at points $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}$ such that $\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{3}$
(vi) Join $\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~B}$.
(vii) Draw a line $A_{3} B^{\prime}$ from point $A_{3}$ parallel to $A_{2} B$ which meets AB produced at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
(viii) From $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ draw a line $\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to BC which meets AC produced at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
$\Delta \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
4. Steps of construction :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-334.jpg?height=531&width=554&top_left_y=424&top_left_x=317)
(i) Draw two concentric circles with centre O and of radii 4 cm and 6 cm .
(ii) Let P be any point on the larger circle.
(iii) Join OP.
(iv) Draw the right bisector of OP . Let M be the midpoint of OP.
(v) Taking M as centre and radius OM , draw a circle which intersects the smaller circle at points A and B.
(vi) Join PA and PB . These PA and PB are the required tangents.
(vii) On measuring with scale $\mathrm{PA}=4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{PB}=4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Calculation : Since the tangent at any point of a circle and radius through the point of contact are perpendicular to each other, therefore $\angle \mathrm{OAP}=90^{\circ}$.
Now in right triangle OAP,

$$
\begin{array}{ll} 
& \mathrm{OP}^{2}=\mathrm{OA}^{2}+\mathrm{PA}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{PA}^{2}=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OA}^{2}=6^{2}-4^{2}=36-16=20 \\
\therefore & \mathrm{PA}=2 \sqrt{5}=2 \times 2.236=4.5 \mathrm{~cm} . \text { (approximate) }
\end{array}
$$

Hence, the measurement is the same as by the calculation.
5. Steps of construction :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-334.jpg?height=547&width=483&top_left_y=1905&top_left_x=383)
(i) Draw a line segment BC of length 4.3 cm .
(ii) At point B , draw perpendicular ray BX to BC .
(iii) With centre B and radius 2.8 cm draw an arc intersecting the ray BX at A .
(iv) Join A and C.
(v) Draw perpendicular BD from B to AC .
(vi) Draw perpendicular bisector of BC , which bisect BC at piont P .
(vii) With PC as a radius draw a circle which passes through $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D .
(viii) With A as centre and radius $\mathrm{AB}(=2.8 \mathrm{~cm})$ draw an arc intersecting the circle at E .
(ix) Join AE.

Thus AB and AE are the two required tangents.
6. Steps of construction :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-334.jpg?height=639&width=684&top_left_y=1050&top_left_x=1073)
(i) Draw $\mathrm{AB}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$
(ii) Draw ray BX perpendicular to AB .
(iii) With centre B and radius 3 cm draw an arc intersecting the ray BX at C .
(iv) Join AC , we get $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with the given data.
(v) Draw any ray AY making an acute angle with AB on the side opposite to vertex $C$ with respect to $A B$.
(vi) Locate 5 points (the greater of 5 and 3 in $\frac{5}{3}$ ) $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \mathrm{~A}_{3}, \mathrm{~A}_{4}$, and $\mathrm{A}_{5}$ on AX such that $\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{3}=\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~A}_{4}=\mathrm{A}_{4} \mathrm{~A}_{5}$
(vii) Join $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}$.
(viii) Through $\mathrm{A}_{5}$, draw a line parallel to $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}$ that intersects the extended line segment AB at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
(ix) Through $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$, draw a line parallel to BC to meet the extended line segment AC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$. Then $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
7. Draw a straight line $\mathrm{AB}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$

Make angles $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ at the point A and B respectively.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-335.jpg?height=227&width=385&top_left_y=463&top_left_x=488)

To construct $\triangle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$
Cut $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$ along straight line AB produced.
Make an angle $60^{\circ}$ at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ or draw parallel line to BC at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-335.jpg?height=272&width=458&top_left_y=836&top_left_x=459)

And, if we extend AC then it cut the parallel line at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
Hence, $\Delta \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
8. To draw tangents on the given circle:

Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a circle of 3 cm radius with centre O .
(ii) Draw a circle of 5 cm radius, taking O as centre. Locate a point P on circle and join OP .
(iii) Bisect OP . Let M be midpoint of OP .
(iv) Taking M as its centre and MO as its radius, draw a circle. Let it intersect the smaller circle at points $Q$ and $R$.
(v) Join PQ and PR . So, PQ and PR are required tangents.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-335.jpg?height=300&width=305&top_left_y=1658&top_left_x=534)

## Justification:

Since PQ and PR are of lengths 4 cm each.
For $\triangle \mathrm{PQO}, \mathrm{PQ}$ is tangent at Q .

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle \mathrm{PQO} & =90^{\circ} \\
\mathrm{PO} & =5 \mathrm{~cm} \\
\mathrm{QO} & =3 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

By Pythagoras theorem in $\triangle \mathrm{PQO}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PQ}^{2}+\mathrm{QO}^{2} & =\mathrm{PQ}^{2} \\
\mathrm{PQ}^{2}+(3)^{2} & =(5)^{2} \\
\mathrm{PQ}^{2} & =25-9 \\
\mathrm{PQ}^{2} & =16 \\
\mathrm{PQ} & =4 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus justified.
9.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-335.jpg?height=405&width=419&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1146)

## Steps of construction:

(i) Draw a line segment $\mathrm{BC}=8 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw a perpendicular bisector XY on BC which intersects BC at point P .
(iii) With P as centre and radius of 4 cm , draw an arc intersecting the ray XY at A above BC .
(iv) Join A to B and C .
(v) Draw a ray BZ below BC . Draw 3 arcs intersecting BZ at $\mathrm{B}_{1}, \mathrm{~B}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{3}$ such that $\mathrm{BB}_{1}=\mathrm{B}_{1} \mathrm{~B}_{2}=\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{3}$.
(vi) Join $\mathrm{B}_{3}$ to C and draw $\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{C}$.
(vii) Draw $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{AC}$, which intersect AB at point $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$. Thus $\triangle \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ is required triangle.
10.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-335.jpg?height=741&width=623&top_left_y=1338&top_left_x=1162)

## Steps of construction:

(i) Draw $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ using SAS rule with $\mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}, \angle \mathrm{ABC} =60^{\circ}$ and $\mathrm{BC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
(ii) Draw any acute angle $\angle \mathrm{CBY}$ below BC
(iii) Cut 4 equal arcs on BY from B at $\mathrm{B}_{1}, \mathrm{~B}_{2}, \mathrm{~B}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{4}$
(iv) Join $\mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{C}$
(v) Draw $\mathrm{B}_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{C}$
(vi) Draw $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{AC}$

Hence $\Delta \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ is the required $\Delta$ with its sides $\frac{3}{4}$ of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
11.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-336.jpg?height=314&width=501&top_left_y=402&top_left_x=342)

Given, $\mathrm{CA}=6 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AB}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{BAC}=45^{\circ}$. To construct a $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line $\mathrm{AC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw an angle $\angle \mathrm{XAC}=45^{\circ}$
(iii) Cut an arc of length 5 cm on AX , taking A as centre. The point obtained is B .
(iv) Join the points B and C .

To construct a triangle whose sides are $3 / 5$ of the corresponding sides of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
(v) Draw any ray AY making an acute angle with AC on the opposite to the vertex $B$.
(vi) Locate 5 (the greater of 3 and 5 in 3/5) points $A_{1}, A_{2}, A_{3}, A_{4}$ and $A_{5}$ on AY so that $\mathrm{AA}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2}=\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{3}=\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~A}_{4}=\mathrm{A}_{4} \mathrm{~A}_{5}$.
(vii) Join $\mathrm{A}_{5} \mathrm{C}$ and draw a line through $\mathrm{A}_{3}$ (the $3{ }^{\text {rd }}$ point, 3 being smaller of 3 and 5 in 3/5) parallel to $\mathrm{A}_{5} \mathrm{C}$ to intersect AC at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
(viii) Draw a line through $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ parallel to the line CB to intersect AB at $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$.
Then $\triangle \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
For justification of the construction, note that $\Delta \mathrm{ABC} \sim \mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
$\because \frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}}=\frac{3}{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-336.jpg?height=435&width=529&top_left_y=1724&top_left_x=264)

Therefore,
$\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}=\frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}+\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}=1+\frac{\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}=1+\frac{2}{3}=\frac{5}{3}$,
i.e., $\frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{3}{5}$
Also, $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ is parallel to CB .
Therefore, $\frac{\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{3}{5}$

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. Steps of construction:
(i) Draw a line segment, $\mathrm{BC}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw BP such that $\angle \mathrm{PBP}=65^{\circ}$.
(iii) Draw BQ such that $\angle \mathrm{PBQ}=90^{\circ}$
(iv) Draw a perpendicular bisector (RS) of BC which intersects BC at the point N .
(v) Mark the intersecting
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-336.jpg?height=492&width=351&top_left_y=438&top_left_x=1468)
point of RS and BQ as O .
(vi) With O as centre and OB as radius draw circle.
(vii) With N as centre 5 cm as radius draw two arcs which intersect the circle at the points A and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$.
(viii) Join AB and AC and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}$.
(ix) Now, ABC or $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}$ is the required triangle.

Proof: By alternate segment theorem, $\angle \mathrm{BAC}=\angle \mathrm{CBP} =\angle \mathrm{BA}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}=65^{\circ}$.
2. Steps of construction:
(i) Draw a line segment, $\mathrm{AC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(ii) Draw AP such that $\angle \mathrm{CAP}=70^{\circ}$.
(iii) Draw AQ such that $\angle \mathrm{PAQ}=90^{\circ}$
(iv) Draw a perpendicular bisector (LM) of AC_which intersects AC at the point N .
(v) Mark the intersecting point of LM and AQ as O .
(vi) With O as centre and OA and radius draw circle.
(vii) With A as centre, 4 cm as radius draw an arc which intersects the circle at the point D .
(viii) With A as centre, 3 cm as radius draw an arc which intersects the circle at the point B .
(ix) Join $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CD}$ and AD .
(x) $\quad \mathrm{ABCD}$ is the required cyclic quadrilateral.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-336.jpg?height=547&width=399&top_left_y=1877&top_left_x=1217)
3. Steps of construction:
(i) Draw XZ.
(ii) Mark the point Y on XZ such that $\mathrm{XY}=a \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{YZ}=b \mathrm{~cm}$.
(iii) Draw the perpendicular bisector $(\mathrm{PQ})$ of XZ which meets XZ at the point O .
(iv) With O as centre and OX as radius draw a semi circle. XZ is the diameter of the semi circle.
(v) Draw YM perpendicular to XZ which meets semicircle at point M .
(vi) Now MY, is the mean proportional of XY and YZ , i.e., $a$ and $b$.
Proof: $\quad \triangle X Y M \sim \Delta M Y Z$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \frac{\mathrm{XY}}{\mathrm{YM}}=\frac{\mathrm{MY}}{\mathrm{YZ}} \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{MY}=\sqrt{(\mathrm{XY})(\mathrm{YZ})}=\sqrt{a b}
\end{array}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-337.jpg?height=428&width=444&top_left_y=1082&top_left_x=431)

## HOTS Questions

1. 

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-337.jpg?height=506&width=716&top_left_y=1621&top_left_x=294)

Steps of construction :
(i) Draw BC of length 7 cm .
(ii) With centre B and radius 6 cm draw an arc above BC .
(iii) With centre C and radius 5 cm draw an arc intersecting the arc drawn in step (ii) at A .
(iv) Join A to B and A to C .
(v) Draw bisectors of $\angle \mathrm{ABC}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ACB}$, which intersects each other at point O .
(vi) Draw perpendicular from O to BC which intersect BC at M .
(vii) With centre O and radius OM draw a circle which is incircle of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
(viii) Draw a line segment OP through the centre O of length 9 cm .
(ix) Draw perpendicular bisector of PO which intersects PO at point Q .
(x) With centre Q and radius QO or QP , draw a circle which intersects the circle drawn in step (vii) at point $R$ and $S$.
(xi) Draw ray PR and PS. Thus, PR and PS are the required tangents.
2.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-337.jpg?height=382&width=556&top_left_y=836&top_left_x=1197)

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment BC of 5 cm .
(ii) Draw a ray BX such that $\angle \mathrm{XBC}=30^{\circ}$
(iii) With centre B and radius 3 cm an arc is drawn which intersects the ray BX at point M .
(iv) Join M to C .
(v) Draw ray CY such that $\angle \mathrm{YCM}=\angle \mathrm{XMC}$, which intersects ray AX at A to form $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
Clearly, AB is the largest side of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$.
Now, we have to divide the line segment AB in the ratio $1: 2$.
(vi) Mark three points $\mathrm{X}_{1}, \mathrm{X}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{X}_{3}$ on the line segment $B C$ such that $\mathrm{BX}_{1}=\mathrm{X}_{1} \mathrm{X}_{2}=\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}$
(vii) Join A to $\mathrm{X}_{3}$.
(viii) Draw a line through $\mathrm{X}_{2}$ parallel to $\mathrm{X}_{3} \mathrm{~A}$ which intersects BA at point T . This point T divides the longest side AB of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ in the ratio $1: 2$.
i.e., $\mathrm{AT}: \mathrm{TB}=1: 2$
3.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-337.jpg?height=534&width=601&top_left_y=2030&top_left_x=1174)

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment AB of length 6 cm .
(ii) Draw a ray $\mathrm{AX} \perp \mathrm{AB}$.
(iii) Mark a point P on the ray AX such that $\mathrm{AP}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$.
(iv) Join P to B .
(v) Draw BC such that $\angle \mathrm{PBC}=\angle \mathrm{CPB}$, which intersects ray AX at C . Thus $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is formed.
(vi) Draw ray CY making an acute angle with CB .
(vii) Mark $Y_{1}, Y_{2}, Y_{3}, Y_{4}, Y_{5}$ or ray $C Y$ such that $C Y_{1}=Y_{1} Y_{2}=Y_{2} Y_{3}=Y_{3} Y_{4}=Y_{4} Y_{5}$
(viii) Join $Y_{5}$ and $B$.
(ix) Draw $Y_{2} M$ such that $Y_{2} M \| Y_{5} B$, which intersect CB at M .
Point M divides the hypotenuse CB in the ratio $2: 3$ i.e., $\mathrm{CM}: \mathrm{MB}=2: 3$

## Text-book Questions

1. Steps of construction :
(i) Draw a line segment AB of length 7.6 cm
(ii) Draw a ray AX below making an acute angle with AB .
(iii) Draw $(5+8=13)$ arcs intersecting the ray AX at $\mathrm{X}_{1}, \mathrm{X}_{2}, \mathrm{X}_{3}, \mathrm{X}_{4}, \mathrm{X}_{5}, \mathrm{X}_{6}, \mathrm{X}_{7}, \mathrm{X}_{8}, \mathrm{X}_{9}, \mathrm{X}_{10}, \mathrm{X}_{11}, \mathrm{X}_{12}$ and $\mathrm{X}_{13}$ such that
$\mathrm{AX}_{1}=\mathrm{X}_{1} \mathrm{X}_{2}=\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}=\mathrm{X}_{3} \mathrm{X}_{4}$
$=\mathrm{X}_{4} \mathrm{X}_{5}=\mathrm{X}_{5} \mathrm{X}_{6}=\mathrm{X}_{6} \mathrm{X}_{7}=\mathrm{X}_{7} \mathrm{X}_{8}$
$=X_{8} X_{9}=X_{9} X_{10}=X_{10} X_{11}=X_{11} X_{12}$
$=\mathrm{X}_{12} \mathrm{X}_{13}$
(iv) Draw $\mathrm{X}_{5} \mathrm{C}$ parallel to $\mathrm{X}_{13} \mathrm{~B}$ which intersects AB at C.

Thus, C is the point which divides the line segment AB in the ratio $5: 8$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-338.jpg?height=594&width=796&top_left_y=1912&top_left_x=196)
2.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-338.jpg?height=654&width=735&top_left_y=397&top_left_x=1050)

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment BC of length 7 cm .
(ii) With centre B and radius 5 cm , draw an arc above BC (or any one side of BC).
(iii) With centre C and radius 6 cm , draw an arc intersecting the arc drawn in step (ii) at A .
(iv) Join A to B and C .
(v) Draw a ray BX below BC (or opposite side of A with respect to BC ) making an acute angle with BC .
(vi) Draw 7 arcs intersecting the ray BX at $\mathrm{X}_{1}, \mathrm{X}_{2}, \mathrm{X}_{3}, \mathrm{X}_{4}, \mathrm{X}_{5}, \mathrm{X}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{X}_{7}$ such that $B X_{1}=X_{1} X_{2}=X_{2} X_{3}=X_{3} X_{4}=X_{4} X_{5}=X_{5} X_{6}=X_{6} X_{7}$.
(vii) Join $\mathrm{X}_{5}$ to C .
(viii) Draw $\mathrm{X}_{7} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{X}_{5} \mathrm{C}$, which intersects BC produced at $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$.
(ix) Draw $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime} \| \mathrm{CA}$, which intersects BA produced at $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$.
Thus, $\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{BC}^{\prime}$ is the required triangle.
3.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-338.jpg?height=677&width=569&top_left_y=1714&top_left_x=1085)

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a line segment BC of length 8 cm .
(ii) Draw a ray BX perpendicular to BC at B .
(iii) With centre B and radius 6 cm , draw an arc intersecting the ray BX at A .
(iv) Join A to C.
(v) Draw perpendicular bisector of BC which intersects BC at point E .
(vi) With centre E and radius EB (or EC ), draw a circle, which intersects AC at point D and passes through $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D .
(vii) With centre A and radius AB , draw an arc intersecting the circle at point F opposite to point B with respect to AC
(viii) Draw ray AF and extend AB to form a ray.

Thus, AB and AF are required tangents.
4.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-339.jpg?height=464&width=686&top_left_y=1014&top_left_x=317)

## Steps of construction :

(i) Draw a circle with the help of a bangle.
(ii) Take a point P outside the circle
(iii) Draw a secant PAB , which intersects the circle at point A and B .
(iv) Produce AP to C such that $\mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{PC}$.
(v) Draw perpendicular bisector of BC , which intersects BC at point O .
(vi) With centre O and radius OB or OC , draw a semicircle.
(vii) Draw PD perpendicular to BC , which intersect the semi-circle drawn in step (vi) at D .
(viii) With centre P and radius PD , draw arcs intersecting the circle at point T and $\mathrm{T}^{\prime}$.
(ix) Draw ray PT and $\mathrm{PT}^{\prime}$.

Thus, PT and $\mathrm{PT}^{\prime}$ are required tangents.

First draw the rhombus ABCD in which $\mathrm{AB}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{ABC}=60^{\circ}$ as given in fig. and join AC . Construct the triangle $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ similar to $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with scale factor $\frac{2}{3}$ as instructed.
Finally draw the line segment $\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}^{\prime}$ parallel to CD .
Now, $\quad \frac{\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{2}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AC}}$
Also, $\quad \frac{\mathrm{AC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{CD}}=\frac{\mathrm{AD}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{AD}}=\frac{2}{3}$
Therefore, $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime}=\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}^{\prime}=\mathrm{AD}^{\prime}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{AB}$.
i.e., $\mathrm{AB}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{D}^{\prime}$ is a rhombus.

1. (d) We know that, to divide a line segment $A B$ in the ratio $m: n$, first draw a ray $A X$ which makes an acute $\angle B A X$ then, marked $m+n$ points at equal distance. Here, $m=2, n=5$
∴ Minimum number of these points $=2+5=7$
2. (c) We know that, in a triangle sum of two sides of triangle is greater than the third side. Here, the sides of triangle given in option (c) does not satisfy this condition. So, with these sides the construction of a triangle is not possible.

## 12 Areas Related to circle

(CONCEPT MAP)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-340.jpg?height=1693&width=1419&top_left_y=852&top_left_x=348)

## TERMS RELATED TO CIRCLE

Chord : A line segment joining any two points of a circle is called a chord. A chord passing through the centre of circle is called its diameter. $A B$ and $C D$ both are chords but the chord $C D$ passes through the centre. Hence, $C D$ is the diameter also.
Diameter of a circle $=$ twice the radius of the circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-341.jpg?height=241&width=273&top_left_y=557&top_left_x=927)

Note : Diameter is the longest chord of a circle.
Arc : A part of a circle is called an arc. In Figure (i), $A C$ is an $\operatorname{arc}$ and is denoted by $\operatorname{arc} A B C$ or $\widetilde{A B C}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-341.jpg?height=227&width=223&top_left_y=909&top_left_x=954)
(i)

Semi-circle : A diameter of a circle divides a circle into two equal arcs, each known as a semi-circle. In Figure (ii), $P Q$ is a diameter and $\overparen{P R Q}$ is a semi-circle and so is $\overparen{P B Q}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-341.jpg?height=255&width=255&top_left_y=1260&top_left_x=936)

Minor arc : An arc of a circle whose length is less than a semi-circle of the same circle is called a minor arc. $P A Q$ is a minor arc.
Major arc : An arc of a circle whose length is greater than a semi-circle of the same circle is called a major arc. In figure (iii), $\operatorname{arc} P B Q$ is a major arc.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-341.jpg?height=254&width=245&top_left_y=1663&top_left_x=941)

Sector : The region bounded by an arc of a circle and two radii at its end points is called a sector. In the figure (iv), the shaded portion is a minor sector formed by the arc $P R Q$ and the unshaded portion is a major sector formed by the $\operatorname{arc} P T Q$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-341.jpg?height=231&width=232&top_left_y=2033&top_left_x=945)
(iv)

Segment : A chord divides the interior of a circle into two parts, each part called a segment. The segment in which the centre of the circle does not lie is called minor segment and the segment in which the centre of the circle lies is called major segment. In the figure (v), the shaded region $P A Q P$ and the unshaded region $P B Q P$ are both segments of the circle.
$P A Q P$ is called a minor segment and $P B Q P$ is called a major segment.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-342.jpg?height=255&width=221&top_left_y=383&top_left_x=897)
(v)

Circumference : Take a wheel and mark a point $P$ on the wheel where it touches the ground. Rotate the wheel along a line till the point $P$ touches back on the ground. Measure the distance between the first and last position of $P$ along the line. This distance is equal to the circumference of the circle. Thus, the length of the boundary of a circle is the circumference of the circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-342.jpg?height=243&width=706&top_left_y=824&top_left_x=651)

The ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter is always a constant. This constant is universally denoted by Greek letter $\pi$.

Therefore, $\frac{c}{d}=\frac{c}{2 r}=\pi$ where $c$ is the circumference, $d$ its diameter and $r$ is its radius of the circle. An approximate value of $\pi$ is $\frac{22}{7}$. Circumference of a circle $=2 \pi r$ or $\pi d$, where $r$ is the radius and $d$ is the diameter of the circle.

The distance travelled by a person sitting on a Ferris wheel can be determined by calculating the Know! circumference of the wheel.

## AREA OF A CIRCLE

Area of a circle $=\pi r^{2}$, where ' $r$ ' is the radius of the circle.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-342.jpg?height=241&width=278&top_left_y=1635&top_left_x=865)

## AREA OF A SEMI-CIRCLE

Area of a semi-circle $=\frac{1}{2} \pi r^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-342.jpg?height=168&width=248&top_left_y=2037&top_left_x=881)

## AREA OF A QUADRANT OF A CIRCLE

Area of a quadrant $=\frac{\pi r^{2}}{4}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-342.jpg?height=154&width=133&top_left_y=2384&top_left_x=941)

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

A square park has each side of $\mathbf{1 0 0 ~ m}$. At each corner of the park, there is a flower bed in the form of a quadrant of radius 14 m as shown in Fig. Find the area of the remaining part of the park (Use $\boldsymbol{\pi}=22 / 7$ ).

## SOLUTION:

Let A be the area of each quadrant of a circle of radius 14 m . Then,
$A=\frac{1}{4}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14 \times 14=154 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
∴ Area of 4 quadrants $=4 \times \mathrm{A}=(4 \times 154)=616 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Area of square park having side 100 m long $=(100 \times 100) \mathrm{m}^{2}=10,000 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Hence, Area of the remaining part of the park $=10,000-616=9384 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-343.jpg?height=367&width=346&top_left_y=518&top_left_x=1530)

## Learn More

Here, $A B C$ is an equilateral triangle and $a$ be the side of $\triangle A B C$.
Then, radius of circumcircle is $\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$
and Area of $\triangle A B C$ can be defined as $\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}\right)$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-343.jpg?height=238&width=232&top_left_y=964&top_left_x=1626)

## CONCENTRIC CIRCLES

Circles having the same centre but different radii are called concentric circles.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-343.jpg?height=245&width=250&top_left_y=1398&top_left_x=936)

## AREA OF CIRCULAR PATH (RING)

If we have a circular field of radius ' $r$ ', surrounded by a path of uniform width ' $d$ ' and $r+d=R$ then, the area of the circular path
$=$ Area of the outer circle - Area of the inner circle
$=\left(\pi R^{2}-\pi r^{2}\right)$ sq. unit $=\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)$ sq. unit

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-343.jpg?height=296&width=293&top_left_y=1758&top_left_x=1578)

A race track is in the form of a ring whose inner circumference is 352 m and the outer circumference is 396 m . Find the width of the track.

## SOLUTION :

Let the outer and inner radii of ring be R meter $\& r$ meter respectively.
According to question,
$2 \pi R=396$ and $2 \pi r=352$
$\Rightarrow 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times R=396$ and $2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times r=352$
$\Rightarrow R=63 \mathrm{~m}$ and $r=56 \mathrm{~m}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-343.jpg?height=256&width=259&top_left_y=2177&top_left_x=1594)

Hence, width of the track $=(R-r)=(63-56)=7$ metres

Circumference of planets helps us to compare their relative sizes \& artificial satellite that are launched in space to travel in orbits of certain circumference.

## CONGRUENT CIRCLES AND ARCS

Two circles or arcs are said to be congruent if we can superpose (place) one over the other such that they cover each other completely.

## PERIMETER AND AREA OF SECTOR OF A CIRCLE

Two radii OA and OB enclose a portion of the circular region which makes a central angle $\theta$. The region is called a sector of the circle. In fig., $A O B$ is the sector with central angle $\theta$. Let ' $l$ ' be the length of $\operatorname{arc} A B$.
Then, $\frac{\overparen{A B}}{\text { circumference }}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}$ [Corresponding arcs subtend proportional central angles] or $\quad \frac{l}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}$ or $l=2 \pi r \cdot \frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}=\frac{\pi r \theta}{180}$, where r is the radius of the circle
$\therefore \quad$ Perimeter of the sector $=O A+O B+\overparen{A B}=2 r+\frac{\pi r \theta}{180}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-344.jpg?height=310&width=279&top_left_y=954&top_left_x=1535)

Also, $\frac{\text { Area of sector } A O B}{\text { Area of the circle }}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \Rightarrow \frac{\text { Area of sector } A O B}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \quad$ or Area of sector $A O B=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \times \frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}$

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

Find the area of the sector of a circle with radius $\mathbf{1 0 ~ c m}$ and of angle $\mathbf{6 0}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$. Also, find the area of the corresponding major sector. (Take $\boldsymbol{\pi}=\mathbf{3 . 1 4}$ )

## SOLUTION :

Here, radius of circle (r) is 10 cm . and $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=60^{\circ}$
In figure, $O A P B$ is the minor sector corresponding to angle $60^{\circ}$ and $O A Q B$ is the major sector.
The area of the sector $O A P B$
$=\frac{60}{360} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{1}{6} \times 3.14 \times(10)^{2}=\frac{314}{6}=\frac{157}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of the circle $=\pi r^{2}=3.14 \times 10 \times 10=314 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$. The area of the major sector $O A Q B$
$=$ Area of circle - Area of minor sector $O A P B=314-\frac{157}{3}=\frac{785}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-344.jpg?height=305&width=250&top_left_y=1516&top_left_x=1562)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-344.jpg?height=150&width=348&top_left_y=1854&top_left_x=196)

- Sides of a triangular field are $15 \mathrm{~m}, 16 \mathrm{~m}$ and 17 m . With the three corners of the field, a cow, a buffalo and a horse are tied separately with ropes of length 7 m each to graze in the field. Find the area of the field which cannot be grazed by the three animals.


## SOLUTION

The three sides of the triangle are $a=15 \mathrm{~m}, b=16 \mathrm{~m}$ and $c=17 \mathrm{~m}$.
Using Heron's formula,
$\mathrm{s}=\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{15+16+17}{2}=24 \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, area of $\triangle A B C=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$
$=\sqrt{24(24-15)(24-16)(24-17)}=\sqrt{24 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7}=109.98 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Let $\alpha^{\circ}, \beta^{\circ}$ and $\gamma^{\circ}$ be the measures of the angles $A, B$ and $C$ respectively.
(Buffalo) B
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-344.jpg?height=336&width=405&top_left_y=2170&top_left_x=1407)

Then, sum of areas of regions I, II and III.

$$
\begin{array}{lc}
=\frac{\pi \alpha}{360} \times(7)^{2}+\frac{\pi \beta}{360} \times(7)^{2}+\frac{\pi \gamma}{360} \times(7)^{2} & (\because \text { Each region is a sector of a circle of same radius } 7 \mathrm{~m}) \\
=\frac{\pi}{360}(\alpha+\beta+\gamma) \times 49=\frac{\pi}{360} \times 180 \times 49 & \left(\because \alpha^{\circ}+\beta^{\circ}+\gamma^{\circ}=180^{\circ}\right)=\left(\frac{22}{7 \times 2}\right) \times 49=77 \mathrm{~m}^{2}
\end{array}
$$

Hence, the area of the region in which the three animals cannot graze $=$ Shaded area $=109.98-77=32.98 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

## AREA OF A SEGMENT

In right $\triangle O P A, \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{A P}{O A} \Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{A P}{r}$
$\Rightarrow A P=r \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right), A B=2 A P=2 r \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
and $\cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{O P}{O A} \Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{O P}{r} \quad \Rightarrow O P=r \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$

Area of minor segment $A Q B P A=($ Area of sector OAQBO $)-($ Area of $\triangle O A B)$
$=\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{360}-\frac{1}{2} \times A B \times O=\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{360}-\frac{1}{2} \times 2 r \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \times r \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{360}-r^{2} \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-345.jpg?height=440&width=373&top_left_y=726&top_left_x=1503)

Area of major segment $A P B R A=($ Area of the circle $)-($ Area of minor segment $A Q B P A)=\pi r^{2}-\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{360}-r^{2} \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$.

## ILLUSTRATION : 4

A chord $A B$ of a circle of radius 10 cm makes a right angle at the centre of the circle. Find the area of the major and minor segments (Take $\boldsymbol{\pi} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{3 . 1 4}$ )

## SOLUTION :

We know that the area of a minor segment of angle $\theta$ (in degrees) in a circle of radius $r$ is given by

$$
A=\left\{\frac{\pi \theta}{360}-\sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right\} r^{2}
$$

Here, $\quad r=10$ and $\theta=90^{\circ}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore A=\left\{\frac{3.14 \times 90}{360}-\sin 45^{\circ} \cos 45^{\circ}\right\} \times(10)^{2} \Rightarrow A=\left\{\frac{3.14}{4}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right\} \times(10)^{2} \\
& \Rightarrow A=\{3.14 \times 25-50\}=(78.5-50)=28.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Area of the major segment $=$ Area of the circle - Area of the minor segment
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-345.jpg?height=287&width=284&top_left_y=1530&top_left_x=1592)

$$
=\left(3.14 \times 10^{2}-28.5\right)=(314-28.5)=285.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

## AREA OF COMBINATIONS OF PLANE FIGURES

Whenever a plane figure is made by combination of two or more plane figures, then the area of combination of plane figures is always equal to the sum of the areas of the constituent figures.

## IMPORTANT POINTS TO BE REMEMBER

(i) Distance moved by a wheel in 1 revolution $=$ Circumference of a wheel
(ii) Number of revolutions in one minute $=\frac{\text { Distance covered in1minute }}{\text { Circumference }}$
(iii) Angle described by minute hand in 60 minute $=360^{\circ}$.
(iv) Angle described by hour hand in 12 hour $=360^{\circ}$.
(v) The sum of the arcs of major and minor sectors of a circle is equal to the circumference of the circle.
(vi) The sum of the areas of major and minor sectors of a circle is equal to the area of the circle.

## CASE STUDY : Area and Perimeter of some specific plan region in a combination of different region.

To find the area of a particular region which is a part of the combinations of plane figures involving circles or parts of circles, we have to divide the combination of plane regions in such a way that their areas can be easily find out using the known formula. After adding, subtracting or both adding and subtracting area of all or some of these regions, we can find the area of particular region.

- Area of the circle $=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$
- Area of semi circle $=\frac{1}{2} \pi r^{2}$
- Area of quadrant of circle $=\frac{1}{4} \pi r^{2}$
- Area enclosed between the two

$$
\text { circles }=\pi R^{2}-\pi r^{2}, \text { where } R>r
$$

- Circumference $=2 \pi r$
- Area of the sector $(O A B)$ having angle $\theta$ at the centre of the circle

$$
=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}
$$

- Arc length ( $l$ ) of a sector having angle $\theta$ at the centre,

$$
l=\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r
$$

- Area of minor segment (ARB)
- Area of major segment $(A P B)$

$$
=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}-r^{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{\theta}{2}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-346.jpg?height=269&width=312&top_left_y=1643&top_left_x=640)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-346.jpg?height=269&width=312&top_left_y=1643&top_left_x=1057)

$$
=\pi r^{2}-\text { Area of minor segment }(A R B)
$$

Let us understand the above concepts in a better way in different cases through illustrative examples.
[Fig. (ii)]

## CASE-I : Combination of square and circles.

Illustration: In fig., $A B C D$ is a square of side 10 cm . Semi-circles are drawn with each side of square as diameter. Find the area of (i) the unshaded region (ii) the shaded region Sol. Let us mark the four unshaded regions as $R_{1}, R_{2}, R_{3}$ and $R_{4}$.

Clearly,
Area of $R_{1}+$ Area of $R_{3}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-346.jpg?height=396&width=354&top_left_y=819&top_left_x=574)
$=$ Area of square $A B C D$ - Area of two semi-circles having centres at $Q$ and $S$
$=\left(10 \times 10-2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 3.14 \times 5^{2}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2} \quad[\because$ Radius $=A P=5 \mathrm{~cm}]$
$=(100-3.14 \times 25) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=(100-78.5) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=21.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

Similarly, we have
Area of $R_{2}+$ Area of $R_{4}=21.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(i) Area of the unshaded region

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\text { Area } R_{1}+\text { Area } R_{2}+\text { Area } R_{3}+\text { Area } R_{4} \\
& =\left(\text { Area } R_{1}+\text { Area } R_{3}\right)+\left(\text { Area } R_{2}+\text { Area } R_{4}\right) \\
& =2(21.5) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=43 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Area of the shaded region

$$
\left.\begin{array}{l}
=\text { Area of square } A B C D-\left(\text { Area of } R_{1}+\text { Area of } R_{2}\right. \\
=(100-43) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=57 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
\end{array}+\text { Area of } R_{3}+\text { Area of } R_{4}\right)
$$

## CASE-II : Combination of only circles.

Illustration: $P Q R S$ is a diameter of a circle of radius 6 cm . The lengths $P Q, Q R$ and $R S$ are equal. Semi-circles are drawn on $P Q$ and $Q S$ as diameters as shown in fig. Find the perimeter and area of the shaded region.

Sol. $P S=$ Diameter of a circle of radius $6 \mathrm{~cm}=12 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\therefore \quad P Q=Q R=R S=\frac{12}{3}=4 \mathrm{~cm}, Q S=Q R+R S=(4+4) \mathrm{cm}=8 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

Let $P$ be the perimeter and $A$ be the area of the shaded region.
$P=$ Arc of semi-circle of radius $6 \mathrm{~cm}+$ Arc of semi-circle of radius 4 cm
$\Rightarrow P=(\pi \times 6+\pi \times 4+\pi \times 2) \mathrm{cm}=12 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$
and,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-347.jpg?height=222&width=237&top_left_y=1889&top_left_x=600)
$A=$ Area of semi-circle with $P S$ as diameter + Area of semi-circle with $P Q$ as diameter - Area of semi-circle with $Q S$ as diameter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(6)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(2)^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(4)^{2} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7}\left(6^{2}+2^{2}-4^{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 24=\frac{264}{7} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=37.71 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Think Out of the Box

Q. 1. A circle has circumference 44 cm inscribed in a square. Find the side of square.
[Hint: Side of square $=2 \mathrm{r}$ ] Ans.
[Hint : $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}=\frac{3}{2} \times a \times r$ ] Ans. $37.68 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.

## CASE-III : Combination of trapezium and sector.

Illustration: In fig., $A B C D$ is a trapezium with $A B \| D C$ and $\angle B C D=60^{\circ}$. If $B F E C$ is a sector of a circle with centre $C$ and $A B=B C=7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $D E=4 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find the area of the shaded region. (Use $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ and $\sqrt{3}=1.732$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-347.jpg?height=551&width=261&top_left_y=610&top_left_x=472)

Sol. Clearly, $C E=C B=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\therefore \quad C D=C E+E D=(7+4) \mathrm{cm}=11 \mathrm{~cm}$

In right $\triangle C L B$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{B L}{B C} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & =\frac{B L}{7} \Rightarrow B L=\frac{7 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~cm} \\
\therefore \quad \text { Area of trapezium } & =\frac{1}{2}(A B+C D) \times B L \\
& =\frac{1}{2}(7+11) \times \frac{7 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{63 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

and,

$$
\text { Area of sector } B F E C=\frac{60^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{77}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

Let $A$ be the area of the shaded region. Then,

$$
A=\left(\frac{63 \sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{77}{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=(54.558-25.666) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=28.89 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

Q. 2. A circle inscribed in an equilateral triangle of side 12 cm . Find the area of inscribed circle. (Use $\pi=3.14$ and $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ ).

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. A path of width 2 meters runs around a circular plot whose circumference is $75 \frac{3}{7}$ metres. Find (i) the area of path.
(ii) the cost of gravelling the path at ₹ $\mathbf{7}$ per square metre.

Sol. Here, $2 \pi r=75 \frac{3}{7}$ i.e., $2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times r=\frac{528}{7}$ or $\quad r=\frac{528}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{7}{22}=12$ i.e., the radius of the plot is 12 m .
(i) Area of path $=\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)=\frac{22}{7}\left(14^{2}-12^{2}\right)=\frac{22}{7} \times 52 \mathrm{~m}^{2}=\frac{1144}{7} \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{m}$.
(ii) Cost $=₹\left(\frac{1144}{7} \times 7\right)=₹ 1144$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-348.jpg?height=346&width=353&top_left_y=644&top_left_x=1464)
2. The inner perimeter of a racetrack (figure) is 400 m and the outer perimeter is 488 m . The length of each straight portion is 90 m . Find the cost of developing the track at the rate of $₹ 12.50 / \mathrm{m}^{2}$.
Sol. Length of each straight portion $=90 \mathrm{~m}$, Outer perimeter of the track $=488 \mathrm{~m}$
∴ length of circular ends of outer perimeter $=488-90 \times 2=308 \mathrm{~m}$
The two circular ends make a complete circle
$\therefore 2 \pi R=308 \Rightarrow R=\frac{308}{2 \times 22} \times 7=49 \mathrm{~m}$
Inner perimeter of the track $=400 \mathrm{~m}$
∴ length of circular ends of inner perimeter $=400-90 \times 2=220 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore 2 \pi \mathrm{r}=220 \Rightarrow r=\frac{220}{2 \times 22} \times 7=35 \mathrm{~m}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-348.jpg?height=163&width=305&top_left_y=1096&top_left_x=1507)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-348.jpg?height=184&width=341&top_left_y=1263&top_left_x=1471)
∴ width of the track $=R-r=49-35=14 \mathrm{~m} \quad \therefore \quad$ Area of straight portion on the track $=2(90 \times 14)=2520 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Area of circular parts $=\pi R^{2}-\pi r^{2}=\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)=\pi(R+r)(R-r)=\frac{22}{7}(49+35)(49-35)=\frac{22}{7} \times 84 \times 14=3696 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
∴ total area of track $=2520+3696=6216 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \therefore$ cost of developing track $=6216 \times 12.50=$ ₹ 77,700
3. A chord of circle 14 cm makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the centre of the circle. Find :
(i) area of minor sector
(ii) area of the minor segment
(iii) area of the major sector
(iv) area of the major segment
[use $\pi=3.14]$
Sol. Given : $r=14 \mathrm{~cm}, \theta=60^{\circ}$
(i) Area of minor sector $\mathrm{OAPB}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=\frac{60^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times 3.14 \times 14 \times 14=102.57 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(ii) Area of minor segment $A P B=\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{360^{\circ}}-\frac{r^{2}}{2} \sin \theta=102.57-\frac{14 \times 14}{2} \sin 60^{\circ}$

$$
=102.57-98 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=17.80 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

(iii) Area of major sector $=$ Area of circle - Area of minor sector $O A P B$

$$
=\pi(14)^{2}-102.57=615.44-102.57=512.87 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

(iv) Area of major segment $\mathrm{AQB}=$ Area of circle - Area of minor segment $A P B$

$$
=615.44-17.80=597.64 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-348.jpg?height=419&width=381&top_left_y=1784&top_left_x=1436)
4. The shape of the top of a table in a restaurant is that of a segment of a circle with centre $O$ and $\angle B O D=90^{\circ}$. If $B O=O D=60 \mathrm{~cm}$, find :
(i) the area of the top of the table.
(ii) the perimeter of the table.
[Take $\pi=3.14]$

Sol. Given that $\angle B O D=90^{\circ}$, so the top of the table is $\frac{3}{4}$ of a circle of radius 60 cm .
(i) Area of the top of the table $=\frac{3}{4} \pi r^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \times 3.14 \times(60)^{2}=8478 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
(ii) Perimeter of the table $=\left(\frac{3}{4} \times 2 \pi r+2 r\right)=\left(\frac{3}{4} \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 60+2 \times 60\right)=(90 \times 3.14+120)=402.6 \mathrm{~cm}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-349.jpg?height=291&width=291&top_left_y=388&top_left_x=1580)
5. Find the area of the shaded region. [All the circles shown in the figure are congruent.]
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-349.jpg?height=339&width=351&top_left_y=788&top_left_x=888)

Sol. It is clear that any two congruent circles intersect orthogonally. In the figure below :
$O_{1} A O_{2} B$ is a square,
Since, $\angle A O_{1} B=90^{\circ}, \angle A O_{2} B=90^{\circ}, \angle O_{2} B O_{1}=90^{\circ}$ and $\angle O_{2} A O_{1}=90^{\circ}$
Sides $O_{1} A=O_{1} B=O_{2} A=O_{2} B=5$ unit.
In the figure, $O_{1} A$ and $O_{1} B$ should be perpendicular to each other.
Also $O_{2} A$ and $O_{2} B$ should be perpendicular to each other.
The area of shaded region in figure I
$=$ Area $\left(\right.$ sector $\left.O_{1} A B\right)+$ Area (sector of $\left.O_{2} B A\right)-$ Area $\left(\diamond O_{1} A O_{2} B\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-349.jpg?height=261&width=426&top_left_y=1192&top_left_x=1448)
$=\frac{1}{4} \pi(5)^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \pi(5)^{2}-(5)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \pi(5)^{2}-(5)^{2}=25(\pi / 2-1)$.
Hence, the required area $=4$ times the area calculated above $=4 \times 25(\pi / 2-1)=100(\pi / 2-1)=57$ sq. unit. (approx).
6. The sides of a rectangle inscribed in a circle are 8 cm and 6 cm . Find the difference of the area of the circle and the rectangle.
Sol. Let $A B C D$ be the given rectangle.
The diagonal $A C$ is the diameter of the circle
$A C^{2}=8^{2}+6^{2}=64+36=100$
$\therefore A C=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Radius of the circle $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Area of the circle $=\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times 5 \times 5=\frac{550}{7}=78.57 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of the rectangle $=8 \times 6=48 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
∴ Difference of the areas $=78.57-48=30.57 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-349.jpg?height=316&width=350&top_left_y=1690&top_left_x=1521)
7. In figure, there are three semi-circles, $A, B$ and $C$ having diameter 7 cm each, and another semi-circle $E$ having a circle $D$ with diameter 10.5 cm are shown. Calculate:
(i) the area of the shaded region.
(ii) the cost of painting the shaded region at the rate of 25 paise per $\mathrm{cm}^{2}$, to the nearest rupee.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-349.jpg?height=350&width=519&top_left_y=2170&top_left_x=804)

Sol. (i) Area of the shaded region $=$ Area of semicircle with diameter $21 \mathrm{~cm}-$ Area of two semicircle with diameter $7 \mathrm{~cm}-$ Area of circle with diameter $10.5 \mathrm{~cm}+$ Area of semi circle with diameter 7 cm .
So, area of the shaded region $=\frac{1}{2} \pi\left(\frac{21}{2}\right)^{2}-2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \pi\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^{2}-\pi \times\left(\frac{21}{4}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \pi\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^{2}$.
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{21}{2} \times \frac{21}{2}-\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \times \frac{7}{2}-\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{21}{4} \times \frac{21}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \times \frac{7}{2}=\frac{22}{7}\left[\frac{21 \times 21}{8}-\frac{49}{4}-\frac{21 \times 21}{16}+\frac{49}{8}\right]$
$=49 \times \frac{22}{16}=67.38 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(ii) Cost of painting the shaded region $=0.25 \times 67.38=₹ 16.84$
8. In figure, $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B} \boldsymbol{C} \boldsymbol{D}$ is a square of side $\mathbf{1 0} \mathbf{~ c m}$ and semicircles are drawn with each side of the square as diameter. Find area of the shaded region.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-350.jpg?height=350&width=328&top_left_y=868&top_left_x=840)

Sol. We mark the area of unshaded regions as $A_{1}, A_{2} A_{3}$ and $A_{4}$ as shown in the figure.
Area of the shaded region $=$ Area of the square $A B C D$ - Area of the unshaded portion ( $A_{1}+A_{2}+A_{3}+A_{4}$ ).
Area of unshaded region $A_{1}+A_{3}=$ Area of square $A B C D-$ Area of semi circle on BC as diameter

- Area of semi-circle on $A D$ as diameter.

$$
=10^{2}-\frac{\pi \cdot 5^{2}}{2}-\frac{\pi \cdot 5^{2}}{2}=100-25 \pi .
$$

Similarly, area of unshaded region $A_{2}+A_{4}=$ Area of square $A B C D-$ Area of the semi-circles on diameter $A B$ and $D C$.
So, Area $A_{2}+A_{4}=10^{2}-2 \times \frac{\pi \times 5^{2}}{2}=100-25 \pi$.
So, Area $A_{1}+A_{2}+A_{3}+A_{4}=200-50 \pi$.
Area of the shaded region $=$ Area of square $A B C D-$ Area ( $A_{1}+A_{2}+A_{3}+A_{4}$ )
$=10^{2}-(200-50 \pi)=(100-200+50 \pi) \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm} .=50 \pi-100=50(\pi-2) \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm} .=50(3.14-2)=50 \times 1.14=57 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
9. Calculate the area of the shaded portion.

The quadrants shown in the figure are each of radius $\mathbf{7} \mathbf{~ c m} \boldsymbol{.}\left(\right.$ Take $\boldsymbol{\pi} \boldsymbol{=} \frac{\mathbf{2 2}}{\mathbf{7}} \boldsymbol{)}$
Sol. Draw square $P Q R S$ as shown in the figure given below.
Here $P Q=Q R=P S=R S=14 \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of the shaded portion $=$ Area of the square $P Q R S-$ Area of four equal quadrants
$=14 \times 14-4 \times \frac{1}{4} \pi \times(7)^{2}=196-\frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7=196-154=42 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-350.jpg?height=346&width=346&top_left_y=1850&top_left_x=1471)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-350.jpg?height=355&width=356&top_left_y=2151&top_left_x=826)
10. A bicycle's wheel makes 5000 revolutions in moving 11 km . Find the diameter of the wheel.

Sol. Distance covered in 5000 revolutions $=11 \mathrm{~km}=11000 \mathrm{~m}$
∴ Distance covered in one revolution $=\frac{11000}{5000}=\frac{11}{5} \mathrm{~m}$. Now, we have $2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\frac{11}{5} \Rightarrow r=\frac{11}{5} \times \frac{7}{22 \times 2}=\frac{7}{20} \mathrm{~m}$
∴ Diameter of bicycle's wheel $=2 \mathrm{r}=2 \times \frac{7}{20} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{7}{10} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{7}{10} \times 100 \mathrm{~cm}=70 \mathrm{~cm}$.
11. A boy is cycling such that the wheels of the cycle are making 140 revolutions per minute. If the diameter of the wheel is 60 cm , calculate the speed per hour with which the boy is cycling.
Sol. Radius of wheel $=\mathrm{r}=\frac{60}{2}=30 \mathrm{~cm}$. Circumference of wheel $=2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\frac{1320}{7} \mathrm{~cm}$
Distance covered in one revolution = Circumference $=\frac{1320}{7} \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Distance covered in 140 revolutions $=\frac{1320}{7} \times 140 \mathrm{~cm}=26400 \mathrm{~cm}=264 \mathrm{~m}=\frac{264}{1000} \mathrm{~km}$
Now, distance covered in one minute $=\frac{264}{1000} \mathrm{~km}$
∵ Distance covered in one minute = Distance covered in 140 revolutions)
⇒ Distance covered in 1 hour $=\frac{264}{1000} \times 60 \mathrm{~km}=15.84 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.
12. The length of minute hand of a clock is $\mathbf{1 4 ~ c m}$. Find the area swept by the minute hand in one minute.

Sol. Clearly, minute hand describes a circle of radius equal to its length i.e., 14 cm .
Since, minute hand rotates through $6^{\circ}$ in one minute.
∴ $\quad$ Area swept by the minute hand in one minute $=$ Area of a sector of angle $6^{\circ}$ in a circle of radius 14 cm .
Thus, the required area $=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}=\left[\frac{6^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(14)^{2}\right]=\frac{154}{15}=10.26 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
13. The short and long hands of a clock are 4 cm and 6 cm long respectively. Find the sum of distances travelled by their tips in 2 days?
Sol. In 2 days, the short hand will complete 4 rounds.
$\therefore \quad$ Distance moved by its tip $=4$ [Circumference of circle] $=\frac{704}{7} \mathrm{~cm}$
In 2 days, the long hand will complete 48 rounds.
∴ Distance moved by its tip $=48$ [Circumference of a circle of radius 6 cm ] $=\frac{12672}{7} \mathrm{~cm}$
Hence, required sum $=\left(\frac{704}{7}+\frac{12672}{7}\right)=1910.85 \mathrm{~cm}$
14. In the figure, ABCD is a trapezium with $\mathrm{AB} \| \mathrm{DC}$ and $\angle \mathrm{BCD}=60^{\circ}$. If BFEC is a sector of a circle with centre C and $A B=B C=7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $D E=4 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find the area of the shaded region. (Take $\sqrt{3}=1.732$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-351.jpg?height=243&width=405&top_left_y=1957&top_left_x=861)

Sol. Clearly, $\mathrm{CE}=\mathrm{CB}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{CE}+\mathrm{ED}=(7+4)=11 \mathrm{~cm}$.
In $\Delta \mathrm{CLB}$, we have, $\sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{BL}}{\mathrm{BC}} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{BL}}{7} \Rightarrow \mathrm{BL}=\frac{7 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~cm} \therefore$ Area of trapezium $=\frac{1}{2}(A B+C D) \times B L=\frac{63 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of sector BFEC $=\frac{60^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7^{2}=\frac{77}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \therefore \quad$ Required area $=\left(\frac{63 \sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{77}{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=28.89 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

## Multiple Choice Questions

## Fill in the Blanks

DIRECTIONS : Complete the following statements with an appropriate word / term to be filled in the blank space(s).

1. A sector of a circle is called a $\_\_\_\_$ sector if the minor arc of the circle is a part of its boundary.
2. The boundary of a sector consists of an arc of the circle and the two $\_\_\_\_$ .
3. The region enclosed by an arc and a chord is called the
$\_\_\_\_$ of the circle.
4. Circumference of a circle is $\_\_\_\_$ .
5. Area of a circle is $\_\_\_\_$ .
6. Length of an arc of a sector of a circle with radius $r$ and angle with degree measure $\theta$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
7. The area of a circle is the measurement of the region enclosed by its $\_\_\_\_$ .
8. If the area of a circle is $154 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$, then its circumference is $\_\_\_\_$ .
9. Area of a sector of a circle with radius 6 cm , if angle of the sector is $60^{\circ}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ .

## True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. A segment corresponding a major arc of a circle is known as the major segment.
2. If the boundary of a segment is a minor arc of a circle, then the corresponding segment is called a minor segment.
3. A minor sector has an angle ' $\theta$ ' subtended at the centre of the circle, whereas a major sector has no angle.
4. The perimeter of a circle is generally known as its circumference.
5. Distance moved by a rotating wheel in one revolution is equal to the circumference of the wheel.
6. In a circle of radius 21 cm , an arc subtends an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the centre, then the length of the arc is 22 cm .
7. If the circumference of a circle is 88 cm , then its radius is 14 cm .
8. The length of an arc of a sector of a circle of radius $r$ units and of centre angle $\theta$ is $\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}$.
9. If a sector of a circle of diameter 21 cm subtends an angle of $120^{\circ}$ at the centre, then its area is $85.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
10. The length of a rope by which a cow must be tethered in order that it may be able to graze of an area of $616 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ is 18 m .

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. In fig., a square in a circle of diameter $d$ and another square is circumscribing the circle. Is the area of the outer square four times the area of inner square? Give reason for your answer.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-356.jpg?height=277&width=277&top_left_y=1471&top_left_x=1279)
2. Is the area of largest circle that can be drawn inside a rectangle of length $a \mathrm{~cm}$ and breadth $b \mathrm{~cm}(a>b)$ is $\pi b^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ ? Justify your answer.
3. Will it be true to say that the perimeter of square circumscribing a circle of radius $a \mathrm{~cm}$ is $8 a \mathrm{~cm}$ ? Give reasons for your answer.
4. A triangle has sides of $48 \mathrm{~cm}, 14 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 50 cm . Find circum-radius of triangle.
5. $\mathrm{H}_{1}$ is a regular hexagon circumscribing a circle. $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle. Find the ratio of areas of $\mathrm{H}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.

## HOTS Questions

1. In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle with $\mathrm{AC}=24 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{AB}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\angle \mathrm{BOD}=90^{\circ}$. Find the area of the shaded region. (take, $\pi=3.14$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-357.jpg?height=383&width=398&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=452)
2. There is a circular park in front of Delhi Public School whose radius is 10 m . Around the park, one circular line is drawn, which is having radius 11 m .
(i) A person takes four round of a circular park. What distance he covers?
(ii) Suppose, two persons $A$ and $B$ are standing on the opposite sides of a circular line. What is the maximum distance between them?
3. A circle of radius ' $a$ ' is divided into 6 equal sectors. An equilateral triangle is drawn on the chord of each sector to lie outside the circle. Find the area of the resulting figure.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-357.jpg?height=302&width=332&top_left_y=1208&top_left_x=484)
4. There are two concentric circles such that the area of the outer circle is four times the area of the inner circle. Let $A, B$ and $C$ be three distinct points on the perimeter of the outer circle such that $A B$ and $A C$ are tangents to the inner circle. If the area of the outer circle is 12 square centimeters then find the area (in square centimeters) of the triangle $A B C$.
5. In an equilateral triangle of side 18 cm , a circle is inscribed touching its sides. Find the area of the remaining portion of the triangle.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. A horse is tied to a peg at one corner of a square shaped grass field of side 15 m .
(Use $\pi=3.14$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-357.jpg?height=408&width=410&top_left_y=534&top_left_x=1265)

Then answer the following questions.
(i) If rope of horse is 5 m long then the area of that part of the field in which the horse can graze is :
(a) $19.625 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(b) $29.625 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(c) $19 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(d) $18.625 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(ii) If rope of horse 10 m long then the area of that part of the field in which the horse can graze is:
(a) $68.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(b) $78.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(c) $58.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(d) $73.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(iii) The increase in the grazing area if the rope were 10 m long instead of 5 m .
(a) $58.875 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(b) $58 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(c) $57.875 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(d) $68.87 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(iv) If rope of horse is 5 m long then the area of that part of the field in which the horse can not graze is:
(a) $204.37 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(b) $200.37 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(c) $205.37 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(d) $205 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(v) If rope of horse 10 m long then the area of that part of the field in which the horse can not graze is :
(a) $146.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(b) $205.37 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(c) $46.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(d) $146 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

## Exercise 2

## Text-book Questions

1. Figure depicts an archery target marked with its five scoring regions from the centre outwards as Gold, Red, Blue, Black and White. The diameter of the region representing Gold score is 21 cm and each of the other bands is 10.5 cm wide. Find the area of each of the five scoring regions.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-358.jpg?height=321&width=327&top_left_y=870&top_left_x=427)
2. Tick the correct answer in the following and justify your choice. If the perimeter and the area of a circle are numerically equal, then the radius of the circle is
(a) 2 units
(b) $\pi$ units
(c) 4 units
(d) 7 units
3. A chord of a circle of radius 10 cm subtends a right angle at the centre. Find the area of the corresponding :
(i) minor segment
(ii) major sector (Use $\pi=3.14$ )
4. A chord of a circle of radius 12 cm subtends an angle of $120^{\circ}$ at the centre. Find the area of the corresponding segment of the circle.
(Use $\pi=3.14$ and $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ )
5. Find the area of the shaded region between the two quadrants of circles of radius 8 cm each shown in the given figure :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-358.jpg?height=264&width=332&top_left_y=1923&top_left_x=429)
6. A brooch is made with silver wire in the form of a circle with diameter 35 mm . The wire is also used in making 5 diameters which divide the circle into 10 equal sectors as shown in figure. Find :
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-358.jpg?height=316&width=314&top_left_y=557&top_left_x=1260)
(i) the total length of the silver wire required.
(ii) the area of each sector of the brooch.
7. An umbrella has 8 ribs which are equally spaced (see Fig.). Assuming umbrella to be a flat circle of radius 45 cm , find the area between two consecutive ribs of the umbrella.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-358.jpg?height=316&width=414&top_left_y=1151&top_left_x=1215)
8. A car has two wipers which do not overlap. Each wiper has a blade of length 25 cm sweeping through an angle of $115^{\circ}$. Find the total area cleaned at each sweep of the blades.
9. To warn ships for underwater rocks, a lighthouse spreads a red coloured light over a sector of angle $80^{\circ}$ to a distance of 16.5 km . Find the area of the sea over which the ships are warned. (Use $\pi=3.14$ )
10. A round table cover has six equal designs as shown in figure. If the radius of the cover is 28 cm , find the cost of making the designs at the rate of $₹ 0.35$ per $\mathrm{cm}^{2}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-358.jpg?height=321&width=293&top_left_y=1987&top_left_x=1270)
(Use $\sqrt{3}=1.7$ )
11. In a circular table cover of radius 32 cm , a design is formed leaving an equilateral triangle ABC in the middle as shown in figure. Find the area of the design.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=318&width=307&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=495)
12. A chord of a circle of radius 12 cm subtends an angle of $120^{\circ}$ at the centre. Find the area of the corresponding segment of the circle. (Use $\pi=3.14$ and $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ )
13. In figure, AB and CD are two diameters of a circle (with centre O ) perpendicular to each other and OD is the diameter of the smaller circle. If $\mathrm{OA}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$, find the area of the shaded region.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=435&width=428&top_left_y=1000&top_left_x=438)
14. The area of an equilateral triangle ABC is $17320.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$. With each vertex of the triangle as centre, a circle is drawn with radius equal to half the length of the side of the triangle (see figure). Find the area of the shaded region.
(Use $\pi=3.14$ and $\sqrt{3}=1.73205$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=309&width=334&top_left_y=1695&top_left_x=482)
15. On a square handkerchief, nine circular designs each of radius 7 cm are made (see Fig.). Find the area of the remaining portion of the handkerchief.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=353&width=328&top_left_y=2142&top_left_x=488)
16. In figure, a square OABC is inscribed in a quadrant OPBQ . If $\mathrm{OA}=20 \mathrm{~cm}$, find the area of the shaded region. (Use $\pi=3.14$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=328&width=391&top_left_y=497&top_left_x=1279)
17. AB and CD are, respectively, arcs of two concentric circles of radii 21 cm and 7 cm and centre O (see figure). If $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=30^{\circ}$, find the area of the shaded region.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=318&width=245&top_left_y=964&top_left_x=1352)
18. In figure, ABC is a quadrant of circle of radius 14 cm and a semicircle is drawn with BC as diameter. Find the area of the shaded region.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=200&width=204&top_left_y=1402&top_left_x=1361)

## Exemplar Questions

1. In fig., arcs are drawn by taking vertices $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C of an equilateral triangle of side 10 cm to intersect the sides $\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CA}$ and AB at their respective mid-points $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}$ and F. Find the area of the shaded region. (Use $\pi=3.14$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=337&width=391&top_left_y=1859&top_left_x=1279)
2. In fig. a circle of radius 7.5 cm is inscribed in a square. Find the area of the shaded region. (Use $\pi=3.14$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-359.jpg?height=213&width=215&top_left_y=2298&top_left_x=1366)
3. A calf is tied with a rope of a length 6 m at the corner of a square grassy lawn of side 20 m . If the length of the rope is increased by 5.5 m , find the increase in area of the grassy lawn in which the calf can graze.
4. Area of a sector of a circle of radius 36 cm is $54 \pi \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$. Find the length of the corresponding arc of the sector.
5. If the area of a circle is $154 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$, then its perimeter is
(a) 11 cm
(b) 22 cm
(c) 44 cm
(d) 55 cm
6. If $\theta$ is the angle (in degree) of a sector of a circle of radius $r$, then, area of the sector is
(a) $\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{360^{\circ}}$
(b) $\frac{\pi r^{2} \theta}{180^{\circ}}$
(c) $\frac{2 \pi r \theta}{360^{\circ}}$
(d) $\frac{2 \pi r \theta}{180^{\circ}}$
7. If the sum of the areas of two circles with radii $\mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}$ is equal to the area of a circle of radius R , then
(a) $\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}$
(b) $R_{1}^{2}+R_{2}^{2}=R^{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}<\mathrm{R}$
(d) $\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}<\mathrm{R}^{2}$
8. It is proposed to build a single circular park equal in area to the sum of areas of two circular parks of diameters 16 m and 12 m in a locality. The radius of the new park would be
(a) 10 m
(b) 15 m
(c) 20 m
(d) 24 m

## Single Option Correct

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.tt

1. The sum of the areas of two circles, which touch each other externally, is $153 \pi$. If the sum of their radii is 15 , then the ratio of the larger to the smaller radius is
(a) $4: 1$
(b) $2: 1$
(c) $3: 1$
(d) None of these
2. A race track is in the form of a ring whose inner and outer circumference are 437 m and 503 m respectively. The area of the track is
(a) $66 \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm}$
(b) $4935 \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm}$
(c) $9870 \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm}$
(d) None of these
3. If the sum of the circumferences of two circles with diameters $d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$ is equal to the circumference of a circle of diameter $d$, then
(a) $\quad d_{1}^{2}+d_{2}^{2}=d^{2}$
(b) $d_{1}+d_{2}=d$
(c) $d_{1}+d_{2}>d$
(d) $d_{1}+d_{2}<d$
4. In the adjoining figure, $O A B C$ is a square of side $7 \mathrm{~cm} . O A C$ is a quadrant of a circle with $O$ as centre. The area of the shaded region is
(a) $10.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(b) $38.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(c) $49 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(d) $11.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

(a) 43.98 sq. cm
(b) 53.67 sq. cm
(c) 47.24 sq. cm
(d) 38.54 sq.cm
6. A sector is cut from a circular sheet of radius 100 cm , the angle of the sector being $240^{\circ}$. If another circle of the area same as the sector is formed, then radius of the new circle is
(a) 79.5 cm
(b) 81.6 cm
(c) 83.4 cm
(d) 88.5 cm
7. The area of a sector of angle $p$ (in degrees) of a circle with radius R is
(a) $\frac{p}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi R$
(b) $\frac{p}{180^{\circ}} \times \pi R^{2}$
(c) $\frac{p}{720^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi R$
(d) $\frac{p}{720^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi R^{2}$
8. If the sector of a circle of diameter 10 cm subtends an angle of $144^{\circ}$ at the centre, then the length of the arc of the sector is
(a) $2 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$
(b) $4 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$
(c) $5 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$
(d) $6 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$
9. The figure below shows two concentric circles with centre $O . P Q R S$ is a square inscribed in the outer circle. It also circumscribes the inner circle, touching it at point $B, C$, $D$ and $A$. The ratio of the perimeter of the outer circle to that of polygon $A B C D$ is
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-360.jpg?height=330&width=333&top_left_y=2030&top_left_x=1251)
(a) $\frac{\pi}{4}$
(b) $\frac{3 \pi}{2}$
(c) $\frac{\pi}{2}$
(d) $\pi$
10. If a circular grass lawn of 35 m in radius has a path 7 m wide running around it on the outside, then the area of the path is
(a) $1450 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(b) $1576 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(c) $1694 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(d) $3368 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
11. In the adjoining figure, $O A C B$ is a quadrant of a circle of radius 7 cm . The perimeter of the quadrant is
(a) 11 cm
(b) 18 cm
(c) 25 cm
(d) 36 cm
12. If the circumference of a circle increases from $4 \pi$ to $8 \pi$, then its area is
(a) halved
(b) doubled
(c) tripled
(d) quadrupled
13. If the radius of a circle is diminished by $10 \%$, then its area is diminished by
(a) $10 \%$
(b) $19 \%$
(c) $36 \%$
(d) $20 \%$
14. If the perimeter of a semi-circular protractor is 36 cm , then its diameter is
(a) 10 cm
(b) 14 cm
(c) 12 cm
(d) 16 cm
15. The area of a circular path of uniform width 'd' surrounding a circular region of radius ' $r$ ' is
(a) $\pi \mathrm{d}(2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{d})$
(b) $\pi(2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{d}) \mathrm{r}$
(c) $\pi(d+r) d$

16. A sector with acute central angle $\theta$ is cut from a circle of diameter 14 cm . The area (in $\mathrm{cm}^{2}$ ) of the circle circumscribing the sector is
[NTSE]
(a) $\frac{22}{7} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}$
(b) $\frac{77}{2} \sec ^{2} \theta$
(c) $\frac{77}{2} \cos ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}$
(d) $\frac{77}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}$
17. In the figure, PQSO is a trapezium in which $\mathrm{PQ} \| \mathrm{OS}$, $\angle \mathrm{POS}=135^{\circ}$ and $\angle \mathrm{OSQ}=90^{\circ}$. Points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and R lie on a circle with centre O and radius 12 cm . The area of the shaded part, in $\mathrm{cm}^{2}$, is
