![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-361.jpg?height=352&width=577&top_left_y=2122&top_left_x=324)
(a) $61 \frac{2}{7}$
(b) $61 \frac{5}{7}$
(c) $73 \frac{5}{7}$
(d) $73 \frac{2}{7}$
18. If Anish is moving along the boundary of a triangular field of sides $35 \mathrm{~m}, 53 \mathrm{~m}$ and 66 m and you are moving along the boundary of a circular field whose area is double the area of the triangular field, then the radius of the circular field is (Take $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ )
[NTSE]
(a) $14 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(b) $3 \sqrt{14} \mathrm{~m}$
(c) $28 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
(d) $7 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$
19. If the area of a square inscribed in a semicircle is $2 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$, then the area of the square inscribed in a full circle of the same radius is $\_\_\_\_$ [NTSE]
(a) $5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(b) $10 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(c) $5 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(d) $25 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
20. Suppose we have two circles of radius 2 each in the plane such that the distance between their centers is $2 \sqrt{3}$. The area of the region common to both circles lies between
(a) 0.5 and 0.6
(b) 0.65 and 0.7
(c) 0.7 and 0.75
(d) 0.8 and 0.9
[KVPY]
21. A circle is inscribed in a right angled triangle of perimeter $7 \pi$. Then the ratio of numerical values of circumference of the circle to the area of the right angled triangle is
(a) $4: 7$
(b) $3: 7$
(c) $2: 7$
(d) $1: 7$
[NTSE]
22. How much time the minute hand of a clock will take to describe an angle of $\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ radians?
[NTSE]
(a) 15 minutes
(b) 20 minutes
(c) 10 minutes
(d) 25 minutes

## More Than One Option Correct

## DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct.

1. If the radius of a circle is $\frac{7}{\sqrt{\pi}} \mathrm{~cm}$, then the area of the
(a) $\frac{49}{\pi} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(b) $\frac{154}{\pi} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(c) $154 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(d) $49 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
2. If the ratio of the areas of the two circles is $25: 16$, then the ratio of their circumferences is
(a) $\frac{625}{500}$
(b) $\frac{4}{5}$
(c) $\frac{5}{4}$
(d) $\frac{500}{625}$
3. If the sector of a circle of diameter 14 cm subtends an angle of $30^{\circ}$ at the centre, then its area is
(a) $49 \pi$
(b) $\frac{49 \pi}{12}$
(c) $\frac{242}{3 \pi}$
(d) $\frac{121}{3 \pi}$
4. Which of the following is/are correct?
(a) Area of a circle with radius 6 cm , if angle of sector is $60^{\circ}$, is $\frac{132}{14} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
(b) If $a$ chord of circle of radius 14 cm makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the centre of the circle, then area of major sector is $512.87 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
(c) The ratio between the circumference and area of a circle of radius 5 cm is $2: 5$.
(d) Area of a circle whose radius is 6 cm , when the length of the $\operatorname{arc}$ is 22 cm , is $66 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
5. Which of the following statement is/are correct?
(a) A chord divides the interior of a circle into two parts.
(b) An arc of a circle whose length is less than that of a semicircle of the same circle is a called a minor arc.
(c) Circles having the same centre but different radii are called concentric circles.
(d) A line segment joining any two points of a circle is called an arc.

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

1. Assertion : If the circumference of a circle is 176 cm , then its radius is 28 cm .
Reason : Circumference $=2 \pi \times$ radius.
2. Assertion : If the outer and inner diameter of a circular path is 10 m and 6 m , then area of the path is $16 \pi \mathrm{~m}^{2}$.
Reason : If $R$ and $r$ be the radius of outer and inner circular path respectively then area of path $=\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)$.
3. Assertion : If a wire of length 22 cm is bent in the shape of a circle, then area of the circle so formed is $40 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
Reason : Circumference of the circle $=$ length of the wire.

6. The perimeter of sector of a circle whose central angle $45^{\circ}$ is 19.5 cm , then the radius of the circle is

## SOLUTIONS <br> (Brief Explanations of Selected Questions)

1. (d) Let AB be the chord of circle such that
$\angle \mathrm{AOB}=90^{\circ}$
Let $\mathrm{OA}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \mathrm{AB}=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of minor segment A X B
$=$ Area of the sector $\mathrm{AOB}-$ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{AOB}$
$=\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi(10)^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-364.jpg?height=255&width=227&top_left_y=1050&top_left_x=479)
$=25 \pi-50=25 \times 3.14-50$
$=78.5-50=28.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
2. (a) Area of the shaded region
$=\frac{40^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(7)^{2}-\frac{40^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(3.5)^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{9} \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(7^{2}-3.5^{2}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{9} \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(49-\frac{49}{4}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{9} \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{49}{4} \times 3=\frac{77}{6} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
3. (b) We have,

Area of square metal plate
$=40 \times 40=1600 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of each hole
$=\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{11}{14} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
∴ Area of 441 holes
$=441 \times \frac{11}{14}=346.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Hence, area of the remaining square plate
$=(1600-346.5)=1253.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
4. (c) Area of the remaining piece of paper
$=$ Area of trapezium - Area of 2 semi-circles
$=\frac{1}{2}(7+19) \times 20-2 \times \frac{1}{2} \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 26 \times 20-\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \times \frac{7}{2}$
$=260-38.5=221.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
5. (b) Diameter of each semi-circle $=\frac{42}{3}=14 \mathrm{~cm}$

Radius of each semi-circle $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of 6 semi-circle $=6 \times \frac{\pi r^{2}}{2}=3 \pi r^{2}$
$=3 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7=462 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of cloth piece $=42 \times 14=588 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of the coloured portion $=588-462$
$=126 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
6. (b) Area of rectangle $=28 \times 23=644 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

Radius of semi-circle $=28 \div 2=14 \mathrm{~cm}$
Radius of quadrant $=23-16=7 \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of unshaded region
$=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14 \times 14\right)+\left(2 \times \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7\right)$
$=385 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
∴ Shaded area $=(644-385)=259 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
7. (b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-364.jpg?height=241&width=218&top_left_y=1324&top_left_x=1146)

Area of sector OACB
$=\frac{45^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 28 \times 28=308 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area $(\triangle \mathrm{AOB})=\frac{1}{2}(28)(28) \sin 45^{\circ}$
$=14 \times 28 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=277.19$
Area of minor segment $=308-277.19$
$=30.81 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
8. (a) Perimeter of sector $=25 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{r}+\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{r}=25$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{r}+\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \mathrm{r}=25$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{r}+\frac{11}{7} \mathrm{r}=25 \Rightarrow \frac{25}{7} \mathrm{r}=25 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=7$
Area of minor segment $=\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{360^{\circ}}-\frac{\sin \theta}{2}\right) \mathrm{r}^{2}$
$=\left(\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}}-\frac{\sin 90^{\circ}}{2}\right)(7)^{2}$
$=\left(\frac{11}{14}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times 49=\frac{4}{14} \times 49=14 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.

## Short Answer Questions

1. Distance covered in 10 minutes
$=\left(\frac{10}{60} \times 66\right) \mathrm{km}=11 \mathrm{~km}=(11 \times 1000 \times 100) \mathrm{cm}$
∴ Distance covered in one revolution $=\pi d$
$=\left(\frac{22}{7} \times 80\right) \mathrm{cm}$
∴ No. of revolutions $=\frac{11 \times 1000 \times 100 \times 7}{22 \times 80}=4375$
2. $\quad 77 \mathrm{~cm}, 63 \mathrm{~cm}$;

Hint. $2 r_{1}+2 r_{2}=2.8 \Rightarrow r_{1}+r_{2}=1.4$
$2 \times \frac{22}{7}\left(r_{1}-r_{2}\right)=0.88 \Rightarrow r_{1}-r_{2}=0.14$
3. The angle through which the minute hand rotates in 5
minutes $=\frac{5}{60^{\circ}} \times 360^{\circ}=30^{\circ}$
∴ The area swept $=\frac{30^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{154}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
4. Length of $\operatorname{arc} A P B=\frac{240^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2}=\frac{44}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Required length $=\left(\frac{7}{2}+\frac{7}{2}+\frac{44}{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}=\frac{65}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
5. Let $r_{1}=$ radius of bigger circle,
$r_{2}=$ radius of smaller circle.
As given $\pi r_{1}^{2}+\pi r_{2}^{2}=180 \pi$.
$\Rightarrow r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}=180$ and
distance between centers i.e.,
$r_{1}-r_{2}=6 \Rightarrow r_{2}=r_{1}-6$
From the equation (i), $r_{1}^{2}+\left(r_{1}-6\right)^{2}=180$
$\Rightarrow r_{1}^{2}+\left(r_{1}^{2}-12 r_{1}+36\right)=180$
$\Rightarrow 2 r_{1}^{2}-12 r_{1}+36=180 \Rightarrow 2 r_{1}^{2}-12 r_{1}-144=0$
$\Rightarrow r_{1}^{2}-6 r_{1}-72=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(r_{1}-12\right)\left(r_{1}+6\right)=0$.
Hence, $r_{1}=12 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $d_{1}=24 \mathrm{~cm}$.
So, diameter of the bigger circle $=24 \mathrm{~cm}$.
6. If the sector makes an angle $\theta$ (in degrees) at the centre of the circle, then

$$
l=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi r \Rightarrow \frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r=\frac{1}{2} l
$$

Now, area of sector $=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r \times r=\frac{1}{2} l r$
7. Area of shaded region
$=$ Area of equilateral $\triangle A B C+$ Area of the semi-circle
$=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times 14 \times 14+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7$
$=1.732 \times 49+77$
$=161.87 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

( ∵ Circumference of circle $=$ Perimeter of square)
side of the square $=\left(\frac{1}{4} \times 84 \pi\right)=21 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ $\frac{\text { Area of circle }}{\text { Area of square }}=\frac{\pi \times 42^{2}}{(21 \pi)^{2}}=\frac{4}{\pi}=\frac{4 \times 7}{22}=\frac{14}{11}$.

10. The arc length $l$ and area $A$ of a sector of angle $\theta$ in a circle of radius $r$ are given by
$l=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi r$ and $A=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}$ respectively.
Here, $r=21 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\theta=150$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad l & =\left\{\frac{150^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 21\right\} \mathrm{cm}=55 \mathrm{~cm} \\
\text { and, } \quad A=\left\{\frac{150^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(21)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2} & =\frac{1155}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \\
& =577.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

11. We have,

Angle described by the minute hand in one minute $=6^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad$ Angle described by the minute hand in 35 minutes

$$
=(6 \times 35)^{\circ}=210^{\circ}
$$

$\therefore \quad$ Area swept by the minute hand in 35 minutes
$=$ Area of a sector of angle $210^{\circ}$ in a circle of radius 10 cm

$$
\begin{array}{r}
=\left\{\frac{210^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(10)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}=183.3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \\
{\left[\text { Using: } A=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}\right]}
\end{array}
$$

12. We have,

Area of square metal plate $=40 \times 40 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=1600 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ Area of each hole
$=\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{11}{14} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
∴ Area of 441 holes $=441 \times \frac{11}{14} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=346.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Hence, area of the remaining square plate
$=(1600-346.5) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=1253.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
13. Since, the difference between the circumference and the radius of a circle is 37 cm .
So, $2 \pi \mathrm{r}-\mathrm{r}=37 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}\left(\frac{44}{7}-1\right)=37$
$\mathrm{r} \times \frac{37}{7}=37 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Therefore, circumference of the circle $=2 \pi \mathrm{r}$
$=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7=44 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
14. Here, the radius of the smaller circle $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$

Area of the sector OBD (The sector angle is $40^{\circ}$ )
$=\frac{40^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi \times(7)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{154}{9} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
The radius of the larger circle $=14 \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of the sector OAC (The sector angle is $40^{\circ}$ )
$=\frac{40^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi \times(14)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{1}{9} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 196 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{616}{9} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
The area of the shaded region
$=$ area of the sector OAC - area of the sector OBD
$=\left(\frac{616}{9}-\frac{154}{9}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=\frac{154}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
15. (i) Area of the quadrant OACB (radius $=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{~cm}$ )
$=\frac{1}{4} \times \pi \times r^{2}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{77}{8} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(ii) In right angled $\triangle \mathrm{OBD}, \mathrm{OB}=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{~cm}$,
$\mathrm{OD}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
The area of $\triangle \mathrm{OBD}=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OB} \times \mathrm{OD}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{7}{2} \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Then, area of the shaded region
$=$ Area of quadrant OACB - Area of $\triangle \mathrm{OBD}$
$=\frac{77}{8}-\frac{7}{2}=\frac{77-28}{8}=\frac{49}{8} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-367.jpg?height=387&width=385&top_left_y=1781&top_left_x=319)

The area of shaded region.
$=$ Area of square ABCD - Area of 4 quadrants
$=12 \times 12-4\left(\frac{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \theta}{360^{\circ}}\right)$
$=144-\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times 6 \times 6 \times 90}{360}$
$=144-3.14 \times 36=144-113.04$
$=30.96 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
17.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-367.jpg?height=346&width=348&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1146)

Area of the rectangle $\mathrm{ABCD}=6 \times 8=48$
Using Pythagoras theorem is $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$,
$(\mathrm{AC})^{2}=(\mathrm{AB})^{2}+(\mathrm{BC})^{2}$
$=8^{2}+6^{2}[\because \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{CD}$ and $\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{BC}]$
$=64+36=100$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AC}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
Since, AC is the diameter of the circle.
Hence, radius of the circle is 5 cm .
∴ Area of the circle $=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}=\pi(5)^{2}=25 \pi$.
Hence, the area of the shaded region is,
$=25 \pi-48=25 \times 3.14-48$
$=78.50-48=30.50 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
18. Area of circular play ground $=\pi r^{2}$
$22176=\frac{22}{7} \times r^{2}$
$r^{2}=\frac{22176 \times 7}{22}=7056$
$r=\sqrt{7056}=84 \mathrm{~cm}$
Length of fencing (circumference of circle)

$$
=2 \pi r=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 84=528 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

Cost of fencing $=528 \times 0.5=₹ 264$

## Long Answer Questions

1. (i) Area of the grassed enclosure
$=P Q \times P T+2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times r^{2}$

$$
\left[\because r=\frac{P T}{2}=\frac{70}{2}=35 \mathrm{~m}\right]
$$

$=200 \times 70+\frac{22}{7} \times 35 \times 35$
$=14000+3850$
$=17850 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$.
(ii) Outer perimeter of $A B C D E F$
$=A F E+E D+D C B+B A=2 A F E+2 E D$
$=2 \times \frac{1}{2}(2 \pi r)+2 \times 200($ where $r=35+7=42 \mathrm{~m})$
$=2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \times 22 \times 42}{7}+400=12 \times 22+400$
$=(264+400) \mathrm{m}=664 \mathrm{~m}$.
2. (a) Let $R=$ radius of circumcircle

$$
\begin{aligned}
h & =\text { height of triangle } \\
a & =\text { side of triangle }
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R & =\frac{2}{3} h \text { and } h=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a \\
\Rightarrow R & =\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} a \Rightarrow 32=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} a \Rightarrow a=32 \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Required area } & =\text { Area of circle }- \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC} \\
& =\pi \times 32^{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(32 \sqrt{3})^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) $\angle A O C=2 \angle A B C=2 \times 60^{\circ}=120^{\circ}$

$$
\text { Required area }=\frac{1}{3}(\text { area of circle }- \text { area of } \triangle A B C) .
$$

3. (i) We have,

Diameter of the semi-circular particle $=2.8 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \quad r=$ Radius of the semi-circular particle $=1.4 \mathrm{~cm}$
Path traced by the ant

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\pi r+2 r=\left(\frac{22}{7} \times 1.4+2 \times 1.4\right) \mathrm{cm} \\
& =(22 \times 0.2+2.8) \mathrm{cm}=(4.4+2.8) \mathrm{cm}=7.2 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Clearly,

Path traced by the ant $=A B+B C+C D+\operatorname{arc} A E D$
$=1.5 \mathrm{~cm}+2.8 \mathrm{~cm}+1.5 \mathrm{~cm}+(\pi \times 1.4) \mathrm{cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\left(1.5+2.8+1.5+\frac{22}{7} \times 1.4\right) \mathrm{cm} \\
& =(5.8+4.4) \mathrm{cm}=10.2 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) We have,

Path traced by the ant $=O A+\operatorname{arc} A C B+O B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(2+\pi \times 1.4+2) \mathrm{cm} \\
& =\left(4+\frac{22}{7} \times 1.4\right) \mathrm{cm}=(4+4.4) \mathrm{cm}=8.4 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Clearly, for (iii) food article ant will have to take a longer round.
4. We have,

Diameter of circular ends $=7 \mathrm{~m}$

$$
\therefore \quad r=\text { Radius of circular ends }=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{~m}=3.5 \mathrm{~m}
$$

Also,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{l}
l=\text { Length of the rectangle }=\{20-(3.5+3.5)\} \mathrm{m} \\
=(20-7) \mathrm{m}=13 \mathrm{~m}
\end{array} \\
& b=\text { Width of the rectangle }=7 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Perimeter of the garden $=2 l+\pi r+\pi r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =2 l+2 \pi r \\
& =\left\{2 \times 13+2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7\right\} \mathrm{m}=(26+44) \mathrm{m}=70 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Area of the garden
$=$ Area of rectangular part + Area of semi-circular ends

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =l \times b+\frac{1}{2}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)=l \times b+\pi r^{2} \\
& =\left\{13 \times 7+\frac{22}{7} \times 7^{2}\right\} \mathrm{m}^{2}=(91+154) \mathrm{m}^{2}=245 \mathrm{~m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ADE}=\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 6=24 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

Area of $\mathrm{ABCD}=10 \times 10=100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of semi-circle $=\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{(5)^{2}}{2}=\frac{25 \times 11}{7}$
Required area $=\left\{\frac{25 \times 11}{7}+[100-24]\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$

$$
=\left(\frac{275}{7}+76\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=115.3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

6. In $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}, \mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB}=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$ (radius)
and $\mathrm{AB}=20 \mathrm{~cm}$ (Given as the side of the rectangle)
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{AB}^{2}=\mathrm{OA}^{2}+\mathrm{OB}^{2}($ Each $=400)$
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOB}=90^{\circ}$
We take a point P on the $\operatorname{arc} \mathrm{AB}$ of the semi-circle.
Now, area of the segment APB
$=$ area of the sector OAPB - area of $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$
$=\left\{\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi \times(10 \sqrt{2})^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \sqrt{2} \times 10 \sqrt{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\left(\frac{1}{4} \times \pi \times 200-\frac{1}{2} \times 200\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right) \times 100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of the shaded region
$=$ Area of the rectangle $\mathrm{ABCD}-$ area of the segment APB
$=20 \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right) \times 100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\left\{400-\frac{\pi}{2} \times 100+100\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\left(5-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \times 100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
7. Since, radius of circle with centre O is OR .

Suppose $\mathrm{OR}=\mathrm{x}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{2}=(42)^{2}$
$\mathrm{x}=21 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}$
So, area of one flower bed
$=$ Area of segment of circle - Area of triangle POS
$=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \times \frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}}-\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OR} \times \mathrm{OR}$
$=\frac{22}{7} \times 21 \sqrt{2} \times 21 \sqrt{2} \times \frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}}-\frac{1}{2} \times 21 \sqrt{2} \times 21 \sqrt{2}$
$=693-441=252 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Hence, area of two flower beds $=2 \times 252=504 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
8. The radius of the circle is $r$ and $\angle A O P=\theta$

In $\triangle \mathrm{AOB}$,
$\sec \theta=\frac{\mathrm{OB}}{\mathrm{OA}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{r} \sec \theta$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{OB}-\mathrm{OP}=\mathrm{rsec} \theta-\mathrm{r}$
$\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{OA}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{r} \tan \theta$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-368.jpg?height=291&width=199&top_left_y=2247&top_left_x=1567)

Length of the arc $\mathrm{AP}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{r}$
$\therefore \quad$ Perimeter of the shaded region $=\mathrm{PB}+\mathrm{AB}+$
Length of the arc AP
$=r \sec \theta-r+r \tan \theta+\frac{\pi \theta}{180^{\circ}} r$
$=\mathrm{r}\left(\tan \theta+\sec \theta+\frac{\pi \theta}{180^{\circ}}-1\right)$
9. Area of shaded region
$=$ Area of rectangle - Area of semi-circle
$=(14 \times 21)-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7^{2}$
$=294-77$
$=217 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Perimeter of shaded region,
$=(2 \times 21)+14+\left(\frac{22}{7} \times 7\right)$
$=42+14+22=78 \mathrm{~cm}$
10. In $\triangle \mathrm{RPQ}, \angle \mathrm{P}=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{RQ}^{2}=\mathrm{PR}^{2}+\mathrm{PQ}^{2}=(7)^{2}+(24)^{2}=49+576$
$\mathrm{RQ}^{2}=625$
$\mathrm{RQ}=\sqrt{625}=25 \quad$ (Negative values is not possible)
Radius of circle $(r)=\frac{\mathrm{RQ}}{2}=\frac{25}{2}$
$\therefore \quad$ Area of the shaded region
$=$ Area of semicircle - Area $\triangle \mathrm{RPQ}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \pi r^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times$ base × height
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{25}{2} \times \frac{25}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times 24 \times 7$
$=245.54-84=161.54 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

## Note

Angle in semicircle is right angle.

## OR

$r_{1}=\frac{20}{2}=10 \mathrm{~m}$
$r_{2}=\frac{6}{2}=3 \mathrm{~m} \Rightarrow h=24 \mathrm{~m}$
$l=\sqrt{\left(r_{1}-r_{2}\right)^{2}+h^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{(10-3)^{2}+(24)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{49+576}=\sqrt{625}=25$
Curved surface area of frustum $=\pi\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) l$
$=\frac{22}{7} \times(10+3) \times 25$
$=\frac{22}{7} \times 13 \times 25=1021.43 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. It is given that d is the diameter of circle.

Since, diameter of circle is equal to side of the outer square.
$\therefore \quad$ Area of outer square $=d^{2}$
and diameter of circle is $\sqrt{2}$ times of side of circumscribed square.
$\therefore \quad d=\sqrt{2} s$, where $s$ is side of square
$\Rightarrow \quad s=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}$
Area of inner square $=\frac{d^{2}}{2}$

From eqs (i) and (ii), the area of inner square is 2 times of area of outer square.
Hence, the area of outer square not equal to 4 times of area of inner square.
2. We know that area of circle $=\pi r^{2}$

It is given that length and breadth of rectangle is $a \mathrm{~cm}$ and $b \mathrm{~cm}$, where $(a>b)$.
$\therefore \quad$ The largest circle that can be drawn will have $b$ as its diameter.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-369.jpg?height=177&width=378&top_left_y=1228&top_left_x=1283)
$\therefore \quad$ Radius of largest circle $=r=\frac{b}{2}$
Now, area of largest circle $=\pi \times\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi b^{2}}{4}$ sq. unit
Hence, the area of largest circle is not $\pi b^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
3. It is given that radius of circle is $a \mathrm{~cm}$.

Since, square circumscribes a circle, then the radius of circle is $\frac{1}{2}$ of length of square i.e., $a=\frac{1}{2}(s)$, where $s$ is side of square.
$\therefore \quad s=2 a \mathrm{~cm}$.
Perimeter of square $=4 \times s=4 \times 2 \mathrm{a}=8 \mathrm{acm}$
Hence, the given statement is true.
4. The given sides of triangle are
$48 \mathrm{~cm}, 14 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 50 cm .
Now,
$(14)^{2}+(48)^{2}=2304+196$
$=2500=50^{2}$
$\therefore(50)^{2}=(48)^{2}+(14)^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-369.jpg?height=260&width=257&top_left_y=1991&top_left_x=1617)

Hence, by Pythagoras theorem the given triangle is right-angle triangle.
In case of right-angle triangle, the hypotenuse is a diameter of circum-circle and its centre is exactly at midpoint of hypotenuse.
∴ $\quad$ Circumradius of triangle $=\frac{1}{2} \times 50=25 \mathrm{~cm}$
5.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-370.jpg?height=204&width=223&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=269)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-370.jpg?height=158&width=161&top_left_y=402&top_left_x=575)

Let $\mathrm{H}_{1}$ be PQRSTU and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ be ABCDEF .
and $r$ be the radius of the circle
$\therefore r=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(P U) \Rightarrow \mathrm{PU}=\frac{2 r}{\sqrt{3}}$
Since, a hexagon inscribed in a circle must have its side equal to radius of circle.
$\therefore A B=R$
Now, area of $\mathrm{H}_{1}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} r\right)^{2}$
and area of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}(r)^{2}$
$\left(\therefore \text { Area of hexagon }=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \times(\text { side })\right)^{2}$
Required ratio $=\frac{2 \sqrt{3} r^{2}}{\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} r^{2}}=\frac{4}{3}$.

## HOTS Questions

1. $\because \mathrm{BOC}$ is a diameter of the circle
$\therefore \angle \mathrm{BAC}=90^{\circ}$ (angle in a semi-circle is right angle)
In $\triangle \mathrm{BAC}$, by using Pythagoras theorem, we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{BC}^{2}=\mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{AC}^{2} \\
& \mathrm{BC}^{2}=(7)^{2}+(24)^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \mathrm{BC}=25 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

∴ Radius of circle with centre, $\mathrm{O}=\frac{25}{2}=12.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Now, area of shaded region
$=$ Area of circle - Area of quadrant COD - Area of $\triangle \mathrm{BAC}$
$=\pi r^{2}-\frac{\pi r^{2}}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \times A B \times A C$
$=\frac{3}{4} \times 3.14 \times(12.5)^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times 7 \times 24=283.97 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Hence, area of shaded region is $283.97 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
2. Given, radius of circular park, $r_{1}=10 \mathrm{~m}$ and radius of circular park $r_{2}=11 \mathrm{~m}$
(i) Now, perimeter of circular line $=2 \pi r$
$=2 \times 3.14 \times 11=69.08 \mathrm{~m}$
As the person takes four round, then total distance covered by him
$=4 \times$ Perimeter of one circular line
$=4 \times 69.08=276.32 \mathrm{~m}$
(ii) Since, the two persons are standing on the opposite sides of a circular line the maximum distance between $A$ and $B$ will be equal to the diameter of the circular park i.e., $2 r=2 \times 11=22 \mathrm{~m}$
3. Each chord of the circle with radius $a$, subtend an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the centre and make an equilateral triangle with centre of circle as vertex. So, each side $=a$.

Since, on each chord an equilateral triangle is made, there are total 12 such triangles.
Area of one equilateral triangle $=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}$.
Total area $=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2} \times 12=3 \sqrt{3} a^{2}$.
4. Area of outer circle $=\pi \cdot O B^{2}$
and area of inner circle $=\pi \times O D^{2}$
As given, $\pi \times O B^{2}=4 \times \pi O D^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{O D^{2}}{O B^{2}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{O D}{O B}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\sin \angle O B D=\frac{O D}{O B}=\frac{1}{2} \therefore \angle O B D=30^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-370.jpg?height=243&width=269&top_left_y=959&top_left_x=1283)
$\Rightarrow \angle A B C=60^{\circ}$.
[Since, $\angle A B C=2 \angle O B D$ ]
Hence, $\triangle A B C$ is equilateral.
The next step is to find side $A B$ and $O D$.
Since, $\pi r^{2}=12$, we get, $r=O B=\sqrt{\frac{12}{\pi}}$ and $O D=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{12}{\pi}}$
Using Pythagoras theorem, we get,
$D B^{2}=O B^{2}-O D^{2}=\frac{12}{\pi}-\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{12}{\pi}=\frac{9}{\pi} \Rightarrow D B=\frac{}{\sqrt{ }}$
Hence, side $A B=2 \times \frac{3}{\sqrt{\pi}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{\pi}}$
Area of $\triangle A B C=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times$ side $^{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times \frac{36}{\pi}=\frac{9 \sqrt{3}}{\pi}$.
5. Let $A B C$ be an equilateral triangle of side 18 cm
$\Rightarrow A B=B C=C A=18 \mathrm{~cm}$
Draw $A D \perp B C$
$\triangle A D B \cong \triangle A D C$ [By R.H.S. criterion of congruence]
$\Rightarrow B D=C D$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-370.jpg?height=445&width=444&top_left_y=1982&top_left_x=1194)
$\Rightarrow B D=C D=\frac{1}{2} B C=\frac{1}{2} \times 18 \mathrm{~cm}=9 \mathrm{~cm}$.

Since, the centre of the inscribed circle coincides with the centroid of the equilateral triangle.
∴ radius of incircle $O D=\frac{1}{3} A D \quad[\because A O: O D=2: 1]$
Now, in right $\triangle A B D$
$A B^{2}=A D^{2}+B D^{2}$
[By Pythagoras theorem]
$\Rightarrow(18)^{2}=A D^{2}+9^{2}$
$\Rightarrow A D^{2}=(18)^{2}-9^{2}=324-81=243$
$\Rightarrow A D= \pm \sqrt{81 \times 3}=9 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\Rightarrow O D=\frac{1}{3} \times 9 \sqrt{3}=3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of incircle $=\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times(3 \sqrt{3}) \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$\left[\because r=O D=\frac{1}{3} A D=\frac{1}{3} \times 9 \sqrt{3}=3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}\right]$
$=\frac{22}{7} \times 27 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=84.86 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of equilateral $\triangle A B C$
$=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(\text { side })^{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times(18)^{2}=\sqrt{3} \times 81 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=1.732 \times 81=140.29 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
Hence, area of the remaining portion of the triangle $=(140.29-84.86) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=55.43 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.

## Case Study Based MCQs

1. (i) (a) Area of field graze $=\frac{1}{4} \times \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{4} \times 3.14 \times 5^{2}=19.625 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(ii) (b) Area of field graze $=\frac{1}{4} \times \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{4} \times 3.14 \times 10^{2}=78.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(iii) (a) Increase in the grazing area
$=78.5-19.625=58.875 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(iv) (c) Area of square field $=15 \times 15=225 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

Area of field not graze $=225-19.625$
$=205.37 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(v) (a) Area of field not graze
$=225-78.5=146.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

## Exercise 2

Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text-book Questions

1. Radius of the Gold scoring region

$$
=\frac{21}{2} \mathrm{~cm}=10.5 \mathrm{~cm} \quad(\because \text { diameter }=21 \mathrm{~cm})
$$

Therefore, the area of the Gold scoring region (circle)
$=\pi \times\left(\frac{21}{2}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{693}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=346.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Radius of the combined circular region scoring Gold and Red
$=$ radius of Gold scoring region +
width of the Red scoring band
$=10.5 \mathrm{~cm}+10.5 \mathrm{~cm}=21 \mathrm{~cm}$
The area of the Red scoring region
= Combined area of Gold and Red scoring region - area of Gold scoring region
$=\left\{\pi \times(21)^{2}-\pi \times\left(\frac{21}{2}\right)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\pi \times\left\{(21)^{2}-\left(\frac{21}{2}\right)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{21}{2} \times \frac{21}{2} \times 3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{693}{2} \times 3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=1039.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Radius of the combined circular region scoring Gold, Red and Blue
$=10.5 \mathrm{~cm}+10.5 \mathrm{~cm}+10.5 \mathrm{~cm}=31.5 \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{63}{2} \mathrm{~cm}$
Then, area of the Blue scoring region
$=\{$ Combined area of Gold, Red and Blue scoring regions $\}-$
\{Combined area of Gold and Red scoring regions\}
$=\pi \times\left(\frac{63}{2}\right)^{2}-\pi \times(21)^{2}$
$=\pi \times(21)^{2} \times\left\{\frac{9}{4}-1\right\}=1732.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Similarly, we find the area of the Black scoring region
$=\pi \times(31.5+10.5)^{2}-\pi \times(31.5)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{22}{7} \times 21 \times 21 \times \frac{7}{4} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{4851}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=2425.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Now, the area of the White scoring region
$=\left\{\pi \times(42+10.5)^{2}-\pi \times(42)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\left\{\pi \times\left(\frac{105}{2}\right)^{2}-\pi r^{2} \times(42)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{6237}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=3118.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
2. Correct option is (a)

Justification : Let the radius of the circle be $r$ units.
When $2 \pi r=\pi r^{2}, \quad$ (numerically)
We have, $2 r=r^{2}$, i.e. $r=2$
3. Here, the radius of the circle is $r=10 \mathrm{~cm}$

Sector angle of the minor sector made corresponding to the chord AB is $90^{\circ}$.
Now, the area of the minor sector $=\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-372.jpg?height=351&width=371&top_left_y=577&top_left_x=408)
$=\frac{1}{4} \times \pi \times(10)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=78.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Then, the area of the minor segment
$=$ area of the minor sector - area of the $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$
$=78.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OA} \times \mathrm{OB}\left(\because \angle \mathrm{AOB}=90^{\circ}\right)$
$=78.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=28.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
The area of the major sector
$=\left(\frac{360^{\circ}-90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}}\right) \times \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}=\frac{270^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times 3.14 \times(10)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{3}{4} \times 314 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=235.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
4. Here, $r=12 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\theta=120$ degrees.
$\pi=3.14, \sqrt{3}=1.73$
The area of the sector $\mathrm{OAB}=\frac{120^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-372.jpg?height=304&width=337&top_left_y=1704&top_left_x=424)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{3} \times 3.14 \times(12)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=150.72 \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \\
& \mathrm{AB}=2 \mathrm{AM}=2 \times \mathrm{OA} \times \frac{\mathrm{AM}}{\mathrm{OA}} \\
& =2 \times 12 \times \sin 60^{\circ}=12 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm} \\
& \mathrm{OM}=\mathrm{OA} \times \frac{\mathrm{OM}}{\mathrm{OA}} \\
& \quad=12 \cos 60^{\circ}=12 \times \frac{1}{2}=6 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-372.jpg?height=238&width=412&top_left_y=379&top_left_x=1210)
area of the $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{AB} \times \mathrm{OM}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \sqrt{3} \times 6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=36 \times 1.73 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=62.28 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
The area of the segment (shaded) made by the chord AB $=$ area of the sector $\mathrm{OAB}-$ area of $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$
$=(150.72-62.28) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=88.44 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
5. Area of the square $=8^{2}=64 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\left(\mathrm{A}_{1}\right)$

Area of quadrant CDB $=\frac{1}{4} \pi(8)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\left(\mathrm{A}_{2}\right)$
Area of quadrant $\mathrm{ADB}=\frac{1}{4} \pi(8)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\left(\mathrm{A}_{3}\right)$
∴ Area of shaded portion $=\mathrm{A}_{2}+\mathrm{A}_{3}-\mathrm{A}_{1}$
$=\frac{1}{4} \pi(8)^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \pi(8)^{2}-64$
$=\frac{1}{2} \pi \times 64-64=32 \pi-64=36.48 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
6. (i) Diameter of the circle $=35 \mathrm{~mm}$

Then, radius $r=\frac{35}{2} \mathrm{~mm}$
The total length of wire required $=$ The length of wire required for making 5 diameters

+ The length of wire required for making the perimeter of the circle
$=5 \times 35 \mathrm{~mm}+2 \pi \times \frac{35}{2} \mathrm{~mm}$
$=\left(175+\frac{22}{7} \times 35\right) \mathrm{mm}=285 \mathrm{~mm}$
(ii) Here, $r=\frac{35}{2} \mathrm{~mm}$

The sector angle of each brooch $=\frac{360}{10}$ degrees, i.e. $\theta=36$ degrees
Therefore, the area of each brooch
$=\frac{36^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{11 \times 35}{4} \mathrm{~mm}^{2}=\frac{385}{4} \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$
7. Here, $r=45 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\theta=$ sector angle between two consecutive ribs.
$=\frac{360^{\circ}}{8^{\circ}}(\because$ There are 8 sectors of same size $)$
$=45^{\circ}$

Therefore, the area between two consecutive ribs of the umbrella
$=$ The area of one sector $=\frac{45^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi r^{2}$
$=\frac{11}{28} \times 45 \times 45 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{22275}{28} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

i.e., $r=25 \mathrm{~cm}$
The area covered by one blade
$=\frac{115^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi \times(25)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Then, the area covered by two blades
$=2 \times \frac{115^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 625 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{158125}{126} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

$\theta=80^{\circ}$
The area of the sea over which the ships are warned

The total area of six designed portions
$=6 \times\left\{\frac{1232}{3}-333.2\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=(2464-1999.2) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=464.8 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
The total cost of making the designs at the rate of $₹ 0.35$ per $\mathrm{cm}^{2}=₹ 0.35 \times 464.8=₹ 162.68$
11. Here, O is the centre of the circular table cover and radius $=32 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is equilateral. Join $\mathrm{OA}, \mathrm{OB}, \mathrm{OC}$.
Now, $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\angle \mathrm{BOC}=\angle \mathrm{COA}=120^{\circ}$
In $\triangle \mathrm{OBC}$, we have $\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{OC}$
Draw $\mathrm{OM} \perp \mathrm{BC}$.
$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{BOM}=\angle \mathrm{COM}=60^{\circ}$
( $\therefore \triangle \mathrm{OMB} \cong \triangle \mathrm{OMC}$ by RHS congruence)
Now, $\frac{\mathrm{BM}}{\mathrm{OB}}=\sin 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{BM}}{32}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{BM}=16 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
Then, $\mathrm{BC}=2 \times \mathrm{BM}=32 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.
Thus, the side of the equilateral triangle ABC
$=32 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.

$=$ area of the circle - area of the $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
$=\left\{\pi \times(32)^{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times(32 \sqrt{3})^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\left\{\frac{22528}{7}-768 \sqrt{3}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
12. Here, $r=12 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\theta=120$ degrees, $\pi=3.14, \sqrt{3}=1.73$

$$
\left[\because \text { area of sector }=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}\right]

$=\frac{1}{3} \times 3.14 \times(12)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=150.72 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

$\mathrm{AB}=2 \mathrm{AM}=2 \times \mathrm{OA} \times \frac{\mathrm{AM}}{\mathrm{OA}}$

\left(\because \sin (\angle \mathrm{AOM})=\sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{AM}}{\mathrm{OA}}\right)

$\mathrm{OM}=\mathrm{OA} \times \frac{\mathrm{OM}}{\mathrm{OA}}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-374.jpg?height=428&width=414&top_left_y=1347&top_left_x=388)
$=\pi \times(7)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times(7)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=154 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

$=\frac{1}{2} \times 14 \times 7 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=49 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

$=\pi \times\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{77}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

The sum of the areas of the shaded region II and III
$=$ The area of the semicircle $A C B-$ The area of the $\triangle A B C$
$=\frac{1}{2}(154) \mathrm{cm}^{2}-49 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=28 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

The total area of the shaded regions I, II and III
$=\left(\frac{77}{2}+28\right) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=66.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.
14. Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ (equilateral) $=17320.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ Let side of an equilateral $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ be $x \mathrm{~cm}$.
Then, $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times x^{2}=17320.5$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1.73205}{4} \times x^{2}=17320.5(\because \sqrt{3}=1.73205)$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}=40000 \Rightarrow x=200 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-374.jpg?height=364&width=394&top_left_y=822&top_left_x=1222)

Then radius of each circle $=100 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Area of the sector APR $=\frac{60^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi \times(100)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10000 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Similarly, area of the sector BPQ
$=$ Area of the sector $\mathrm{CQR}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10000 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Total area of regions I, II and III (.e.e, non-shaded region of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ )
$=3 \times \frac{\pi}{6} \times 10000 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3.14 \times 10000 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=15700 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Then, the required area of the shaded region of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
$=17320.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}-15700 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=1620.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
15. Radius of the circle $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$

Then, diameter of the circle $=14 \mathrm{~cm}$
Then, length of the side of the square ABCD
$=3 \times 14 \mathrm{~cm}=42 \mathrm{~cm}$
So, the total area of the handkerchief
$=(42)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=1764 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of the nine circle designs $=9 \times \pi r^{2}$
$=9 \times \frac{22}{7} \times(7)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=9 \times 22 \times 7 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=1386 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Then, the required area of the remaining portion (other than designed portion)
$=1764 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}-1386 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=378 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
16. $\mathrm{OB}=\sqrt{\mathrm{OA}^{2}+\mathrm{AB}^{2}}=\sqrt{(20)^{2}+(20)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{800}=20 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$

Then, for the quadrant $\mathrm{OPBQ}, r=20 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$ area of the shaded region $=$ area of the quadrant OPBQ - area of the square OABC
$=\left\{\frac{1}{4} \pi \times(20 \sqrt{2})^{2}-(20)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=\left\{\frac{1}{4} \times 3.14 \times 800-400\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
$=(628-400) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=228 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
17. Area of the shaded region
$=$ area of the sector OAB - area of the sector OCD
$=\left\{\frac{30^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi(21)^{2}-\frac{30^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi(7)^{2}\right\} \mathrm{cm}^{2}=\frac{308}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
18. Required area $=$ area of semicircle with diameter BC

- area of segment with chord BC

Now, $\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BA}=14 \mathrm{~cm}(\because \mathrm{AB} \& \mathrm{AC}$ are radii)
$\therefore \mathrm{BC}=\sqrt{14^{2}+14^{2}}=14 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Area of semicircle on BC
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(7 \sqrt{2})^{2}=154 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
Area of segment on chord BC
$=$ area of quadrant $\mathrm{BAC}-$ area of $\triangle \mathrm{BAC}$
$=\frac{1}{4} \times \pi \times 14^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \times 14 \times 14$
$=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14^{2}-98=154-98=56 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
∴ Required area $=154-56=98 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.

10 cm

$$

So, required increase in area

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\left[\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \pi \times 11.5^{2}-\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \pi \times 6^{2}\right] \mathrm{m}^{2} \\
& =\frac{\pi}{4} \times(11.5+6)(11.5-6) \mathrm{m}^{2} \\
& =\frac{22}{7 \times 4} \times 17.5 \times 5.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}=75.625 \mathrm{~m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, length of the $\operatorname{arc}=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi r$
$=\frac{15}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi \times 36 \mathrm{~cm}=3 \pi \mathrm{~cm}$

& =94.2+62.352=156.552 \mathrm{~cm}^{2} .

$$

$$

=\pi(25-9)=16 \pi

## Multiple Matching Questions

1. $\quad(\mathrm{A}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{q}) ;(\mathrm{B}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{s}) ;(\mathrm{C}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{s}) ;(\mathrm{D}) \rightarrow(\mathrm{q})$
(A) Area of the shaded region $=\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \pi\left[(26)^{2}-(23)^{2}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{7}\left[(26)^{2}-(23)^{2}\right]=115.5 \approx 115 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(B) $A B=2 \times A D=2 \times 6.5=13 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{BC}=\sqrt{(A B)^{2}-(A C)^{2}}=\sqrt{(13)^{2}-(5)^{2}}=12 \mathrm{~cm}$
Area $(\triangle A B C)=\frac{1}{2} \times A C \times B C=\frac{1}{2}(5)(12)=30 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
$=0.003 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
(C) $(A C)^{2}=(A B)^{2}+(B C)^{2}=64+36=100$
$A C=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
Area of the shaded region
$=($ area of the circle $)-($ area of the rectangle $A B C D)$
$=\left[\frac{22}{7} \times\left(\frac{10}{2}\right)^{2}\right]-(8 \times 6)$
$=(78.57-48)=30.57 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
(D) Area of the shaded region $=\frac{60^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \times \frac{22}{7} \times(14.8)^{2} =114.7 \approx 115 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

## Integer Type Questions

1. Ans : 9
$\frac{2 \pi r_{1}}{2 \pi r_{2}}=\frac{4}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{4}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}=\frac{16}{81}$
$\frac{\pi r_{1}^{2}}{\pi r_{2}^{2}}=\frac{16}{k} \Rightarrow \frac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}=\frac{16}{k}$

On comparing (i) and (ii), we get $k=81$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{k}=9$
2. Ans: 4

Circumference = area

$$
\begin{array}{rlrl}
2 \pi \mathrm{r} & =\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad r & r=2
\end{array}
$$

∴ $\quad$ Diameter $=2 r=2 \times 2=4$
3. Ans : 5
$\frac{\pi r_{1}^{2}}{\pi r_{2}^{2}}=\frac{16}{25} \Rightarrow \frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{4}{5}$

Also, $\frac{2 \pi r_{1}}{2 \pi r_{2}}=\frac{4}{k} \Rightarrow \frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{4}{k}$

On comparing (i) and (ii), we get $k=5$.
4. Ans : 2
$\mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{C}=2 \pi \mathrm{r}$
$\Rightarrow C=2\left(\frac{A}{r}\right) \Rightarrow r \mathrm{C}=2 \mathrm{~A}$
$\Rightarrow \quad k=2$
5. Ans: $\mathbf{4}$
$2 \pi r=\frac{1}{2}\left(\pi r^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 4 \pi \mathrm{r}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=4$
6. Ans : 7

Perimeter of sector of circle
$=\frac{\theta}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \pi r+2 r$
$\Rightarrow \quad 19.5=\frac{45}{360^{\circ}} \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7} r+2 r$
$\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{19.5 \times 14}{39}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$

## 13. Surface Areas and Volumes

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=378&width=449&top_left_y=388&top_left_x=1368)

## CCONCEPT MAP

## Surface Areas and Volumes of Solids <br> \section*{Volumes of Solids}

(i) Cuboid :

Volume $=l \times b \times h$
Total surface area
$=2[l b+b h+h l]$
Lateral surface area
$=2[b h+h l]$
Diagonal of a coboid $=\sqrt{l^{2}+b^{2}+h^{2}}$
(ii) Cube :

Volume $=a^{3}$
Total surface area $=6 a^{2}$
Lateral surface area $=4 a^{2}$
Diagonal of a cube $=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}$
(iii) Cylinder and cone:
(a) Right circular cylinder

Volume $=\pi r^{2} h$
C.S.A. $=2 \pi r h$
T.S.A. $=2 \pi r h+2 \pi r^{2}=2 \pi r(r+h)$
(b) Right circular hollow
cylinder: Let $r$ and $R$ be internal and external radii

Volume $=\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) h$
C.S.A. $=2 \pi(R+r) h$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { T.S.A. } & =2 \pi(R+r) h+2 \pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) \\
& =2 \pi(R+r)(h+R-r)
\end{aligned}
$$

(c) Right circular cone

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Volume }=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h \\
& \text { C.S.A. }=\pi r l \\
& \text { T.S.A. }=\pi r l+\pi r^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=230&width=120&top_left_y=1908&top_left_x=467)

Slant height, $l=\sqrt{r^{2}+h^{2}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=146&width=143&top_left_y=1906&top_left_x=747)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=111&width=150&top_left_y=1910&top_left_x=937)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=131&width=184&top_left_y=1906&top_left_x=1139)

## (2)

## \section*{Surface Areas and Volumes of <br> <br> Surface Areas and Volumes of Sphere and Hemisphere Sphere and Hemisphere}

(i) Sphere :

Sphere :
Volume $=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$
Volume $=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$
Surface area $=4 \pi r^{2}$
Surface area $=4 \pi r^{2}$
(ii) Hemisphere :

Volume $=\frac{2}{3} \pi r^{3}$
C.S.A. $=2 \pi r^{2}$
T.S.A. $=3 \pi r^{2}$
(iii) Hemispherical shell :

Volume $=\frac{2}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)$
C.S.A. $=2 \pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)$

TSA $=2 \pi\left(r^{2}+R^{2}\right)+\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)$
$=\pi\left(r^{2}+3 R^{2}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=130&width=131&top_left_y=1344&top_left_x=525)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=129&width=85&top_left_y=1345&top_left_x=701)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=130&width=95&top_left_y=1344&top_left_x=907)

## Surface Areas of a Combination of Solids

The surface area of a solid which is a combination of other solids, is calculated by adding the curved surface areas of the individual solids.
(4)

## Conversion of Solid Form One Shape to Another

(i) When a solid is converted from one shape to other, then its volume remains same only its shape and size changes.
(ii) If a solid is converted into a number of small identical solids, then

Number of small items
$=\frac{\text { Volume of larger object }}{\text { Volume of smaller object }}$ where $l=\sqrt{h^{2}+\left(r_{1}-r_{2}\right)^{2}}$

## Frustum of a Cone

(i) Volume of the frustum of cone $=\frac{1}{3} \pi h\left(r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+r_{1} r_{2}\right)$
(ii) C.S.A of the frustum of cone $=\pi\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) l$
(iii) T.S.A of the frustum of cone $=\pi l\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right)+\pi r_{1}^{2}+\pi r_{2}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=182&width=169&top_left_y=890&top_left_x=1260)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-380.jpg?height=784&width=328&top_left_y=887&top_left_x=1497)

## BASIC CONCEPTS

- Solid : A figure which occupies a portion of space by plane or curved surface is called a solid.

A solid has three dimensions - length, breadth and height.

- Volume of a solid : The measurement of the space enclosed by a solid is called its volume.

It is also known as capacity. It is measured in cubic units.

- Surface area of a solid : The sum of the areas of the plane and curved surfaces (faces) of a solid is called its (total) surface area.
It is measured in square units.
Total surface area of a solid $=$ curved (lateral) surface area + plane surface area


## PRISM

A prism is a solid object with

- identical ends
- flat sides and
- the same cross-section all along its length, where cross-section is the shape made by cutting straight across an object. In other words, we can say 'prism' is a solid geometric figure whose two ends are similar, equal and parallel rectilinear figures and whose sides are parallelogram.
In geometry, there are different types of prisms - Triangular prisms, Rectangular prisms, Square prisms, etc.
For eg : Cubes and cuboids are prisms but cylinder is not a prism.
BASES : The top and bottom faces of prism are called bases.
Some important types of prisms and their cross-sections are shown below :

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-381.jpg?height=293&width=273&top_left_y=1281&top_left_x=287)

## Lateral surface area (LSA) of a prism :

L.S.A $=($ Perimeter of a base $) \times$ height $=\mathrm{Ph}$

## Total surface area (TSA) of a prism :

T.S.A = L.S.A + 2 (area of a base)

## Volume of a prism :

Volume $=$ Area of a base × height $=$ Ah .

## ILLUSTRATION : 1

Find the lateral surface area of a triangular prism with bases edges $3 \mathrm{~cm}, 4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 5 cm and altitude 8 cm .

## SOLUTION :

Perimeter of base $=3+4+5=12$
Lateral surface area $=$ Perimeter × height

$$
=12 \times 8=96 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-382.jpg?height=295&width=321&top_left_y=411&top_left_x=1228)

## ILLUSTRATION : 2

Find the volume of a triangular prism with base is 10 cm , height is 11 cm and length is 16 cm .

## SOLUTION :

Volume $=$ Area of base × height

$$
=\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 11 \times 16=80 \times 11=880 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-382.jpg?height=174&width=504&top_left_y=797&top_left_x=1228)

## SURFACE AREAS AND VOLUMES OF SOLIDS

(i) Cuboid

If ' $l$ ' be the length, ' $b$ ' be the breadth and ' $h$ ' be the height (or depth) of a cuboid, then
Volume $=$ length × breadth × height $=l \times b \times h$
Total surface area $=2(l b+b h+h l)$
Lateral surface area $=2(b h+h l)$
Diagonal of a cuboid $=\sqrt{l^{2}+b^{2}+h^{2}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-382.jpg?height=252&width=430&top_left_y=1073&top_left_x=1384)

Area of four walls of a room $=2(l+b) h$
(ii) Cube

In a cube, all the three dimensions i.e., its length, breadth and height are equal.
If ' $a$ ' be the edge of a cube, then
Volume of cube $=a^{3}$
Total surface area $=6 a^{2}$
Lateral surface area $=4 a^{2}$
Diagonal of a cube $=\sqrt{3} \times$ edge $=\sqrt{3} a$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-382.jpg?height=332&width=307&top_left_y=1345&top_left_x=1457)
(iii) Cylinder
(a) Right circular cylinder : For a right circular cylinder of base radius $r$ and height $h$, we have

- Area of each end $=$ Area of base $=\pi r^{2}$
- Area of curved surface $=2 \pi r h$
- Total surface area $=2 \pi r(h+r)$
- Volume of the cylinder $=\pi r^{2} h$
(b) Right circular hollow cylinder : A cylinder from which a smaller cylinder of the same
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-382.jpg?height=374&width=179&top_left_y=1726&top_left_x=1633)
height and of the same axis is cut out is called a hollow cylinder.
A line passing through the top and bottom faces (or bases) is called the axis of the cylinder.
If ' $r$ ' \& ' $R$ ' be the internal \& external radii respectively of a hollow cylinder of height ' $h$ ', then
- Volume of the hollow cylinder $=\left(\pi R^{2}-\pi r^{2}\right) h=\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) h$
- Area of the curved surface $=2 \pi R h+2 \pi r h=2 \pi(R+r) h$
- Total surface area $=$ area of curved surface +2 (area of a base)

$$
=2 \pi(R+r) h+2 \pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)=2 \pi(R+r) h+2 \pi(R+r)(R-r)=2 \pi(R+r)(h+R-r)
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-382.jpg?height=359&width=214&top_left_y=2115&top_left_x=1603)
(c) Right circular cone : It is a solid generated by the revolution of a right angled triangle about one of its sides containing the right angle as axis.
For a right circular cone of height ' $h$ ', slant height $\ell$ and radius of base ' $r$ ' we have

- $\quad \ell^{2}=h^{2}+r^{2}$
- Area of base $=\pi r^{2}$
- Curved surface area $=\pi r \ell$
- Total surface area $=$ Curved surface area + Area of the base $=\pi r \ell+\pi r^{2}$
- Volume of cone $=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-383.jpg?height=369&width=371&top_left_y=424&top_left_x=1500)

Time to
Check Your
Knowledge
A cone made of paper has height $3 h$ and vertical angle $2 \alpha$. It contains two other cones of height $2 h$ and $h$ and vertical angles $4 \alpha$ and $6 \alpha$ respectively. Find the ratio of the two volumes in between the cones.

## SOLUTION

Let $L, M$ and $N$ be the volumes of cones VAB, $V_{1} A_{1} B_{1}$ and $V_{2} A_{2} B_{2}$ respectively.
For cone VAB , we have $\mathrm{VO}=3 h$
In right $\triangle \mathrm{AOV}, \frac{\mathrm{OV}}{\mathrm{OA}}=\tan \alpha$
$\Rightarrow \frac{3 h}{\mathrm{OA}}=\tan \alpha \Rightarrow \mathrm{OA}=3 h \tan \alpha$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{3} \pi(3 h \tan \alpha)^{2} \times 3 h=\frac{27 \neq}{3} h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha$
For cone $\mathrm{V}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{1} \mathrm{~B}_{1}$, we have, $\mathrm{V}_{1} \mathrm{O}=2 h$
In right $\Delta \mathrm{V}_{1} \mathrm{OA}_{1}, \frac{\mathrm{OV}_{1}}{\mathrm{OA}_{1}}=\tan 2 \alpha \Rightarrow \mathrm{OA}_{1}=2 h \tan 2 \alpha$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{M}=\frac{1}{3} \pi(2 h \tan 2 \alpha)^{2} \times 2 h=\frac{8}{3} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha$
For cone $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{2}$, we have, $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}=h$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-383.jpg?height=553&width=522&top_left_y=1190&top_left_x=1354)

In right $\Delta \mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{OA}_{2}, \frac{\mathrm{OV}_{2}}{\mathrm{OA}_{2}}=\tan 3 \alpha \Rightarrow \frac{h}{\mathrm{OA}_{2}}=\tan 3 \alpha$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{OA}_{2}=h \tan 3 \alpha$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{N}=\frac{1}{3} \pi(h \tan 3 \alpha)^{2} \times h=\frac{1}{3} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} 3 \alpha$
Now, $\quad \mathrm{L}-\mathrm{M}=\frac{27 \neq}{3} h^{3} \tan ^{2} \alpha-\frac{8 \neq}{3} h^{3} \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha=\frac{\neq h^{3}}{3}\left(27 \tan ^{2} \alpha-8 \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha\right)$
and $\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{N}=\frac{8}{3} \pi h^{3} \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha-\frac{\neq}{3} h^{3} \tan ^{2} 3 \alpha=\frac{\neq h^{3}}{3}\left(8 \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha-\tan ^{2} 3 \alpha\right)$
$\therefore \quad$ Required ratio $=(\mathrm{L}-\mathrm{M}):(\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{N})=\left(27 \tan ^{2} \alpha-8 \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha\right):\left(8 \tan ^{2} 2 \alpha-\tan ^{2} 3 \alpha\right)$.

## SPHERE

Sphere is a set of points in the space which are equidistant from a fixed point. The fixed point is called the centre of the sphere and the distance is called the radius of the sphere.
A football, a cricket ball, the earth are some examples of sphere.

## \section*{Did You <br> <br> Know! <br> <br> $\stackrel{\text { Did You }}{\text { T }}$ ?}

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-384.jpg?height=171&width=101&top_left_y=609&top_left_x=420)

The word 'sphere' is simply an English form of the Greek word 'Sphaira' means 'a ball'.
(a) Sphere

For a sphere of radius ' $r$ ', we have.

- Surface area $=4 \pi r^{2}$
- Volume $=\frac{4}{3} \neq r^{3}$
(b) Hemisphere
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-384.jpg?height=241&width=243&top_left_y=776&top_left_x=1571)

If a solid sphere is cut into two halves by a plane passing through the centre of sphere, then each of the two halves is called a hemisphere.

For a hemisphere of radius $r$, we have

- Curved surface area $=2 \pi r^{2}$
- Total surface area $=3 \pi r^{2}$
- Volume $=\frac{2}{3} \neq r^{3}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-384.jpg?height=282&width=424&top_left_y=1087&top_left_x=1388)


## (c) Hollow Sphere/Spherical Shell

From a sphere, a smaller sphere having the same centre of the sphere, is cut off, then hollow sphere is obtained.

- External surface area $=4 \pi R^{2}$
- Internal surface area $=4 \pi r^{2}$
- Volume $=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-384.jpg?height=250&width=250&top_left_y=1498&top_left_x=1562)


## (d) Hemispherical Shell

If a spherical shell is cut into two halves by a plane passing through the centre of spherical shell, then each of the two halves is called a hemispherical shell.

- Internal curved surface area $=2 \pi r^{2}$ sq. units
- External curved surface area $=2 \pi R^{2}$ sq. units
- Total surface area $=$ Internal surface area + Ext. surface area + Area of ring

$$
=2 \pi r^{2}+2 \pi R^{2}+\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)=\pi r^{2}+3 \pi R^{2}=\pi\left(r^{2}+3 R^{2}\right) \text { units }
$$

- Volume of the material used to form hemispherical shell
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-384.jpg?height=344&width=552&top_left_y=1854&top_left_x=1260)

$$
=\frac{2}{3} \pi\left(R^{3}-r^{3}\right) \text { cubic units }
$$

## Learn

## তি⿴囗大

## More

Let us consider a cone exactly fitted in a cylinder．
Volume of the cylinder $=\pi \mathrm{r} 2 \mathrm{~h}=\pi \mathrm{r} 2 \times 2 \mathrm{r}=2 \pi \mathrm{r} 3$
Volume of a cone $=\frac{1}{3} \pi \mathrm{r} 2 \mathrm{~h}=\frac{2}{3} \pi \mathrm{r} 3$
In this case，volume of cone $=\frac{1}{3}$（Volume of cylinder）
Again，consider a sphere exactly fitted in a cylinder
Volume of a sphere $=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r} 3=\frac{2}{3}$（Volume of cylinder）
In this case，volume of a sphere $=\frac{2}{3}$（Volume of cylinder）
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-385.jpg?height=259&width=266&top_left_y=470&top_left_x=1594)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-385.jpg?height=282&width=266&top_left_y=744&top_left_x=1592)

So，volume of a cone and a sphere together makes a cylinder，if radius and height of solids are same，having height is 2 r ．
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-385.jpg?height=273&width=828&top_left_y=1087&top_left_x=648)

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## PYRAMID ：

A＇Pyramid＇is a solid（three dimensional）object made of a polygonal base（bottom face）and triangular faces that meet at a top common point．This top common point is called the vertex of the cone．

Faces ：All faces of the pyramid are triangular．
In the figure，the base $A B C D$ is a square．All the conrners of the base are joined to the vertex $O$ in space，by straight lines．

The resultant solid so obtained is called a pyramid．
Types of Pyramid ：Some important types of pyramid are shown below：
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-385.jpg?height=469&width=252&top_left_y=1562&top_left_x=1619)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-385.jpg?height=531&width=1573&top_left_y=2017&top_left_x=287)

## In each pyramid,

(i) The length of the line segment joining the vertex to the centre of the base is called the height of the pyramid and is represented by ' $h$ '.
(ii) The distance between the vertex and the mid point of any of the sides of the base of a pyramid is called its slant height and is represented by ' $l$ '.
(iii) For a pyramid with perimeter of base $=p$

Lateral surface area $=\frac{1}{2}($ Perimeter of base $) \times($ Slant height $)=\frac{1}{2} p l$
Total surface area $=$ Lateral surface area + Area of base
Volume of a pyramid $=\frac{1}{3} \times$ Area of base × Height.

## SURFACE AREA OF A COMBINATION OF SOLIDS

Whenever we have to find the surface area of a solid which is a combination of other solids, then the surface area of the combination of solids is calculated by adding the surface area of individual solid.

## ILLUSTRATION : 3

Three cubes each of side 5 cm are joined end to end. Find the surface area of the resulting cuboid.

## SOLUTION :

The dimensions of the cuboid so formed are as under :
$l=$ Length $=5+5+5=15 \mathrm{~cm}, b=$ Breadth $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$,
and $h=$ Height $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Surface area of the cuboid $=2(15 \times 5+5 \times 5+15 \times 5) \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
⇒ Surface area of the cuboid $=2(75+25+75) \mathrm{cm}^{2}=350 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-386.jpg?height=270&width=616&top_left_y=1199&top_left_x=1201)

## VOLUME OF A COMBINATION OF SOLIDS

Whenever a solid is formed by combining two or more solids, then the amount of matter present in the combination of solids is equal to the sum of amounts of matter in the constituting solids.
$\therefore \quad$ Volume of new solid $=$ Sum of the volumes of the individual solids.

Did You
Know!
If the volume is same for different solids then sphere is shape that has smallest surface area. That is why it appears in nature in the form of water drops, bubbles and planets.

## ILLUSTRATION : 4

A solid wooden toy is in the shape of a right circular cone mounted on a hemisphere. If the radius of the hemisphere is 4.2 cm and the total height of the toy is 10.2 cm , find the volume of the wooden toy.

## SOLUTION :

We have, $V O^{\prime}=10.2 \mathrm{~cm}, O A=O O^{\prime}=4.2 \mathrm{~cm}$
Let $r$ be the radius of the hemisphere and h be the height of the conical part of the toy.
Then, $r=O A=4.2 \mathrm{~cm}$.

$$
h=V O=V O^{\prime}-O O^{\prime}=(10.2-4.2) \mathrm{cm}=6 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

Also, radius of the base of the cone $=O A=r=4.2 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Volume of the wooden toy $=$ Volume of the conical part

$$
\begin{aligned}
& + \text { Volume of the hemispherical part }=\left(\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h+\frac{2 \pi}{3} r^{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{3}=\frac{\pi r^{2}}{3}(h+2 r) \mathrm{cm}^{3} \\
& =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 4.2 \times 4.2 \times(6+2 \times 4.2) \mathrm{cm}^{3}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 4.2 \times 4.2 \times 14.4 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}=266.11 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-386.jpg?height=405&width=308&top_left_y=2083&top_left_x=1509)

## CONVERSION OF SOLID FROM ONE SHAPE TO ANOTHER

- When a solid is converted from one shape to other, then its volume remains same only its shape and size changes
- If a solid is converted into a number of small identical solids of same or a different shape then

$$
\text { Number of small items }=\frac{\text { Volume of larger object }}{\text { Volume of smaller object }}
$$

## ILLUSTRATION : 5

A metallic sphere of radius 4.2 cm is melted and recast into the shape of a cylinder of radius 6 cm . Find the height of the cylinder. Take $\boldsymbol{\pi}=\frac{\mathbf{2 2}}{\mathbf{7}}$ unless stated otherwise.

## SOLUTION :

Let the height of the cylinder made $=h \mathrm{~cm}$.
Its radius $=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
Volume of the cylinder made = Volume of the given metallic sphere of radius 4.2 cm
$\Rightarrow \pi \times(6)^{2} \times h=\frac{4}{3} \pi \times(4.2)^{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \Rightarrow h=\frac{4.2 \times 4.2 \times 4.2}{3 \times 9} \mathrm{~cm}=2.774 \mathrm{~cm}$

## ILLUSTRATION : $\mathbf{6}$

A container shaped like a right circular cylinder having diameter 12 cm and height 15 cm is full of ice-cream. The ice-cream is to be filled into cones of height 12 cm and diameter 6 cm , having a hemispherical shape on the top. Find the number of such cones which can be filled with ice-cream.

## SOLUTION :

Volume of one ice-cream cone as shown in figure
$=$ Volume of conical part + Volume of hemispherical part
$=\frac{1}{3} \pi \times(3)^{2} \times 9+\frac{2}{3} \pi(3)^{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{3}=45 \pi \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
Volume of the ice-cream in the cylindrical container (of height 15 cm and diameter 12 cm )
$=\pi \times(6)^{2} \times 15$
Let the number of cones made be $n$.
Then, $n \times 45 \pi=\pi \times(6)^{2} \times 15 \Rightarrow 45 n=36 \times 15 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=12$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-387.jpg?height=503&width=458&top_left_y=1448&top_left_x=1416)
∴ Required number of ice-cream cone $=12$

## ILLUSTRATION : 7

How many silver coins, 1.75 cm in diameter and of thickness 2 mm , must be melted to form a cuboid of dimensions $5.5 \mathrm{~cm} \times 10 \mathrm{~cm} \times 3.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?

## SOLUTION :

Let the required number of coins be $n$. Volume of $n$ coins $=$ Volume of the cuboid
Then, $n \times\left\{\pi \times\left(\frac{1.75}{2}\right)^{2} \times(.2)\right\}=5.5 \times 10 \times 3.5 \Rightarrow n \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{175}{200} \times \frac{175}{200} \times \frac{2}{10}=55 \times \frac{35}{10}$
$\Rightarrow n \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{8} \times \frac{7}{8} \times 2=55 \times 35 \Rightarrow n=\frac{55 \times 35 \times 16}{7 \times 11}=5 \times 5 \times 16=400$

## FRUSTUM OF A CONE

If a cone is cut by a plane parallel to the base of the cone, then the portion between the plane and base is called the frustum of the cone.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-388.jpg?height=511&width=1191&top_left_y=479&top_left_x=443)

Let $h$ be the height, $\ell$ the slant height and $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ the radii of the ends ( $r_{1}>r_{2}$ ) of the frustum of a cone. Then we can directly find the volume, the curved surface area and the total surface area of frustum by using the formulae given below :
(i) Volume of the frustum of cone $=\frac{1}{3} \pi h\left(r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+r_{1} r_{2}\right)$
(ii) Curved surface area of the frustum of cone $=\pi\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) \ell$, where $\ell=\sqrt{h^{2}+\left(r_{1}-r_{2}\right)^{2}}$
(iii) Total surface area of the frustum of the cone $=\pi \ell\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right)+\pi r_{1}^{2}+\pi r_{2}^{2}$
(iv) Total surface area of a bucket $=\pi\left[\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) \ell+r_{2}\right]$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-388.jpg?height=369&width=337&top_left_y=1009&top_left_x=1480)

## ILLUSTRATION : 8

If the radii of the circular ends of a conical bucket which is 45 cm high, are 28 cm and 7 cm , find the capacity of the bucket (Use $\boldsymbol{\pi}=22 / 7$ ).

## SOLUTION :

Clearly, bucket forms a frustum of a cone such that the radii of its circular ends are $r_{1}=28 \mathrm{~cm}, r_{2}=7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and height $h=45 \mathrm{~cm}$. Therefore, capacity of the bucket = volume of the frustum.
Capacity of the bucket $=\frac{1}{3} \pi h\left(r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+r_{1} r_{2}\right) \Rightarrow$ Capacity of the bucket $=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 45\left(28^{2}+7^{2}+28 \times 7\right)$
⇒ Capacity of the bucket $=22 \times 15 \times(28 \times 4+7+28) \Rightarrow$ Capacity of the bucket $=330 \times 147 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
⇒ Capacity of the bucket $=48510 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$

## ILLUSTRATION : 9

The slant height of a frustum of a cone is $\mathbf{4 ~ c m}$ and the perimeters (circumference) of its circular ends are $\mathbf{1 8 ~ c m}$ and 6 cm . Find the curved surface area of the frustum.

## SOLUTION :

$r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ be the radii of the ends of the frustum. $2 \pi r_{1}=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $2 \pi r_{2}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ i.e., $r_{1}=\frac{9}{\neq} \mathrm{cm}$ and $r_{2}=\frac{3}{\neq} \mathrm{cm}$
Slant height, $\ell=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. Then, curved surface area $=\pi\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) \times \ell=48 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$

## IMPORTANT POINTS TO BE REMEMBER

- Volume of water released by a pipe/canal = Area of cross-section × Rate of flow × Time
- The cross-section is a circle in the case of a pipe.
- The cross-section is a rectangle or a trapezium in the case of a canal.
- Volume of water standing in a field $=$ Area of field × Height of standing water
- If rate of flow is given in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$, it can be converted into $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{sec}$ by multiplying with $5 / 18$.


## CASE STUDY-1 : Irrigation of the field by Canal

When a field is irrigated by the water of canal, then the required to irrigate it is dependent on the speed of the water, area of cross-section of the canal, area of the field and height of the water standing in the field required. It is to be noted that in the given problem, we always consider that canal is fully filled with water it means height of the water in the canal and height of canal are same if it is not mentioned any thing about the height of the water in the canal.

Here we also consider that water in the field does not absorbed by field or vaporise by such rays during the irrigation period. The cross-section of the canal means the section of the canal appeared when we cut the canal by a plane across its length.

The cross-section of the canal may be in a shape of a trapezium, rectangle or square etc.

When a field is irrigated by canal, then,
Volume of water comes out from the canal in given time period
$=$ Volume of water standing in the field in the given time period.

⇒ (Area of the cross-section of a canal)
$=($ Area of the field $) \times$ Height of the standing water
⇒ (Area of cross section of the canal) $\times$ (Speed of water) × (Given time period)
$=$ (Area of the field) $\times$ (Height of the standing water)

Note :
(ii) $1 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=\frac{5}{18} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$.
(iv) 1 hectare $=10000 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-389.jpg?height=505&width=199&top_left_y=1725&top_left_x=938)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-389.jpg?height=397&width=269&top_left_y=1441&top_left_x=367)
× (Distance travelled by water in given time period)
(i) If cross-section of canal is a trapezium, then its area
$=\frac{1}{2}[($ width of the base $)]+[($ width of the top $)] \times[($ Height of the canal $)]$
(iii) 1 acre $=4046$ sq. m.

CASE-I : Area of the irrigated field
Illustration: Water in a canal $4 m$ wide \& $1.5 m$ deep is flowing with velocity
$12 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hour}$. How much area will it irrigate in 30 minutes, if 9 cm of standing water is required for irrigation?

Sol. Given, speed of water $=12 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=12000 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{hr}$.
width of water canal $=4 \mathrm{~m}$; depth of water canal $=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$
Irrigation time $=30 \mathrm{~min}$ and required standing water $=9 \mathrm{~cm}$
Cross-section area of canal $=$ width of canal × depth of canal $=(4 \times 1.5)=6 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

Volume of water flowed from canal in 1 hour
$=$ cross-section area × speed of water $=6 \times 12000=72000 \mathrm{~m}^{3}$.
Volume of water flowed from canal in $30=\frac{72000}{2}=36000 \mathrm{~m}^{3}$.

Height of standing water $=9 \mathrm{~cm}=0.09 \mathrm{~m}$.
Vol. of water flowed in $30 \mathrm{~min}=$ Vol. of water standing in field
$\Rightarrow 36000=$ area of irrigated field × height
⇒ Area of irrigated field $=\frac{36000}{0.09}=400,000 m^{2}=40$ hectares

CASE-II : Time required to irrigate the given field.
Illustration: Water is flowing at the rate of $5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ through a pipe of diameter 14 cm into a field which is 55 m long and 44 m wide. Determine the time required to irrigate the field if 7cm of standing water is required for irrigation.

Sol. Suppose the required time $=x$ hours. Since, the water is flowing at the rate of $5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.
∴ Length of water column in $x$ hours $=5 x \mathrm{~km}=5000 x$ metres.
Clearly, the water column forms a cylinder whose radius (r)
$=\frac{14}{2} \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{7}{100} \mathrm{~m}$. and length $(\mathrm{h})=5000 x$ metres.
Volume of water flowing through cylindrical pipe in x hours $= 77 \times \mathrm{m}^{3}$. Also,

Volume of the water standing in the field in x hours $=154 \mathrm{~m}^{3}$.

But, volume of water flowing through the pipe in x hours
$=$ volume of water standing in field in x hours.
$\Rightarrow 77 \mathrm{x}=154 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2$
Hence, the time required to irrigate the field is 2 hours.

## CASE-III : Height of standing water.

Illustration: 21 hectare field is irrigated through a canal of 5.8 m wide and 1.5 m deep with speed of water $3.5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. Find the level of water in the field during 45 minutes.
Sol. Width of canal $=5.8 \mathrm{~m}$, Depth of canal $=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$
Distance travelled by water in l hours $=35 \mathrm{~km}$
Distance travelled by water in $45 \mathrm{~min} .=\frac{3.5}{60} \times 45=2.625 \mathrm{~km}=2625 \mathrm{~m}$
Now, volume of the water comes out from the canal in 45 minutes $=$ Volume of water standing in field in 45 minutes.
$\Rightarrow 1.5 \times 5.8 \times 2625=210000 \times$ Height of standing water in field
$\frac{22837.5}{210000}=$ Height of standing water in field $=0.108 \mathrm{~m}=10.8 \mathrm{~cm}$.

Q. 1 A pipe is connects into a cylindrical tank, which is 10 m is diameter and 7 m deep. If water flows through the pipe at the rate $22 \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{h}$, in how much time will the tank be filled?
